    "doc": "<h2 class=\"doc_title\">Punjab National Bank vs Nct Of Delhi &amp; Anr on 25 June, 2021</h2>\n\n<h3 class=\"doc_author\">Author: <a href=\"/search/?formInput=authorid:a-malhotra\">Anu Malhotra</a></h3>\n\n<h3 class=\"doc_bench\">Bench: <a href=\"/search/?formInput=benchid:a-malhotra\">Anu Malhotra</a></h3>\n\n<pre id=\"pre_1\">                          *      IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI\n                          +      CRL.M.C. 2696/2019 &amp; CRL.M.A.31239/2019\n                                                               Order reserved on: 05.11.2019\n                                                               Date of decision : 25.06.2021\n\n                                 PUNJAB NATIONAL BANK                            ..... Petitioner\n                                                   Through:    Mr. Dayan Krishnan, Sr. Adv. with\n                                                               Mr. Nishant Joshi, Mr. Nikhil\n                                                               Varshney, Advs.\n                                                   Versus\n                                 NCT OF DELHI &amp; ANR                               ..... JUSTICE ANU MALHOTRA\n\n                                                   JUDGMENT\n</pre><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_1\">                          ANU MALHOTRA, J.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_2\">                          1.     The petitioner, the Punjab National Bank, vide the present petition\n                          instituted against the National Capital Territory of Delhi, through its\n                          Standing Counsel (Criminal) arrayed as the respondent No.1 and the\n                          Reserve Bank of India, arrayed as the respondent No.2 has assailed the\n                          impugned order dated 11.3.2019, of the learned Trial Court of the\n                          Metropolitan Magistrate-05, Patiala House Courts, New Delhi in CC\n                          No.16711-18 whereby the proposed accused persons was sought to be\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_1\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_2\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_3\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                           1</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_4\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_5\">18:28</span>\n                           summoned as accused by the respondent No.2 herein, i.e., the Reserve Bank\n                          of India, through its complaint No. 16711/18 were summoned in respect of\n                          an offence punishable under <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_1\">Section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation Act,\n                          1949, for the date of hearing 24.5.2019.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_3\">                          2.      The said complaint was instituted by the Reserve Bank of India, under\n                          <a href=\"/doc/444619/\" id=\"a_2\">Section 200</a> of the Cr.P.C., 1973, read with <a href=\"/doc/931358/\" id=\"a_3\">Section 47</a> of the Banking\n                          Regulation Act, 1949 on behalf of the Reserve Bank of India, the\n                          complainant corporation seeking the prosecution of all the said 11 accused\n                          persons for the commission of the offences punishable under <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_4\">Section 46</a> of\n                          the Banking Regulation Act, 1949, read with <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_5\">Section 120B</a> of the Indian\n                          Penal Code, 1860.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_4\">                          3.      The proposed accused summoned as accused are arrayed in the\n                          Memo of Parties in the Ct. No. 16711-18 which reads to the effect:-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1\">                               &quot; 1. PUNJAB NATIONAL BANK\n                                  A bank constituted under Banking Companies\n                                  (Acquisition and Transfer of Undertaking)Act, 170.\n                                  Having its corporate office at:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_2\">                                  Plot No.4, Sector-10 Dwark\n                                  New Delhi-110075\n                                  Through its Managing Director and CEO Mr.Sunil Mehta\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_3\">                               2. Shri S.K.Srivastava\n                                    Chief Manager, Punjab National Bank\n                                   International Banking Division\n                                   Corporate Office, Plot No.4\n                                   Sector 10, Dwarka\n                                   New Delhi-110075.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_5\">                          4.     Vide order dated 20.5.2019 whilst notice of the petition was issued to\n                          the respondent Nos. 1 and 2 to enter appearance, it was observed to the\n                          effect:-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_13\">                                     &quot;The counsel for the second respondent (complainant) on\n                                     being asked was unable to give any explanation as to why a\n                                     general reference has been made in the complaint leading to\n                                     similar general mention in the summoning order to <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_6\">Section\n                                     46</a> of Banking Regulation Act, 1949, without the specific\n                                     penal clause being indicated.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_14\">                                     The second respondent may file response within two weeks.\n data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_6\">                          5.      Reply to the petition was filed by the respondent No.2 to which the\n                          petitioner herein also filed the rejoinder.\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_7\">                          6.      Crl.M.A.No.35018/2019 filed on behalf of the petitioner seeking\n                          condonation of delay in filing the rejoinder was allowed and the delay\n                          condoned. Likewise Crl.M.A. No. 35019/2019 though erroneously\n                          mentioned as Crl.M.A. No. 35018/2019 in proceedings dated 5.11.2019 filed\n                          on behalf of the petitioner seeking condonation of two days\u201f delay in filing\n                          the reply to the application, i.e., Crl.M.A. No.31239/2019, filed on behalf of\n                          the respondent No.2 seeking a vacation of stay granted vide order dated\n                          20.5.2019 was also allowed and the delay condoned. The typographical\n                          error in proceedings dated 5.11.2019 mentioning the said application as\n                          being the Crl.M.A. No. 35018/2019 is rectified to read as Crl.M.A. No.\n                          35019/2019 as filed on behalf of the petitioner.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_8\">                          7.     Submissions were addressed on behalf of the petitioner and the\n                          respondents qua Crl.M.C. No. 2696/2018 &amp; Crl.M.A. No. 10783/2019 i.e.,\n                          the application that was filed by the petitioner for ad interim relief qua\n                          which vide order dated 20.5.2019, the proceedings before the learned Trial\n                          Court had been stayed which interim order continued vide order dated\n                          17.7.2019 to 20.5.2019 and the said interim order vide order dated 5.11.2019\n                          was extended till pronouncement of the judgment. CRL.M.A.31239/2019\n                          has been filed by the Respondent no.2, the RBI seeking vacation of order\n                          dated 20.05.2019.\n</p>\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_16\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_17\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_18\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                               4</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_19\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_20\">18:28</span>\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_9\">                           8.      As per the averments made in the complaint filed by the complainant\n                          of Ct. 16711/2018 i.e., the respondent No.2, arrayed as petitioner therein, it\n                          was submitted that the complainant corporation i.e., the Reserve Bank of\n                          India had been constrained to invoke the jurisdiction of the Court under\n                          <a href=\"/doc/444619/\" id=\"a_7\">Section 200</a> of the Cr.P.C., 1973, seeking the prosecution against the\n                          persons named as accused and thereby their punishment inter alia under\n                          <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_8\">Section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949, read with <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_9\">Section 120B</a> of\n                          the Indian Penal Code, 1860 as the said accused persons had conspired with\n                          each other wilfully, deliberately and made false and misleading statements\n                          and had furnished false information relating to the affairs of the accused\n                          No.1, bank to the complainant corporation. It was submitted further through\n                          the said complaint that the Punjab National Bank had been arrayed through\n                          its Managing Director and CEO Sh. Sunil Mehra who was responsible for\n                          the day-to-day affairs of the Punjab National Bank situated at Plot No.4,\n                          Sector-10, Dwarka, New Delhi, 110075 and had superintendence, control\n                          and direction over the affairs of the bank.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_10\">                          9.      The respondent no.2 Smt. Usha Ananthasubramanian, as per the\n                          complaint was appointed as Managing Director &amp; CEO of the accused no.1,\n                          the Punjab National Bank, the petitioner herein by the Government of India\n                          vide its Notification dated F.No.4/2/2015-BO-1 dated 14.8.2015 and she was\n                          the Managing Director and CEO till 4.5.2017 and had assumed charge of the\n                          Managing Director &amp; CEO of the accused No.1, Punjab National Bank, on\n                          14.8.2015 and continued in the post till 4.5.2017. The complainant alleged\n                          that the accused No.2, Smt. Usha Ananthasubramanian, being a Managing\n                          Director &amp; CEO of the accused No.1, Punjab National Bank, during the\n                          relevant period was the person responsible for the day-to-day affairs of the\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_21\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_22\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_23\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                               5</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_24\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_25\">18:28</span>\n                           bank having superintendence, control and direction over the affairs of the\n                          bank. It was alleged further in the complainant that during the relevant\n                          period in which the violation occurred on behalf of the bank she exercised\n                          her powers and wilfully approved and signed false statements and the said\n                          false statements were made to the Reserve Bank of India and that her act\n                          denoted that she had knowledge of the said false statements which were\n                          approved by her.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_11\">                          10.     It was alleged through the complaint further that the accused No.3,\n                          R.S.Sangapure was the Executive Director of the accused No.1, Punjab\n                          National Bank, and had been appointed in his present post by the\n                          Government of India vide its Notification bearing No. F. No. 4/5/2012-BO-1\n                          dated 13.3.2014 and as an Executive Director of the accused No.1, he was\n                          the person responsible for the Information Technology Division of the\n                          accused No.1, Punjab National Bank, in addition to other divisions and had\n                          assumed the charge of Executive Director of the accused No.1, Punjab\n                          National Bank on 13.3.2014 and was in the said post till 28.2.2018 and also\n                          so posted during the period in which the violation of the said <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_10\">Banking\n                          Regulation Act, 1949</a>, occurred . The complainant further alleged that the\n                          said accused No.3, R.S.Sangapure wilfully made false statements which bear\n                          his signatures stating his knowledge and approval to the false statements\n                          made by accused No.1, Punjab National Bank to the Reserve Bank of India.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_12\">                          11.     As per the complaint, the accused No.4, Sanjiv Sharan, is the\n                          Executive Director of the accused No.1 and was appointed in his present\n                          post by the Government of India vide its notification bearing No.\n                          F.No.4/5(2)/2016-BO.1 dated 15.9.2016 and was continuing in the post of\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_26\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_27\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_28\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                            6</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_29\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_30\">18:28</span>\n                           the Executive Director of the accused No.1 and had assumed charge as\n                          Executive Director of the accused No.1, Punjab National Bank, on\n                          15.1.2016.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_13\">                          12.       As per the complaint, accused No.5 Nehal Ahad was the General\n                          Manager (under suspension) of the accused No.1, accused No.6 Rakesh\n                          Kumar was the General Manager (Retired) of the accused No.1, accused No.\n                          Sunil Mohan was the Deputy General Manager (Retired) of the accused\n                          No.1, accused No.8 I.J.Arora was the General Manger of the Accused No.1,\n                          accused No.9 T.R. Venkateswaran was the Assistant General Manager of\n                          the accused No.1, the accused No.10 I.P.Singh, was the Assistant General\n                          Manager of the accused No.1 and the accused No.11 S.K.Srivastava, was the\n                          Chief Manager of the accused no.1.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_14\">                          13.       The complainant, i.e., the Reserve Bank of India, further submitted\n                          that the necessary permission under <a href=\"/doc/12704/\" id=\"a_11\">Section 197</a> of the Cr.P.C., 1973, had\n                          been obtained from the Government of India to prosecute the accused Nos.\n                          2, 3 and 4.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_15\">                          14.       Inter alia, the complainant, i.e., the Reserve Bank of India, placed\n                          reliance on the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_12\">Section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation Act,\n                          1949, seeking prosecution of the accused persons thereunder. The said\n                          provision i.e., <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_13\">Section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949, reads to the\n                          effect:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_18\">                                    &quot;46. Penalties.--\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_19\">                                    (1) Whoever in any return, balance-sheet or other document 1[or\n                                    in any information required or furnished] by or under or for the\n                                    purposes of any provision of this Act, wilfully makes a statement\n                                    which is false in any material particular, knowing it to be false,\n                                    or wilfully omits to make a material statement, shall be\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_31\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_32\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_33\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                               7</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_34\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_35\">18:28</span>\n                                  punishable with imprisonment for a term which may extend to\n                                 three years and shall also be liable to fine.\n                                 (2) If any person fails to produce any book, account or other\n                                 document or to furnish any statement or information which under\n                                 sub-section (2) of <a href=\"/doc/222598/\" id=\"a_14\">section 35</a> it is his duty to produce or furnish,\n                                 or to answer any question relating to the business of a banking\n                                 company which is asked by 2[an officer making an inspection or\n                                 scrutiny under that section] he shall be punishable with a fine\n                                 which may extend to 3[two thousand rupees] in respect of each\n                                 offence, and if he persists in such refusal, to a further fine which\n                                 may extend to 4[one hundred rupees] for every day during which\n                                 the offence continues.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_20\">                                 (3) If any deposits are received by a banking company in contra-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_16\">                                 vention of an order under clause (a) of sub-section (4) of <a href=\"/doc/222598/\" id=\"a_15\">section\n                                 35</a>, every director or other officer of the banking company, unless\n                                 he proves that the contravention took place without his\n                                 knowledge or that he exercised all due diligence to prevent it\n                                 shall be deemed to be guilty of such contravention and shall be\n                                 punishable with a fine which may extend to twice the amount of\n                                 the deposits so received.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_17\">                                 (4) If any other provision of this Act is contravened or if any\n                                 default is made in--\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_18\">                                 (i) complying with any requirement of this Act or of any order,\n                                 rule or direction made or condition imposed thereunder, or\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_19\">                                 (ii) carrying out the terms of, or the obligations under, a scheme\n                                 sanctioned under sub-section (7) of <a href=\"/doc/1829498/\" id=\"a_16\">section 45</a>, by any person,\n                                 such person shall be punishable with fine which may extend to\n                                 8[fifty thousand rupees or twice the amount involved in such\n                                 contravention or default where such amount is quantifiable,\n                                 whichever is more, and where a contravention or default is a\n                                 continuing one, with a further fine which may extend to two\n                                 thousand and five hundred rupees] for every day, during which\n                                 the contravention or default continues.]]\n                                 (5) Where a contravention or default has been committed by a\n                                 company, every person who, at the time the contravention or\n                                 default was committed, was in charge of, and was responsible to,\n                                 the company, for the conduct of the business of the company, as\n                                 well as the company, shall be deemed to be guilty of the contra-\n</p>\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_36\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_37\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_38\">By:SUMIT GHAI      punished accordingly: Provided that nothing contained in this\n                                 sub-section shall render any such person liable to any\n                                 punishment provided in this Act if he proves that the\n                                 contravention or default was committed without his knowledge or\n                                 that he exercised all due diligence to prevent the contravention\n                                 or default.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_21\">                                 (6) Notwithstanding anything contained in sub-section (5), where\n                                 a contravention or default has been committed by a company,\n                                 and it is proved that the same was committed with the consent or\n                                 connivance of, or is attributable to any gross negligence on the\n                                 part of, any director, manager, secretary or other officer of the\n                                 company, such director, manager, secretary or other officer shall\n                                 also be deemed to be guilty of that contravention or default and\n                                 shall be liable to be proceeded against and punished\n                                 accordingly.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_22\">                                 Explanation.--For the purposes of this section,--\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_23\">                                 (a) &quot;company&quot; means any body corporate and includes a firm or\n                                 other association of individuals, and\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_24\">                                 (b) &quot;director&quot;, in relation to a firm, means a partner in the\n                                 firm.]&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_25\">                          15.     The complainant corporation further submits that it is a statutory\n                          corporation constituted in accordance with the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/222598/\" id=\"a_17\">Section 35</a> of\n                          the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 and that the complainant corporation is\n                          inter alia vested with power to &quot; cause an inspection by one or more of its\n                          officers of any banking company and its books and accounts&quot; and that from\n                          8.2.2016 to 12.2.2016, the complainant corporation,       in exercise of the\n                          power conferred on it under <a href=\"/doc/222598/\" id=\"a_18\">Section 35</a> of the Banking Regulation Act.\n                          1949, carried out a detailed inspection of the systems and procedures of the\n                          Information Technology (IT) aspects being followed in accused No.1, Punjab\n                          National Bank, including IT operations, Information security, IS/IT Audit,\n                          function and extent of IT risk assessment. The said inspection was\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_41\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_42\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_43\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                             9</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_44\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_45\">18:28</span>\n                           conducted by the complainant corporation through its officers Shri R\n                          Lakshmi Kanta Rao, General Manager, Shri. Manmohan- Assistant General\n                          Manager        and   Shri   Praveen     Bhavsar-   Manager    and    the   said\n                          order/communication vide e mail dated 29.01.2016 was issued by the\n                          complainant corporation, Reserve Bank of India, to the accused No.1,\n                          Punjab National Bank informing them of the decision to carry out such\n                          inspection, copy of which is Annexure-P-4.\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_21\">                                      &quot;It has been decided to carry out a focused IT Examination\n                                      of Punjab National Bank from 8th February to 12th\n                                      February, 2016.The RBI team would be headed by Shri RLK\n                                      Rao, GM, RBI, Chennal with the following team members:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_22\">                                      Shri Manmohan, DBS, CO, AGM and Shri Pravin Bhavsra,\n                                      DBS CO, Manager\n                                      Sl.No.\n                                      Name\n                                      Designation\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_46\">                                1.</span>\n                                      Shri Manmohan, DBS, Co\n                                      Assistant General Manager\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_47\">                                2.</span>\n                                      Shri Pravin Bhavasr, DBS Co\n                                      Manager\n                                      We request your bank to provide them necessary support and\n                                      co-operation in conducting the IT examination.\n                                      Regards\n                                      Minal A. Jain\n                                      GM, RBI &amp; SSM-PNB&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_26\">                          16.        It was further submitted through the complaint that after conducting\n                          the detailed inspection of the systems and procedures of Information\n                          Technology (IT) aspects including its IT operations, Information security,\n                          IS/IT Audit function and extent of IT risk assessment being followed in\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_48\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_49\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_50\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                                10</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_51\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_52\">18:28</span>\n                           accused No.1, Punjab National Bank, the complainant corporation prepared\n                          a comprehensive report       made during the said examination which was\n                          forwarded to the accused No.1, Punjab National Bank vide an e-mail dated\n                          06.06.2016.            ix) Information security policy did not cover important\n                                     aspects like alignment of IT strategy /plans with business\n                                     strategy, specific knowledge and skill sets required at\n                                     various levels, periodic training and continuous professional\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" Further,\n                                     legal risks arising out of cyber laws were not assessed.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_28\">                                       3          IT Operations.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_29\">                                    3.1 Bank had implemented ISO 27001 standards which was\n                                    valid till May 2016. It had no plans to evaluate its IT function\n                                    in respect of frameworks like COBIT 5.\n</p>\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_58\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_59\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_60\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                           12</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_61\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_62\">18:28</span>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_30\">                                     3.2As per the information provided, there are 80 applications\n                                    in use in the bank and as many as 71 of them have been treated\n                                    as critical. However, the basis of categorizing them as critical\n                                    and whether Business Impact Analysis (BIA) was carried out\n                                    or not was not clearly documented. Further the purpose of\n                                    such categorization as to whether closer monitoring would be\n                                    needed in respect of them was not clearly articulated.\n                                    3.3 In respect of 12 out of 71 critical applications of the bank,\n                                    neither the source code was available with the bank nor\n                                    Escrow Agreement with the vendors for safe custody of the\n                                    source code was in place. In respect of 15 critical applications,\n                                    no information was available with the bank regarding escrow\n                                    status (e.g. Kondor + in Treasury, Credit Card issuance and\n                                    SOC etc.).\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_31\">                                    3.4 Out of 71 critical applications, 18 applications were\n                                    developed in house. However, there were no enabling HR\n                                    policy provisions to ensure that the staff involved in\n                                    development continues to be available for debugging,\n                                    enhancements and changes in the application.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_32\">                                    3.5 Bank had not planned or envisioned implementation of\n                                    Single Enterprise Application as it had 80 applications\n                                    consisting of in-house developed, purchased and outsourced\n                                    activities. CBS was not integrated in automated fashion with\n                                    various critical applications i.e., no Straight through\n                                    processing (STP) was envisaged and the middleware used\n                                    involved manual interventions giving rise to various risks.\n                                    3.6 Back up policy of the bank did not stipulate that data in the\n                                    backup tapes needed to be in encrypted form. Data tapes\n                                    containing data were being held at alternate locations but the\n                                    data was not encrypted except in case of CBS data. Concurrent\n                                    audit pointed out that media of software kept in the library was\n                                    not periodically tested for integrity of data. Back up\n                                    restoration periodic testing / readability of backed up tapes\n                                    was not done for RTGS as pointed out by Deloitte.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_33\">                                    3.7 Process audit reports of Deloitte pointed out that Network\n                                    racks were kept in unlocked condition on many occasions.\n                                    3.8 It was observed that the employee verification was done\n                                    only at the recruitment level. The bank needed to have a policy\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_63\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_64\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_65\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                            13</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_66\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_67\">18:28</span>\n                                     to perform additional background checks of the employee\n                                    while assigning him/her any critical responsibility\n                                    (particularly related to Information Technology) like posting\n                                    in Datacenter etc.\n                                    3.9The bank did not carry out financial impact of non-fixing of\n                                    bugs.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_34\">                                    3.10 User manuals pertaining to CBS, GBM and IBS\n                                    applications have not been updated since September 2013.\n                                    3.11 In case of incidents reported at Incident Management\n                                    Portal, Root Cause Analysis (RCA) of the incidents had to be\n                                    reported within 15 days of reporting of incident. It was\n                                    observed that in case of many incidents RCA was not done.\n                                    3.12 During the period from 01-04-2014 to 31-01-2016 there\n                                    were numerous occasions when same transactions in CBS\n                                    were executed multiple times due to wrong structure of\n                                    transaction file or wrong name of transaction file or due to\n                                    other technical glitches.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_35\">                                    3.13 There were incidents during the period from 01-04-2014\n                                    to 31-01-2016 wherein many transactions were declined as\n                                    UTR series was exhausted at CBS. On many occasions,\n                                    number of transactions was declined due to slow response\n                                    from CBS which was mainly due to congestion at CBS ports\n                                    assigned to ATM and POS transactions.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_36\">                                    3.14 There is need to improve the monitoring of capacity\n                                    utilization of storages, databases, memory, CPU etc. There\n                                    was an instance when IMPS transactions were stopped due to\n                                    database log storage got full, AML server stopped functioning\n                                    as archival logs of AML server got full and SMS delivery was\n                                    delayed as indexes on tables of SMS alerts database reached\n                                    watermark level.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_37\">                                    3.15 Though the bank had policy of testing any sort of\n                                    modification in test environment before implementing the same\n                                    in production, it was observed that post version migration of\n                                    IBS, done during 13-07-2014 and 23-07-2014, the application\n                                    was not functioning as intended and several performance\n                                    issues were encountered. Similarly in another case, ESQ\n                                    Database Server running on Windows 2008R Service Pack 1\n                                    crashed when the server was restarted after deployment of\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_68\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_69\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_70\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                          14</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_71\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_72\">18:28</span>\n                                     Microsoft Security Bulletin patches for November 2015. The\n                                    bank needed to test each aspect of the application / patches in\n                                    DAT before implementing it in production.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_38\">                                    3.16 During the month of Sep 2015, on four days the bank had\n                                    not taken back-up of PNBFIN database due to some problem\n                                    in tape library. The bank was needed to ensure that daily\n                                    backup was taken without fail.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_39\">                                    3.17 As per banks policy, software developed in-house should\n                                    be audited by IAD before shifting to production. But it was\n                                    observed by concurrent auditor that &quot;online loan eligibility\n                                    assessment and lead generation system&quot; was made live on 04-\n                                    11-2015 without any audit sign-off.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_40\">                                    3.18 Physical security of IT assets and periodic physical\n                                    verification was being done annually. The complainant further alleged through the complaint that the\n                          accused persons wilfully made false statement despite being aware of the\n                          risks and repercussions of failing to integrate SWIFT with CBS and\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_93\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_94\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_95\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                             19</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_96\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_97\">18:28</span>\n                           introducing STP between SWIFT messaging and CBS. The complainant\n                          submits that risks were frequently flagged and highlighted by the Reserve\n                          Bank of India and that it was shocking that the accused consistently and\n                          deliberately made false statements to Reserve Bank of India and it was\n                          always the same set of persons from the accused No.l bank, Punjab\n                          National Bank        making those false statements thereby indicating a\n                          conspiracy to commit fraud.     It is further submitted on behalf of the\n                          complainant corporation that it was pertinent to note that on or about\n                          3.8.2016, the complainant corporation had issued a confidential circular\n                          bearing No. DBS.CO/CSITE/BC.4/33.01.001/2016-17 to all scheduled\n                          commercial banks including the accused No.1, Punjab National Bank and\n                          the subject matter of the circular is \u201e Cyber Security Controls-SWIFT\u201f by\n                          which circular the banks were advised to strengthen the controls around\n                          the operating environment for fund transfers through SWIFT or similar\n                          interfaces and the banks were advised to minimize the practice of direct\n                          creation of payment messages in the SWIFT environment without routing\n                          the same through the CBS. The copy of the said circular is stated to have\n                          been annexed as Annexure-P/10 to the complaint. The petitioner further submitted that it has been held by this Court\n                          that a criminal trial is a serious matter, having grave implications for an\n                          accused, who not only has to engage a lawyer and incur substantial\n                          expenditure for defending himself but has also to undergo the ordeal of\n                          appearing in the Court on every date of hearing, sacrificing all his\n                          engagements fixed for that day and if he is in business or a profession, he\n                          has to do it at the cost of affecting his business or profession, as the case\n                          may be and if he is in service, he has to take leave on every date of hearing.\n                          The petitioner further submitted that besides inconvenience and expenditure\n                          involved, a person facing a criminal trial undergoes constant anxiety and\n                          mental agony, as the sword of possible conviction keeps hanging on his head\n                          throughout the trial and therefore when there is a reasonable certainty that\n                          the trial is not going to result in conviction, it would be neither fair nor\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_144\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_145\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_146\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                                29</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_147\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_148\">18:28</span>\n                           reasonable to allow it to proceed against a person. Reliance was placed on\n                          behalf of the petitioner on the verdict of this Court in <a href=\"/doc/85946093/\" id=\"a_31\">Raj Chawla v.\n                          Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) and Ors.</a>; (2010) 154\n                          Comp Cas 16 decided on 12.1.2010.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_59\">                          <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"172610348\" id=\"span_149\">42.     The petitioner has further submitted that in view of the verdict of the\n                          Hon\u201fble Supreme Court in <a href=\"/doc/172610348/\" id=\"a_32\">Asian Resurfacing of Road Agency Pvt. Ltd,\n                          and Ors. v. Central Bureau of Investigation</a>, (2018) 16 SCC 299, it has\n                          been laid dsown by the Hon\u201fble Supreme Court that challenge against an\n                          order of framing of charge may not require meticulous examination of\n                          voluminous material which may be in the nature of a mini trial, as had been\n                          projected by Respondent No. 2 in their reply, as the Court has to only see as\n                          to whether the material on record reasonably connects the accused with the\n                          crime, Inter alia it was submitted that the issue of process by Court is\n                          indubitably a step before framing of charges against the accused and thus\n                          even at the preliminary stage of issue of process, the Court only needs to\n                          examine if there is a reasonable connection between the Petitioner Bank and\n                          purported crime and thus, the argument by the Respondent No. 2, the\n                          Reserve Bank of India, that meticulous examination of voluminous material\n                          would be required by this Court, is impermissible in law and is untenable.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_60\">                          43.     The petitioner through its response to the Crl.M.A. No. 31239/2019\n                          filed by the respondent No.2 reiterated that the submissions made by the\n                          respondent No.2 through its application that the petitioner had filed the\n                          petition seeking quashing of a complaint under <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_33\">Section 46(1)</a> of the Banking\n                          Regulation Act, 1949, was wholly misconceived in as much as the criminal\n                          complaint filed by the respondent No.2, Reserve Bank of India, before the\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_150\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_151\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_152\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                                30</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_153\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_154\">18:28</span>\n                           Trial Court does not specifically state that the complaint is under <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_34\">Section\n                          46(1)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949, but only stated that the same\n                          was under <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_35\">Section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949, and the\n                          petitioner thus reiterated that the respondent No.2 was attempting to revise\n                          its complaint, by making the false averments in the application Crl.M.A. No.\n                          31239/2019 which was impermissible in law.            The petitioner further\n                          submitted that the petitioner bank has exercised its rights available under\n                          law, by filing a petition under <a href=\"/doc/1679850/\" id=\"a_36\">Section 482</a> of the Cr.P.C., 1973, and thus the\n                          submissions to the effect that it was only an outcome of clever attempt and\n                          malafides of the Petitioner Bank to evade prosecution before the Learned\n                          Trial Court, by Respondent No. 2 were puerile, absurd and vehemently\n                          opposed.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_61\">                          44.     The petitioner reiterated through its response to the averments in\n                          paragraph 4 of the application filed by the respondent No.2, Reserve Bank\n                          of India, Crl.M.A. No. 31239/2019 that the paragraphs 2, 8(e), (f), (l) (m)\n                          and (n) of the criminal complaint filed by them before the learned Trial\n                          Court amply made it clear that the complaint was filed for wilfully\n                          furnishing false and incorrect information, which attracted <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_37\">Section 46(1)</a> of\n                          the Banking Regulation Act, 1949. The petitioner further reiterated that it\n                          being named as an accused had all the rights to know under which\n                          provisions of law it is proposed to be prosecuted and non-mentioning of the\n                          exact provision under which the petitioner bank was proposed to be\n                          prosecuted is certainly a fatal flaw that impinges on the entire trial. Inter\n                          alia, the petitioner bank submitted that the summoning of an accused is a\n                          serious matter and that the rights of the petitioner Bank cannot be savagely\n                          prejudiced by not even specifying under which provision of law the\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_155\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_156\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_157\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                               31</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_158\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_159\">18:28</span>\n                           petitioner bank was proposed to be prosecuted.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_62\">                          45.     The petitioner bank submits that it was in the Crl.M.A. No.\n                          31239/2019 filed by the respondent No.2, the complainant, that it for the\n                          first time stated that the complaint had been filed under <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_38\">Section 46(1)</a> of the\n                          Banking Regulation Act, 1949 whilst the petitioner bank had legitimately\n                          expected that it was proposed to be prosecuted under <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_39\">section 46(4)</a> of the\n                          Banking Regulation Act, 1949 as Respondent No. 2 had issued a show cause\n                          notice dated 23.08.2018 to thes Petitioner Bank under <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_40\">Section 46(4)</a> read\n                          with <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_41\">Section 47A(l)(c)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 and the said\n                          show cause notice was an indicator of the charges, in the absence of specific\n                          provisions being set out in the criminal complaint.\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_63\">                          46.     The petitioner bank has thus submitted that as <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_42\">Section 46(4)</a> of the\n                          Banking Regulation Act, 1949, provides for a fine of INR 1,00,00,000/- as\n                          punishment the Petitioner Bank had submitted that upon entering appearance\n                          and that it would be subject to framing of notice under <a href=\"/doc/429078/\" id=\"a_43\">section 251</a> of the\n                          CrPC without having the advantage of knowing the substance of accusation\n                          against it. Inter alia the petitioner submitted that summoning in a criminal\n                          case itself being a serious matter, the argument that the stage of being\n                          informed by the Court of the exact nature of charge is at the stage of\n                          framing of charge is untenable.\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_64\">                          47.     Inter alia, the petitioner submitted that the issue raised by the\n                          Respondent No. 2 that in a warrants trial, the substance of the charge is only\n                          required to be explained at the stage of framing of the charge, is contrary to\n                          the law and irrelevant for the present proceedings with submissions made\n                          by the petitioner to the effect:-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_49\">                                 a. Neither the impugned criminal complaint nor the impugned\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_160\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_161\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_162\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                               32</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_163\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_164\">18:28</span>\n                                  summoning order dated 11.03.2019 make clear what offence the\n                                 accused is summoned for. Under the various offences provided for in\n                                 <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_44\">section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949, only one offence i.e.,\n                                 <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_45\">section 46(1)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 would be an\n                                 offence requiring a warrants trial. In the present case, as indicated\n                                 above, there is no indication as to what offence the accused are being\n                                 summoned.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_50\">                                 b. Even in a warrants trial case, the accused would be entitled to\n                                 know the substance of the charge he is summoned for. A bare perusal\n                                 of Form I, Schedule II of the <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_46\">CrPC</a> establishes that the summary of\n                                 the charge is to be conveyed even on the summons.\n                                 c. Thus, it is not open to either the\n                                 Petitioner Bank nor this Hon&#x27;ble Court to assume that proceedings in\n                                 the instant case are under <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_48\">section 46(1)</a> of the Banking Regulation\n                                 Act, 1949.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_65\">                          48.     <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"574884\" id=\"span_165\">Inter alia the petitioner submits that in the instant case this Court very\n                          carefully and cautiously exercised its right to stay the Trial Court\n                          proceedings and submitted that in the case of Pepsi foods <a href=\"/doc/574884/\" id=\"a_49\">Ltd. V. Judicial\n                          Magistrate</a>, (1998) 5 SCC 749, the Hon\u201fble Supreme Court has observed\n                          that the summoning of an accused in a criminal case is a serious matter and\n                          criminal law cannot be set into motion as a matter of course and it is not that\n                          the complainant has to bring only two witnesses to support his allegations in\n                          the complaint to have criminal law set into motion and that it has also been\n                          submitted that it has also been laid down therein that the order of the\n                          Magistrate summoning the accused must reflect that he has applied his mind\n                          to the facts of the case and the law applicable thereto and has to examine the\n                          nature of allegations made in the complaint and the evidence, both oral and\n                          documentary in support thereof and that would be sufficient for the\n                          complainant to succeed in bringing the charge home to the accused. Inter\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_166\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_167\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_168\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                                   33</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_169\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_170\">18:28</span>\n                           alia the petitioner submitted that, the Magistrate is not a silent spectator at\n                          the time of recording of preliminary evidence before summoning the\n                          accused and has to carefully scrutinize the evidence brought on record and\n                          may even himself put questions to the complainant and his witnesses to\n                          elicit answers to find out the truthfulness of the allegations or otherwise and\n                          then examine if any offence is prima facie committed by all or any of the\n                          accused.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_66\">                          49.     Inter alia the petitioner in response to Crl.M.A. 31239/2019 has\n                          denied that the continuance of stay qua the strial of the complaint before the\n                          Trial Court would fetter the smooth administration of justice and object of\n                          legislature for final and expeditious disposal of criminal proceedings would\n                          become infructuous and rather submitted that it was rather the petitioner\n                          bank which had been significantly prejudiced and inconvenienced in the\n                          event the trial continued against the Petitioner Bank more specifically when\n                          it is not even clear under what sub-section of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_50\">section 46</a> of the Banking\n                          Regulation Act, 1949, the petitioner bank was being charged under.\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_67\">                          50.     It was inter alia submitted by the petitioner that there was no\n                          prejudice whatsoever that been caused to the respondent No.2 with the trial\n                          being stayed till the disposal of the quashing petition. Vide its rejoinder filed by the petitioner to the reply filed by the\n                          respondent No.2, Reserve Bank of India, to the petition Crl.M.C. No.\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_171\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_172\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_173\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                                34</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_174\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_175\">18:28</span>\n                           2696/2019 and the submission as made to Crl.M.A. No.31239/2019, to the\n                          effect that there was no reference to any of the different sub-sections under\n                          <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_51\">Section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 under which the Petitioner\n                          was proposed to be punished having mentioned the impugned criminal\n                          complaint filed by the respondent No.2 nor in the impugned order dated\n                          11.3.2019 of the learned Chief Metropolitan Magistrate, Patiala House\n                          Courts, New Delhi was reiterated and thus it was submitted by the petitioner\n                          bank that on a plain reading of the impugned criminal complaint filed by\n                          Respondent No.2, the Petitioner Bank was not in a position to comprehend/\n                          ascertain under which sub-section of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_52\">Section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation\n                          Act, 1949 it had been called upon to answer. The petitioner bank has thus\n                          submitted that each sub-section of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_53\">Section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation\n                          Act, 1949 is different, and has different ingredients in as much as <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_54\">Section\n                          46(1)</a> punishes who ever wilfully furnishes a statement which is false in any\n                          material particular while <a href=\"/doc/1638992/\" id=\"a_55\">Section 46(2)</a> punishes who ever fails to furnish\n                          any statement under sub Section (2) of <a href=\"/doc/222598/\" id=\"a_56\">Section 35</a> of the Banking Regulation\n                          Act, 1949, and <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_57\">Section 46(4)</a> punishes any other violation of the provisions\n                          of the <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_58\">Banking Regulation Act, 1949</a> and to that extent each sub-section\n                          contemplates some different elements but at the same time prescribes widely\n                          varying punishments i.e., from imprisonment of upto 3 years to a fine which\n                          may extend to INR 1,00,00,000/- (Rupees One Crore only) or fine extending\n                          to INR 1,00,000 (Rupees One Lakh only) for every day, during which\n                          contravention or default continues. The petitioner further submitted that\n                          keeping in mind the differing elements, the defence of Petitioner Bank\n                          would vary vastly and to that extent the fact that the exact sub-section has\n                          not been identified the same prejudices the accused (Petitioner Bank)\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_176\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_177\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_178\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                              35</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_179\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_180\">18:28</span>\n                           greatly. The Petitioner Bank reiterates that considering that the impugned\n                          criminal complaint filed by Respondent No. 2 is to be tried as a summons\n                          trial under Chapter XX of the CrPC, the Petitioner Bank upon entering\n                          appearance would be subjected to the framing of notice under Section 251 of\n                          the CrPC without having had the advantage of knowing the substance of the\n                          accusation against it.\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_69\">                          52.     The petitioner Bank reiterated further that the only indicator of which\n                          sub-section of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_59\">section 46</a> Petitioner Bank is charged with is provided in the\n                          show cause notice issued by Respondent No. 2 dated 23.08.2018 under\n                          <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_60\">Section 47A</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 which states that the same\n                          is punishable under <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_61\">Section 46(4)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949. Inter alia, the petitioner has further submitted that there had been a\n                          total non-application of mind by the learned Trial Court whilst issuing the\n                          impugned summons as nowhere in the impugned criminal complaint nor in\n                          the impugned summoning order dated 11.3.2019 passed by the Trial Court\n                          was there any refence to which provision under the <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_64\">Banking Regulation Act,\n                          1949</a>, the accused was being summoned.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_71\">                          54.     Further preliminary submissions made by the petitioner as a rejoinder\n                          to the response to the petition to the effect that respondent No.2 cannot\n                          amend the criminal complaint filed before the learned Trial Court by way of\n                          issuing a clarification in the reply filed before this Court.\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_72\">                          55.     Inter alia the petitioner submitted to the effect that the reply filed by\n                          Respondent No. 2 before this Court is an egregious abuse of process of law\n                          as Respondent No. 2 desires this Court to construe the clarification issued by\n                          them in their reply, in respect of the charging section under the <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_65\">Banking\n                          Regulation Act, 1949</a>, as an amendment to the impugned criminal complaint\n                          filed by them before the Learned Trial Court, which is unheard of and not\n                          permissible in law.
