The petitioner further submitted that there is an error apparent\n                          on the face of the impugned criminal complaint and the impugned\n                          summoning order passed by the Ld. Trial Court and the same could not be\n                          rectified through the route of issuing a clarification in the reply filed by\n                          Respondent No. 2 before this Hon&#x27;ble Court. Furthermore, the petitioner\n                          submitted that the RespondentNo. 2 is treating the case instituted by them as\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_186\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_187\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_188\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                                  37</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_189\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_190\">18:28</span>\n                           a warrant-triable case solely on the premise that the Petitioner Bank has\n                          acted in violation of <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_66\">Section 46(1)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949\n                          whilst the impugned criminal complaint and also, the impugned summoning\n                          order dated 11.03.2019 passed by the Learned Trial Court do not even make\n                          any reference to <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_67\">Section 46(1)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949. The\n                          petitioner thus submits that such a material improvement in the charging\n                          section, which Respondent No. 2 desires to make, is indefensible and cannot\n                          be allowed.\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_73\">                          56.     Inter alia the petitioner submitted that the complaint must be read as\n                          it is while dealing with issues of cognizance and or summons, especially in a\n                          case where there is no pre-summoning evidence led. The petitioner\n                          reiterated that the Respondent no.2 cannot by way of a counter-\n                          affidavit/reply add or subtract from a complaint already filed before the\n                          Learned Trial Court and claim that the complaint must be read with their\n                          submissions and that such an attempt is contrary to settled legal principles.\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_74\">                          <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"49712\" id=\"span_191\">57.     The petitioner has further submitted that the respondent No.2 has\n                          taken it as if summoning of the petitioner bank in itself is not a serious\n                          matter and the ex-facie illegality in the impugned criminal complaint could\n                          have been rectified at a later juncture, at the time of framing of charges in\n                          terms of <a href=\"/doc/1859700/\" id=\"a_68\">section 246</a> of the of the Cr.P.C., 1973 which is sought to be\n                          negated by the petitioner by placing reliance on the verdict of the Hon\u201fble\n                          Supreme Court in <a href=\"/doc/49712/\" id=\"a_69\">Punjab National Bank and Ors. v. Surendra Prasad\n                          Sinha</a>; 1993 Supp (1) SCC 499 submitting to the effect that thereby the\n                          Hon\u201fble Supreme Court had held that the issuance of process should not be\n                          mechanical nor should be made an instrument of oppression or needless\n                          harassment.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"160796086\" id=\"span_192\">The petitioner has further placed reliance on the verdict of\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_193\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_194\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_195\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                               38</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_196\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_197\">18:28</span>\n                           Hon\u201fble Supreme Court in <a href=\"/doc/160796086/\" id=\"a_70\">Birla Corporation Limited and Ors. v. Adventz\n                          Investments and Holdings Limited and Ors.</a>; 2019 SCC Online 682,\n                          submitting to the effect that it had been <a href=\"/doc/160796086/\" id=\"a_71\">laid down by</a> the Hon\u201fble Supreme\n                          Court to be summoned/to appear before the criminal court as an accused is a\n                          serious matter affecting one&#x27;s dignity and reputation in the society. The\n                          petitioner has thus submitted that in taking recourse to such a serious matter\n                          in summoning the accused in a case filed on a complaint otherwise than on a\n                          police report, there has to be application of mind as to whether the\n                          allegations in the complaint constitute essential ingredients of the offence\n                          and whether there are sufficient grounds for proceeding against the accused.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_75\">                          58.     The petitioner has further submitted that the requirement of\n                          examination of the complainant as envisaged under <a href=\"/doc/444619/\" id=\"a_72\">Section 200</a> of the\n                          Cr.P.C., 1973 could not have been dispensed with in the instant case which\n                          had been so dispensed with by the learned Trial Court on the wrong premise\n                          that the complaint had been filed by a \u201epublic servant\u201f in discharge of his\n                          official duties. Inter alia the petitioner submitted that Mr.Tarun Kumar\n                          Singh, an officer working with the respondent no.2, Reserve Bank of India,\n                          is not a public servant and hence the Trial Court could not have dispensed\n                          with his examination before issuing the process under <a href=\"/doc/1827798/\" id=\"a_73\">section 204</a> of the\n                          Cr.P.C., 1973 and that none of the provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/462219/\" id=\"a_74\">Reserve Bank of India\n                          Act, 1934</a> nor <a href=\"/doc/1052367/\" id=\"a_75\">section 21</a> of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 which defines\n                          &#x27;public servant&#x27; is indicative of the fact that an employee of Respondent No.\n                          2 is a public servant. The petitioner has thus submitted that the impugned\n                          summoning order dated 11.3.2018 whereby the examination of Tarun\n                          Kumar Singh, an authorized representative of the respondent No.2, Reserve\n                          Bank of India, was dispensed with is perverse, grossly erroneous and ought\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_198\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_199\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_200\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                               39</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_201\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_202\">18:28</span>\n                           to be set aside.\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_76\">                          <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"105333982\" id=\"span_203\">59.       The petitioner has further submitted that in the case <a href=\"/doc/105333982/\" id=\"a_76\">E. Manoj Kumar\n                          v. Tamil Nadu Public Service Commissions</a> 2019 LabIC 2058 the Hon&#x27;ble\n                          Madras High Court did not subscribe to the view taken by a party that\n                          service in Reserve Bank of India (Respondent No. 2 herein) is a Central\n                          Government service. The petitioner further submitted that the Hon&#x27;ble\n                          Madras High <a href=\"/doc/105333982/\" id=\"a_77\">Court further held</a> that <a href=\"/doc/1651679/\" id=\"a_78\">Sections 7</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1124138/\" id=\"a_79\">11</a> of the Reserve Bank\n                          of India Act, 1934 do not show that the employees of Reserve Bank of India\n                          (Respondent No. 2 herein) are government servants and that the fact that the\n                          Central Government has persuasive control over Reserve Bank of India\n                          would not make its employees central government employees.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_77\">                          60.       <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"95982864\" id=\"span_204\">Inter alia the petitioner has submitted while placing reliance on the\n                          observations of the Hon\u201fble High Court of Gauhati, in <a href=\"/doc/95982864/\" id=\"a_80\">Sri Padumi Das &amp;\n                          Ors. v. Bibhashree Das</a>, (2012) 5 GLR 597, wherein it has been held to the\n                          effect:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_51\">                                       &quot;In the present case, since the complainant was not a public\n                                       servant and the complaint had not been made, while acting\n                                       or purporting to act in discharge of the complainant&#x27;s\n                                       official duties and when no Court had made the complaint,\n                                       in question, the learned Magistrate ought to have examined\n                                       the complainant, under <a href=\"/doc/444619/\" id=\"a_81\">Section 200</a> Cr.PC, before deciding\n                                       to issue process in exercise of his powers under <a href=\"/doc/1827798/\" id=\"a_82\">Section 204</a>\n                                       Cr.PC. Examination of the complainant unless, otherwise,\n                                       dispensed with by the proviso to <a href=\"/doc/444619/\" id=\"a_83\">Section 200</a> Cr.PC., is\n                                       mandatory, for, examination of the complainant is not a\n                                       mere formality, but is aimed at ascertaining as to how far\n                                       the complainant, when examined on oath, supports the\n                                       allegations made in the complaint.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_78\">                          61.            The petitioner has thus reiterated that it was incumbent upon\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_205\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_206\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_207\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                                40</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_208\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_209\">18:28</span>\n                           the Trial Court to examine Tarun Kumar Singh on oath as he is not a public\n                          servant and thus, the impugned criminal complaint and the impugned\n                          summoning order passed by the Learned Trial Court ought to be set aside by\n                          this Court.\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_79\">                          62.     The petitioner has further submitted that the information furnished\n                          by petitioner bank was not &quot; by or under or for the provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_84\">Banking\n                          Regulation Act, 1946</a> and hence <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_85\">Section 46(1)</a> of the Banking Regulation\n                          Act, 1949 is no application on the petitioner bank.\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_80\">                          63.     The petitioner has thus submitted further that without prejudice to\n                          submissions made in \u201ea\u201f as reflected in para 47 hereinabove, the petitioner\n                          Bank has submitted that the Respondent No. 2 did not make any reference to\n                          any of the provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_86\">Banking Regulation Act, 1949</a> before\n                          conducting IT examination of Petitioner Bank, at the time of conducting IT\n                          examination or anytime thereafter. The petitioner submits further that a bare\n                          perusal of the IT Examination Report, the communications/letters/emails\n                          exchanged between Petitioner Bank and Respondent No. 2 makes the\n                          aforesaid fact amply clear. The petitioner reiterated that the Respondent No.\n                          2 solely intended to conduct a study of IT system of Petitioner Bank.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_81\">                          64.     Inter alia whilst placing reliance on the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_87\">Section 46(1)</a>\n                          of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 which reads to the effect:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_52\">                                 &quot; Whoever in any return, balance-sheet or other document or in any\n                                 information required or furnished by or under or for the purposes of\n                                 any provision of this Act, wilfully makes a statement which is false in\n                                 any material particular, knowing it to be false, or willfully omits to\n                                 make a material statement, shall be punishable with imprisonment for\n                                 a term which may extend to three years or with fine, which may\n                                 extend to one crore rupees or with both.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_210\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_211\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_212\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                               41</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_213\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_214\">18:28</span>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_82\">                           the Petitioner Bank submitted that an accused person is punishable for\n                          contravention of <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_88\">Section 46(1)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 only\n                          when the return/balance-sheet/other document/information has been\n                          provided by or under or for the purposes of any of the provisions of the\n                          <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_89\">Banking Regulation Act, 1949</a>. The petitioner thus submitted that the\n                          information provided by Petitioner Bank was not under or for the purposes\n                          of the provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_90\">Banking Regulation Act, 1949</a> and was an\n                          information provided solely to assist Respondent No. 2 in the study which\n                          Respondent No. 2 proposed to conduct and that thus the Petitioner Bank\n                          cannot be charged for contravention of <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_91\">Section 46(1)</a> of the Banking\n                          Regulation Act, 1949.\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_83\">                          65.     Inter alia the petitioner submitted that the respondent no.2 in its\n                          impugned criminal complaint has not suggested/stated that the petitioner\n                          bank made any false statement wilfully or knew that the statement made is\n                          false or wilfully omitted to make a material statement and therefore the\n                          petitioner bank cannot be charged under <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_92\">Section 46(1)</a> of the Banking\n                          Regulation Act, 1949.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_84\">                          66.     Furthermore, the petitioner submitted that <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_93\">Section 46(1)</a> of the\n                          Banking Regulation Act, 1949 penalizes making of a false statement\n                          &quot;wilfully&quot; or if the statement made is &quot;known&quot; to be false or there is a\n                          &quot;wilful&quot; omission to make a material statement and that it is thus clear that\n                          there has to be a mens rea on part of an accused person before the accused\n                          person could be charged/penalized under <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_94\">Section 46(1)</a> of the Banking\n                          Regulation Act, 1949.\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_85\">                          67.     Inter alia the petitioner submits that the respondent No.2 in its\n                          impugned criminal complaint filed before the Learned Trial Court has not\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_215\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_216\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_217\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                              42</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_218\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_219\">18:28</span>\n                           stated or made any suggestion towards the fact that the Petitioner Bank has\n                          wilfully made any false statement or knew that the statement made by it was\n                          false or had omitted to make a material statement wilfully. The petitioner\n                          submits that there was no malafide on part of Petitioner Bank and that\n                          Respondent No. 2 implicitly admits the same as Respondent No. 2 has not\n                          stated in its impugned criminal complaint filed before the Learned Trial\n                          Court that the Petitioner Bank had acted malafidely or that Petitioner Bank\n                          wilfully made any false statement and that thus Petitioner Bank cannot be\n                          charged under <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_95\">Section 46(1)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_86\">                          68.     Furthermore, the petitioner submits that the summons issued to\n                          the petitioner bank by the trial Court is a summon under <a href=\"/doc/1823824/\" id=\"a_96\">Section 138</a> of\n                          the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 whereas the impugned criminal\n                          complaint filed by the respondent No.2 against the petitioner bank and\n                          other accused persons is under <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_97\">Section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation\n                          Act, 1949, and the Learned Trial Court has summoned the Petitioner\n                          Bank under <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_98\">Section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 and that\n                          the impugned criminal complaint has no remote connection to <a href=\"/doc/1823824/\" id=\"a_99\">section\n                          138</a> of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881.\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_87\">                          69.     The petitioner further submitted that the plain reading of the\n                          summons at page 65 of the paper book and the impugned summoning\n                          order dated 11.3.2019 passed by the trial Court reveals that there could\n                          not be a more blatant case than the present one for a total non-\n                          application of mind for the reasons:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_53\">                                 &quot;a. A bare perusal of the summons reveals that it is a summons to\n                                 an accused person in a summons trial case and not as is contended\n                                 by Respondent No. 2, before this Hon&#x27;ble Court for a warrants trial\n                                 case.\n</blockquote>\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_220\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_221\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_222\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                            43</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_223\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_224\">18:28</span>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_54\">                                  b. The petitioner has thus submitted that the summons issued by the\n                          learned Trial Court are perverse and ought to be set aside by this Court.\n</p>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_226\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_227\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_228\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                            44</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_229\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_230\">18:28</span>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_90\">                           <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"574884\" id=\"span_231\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"159121041\" id=\"span_232\">71.     The petitioner has reiterated that the law <a href=\"/doc/1594211/\" id=\"a_105\">laid down in</a> the case of\n                          <a href=\"/doc/159121041/\" id=\"a_106\">Sunil Bharti Mittal V. Judicial Magistrate</a>, (1998) 5 SCC 749, is squarely\n                          applicable to the facts of the present case and that it is a settled position of\n                          law that the order summoning an accused could not be passed mechanically\n                          and in a cavalier manner. The petitioner has thus submitted that the\n                          respondent No.2 had approached the Trial Court with unclean hands as the\n                          respondent No.2 did not plead/inform about the show cause notice dated\n                          23.8.2018, that was issued to the petitioner bank under Section 47A(l)(c)\n                          read with <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_108\">Section 46(4)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.</span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_91\">                          <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"116708225\" id=\"span_233\">72.     Reliance was placed by the petitioner on the verdict of the Hon\u201fble\n                          Supreme Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/116708225/\" id=\"a_109\">Mehmood Ul Rehman and Ors. v. Khazir\n                          Mohammad Tunda and Ors.</a>, (2015) 12 SCC 420, submitting to the effect\n                          that it has been <a href=\"/doc/116708225/\" id=\"a_110\">laid down by</a> the Hon\u201fble Supreme Court that application of\n                          mind is best demonstrated by disclosure of mind on the satisfaction and that\n                          if there is no such indication in a case where the Magistrate proceeds under\n                          <a href=\"/doc/686759/\" id=\"a_111\">sections 190</a>/<a href=\"/doc/1827798/\" id=\"a_112\">204</a> of the Cr.P.C, the High Court under <a href=\"/doc/1679850/\" id=\"a_113\">Section 482</a> of the\n                          Cr.P.C is bound to invoke its inherent power in order to prevent abuse of the\n                          power of the Criminal Court.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_92\">                          73.     The petitioner has further submitted that the complaint filed by the\n                          respondent No.2 made no reference to any of such Sections of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_114\">Section 46</a> of\n                          the of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949, nor have they been included in the\n                          summoning order, which made it amply clear that no reference was made to\n                          any of the sub-sections of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_115\">section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949\n                          and the petitioner bank was left to make its own guesses as to under what\n                          sub-section of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_116\">Section 46</a>, Respondent No. 2 would be intending to\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_234\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_235\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_236\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                                 45</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_237\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_238\">18:28</span>\n                           prosecute the petitioner Bank. The petitioner has thus submitted through its\n                          petition that the ingredients of none of the sub-sections of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_117\">Section 46</a> were\n                          satisfied and the impugned criminal complaint is a mere instrument of\n                          harassment. The petitioner has denied that the provision under which the\n                          complaint is filed is not relevant and submits that such an interpretation\n                          would open a pandora&#x27;s box of criminal complaints being filed without\n                          specifying any sections of relevant laws under which an accused is charged\n                          under and if an accused is not even informed of the provision of law it is\n                          being prosecuted under it would be nothing but a serious attack on the rights\n                          of an accused and a grave travesty of justice. The petitioner submits that the\n                          summoning in itself is a serious matter and the Court could not be left to\n                          specify the exact charge at a later stage, when it is not even clear if the case\n                          is a summons-triable case or a warrants triable case and the only indicator\n                          hints it to be a summons-triable case. The   petitioner   submits   that   there   is   no\n                          communication before 20.02.2018 wherein the Petitioner Bank had\n                          mandated all banks to ensure that no SWIFT message is sent without\n                          first ensuring that the underlying transaction has been duly reflected in\n                          CBS/accounting system with immediate effect.\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_104\">                          85.     The petitioner has further submitted that there is no relationship\n                          whatsoever between the impugned criminal complaint and the fraud\n                          perpetrated upon Brady House branch of the petitioner bank and has\n                          submitted to the effect:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_60\">                                     &quot;Respondent No. 2 is attempting to link the fraud\n                                     committed upon Brady House branch of Petitioner Bank\n                                     with the IT examination conducted in 2016. Insofar as\n                                     fraud perpetrated upon Petitioner Bank is concerned, it is\n                                     respectfully submitted that Petitioner Bank is a\n                                     nationalized public sector bank which has suffered losses\n                                     to the tune of INR 130,00,00,00,000 (Rupees Thirteen\n                                     Thousand Crores only) (approx.). Petitioner Bank was the\n                                     first party to file complaint in the matter which led to\n                                     registration      of     FIR/RC       bearing     number\n                                     RCBSM2018E0001, which was registered against Nirav\n                                     Modi and others under section 120-B read with <a href=\"/doc/1436241/\" id=\"a_122\">section 420</a>\n                                     of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 and <a href=\"/doc/681534/\" id=\"a_123\">section 13(2)</a> r/w\n                                     <a href=\"/doc/178303/\" id=\"a_124\">section 13(l)(d)</a> of the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988\n                                     by the CBI:BS&amp;FC, Mumbai on 31.01.2018 followed by\n                                     another FIR/RC bearing number RCBSM2018E0003\n                                     registered against Nirav Modi and others under section\n                                     120-B r/w <a href=\"/doc/1436241/\" id=\"a_125\">section 420</a> of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 and\n                                     <a href=\"/doc/681534/\" id=\"a_126\">section 13(2)</a> r/w <a href=\"/doc/178303/\" id=\"a_127\">section 13(l)(d)</a> of the Prevention of\n                                     Corruption Act, 1988 by the CBI:BS&amp;FC, Mumbai on\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_259\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_260\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_261\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                               50</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_262\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_263\">18:28</span>\n                                      04.03.2018. Similarly, pursuant to criminal complaint\n                                     dated filed by Petitioner Bank, an FIR bearing number RC\n                                     02(E)/2018/CBI/BS&amp;FC/Mumbai was registered by CBI\n                                     against Mehul Choksi and others. Petitioner Bank had\n                                     always acted bona fide and as soon as Petitioner Bank\n                                     became aware of fraud, it was Petitioner Bank which had\n                                     filed complaints with Directorate of Enforcement pursuant\n                                     to which ECIR/MBZO-I/04/2018/3486 was registered by\n                                     Directorate of Enforcement on 16.02.2018 against Mehul\n                                     Choksi and accomplices, and ECIR/MBZO- 1/03/2018\n                                     dated 14.02.2018 was registered against Nirav Modi and\n                                     accomplices. In fact, post surfacing of fraud committed\n                                     upon Petitioner Bank by Nirav Modi and Mehul Choksi,\n                                     Petitioner Bank itself had filed Fraud Monitoring Reports\n                                     (FMR)/FMR Update Applications with Respondent No. 2,\n                                     which clearly shows that Petitioner Bank always acted\n                                     bona fide.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_105\">                          86.     Inter alia the petitioner has reiterated that the allegations contained in\n                          the show cause notice 23.08.2018 and the impugned criminal complaint are\n                          largely the same. The petitioner further submits that the respondent No.2 had\n                          taken a lenient view in the order dated 25.2.2019 in respect of allegations\n                          relating to STP and creation of payment messages in SWIFT after passing\n                          the entries in CBS/accounting system. It was further submitted by the\n                          petitioner that the respondent No. 2 thus has consciously waived its right to\n                          prosecute the Petitioner Bank for the same allegations for which a lenient\n                          view has already been taken in another set of proceedings initiated by\n                          Respondent No. 2.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_106\">                          87.     The petitioner reiterated that nothing prevented the respondent No.2\n                          from informing the learned Trial Court about the proceedings initiated by it\n                          under Section 47A(l)(c) read with <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_128\">Section 46(4)</a> of the Banking Regulation\n                          Act, 1949.\n</p>\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_264\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_265\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_266\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                                   51</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_267\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_268\">18:28</span>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_107\">                           88.     The petitioner has further submitted that it has not even made a\n                          remote violation of any of the provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_129\">Banking Regulation Act,\n                          1949</a> by Petitioner Bank and that the impugned criminal complaint and the\n                          impugned summoning order suffer from gross illegalities and infirmities.\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_108\">                          89.    Written and oral submissions were made on behalf of the petitioner,\n                          on behalf of the respondent no.2 i.e. RBI and on behalf of the State. The\n                          State adopted the submissions that were made on behalf of the RBI.\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_109\">                          90.    Through written submissions submitted on behalf of the petitioner and\n                          through the reply to Crl.M.A.31239/2019 filed by the respondent no.2/ RBI\n                          seeking a vacation of the order of stay of the learned trial Court in the\n                          present proceedings vide order dated 20.05.2019 in the present petition were\n                          reiterated.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_110\">                          91.    It was submitted as under by the petitioner vide written submissions\n                          filed on 13.11.2019, which read to the effect:-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_61\">                                A. THE IMPUGNED CRIMINAL COMPLAINT FILED BY\n                                RESPONDENT NO. 2 NOR THE IMPUGNED ORDER DATED\n                                11.03.2019 PASSED BY THE LD. TRIAL COURT GIVE ANY\n                                INDICA TION AS TO WHICH OF THE DIFFERENT SUB-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_111\">                                SECTIONS UNDER SECTION 46 OF THE BANKING\n                                REGULATION ACT, 1949 THE PETITIONER IS PROPOSED TO\n                                BE PUNISHED.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_112\">                                I. Neither the impugned criminal complaint filed by the Reserve\n                                Bank of India (&quot;Respondent No.2&quot;) nor the impugned order dated\n                                11.03.2019 passed by the Court of Learned Metropolitan\n                                Magistrate, Patiala House Cou11S, New Delhi (the &quot;Learned Trial\n                                Court) make any reference to any of the different sub-sections\n                                under <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_130\">section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 (the &quot;1949\n                                Act) for which the Petitioner is proposed to be punished.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_113\">                                2. On a plain reading of the impugned criminal complaint filed by\n                                the Respondent No. 2, the Petitioner is not in a position to\n                                comprehend ascertain under which sub-section of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_131\">section 46</a> of the\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_269\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_270\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_271\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                             52</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_272\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_273\">18:28</span>\n                                1949 Act it is called upon to answer. It is pertinent to note that\n                               each sub-section of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_132\">section 46</a> of the 1949 Act is distinct and has\n                               different ingredients in as much as <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_133\">section 46(I)</a> punishes who ever\n                               wilfully furnishes a statement which is false in any material\n                               particular while <a href=\"/doc/1638992/\" id=\"a_134\">section 46(2)</a> punishes who ever fails to furnish\n                               any statement under sub section (2) of <a href=\"/doc/222598/\" id=\"a_135\">section 35</a> of the 1949 Act\n                               and <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_136\">section 46(4)</a> punishes any other violation of the provisions of\n                               the 1949 Act. To that extent each subsection contemplates some\n                               different yet overlapping elements but at the same time prescribes\n                               widely varying punishments i.e., from imprisonment of up to 3\n                               years to a fine which may extend to INR 1,00,00,0001- (Rupees\n                               One Crore only) or fine extending to INR 1,00,000 (Rupees One\n                               Lakh only) for every day, during which contravention or default\n                               continues.\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_114\">                               3. Moreover, keeping in mind the differing elements, the defence of\n                               Petitioner would vary vastly and to that extent the fact that the\n                               exact sub-section has not been identified prejudices the accused\n                               (Petitioner) greatly. Petitioner submits that upon entering\n                               appearance before the Learned Trial Court, Petitioner would be\n                               subjected to the framing of notice under <a href=\"/doc/429078/\" id=\"a_137\">section 251</a> of the Code of\n                               Criminal Procedure, 1973 (the &quot;<a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_138\">CrPC</a>&#x27;) without having had the\n                               advantage of knowing the substance of the accusation against it.\n                               B. AT THE HIGHEST, THE IMPUGNED CRIMINAL\n                               COMPLAINT INDICATES THAT IT IS A CRIMINAL\n                               COMPLAINT UNDER SECTION 46(4) OF THE BANKING\n                               REGULATION ACT, 1949 OR IN THE ALTERNATI VE SECTION\n                               46(2) OF THE BANKING REGULATION ACT. 1949:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_62\">                               1. Each opportunity is important for an accused in a criminal\n                               matter. Respondent No.2 has wrongly projected that the\n                               summoning order dated 1 1.03.20 19 and also, the impugned\n                               criminal complaint fil ed by Respondent No. 2 could only be under\n                               sub-section ( I) of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_139\">section 46</a> of the 1949 Act as it could also be a\n                               complaint under sub-section (2) or under sub-section (4) of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_140\">section\n                               46</a> of the 1949 Act.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_63\">                               2. The Petitioner has strong apprehensions that the complaint fi\n                               led by Respondent No. 2 is a complaint under <a href=\"/doc/1638992/\" id=\"a_141\">section 46(2)</a> of the\n                               1949 Act as <a href=\"/doc/1638992/\" id=\"a_142\">Section 46(2)</a> contemplates an inspection. Respondent\n                               No.2 conducted an inspection of Petitioner in February, 20 16,\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_274\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_275\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_276\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                          53</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_277\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_278\">18:28</span>\n                                and has in fact made reference to the aforementioned Inspect ion\n                               Repol1 in the Impugned Criminal Complaint, hence, probabilities\n                               of it being a complaint under <a href=\"/doc/1638992/\" id=\"a_143\">section 46(2)</a> of the 1949 Act are\n                               much higher as opposed to it being a complaint under <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_144\">section 46</a>(\n                               I) of the 1949 Act.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_64\">                               3. Similarly, there are strong reasons that the impugned criminal\n                               complaint filed by Respondent No.2 is a complaint under <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_145\">section\n                               46(4)</a> of the 1949 Act as it is a general provision providing\n                               punishment in case of violation of any other provisions of the 1949\n                               Act. During hearing dated 05 .1 1.201 9, Senior Counsel\n                               appearing for Respondent o. 2 submitted before this Hon&#x27;ble Court\n                               that Petitioner has contravened <a href=\"/doc/150087822/\" id=\"a_146\">section 27(2)</a> of the 1949 Act.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_115\">                               Punishment for violation of <a href=\"/doc/150087822/\" id=\"a_147\">section 27(2)</a> of the 1949 Act could fall\n                               only under <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_148\">section 46(4)</a> of the 1949 Act. More so, the show cause\n                               notice issued by Respondent No.2 dated 23.08.2018 states that\n                               Petitioner had not compiled with various directions/guidelines\n                               issued by Respondent, which is punishable under <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_149\">section 46(4)</a> of\n                               the 1949 Act.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_116\">                               4.Thus, it is submitted that chances of it being a complaint under\n                               <a href=\"/doc/1638992/\" id=\"a_150\">section 46(2)</a> or 46(4) of the 1949 Act are much higher as opposed\n                               to <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_151\">section 46</a>( I) of the 1949 Act. If it is a complaint under <a href=\"/doc/1638992/\" id=\"a_152\">section\n                               46(2)</a> or <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_153\">section 46(4)</a> of the 1949 Act, bar under <a href=\"/doc/931358/\" id=\"a_154\">section 47</a> A( 4)\n                               of the 1949 Act would come into play and the complaint fi led by\n                               Respondent No.2 would be hit by <a href=\"/doc/931358/\" id=\"a_155\">section 47</a> A( 4) which bars filing\n                               of a complaint in a court of law when penalty has already been\n                               imposed by Respondent No.2.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_117\">                               5. In the present case, there have been penalty proceedings\n                               instituted against the Petitioner under <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_156\">Section 47 A </a>of the Banking\n                               Regulation Act, 1949 and a penalty of INR 2,00,00.000 (Rs. Two\n                               Crore Only) has been imposed by order dated 25.03.20 19 and the\n                               said penalty has been paid. In these circumstances, the immunity\n                               provided under <a href=\"/doc/931358/\" id=\"a_157\">Section 47</a> A( 4) of the <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_158\">Banking Regulation Act</a>.\n                               1949 would inure to the benefit of the Petitioner and the Impugned\n                               Criminal Complaint must be quashed on that point alone. This\n                               aspect of the matter has been elaborated as follows in Point C of\n                               these Synopsis.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_118\">                               6. In cases where two views are possible, the Court ought to take a\n                               view which is more favourable to the accused person. In a case as\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_279\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_280\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_281\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                           54</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_282\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_283\">18:28</span>\n                                in the present, where the Complaint is vague and bereft of material\n                               pal1iculars, this Hon&#x27;ble COUI1 ought to construe the same in a\n                               manner so as to benefit the Petitioner, resulting here in it being\n                               barred by operation of statute.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_119\">                               C. ASSUMING THE IMPUGNED CRIMINAL COMPLAINT TO\n                               BE UNDER SECTIO 46(4) OF THE BANKING REGULATION\n                               ACT. 1949 THE PROSECUTION IS BARRED BY OPERATION\n                               OF SECTION 47A(4) OF THE BANKING REGULATION ACT.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_120\">                               1949:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_65\">                               I. The only indicator of which sub-section of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_159\">section 46</a> Petitioner\n                               is charged with is provided in the show cause notice issued by\n                               Respondent No.2 dated 23.08.2018 under Section 47 A of the 1949\n                               Act which states that same is punishable under <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_160\">Section 46(4)</a> of the\n                               1949 Act. The impugned criminal complaint filed by Respondent\n                               No.2 and the impugned summoning order da ted I 1.03.2019\n                               passed by the Learned Trial Court are barred in law in as much as\n                               <a href=\"/doc/931358/\" id=\"a_161\">section 47</a> A( 4) of the 1949 Act provides that no complaint shall\n                               be filed against a banking company (such as Petitioner) in respect\n                               of any contravention or default in respect of which any penalty has\n                               been imposed by Respondent No.2.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_66\">                               2. It is relevant to point out that in the show cause notice dated\n                               23.08.2018, reference has only been made to <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_162\">section 46(4)</a> of the\n                               1949 Act clearly indicating that Respondent No.2 did not believe\n                               any other provisions were attracted in the instant case.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_121\">                               Respondent No. 2 has already imposed a penalty on Petitioner of\n                               an amount of I R 2,00,00,000 (Rupees Two Crores Only) by way of\n                               order dated 25.02.20 19 (Annexure-P123 of Petition) and,\n                               therefore, the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/931358/\" id=\"a_163\">Section 47</a> A(4) would act as a\n                               complete legal bar to the prosecution of Petitioner by the\n                               impugned criminal complaint and impugned summoning order\n                               dated 11.03.2019. Petitioner has also paid the penalty imposed by\n                               Respondent o. 2 on 06.04.20 19.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_122\">                               D. THE IMPUGNED SUMMONING ORDER SUFFERS FROM A\n                               TOTAL NON - APPLICATION OF MIND AND DESERVES TO\n                               BE SET ASIDE ON THIS SHORT GROUN D:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_67\">                               I. The summoning order passed by the Learned Trial Court is a\n                               mere recapitulation of the allegations made by Respondent No.2 in\n                               its complaint and does not show any application of mind by the\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_284\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_285\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_286\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                         55</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_287\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_288\">18:28</span>\n                                Learned Trial Court. It is a settled position of law that a\n                               summoning order must reflect the due application of mind and the\n                               Magistrate must satisfy himself that there is sufficient basis for\n                               proceeding against the accused. The absolute absence of\n                               application of mind in the impugned summoning order is also\n                               demonstrated by the fact that akin to criminal complaint filed by\n                               Respondent No. 2, even in the impugned summoning order, there is\n                               no indication as to under which sub-section of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_164\">section 46</a> of the\n                               1949 Act, Petitioner has been summoned under.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_68\">                               2. Summons issued by a criminal court are equally important and\n                               the summons issued by the Learned Trial Court (Annexure-P12 @\n                               pg. 65 of Petition) to Petitioner, in the present case, refer to\n                               <a href=\"/doc/1823824/\" id=\"a_165\">section 138</a> of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 188 1, which shows\n                               the lack of application of mind by the Learned Metropolitan\n                               Magistrate.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_123\">                               E. THE IMPUGNED CRIMINAL COMPLAINT CANNOT BE\n                               SUPPLEMENTED OR CLARIFIED BY THE RESPONDENT\n                               NO.2 BY WAY OF ITS REPLY BEFORE THIS HON&#x27;BLE COURT.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_124\">                               IN ANY EVENT. THE CLARIFICATIONS SOUGHT TO BE\n                               MADE ARE WITHOUT MERIT:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_69\">                               I. Reply filed by Respondent No. :2 before this Hon&#x27;ble Court is an\n                               egregious abuse of process of law as Respondent No.2 desires this\n                               Hon&#x27;ble Court to construe the clarification issued by them in their\n                               Reply, in respect of charging section under the 1949 Act as an\n                               amendment to the impugned criminal complaint filed by them\n                               before the Learned Trial Court, which is unheard of and not\n                               permissible in law. The clarification which Respondent No.2 has\n                               tendered in the Reply is a mere afterthought. There is an error\n                               apparent on the face of impugned criminal complaint and\n                               impugned summoning order passed by the Learned Trial Court\n                               and same could not be rectified through the route of issuing a\n                               clarification in the Reply filed by Respondent No.2 before this\n                               Hon&#x27;ble Court. It is a settled position in law that a complaint must\n                               be read as it is, while dealing with issues of cognizance and/or\n                               summons, especially in a case where there is no pre-summoning\n                               evidence led. Respondent No.2 cannot by way of a counter-\n                               affidavit/Reply add or subtract from a complaint already fil ed\n                               before the Learned Trial Court and claim that the complaint must\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_289\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_290\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_291\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                          56</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_292\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_293\">18:28</span>\n                                be read with their submissions. Such an attempt is contrary to\n                               settled legal principles.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_70\">                               2. Respondent No.2 is treating the case instituted by them as a\n                               warrant-triable case solely on the premise that Petitioner has\n                               acted in violation of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_166\">section 46</a>( I) of the 1949 Act whilst the\n                               impugned criminal complaint and also, the impugned summoning\n                               order dated 11.03.2019 do not even make any reference to <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_167\">section\n                               46</a>( I) of the 1949 Act. Such a material improvement in charging\n                               section, which Respondent No. 2 desires to make, is indefensible\n                               and cannot be allowed. The issue raised by the Respondent No.2\n                               that in a warrants trial. the substance of the charge is only\n                               required to be explained at the stage of framing charge, is not only\n                               contrary to law and is irrelevant for the present proceedings as\n                               well for the following reasons:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_71\">                               a. Neither the impugned criminal complaint nor the impugned\n                               summoning order make clear what offence the accused is\n                               summoned for. Under the various offences provided for in <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_168\">section\n                               46</a> of the 1949 Act, only one offence i.e., <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_169\">section 46</a>(1 ) would be\n                               an offence requiring a warrants trial. In the present case, as\n                               indicated above, there is no indication as to what offence\n                               Petitioner is being summoned.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_125\">                               b. Even in a warrants trial case, the accused would be entitled to\n                               know the substance of the charge he is summoned for. A bare\n                               perusal of Form I, Schedule II of the <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_170\">CrPC</a> establishes that the\n                               summary of the charge is to be conveyed even on the summons.\n                               c. In this particular case, there is no particular reference that has\n                               been made to any particular sub- section under <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_171\">section 46</a> of the\n                               1949 Act. Thus, it is not open to either the Petitioner nor this\n                               Hon&#x27;ble Court to assume that proceedings in the present case are\n                               under <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_172\">section 46</a>( I) of the 1949 Act.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_126\">                               F. ARGUENDO, SECTION 46(1) OF THE BANKING\n                               REGULATION ACT, 1949 IS NOT ATTRACTED IN THE\n                               INSTANT CASE AS THERE WAS NO MENS REA ON PART OF\n                               THE ACCUSED PERSONS AND IN ANY EVENT THE\n                               PETITIONER HAS ALREADY BEEN EXONERATED OF THE\n                               SAID CHARGE IN THE PENALTY PROCEEDINGS UNDER\n                               SECTION 47A OF THE BANKING REGULATION ACT, 1949:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_72\">                               1. Even if it is assumed, though not admitted, that the impugned\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_294\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_295\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_296\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                           57</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_297\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_298\">18:28</span>\n                                criminal complaint has been filed by Respondent No.2 under\n                               <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_173\">section 46</a>( I) of the 1949Act, it is submitted that <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_174\">section 46</a>( 1) of\n                               the 1949 Act penalizes making of a false statement &quot;wilfully&quot;&#x27; or if\n                               the statement made is &quot;known&quot; to be false or there is a &quot;wilful&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_127\">                               omission to make a material statement. It is thus clear that there\n                               has to be a mens rea on part of accused person before the accused\n                               person could be charged/penalized under <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_175\">section 46(I)</a> of the 1949\n                               Act.
