data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"159121041\" id=\"span_309\">92.Reliance was placed on behalf of the petitioner on the verdict of the\n                             Hon\u201fble Supreme Court in <a href=\"/doc/159121041/\" id=\"a_182\">Sunil Bharti Mittal Vs. Central Bureau of\n                             Investigation</a> (2015) 4 SCC 609 with specific reference placed on\n                             observations in paras 52 &amp; 53 thereof, which read to the effect:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_76\">                               &quot;52.A wide discretion has been given as to grant or refusal of\n                               process and it must be judicially exercised. A person ought not to\n                               be dragged into Court merely because a complaint has been filed.\n                               If a prima facie case has been made out, the Magistrate ought to\n\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_310\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_311\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_312\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                         60</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_313\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_314\">18:28</span>\n                                 issue process and it cannot be refused merely because he thinks\n                                that it is unlikely to result in a conviction.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_77\">                                53.However, the words &quot;sufficient grounds for proceeding&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_137\">                                appearing in the Section are of immense importance. It is these\n                                words which amply suggest that an opinion is to be formed only\n                                after due application of mind that there is sufficient basis for\n                                proceeding against the said accused and formation of such an\n                                opinion is to be stated in the order itself. The order is liable to be\n                                set aside if no reason is given therein while coming to the\n                                conclusion that there is prima facie case against accused, though\n                                the order need not contain detailed reasons. A fortiori, the order\n                                would be bad in law if the reason given turns out to be ex facie\n                                incorrect.&quot;,\n                          to submit to the effect that the order vide which the learned trial Court comes\n                          to the conclusion that there is sufficient ground for proceedings in terms of\n                          <a href=\"/doc/1827798/\" id=\"a_183\">Section 204</a> of the Cr.PC, 1973 which are of immense importance and has to\n                          be necessarily made after due application of mind that there is sufficient\n                          basis for proceedings against the said accused and formation of such an\n                          opinion is to be stated in the order itself and that the order is liable to be set\n                          aside if no reason is given therein while coming to the conclusion that there\n                          is prima facie case against accused, though the order need not contain\n                          detailed reasons and that the order would be bad in law if the reason given\n                          turns out to be ex facie incorrect.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_138\">                          <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"574884\" id=\"span_315\">93.     Reliance was also placed on behalf of the petitioner on the verdict of\n                          the Hon\u201fble Supreme Court in <a href=\"/doc/574884/\" id=\"a_184\">Pepsi Foods Ltd. and Another Vs. Special\n                          Judicial Magistrate and Others</a> (1998) 5 SCC 749 with specific reference in\n                          observations in para 28 thereof, which read to the effect:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_78\">                                &quot;Summoning of an accused in a criminal case is a serious matter.\n                                Criminal law cannot be set into motion as a matter of course. it is\n                                not that the complainant has to bring only two witnesses to\n                                support his allegations in the complaint to have the criminal law\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_316\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_317\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_318\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                                   61</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_319\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_320\">18:28</span>\n                                 set into motion. The order of the magistrate summoning the\n                                accused must reflect that he has applied his mind to the facts of the\n                                case and the law applicable thereto. He has to examine the nature\n                                of allegations made in the complaint and the evidence both oral\n                                and documentary in support thereof and would that be sufficient\n                                for the complainant to succeed in bringing charge home to the\n                                accused. It is not that the Magistrate is a silent spectator at the\n                                time of recording of preliminary evidence before summoning of the\n                                accused. Magistrate has to carefully scrutinise the evidence\n                                brought on record and may even himself put questions to the\n                                complainant and his witnesses to elicit answers to find out the\n                                truthfulness of the allegations or otherwise and then examine if\n                                any offence is prima facie committed by all or any of the accused.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_79\">                          to reiterate the contention that the order of the Magistrate summoning the\n                          accused must reflect that he has applied his mind to the facts of the case and\n                          the law applicable thereto and that the Magistrate is a silent spectator at the\n                          time of recording of preliminary evidence before summoning of the accused\n                          and that the Magistrate has to carefully scrutinize the evidence brought on\n                          record and may even himself put questions to the complainant and his\n                          witnesses to elicit answers to find out the truthfulness of the allegations or\n                          otherwise and then examine if any offence is prima facie committed by all or\n                          any of the accused.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_139\">                          <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"574884\" id=\"span_321\">94.    Reliance was placed on behalf of the petitioner on the verdict of the\n                          High Court of Bombay at Nagpur in Volvo India Private Limited,\n                          Yalachachally Village, Tavarakere Post, Ta. Hosakote, Banglore-562122\n                          Vs. State of Maharashtra 2017 SCC OnLine Bom 8540 wherein the\n                          observations of the Hon\u201fble Supreme Court in <a href=\"/doc/574884/\" id=\"a_185\">Pepsi Foods Ltd. and\n                          Another</a> (supra) were reiterated in Para-15 submitting to the effect that the\n                          impugned order passed by the Magistrate completely fails to show what\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_322\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_323\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_324\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                                62</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_325\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_326\">18:28</span>\n                           weighed in the mind of the learned Magistrate to issue the process against\n                          the applicant, the same was liable to be set aside.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_140\">                          <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"49712\" id=\"span_327\">95.    Reliance was also placed on behalf of the petitioner on the verdict of\n                          the Hon\u201fble Supreme Court in <a href=\"/doc/49712/\" id=\"a_186\">Punjab National Bank and Others Vs.\n                          Surendra Prasad Sinha</a> 1993 SCC (Cri) 149 to contend to the effect that the\n                          judicial process should not be an instrument of oppression and harassment\n                          and that there lies responsibility and duty on the Magistracy to find whether\n                          the concerned accused should be legally responsible for the offence charged\n                          for and only on satisfying that the law casts liability or creates offence\n                          against the juristic person or the person impleaded then only process would\n                          be issued. The specific reliance was placed on behalf of the petitioner on\n                          observations in para 6 of the said verdict, which read to the effect:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_80\">                                &quot;It is also salutary to note that judicial process should not be an\n                                instrument of oppression or needles harassment. The complaint\n                                was laid impleading the Chairman, the Managing Director of the\n                                Bank by name and a host of officers. There lies responsibility and\n                                duty on the Migistracy to find whether the concerned accused\n                                should be legally responsible for the offence for. Only on satisfying\n                                that the law casts liability or creates offence against the juristic\n                                person or the person impleaded then only process would be issued.\n                                At that stage the court would be circumspect and judicious in\n                                exercising discretion and should take all the relevant facts and\n                                circumstances into consideration before issuing process lest it\n                                would be an instrument in the hands of the private complaint as\n                                vendetta to harass the persons needlessly. Vindication of majesty\n                                of justice and maintenance of law and order in the society are the\n                                prime objects of criminal justice but it would not be the means to\n                                wreak personal vengeance. Considered from any angle we find\n                                that the respondent had abused the process and laid complaint\n                                against all the appellants without any prima facie case of harass\n                                them for vendetta.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_328\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_329\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_330\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                              63</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_331\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_332\">18:28</span>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_141\">                           <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"160796086\" id=\"span_333\">96.    Reliance was placed on behalf of the petitioner on the verdict of the\n                          Hon\u201fble Supreme Court in <a href=\"/doc/160796086/\" id=\"a_187\">Birla Corporation Limited Vs. Adventz\n                          Investment and Holdings Limited and Others</a> 2019 SCC OnLine SC 682\n                          with specific reliance on the observations in para 60 of the said verdict,\n                          which read to the effect:-</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_81\">                                &quot;The object of investigation under <a href=\"/doc/1149595/\" id=\"a_188\">Section 202</a> Cr.P.C. is &quot;for the\n                                purpose of deciding whether or not there is sufficient ground for\n                                proceeding&quot;. The enquiry under <a href=\"/doc/1149595/\" id=\"a_189\">Section 202</a> Cr.P.C. is to\n                                ascertain the fact whether the complaint has any valid foundation\n                                calling for issuance of process to the person complained against\n                                or whether it is a baseless one on which no action need be taken.\n                                The law imposes a serious responsibility on the Magistrate to\n                                decide if there is sufficient ground for proceeding against the\n                                accused. The issuance of process should not be mechanical nor\n                                should be made as an instrument of harassment to the accused. As\n                                discussed earlier, issuance of process to the accused calling upon\n                                them to appear in the criminal case is a serious matter and lack of\n                                material particulars and non-application of mind as to the\n                                materials cannot be brushed aside on the ground that it is only a\n                                procedural irregularity. In the present case, the satisfaction of the\n                                Magistrate in ordering issuance of process to the respondents is\n                                not well founded and the order summoning the accused cannot be\n                                sustained. The impugned order of the High Court holding that\n                                there was compliance of the procedure under <a href=\"/doc/1149595/\" id=\"a_190\">Section 202</a> Cr.P.C.\n                                cannot be sustained and is liable to be set aside.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_142\">                          to reiterate to the effect that the issuance of process should not be mechanical\n                          nor should be made as an instrument of the harassment of the accused and\n                          that the issuance of process to the accused calling upon them to appear in the\n                          criminal case is a serious matter and lack of material particulars and non-\n                          application of mind as to the materials cannot be brushed aside on the ground\n                          that it is only a procedural irregularity.\n</p>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_334\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_335\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_336\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                                 64</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_337\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_338\">18:28</span>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_143\">                           <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"116708225\" id=\"span_339\">97.    Reliance was also placed on behalf of the petitioner on the verdict of\n                          the Hon\u201fble Supreme Court in <a href=\"/doc/116708225/\" id=\"a_191\">Mehmood Ul Rehman Vs. Khazir\n                          Mohammad Tunda and Others</a> (2015) 12 SCC 420 with reliance placed on\n                          observations in para 22 thereof, which read to the effect:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_82\">                                &quot;22. The steps taken by the Magistrate Under <a href=\"/doc/545340/\" id=\"a_192\">Section 190(1) (a)</a> of\n                                Code of Criminal Procedure followed by <a href=\"/doc/1827798/\" id=\"a_193\">Section 204</a> of Code of\n                                Criminal Procedure should reflect that the Magistrate has applied\n                                his mind to the facts and the statements and he is satisfied that\n                                there is ground for proceeding further in the matter by asking the\n                                person against whom the violation of law is alleged, to appear\n                                before the court. The satisfaction on the ground for proceeding\n                                would mean that the facts alleged in the complaint would\n                                constitute an offence, and when considered alongwith the\n                                statements recorded, would, prima facie, make the accused\n                                answerable before the court. No doubt, no formal order or a\n                                speaking order is required to be passed at that stage. The <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_194\">Code of\n                                Criminal Procedure</a> requires speaking order to be passed Under\n                                <a href=\"/doc/443138/\" id=\"a_195\">Section 203</a> of Code of Criminal Procedure when the complaint is\n                                dismissed and that too the reasons need to be stated only briefly.\n                                In other words, the Magistrate is not to act as a post office in\n                                taking cognizance of each and every complaint filed before him\n                                and issue process as a matter of course. There must be sufficient\n                                indication in the order passed by the Magistrate that he is satisfied\n                                that the allegations in the complaint constitute an offence and\n                                when considered alongwith the statements recorded and the result\n                                of inquiry or report of investigation Under <a href=\"/doc/1149595/\" id=\"a_196\">Section 202</a> of Code of\n                                Criminal Procedure, if any, the accused is answerable before the\n                                criminal court, there is ground for proceeding against the accused\n                                Under <a href=\"/doc/1827798/\" id=\"a_197\">Section 204</a> of Code of Criminal Procedure, by issuing\n                                process for appearance. Application of mind is best demonstrated\n                                by disclosure of mind on the satisfaction. If there is no such\n                                indication in a case where the Magistrate proceeds Under <a href=\"/doc/686759/\" id=\"a_198\">Sections\n                                190</a>/<a href=\"/doc/1827798/\" id=\"a_199\">204</a> of Code of Criminal Procedure, the High Court Under\n                                <a href=\"/doc/1679850/\" id=\"a_200\">Section 482</a> of Code of Criminal Procedure is bound to invoke its\n                                inherent power in order to prevent abuse of the power of the\n                                criminal court. To becalled to appear before criminal court as an\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_340\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_341\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_342\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                              65</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_343\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_344\">18:28</span>\n                                 accused is serious matter affecting one&#x27;s dignity, self respect and\n                                image in society. Hence, the process of criminal court shall not be\n                                made a weapon of harassment.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_144\">                          thus to contend that the <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_201\">Code of Criminal Procedure</a> requires a speaking\n                          order to be passed under <a href=\"/doc/443138/\" id=\"a_202\">Section 203</a> of the Cr.PC, 1973 when the complaint\n                          is dismissed and that too the reasons need to be stated only briefly and that\n                          even the Magistrate is not to act as a post office in taking cognizance of each\n                          and every complaint filed before it and issue process as a matter of course\n                          and that the process could only be issued when the Magistrate is satisfied\n                          that the allegations in the application constitute an offence and when\n                          considered along with the statements recorded and the result of inquiry or\n                          report of investigation Under <a href=\"/doc/1149595/\" id=\"a_203\">Section 202</a> of Code of Criminal Procedure, if\n                          any, the accused is answerable before the criminal court, there is ground for\n                          proceeding against the accused Under <a href=\"/doc/1827798/\" id=\"a_204\">Section 204</a> of Code of Criminal\n                          Procedure, by issuing process for appearance.\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_145\">                                 As per the compendium of cases relied upon on behalf of the\n                          petitioner, the said six verdicts relied upon on behalf of the petitioner are\n                          qua the proposition that the summoning order must reflect due application\n                          of mind.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_146\">                          98.    <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"1638938\" id=\"span_345\">Inter alia reliance was placed on behalf of the petitioner on the verdict\n                          of this Court in M/s <a href=\"/doc/1638938/\" id=\"a_205\">Lalji &amp; Co. And Others vs. Delhi Administration and\n                          Others</a> 1984 SCC online Del 169 with specific reliance on observations in\n                          para 11 of the said verdict which read to the effect:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_83\">                                &quot;(11) As already noted above, so far as the raising of loan of Rs.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_147\">                                13,25,000 the chapter already stands closed and is not germane to\n                                the trial of the present case. It is with respect to the accused No. 6\n                                having introduced accused No. 1 to those parties who had\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_346\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_347\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_348\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                                 66</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_349\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_350\">18:28</span>\n                                purported to give loans to accused No. 1 that the complainant has\n                               sought to implicate accused No. 6 in the abetment and inducement\n                               as envisaged by <a href=\"/doc/1989338/\" id=\"a_206\">Section 278</a> of the Income-tax Act. Except for this\n                               allegation, there is no averment against accused No. 6. Of course,\n                               in para 11 of the complaint it is further added as under : &quot;Accused\n                               No. 6 to 17 abetted and / or induced the accused 1 to 5 to make\n                               and deliver accounts / statements and declaration relating to\n                               income of accused No. 1 which was chargeable to tax which was\n                               false and which they either knew to be false and/ or did not believe\n                               to be true. They have committed an offence punishable under\n                               <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_207\">Section 278</a> of the Act.&quot; In the reply which the Income-tax Officer\n                               has filed to the present petition before this Court, various other\n                               allegations have been made against accused No. 6 and its\n                               partners, and it is pointed out that some loans had been purported\n                               to be obtained by accused No. 6 from the same persons as well.\n                               Reference has also been made to earlier dealings between accused\n                               No. 1 and 6 and passing, off some money or adjustment of the\n                               amounts in the course of those transactions. However, I am not\n                               inclined to allow new allegations and averments relating to the\n                               commission of the offence by accused No. 6 and its partners to be\n                               raised which go beyond the scope of the complaint. It is the\n                               complaint which has been filed in the trial court which has to be\n                               looked into for ascertaining the nature and extent of allegations\n                               and the complainant cannot travel beyond the same. The accused\n                               persons too have to meet the allegations made in the complaint,\n                               and not that they are to be confronted unaware and taken by\n                               surprise with new or additional allegations. The basic document\n                               thus remains the complaint and it has to be ascertained on its\n                               plain reading whether any case at all is made out against accused\n                               No. 6 and its partners.&quot;\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_148\">                          to contend to the effect that the new allegations and averments relating\n                          to the commission of the offence by an accused cannot go beyond the\n                          scope of the complaint and that it is the complaint which has been filed\n                          in the trial court which has to be looked into for ascertaining the nature\n                          and extent of allegations and the complainant cannot travel beyond the\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_351\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_352\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_353\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                           67</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_354\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_355\">18:28</span>\n                           same with it having been observed therein to the effect that the accused\n                          persons too have to meet the allegations made in the complaint, and not\n                          that they are to be confronted unaware and taken by surprise with new\n                          or additional allegations and that the basic document thus remains the\n                          complaint and it has to be ascertained on its plain reading whether any\n                          case at all is made out against accused to submit to the effect that the\n                          learned trial Court cannot travel beyond the complaint filed in the\n                          Court to ascertain the nature and extent of allegations against an\n                          accused in support of this submissions that have been made on behalf of\n                          the petitioner at the most the impugned criminal complaint would be\n                          one under <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_208\">Section 46(4)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 qua which\n                          the embargo on any further proceedings is applicable to the petitioner in\n                          terms of <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_209\">Section 47A</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 as the\n                          petitioner has already paid the penalty of Rs.2 crores imposed on it vide\n                          an order dated 25.03.2019 under <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_210\">Section 47A</a> of the Banking Regulation\n                          Act, 1949 though in case where two views are possible, the Court ought\n                          to take a view which is most favourable to the petitioner especially as in\n                          the instant case, the complaint is vague and bereft of particular\n                          materials.\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_149\">                          99.     Through its written submissions dated 16.11.2019, the RBI, the\n                          respondent no.2 has reiterated the submissions made through its reply to the\n                          petition as well as to Crl.M.A.31239/2019 seeking a vacation of the order\n                          dated 20.5.2019 of stay of the proceedings before the learned trial Court\n                          dated 20.05.2019 as passed in the present proceedings. It was submitted on\n                          behalf of the respondent no.2 qua the submissions made on behalf of the\n                          petitioner that the neither the complaint nor the order issuing the process\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_356\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_357\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_358\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                             68</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_359\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_360\">18:28</span>\n                           discloses that Sub-section of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_211\">Section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949\n                          was alleged to have been violated by the petitioner/ Bank which had thus\n                          caused great prejudice to the petitioner as he did not have sufficient notice as\n                          to the exact charge for which has been summoned and that the failure of the\n                          Magistrate to mention the precise subsection of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_212\">Section 46</a> of the Banking\n                          Regulation Act, 1949 qua which PNB had been summoned reflects that the\n                          order issuing process had been passed mechanically without application of\n                          mind in relation to which, the respondent no.2 submitted to the effect:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_84\">                                &quot;1. That section 2 (d) of CrPC defines a complaint as:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_85\">                                &quot;any allegation made orally or in writing to a magistrate. with a\n                                view to his taking action under this Code, that some person,\n                                whether known or unknown, has committed an offence. but does\n                                not include a police report. &quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_150\">                                2. It is sett led law that a complaint need only set out the facts\n                                which disclose an offence and not mention the exact sections\n                                that are attracted. Thus, In Emperor v. Ram Bricha Misra 1947\n                                ILR 796, the Allahabad High Court held that:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_86\">                                &quot; .. .It is the allegations of facts which constitute the complaint.\n                                Reference of specific sections relating to the offences made out\n                                by these facts is no essential ingredient of a complaint...The\n                                definition of the word complaint does not contemplate reference\n                                to the specific sections of the enactment making a certain\n                                conduct an offence. It follows, therefore, that omission to\n                                mention the offence made out by the facts or mentioning wrong\n                                section does not invalidate a complaint. &quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_151\">                                3. In Gulabchand Chandani v. State of MP. 2001(3) M.P.LJ.467\n                                the Madhya- Pradesh High Court held that,\n                                &quot; .. from the facts which has to be taken cognizance of and it is\n                                not material that the complainant has mentioned a wrong Act or\n                                a wrong section in the complaint. Where the facts alleged in the\n                                complaint disclose an offence, the Magistrate should take\n                                cognizance of the complaint and any error in the recital of the\n                                statutory provision is not of much significance.&quot;\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_152\">                                4. In Shyamlal v State AIR 1958 All 76 the High Court of\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_361\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_362\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_363\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                                 69</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_364\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_365\">18:28</span>\n                                 Allahabad held that, &quot;the word &#x27;complaint &#x27; as defined in<a href=\"/doc/252634/\" id=\"a_213\"> s.4(h)</a>\n                                does not show that it should also mention the sections under\n                                which it has been made. What is required to be stated in the\n                                complaint are the facts which would enable the Magistrate to\n                                take cognizance of the offence under<a href=\"/doc/1868826/\" id=\"a_214\"> s.498</a> IPC. &quot;\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_153\">                                <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1922701\" id=\"span_366\">5. Further, the Hon&#x27;ble Supreme Court in <a href=\"/doc/1922701/\" id=\"a_215\">Rajesh Bajaj vs. State\n                                of NCT of Delhi</a> 1999 3 SCC 259 when dealing with power of\n                                the High Court to quash proceedings under section 482, stated\n                                in paragraph 9 of the judgement that:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_87\">                                &quot;If factual foundation for the offence has been laid in the\n                                complaint the court should not hasten to quash criminal\n                                proceedings during investigation stage merely on the premise\n                                that one or two ingredients have not been stated with details.\n                                For quashing an FIR (a step which is permitted only in\n                                extremely rare cases) the information in the complaint must be\n                                so bereft of even the basic facts which are absolutely necessary\n                                for making out the offence. &quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_154\">                                6. As enumerated above, it is well settled in law that the\n                                complaint need only disclose facts constituting an offence &amp; not\n                                the sections applicable. In the instant case the complaint is\n                                replete with facts disclosing the offence prescribed under\n                                <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_216\">section 46(1)</a> of the Act, i.e., The act of wilfully making a\n                                statement which is false in any material particular, knowing it\n                                be false, or wilfully omits to make a material statement, in any\n                                return, balance-sheet or other document. These acts punishable\n                                under <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_217\">section 46(1)</a> of the Act are set out in the complaint at\n                                paragraphs 2, 8(e) , (t), (I), (m) and (n) of the complaint.\n                                Further, these averments are also supported by documents\n                                annexed to the complaint. In any event the complaint alleges\n                                that the facts disclose an offence under <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_218\">section 46</a> of the\n                                Banking Regulation Act but only does not mention the sub-\n                                section prescribing &amp; punishing the offence.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_155\">                                The factual conspectus disclosing the offence under <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_219\">section\n                                46(1)</a> of the Act:\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_156\">                                7. (i) The first instance of PNB furnishing false information to\n                                the RBI was on 08.07.2016. Pursuant to an inspection carried\n                                out by it in February 2016 of all aspects of PNB&#x27;s Information\n                                Technology (IT) systems, the bank prepared a comprehensive\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_367\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_368\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_369\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                       70</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_370\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_371\">18:28</span>\n                                 report and forwarded a copy of the same to PNB vide email\n                                dated 06.06.2016. One of the crucial observations made in the\n                                IT examination report was in paragraph3.5 of the report, which\n                                is reproduced below:-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_88\">                                &quot;3.5 Banks had not planned or envisioned implementation of\n                                Single Enterprise Application as it had 80 applications\n                                consisting of in-house developed. purchased and outsourced\n                                activities. CBS was not integrated in automated fashion with\n                                various critical applications i.e., no Straight Through\n                                Processing (STP) was envisaged and the middleware used\n                                involved manual interventions giving rise to various risks.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_157\">                                Through the said mail PNB was called on to submit a\n                                Compliance Report inter alia on the above observation. PNB&#x27;s\n                                response to RBI vide letter dated 08.07.2016 furnished false\n                                information regarding its compliance with the observations\n                                cited above. PNB&#x27;s response to paragraph 3.5 of the IT\n                                Exam/Report is reproduced below:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_89\">                                &quot;Bank has a core banking solution which takes care of its main\n                                business requirements. All applications around CBS have been\n                                integrated with STP except Treasury. The observations may be\n                                treated as complied&#x27;\n                                       This assertion by PNB was subsequently discovered by\n                                RBI to be totally false in January 2018 onwards when it\n                                received a series of Fraud Monitoring Reports (&quot;FMRs&quot;)\n                                from PNB. In column 9c of these FMRs titled &quot;modus\n                                operandi&quot;, the report stated as under:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_90\">                                       &quot;9c. Issuance of Letters of Undertaking (LOU) by\n                                SWIFT without obtaining approval of the Authority, without\n                                necessary applications. documents and without making entries\n                                in the CBS system&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_158\">                                       Thus, while PNB&#x27;s compliance response on 08.07.2016\n                                informed RBI that &quot;all applications around CBS have been\n                                integrated with STP except Treasury&quot; the FMRs as above\n                                reported that the Modus Operandi of the fraud was facilitated\n                                by Issuance of LOUs by SWIFT inter-alia without necessary\n                                applications.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_159\">                                       (ii) On 21.06.2017 the Dy. General Manager of\n                                Department of Banking Supervision of RBI vide an email also\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_372\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_373\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_374\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                     71</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_375\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_376\">18:28</span>\n                                 called upon PNB to submit a Current Status on those paras of\n                                its IT report communicated on 06.06.2016 where the PNB had\n                                assured compliance by a target date. In response, PNB filed not\n                                just a response to compliance by it which remained to be\n                                carried out by a target date but furnished a response to every\n                                paragraph of the said report of RBI. In response to paragraph\n                                3.5 of the IT report, PNB again reiterated that:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_91\">                                       &quot;Bank has a core banking solution which takes care of its\n                                main business requirements. All applications around CBS have\n                                been integrated with STP except Treasury. The observations\n                                may be treated as complied&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_160\">                                       This was a reiteration of the earlier falsehood\n                                communicated to RBI on 08.07.2016, which as aforestated was\n                                found to be manifestly false by column 9c of the FMRs\n                                submitted to it by PNB from January 20 18 onwards. In other\n                                words, the offence of furnishing the same false information was\n                                committed on two separate occasions - 08.07.2016 and\n                                27.06.2017.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_161\">                                       (iii) On 03.08.2016 RBI sent a confidential letter to all\n                                scheduled commercial banks including PNB. Paragraph 3 of the\n                                said letter stated as follows:-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_92\">                                       &quot;You are also advised to get the SWIFT infrastructure\n                                comprehensively audited immediately for malicious software\n                                script/activities. In the meantime, you may take steps to\n                                ensure strict vigil on the transactions made through your\n                                SWIFT infrastructure.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_162\">                                       PNB replied to RBI&#x27;s said letter of 03.08.2016 vide letter\n                                dated 19.08.20 16. Its response to the said paragraph 3 of the\n                                RBI letter was as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_93\">                                       &quot;We confirm SWIFT best practices are being adopted in\n                                the Bank by puffing in place a control mechanism at source\n                                level to carry out additional checks and balances before\n                                transmission of financial messages takes place. Swift system\n                                Configuration is in line with SWIFT protocol and all patches\n                                have been updated to mitigate the cyber security risk. &quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_163\">                                       The FMRs of 2018 disclosed that far from adopting\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_377\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_378\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_379\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                        72</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_380\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_381\">18:28</span>\n                                 SWIFT best practices &quot;by putting in place a control mechanism\n                                at source level to carry out additional checks and balances\n                                before transmission of financial messages takes place&quot; by P B\n                                as informed in its reply dated 19.08.2016, Letters of\n                                Undertakings (LOUs) were issued by the Brady House branch\n                                of the bank, as per column 9c of the FMRs &quot;without obtaining\n                                approval of the Authority, without necessary applications,\n                                documents and without making entries in the CBS 5ystem &#x27;.\n                                Thus PNB once again furnished false information to the RBI.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_164\">                                       (iv) Under a covering letter of 27.10.2016, the RBI sent\n                                PNB a questionnaire about the practices followed by PNB\n                                regarding SWIFT framework. QA(i) read as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_94\">                                       &#x27;Does the bank undertake any reconciliation to verify\n                                whether all outward SWIFT messages transmitted by the bank\n                                have been captured in CBS?&quot;\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_95\">                                       The response to QA(i) given by PNB was as follows:-\n                                       &quot;Outward SWIFT message is sent after entering in CBS\n                                and passing/verification by two officials, without the entry in\n                                CBS, 110 financial SWIFT message can be sent. However.\n                                reconciliation to verify that all outward messages transmitted by\n                                the bank are captured in CBS is being done by A D branches&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_165\">                                       The statement was again found to be false by FMRs of\n                                2018 as aforestated column 9c dealing with modus operandi of\n                                the fraud categorically disclosed that Letters of Undertaking by\n                                SWIFT were issued without making any entries in the CBS\n                                (Core Banking System).\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_166\">                                       v) The final instance consists not of a making false\n                                statement but of the alternative offence U/S 46(1) of the Act, i.e.,\n                                willful omission to make a material statement. RBI vide its email\n                                dated 21.06.2017 had sought from PNB. A Current Status report\n                                on those paras of its IT Compliance Report communicated on\n                                06.06.2016 where it had assured compliance by a target date.\n                                The original observation made by RBI in paragraph 10.7 of its\n                                IT exam report of 06.06.20 16 was as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_96\">                                       &quot;10.7 As regards systems relating to SWIFT operations\n                                were concerned. it was ascertained from the bank that the Base\n                                Software (application) patching has been done upto 7.1.14.\n                                However, patching status of other components of SWIFT\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_382\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_383\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_384\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                           73</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_385\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_386\">18:28</span>\n                                 infrastructure like OS/Data base server. Network devices\n                                enabling SWIFT messaging platform was not available. There\n                                was 110 online integration of SWIFT with CBS, Treasury,\n                                EDMS or Overseas systems. Bank also confirmed that no\n                                security incidents have been reported in the SWIFT operations\n                                for the last two years .. .. &quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_167\">                                       In response to paragraph 10.7 of the IT Exam Report of\n                                06.06.2016, PNB in its IT Compliance/Action Taken Report of\n                                08 .07.20 16 had responded with the following:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_97\">                                       &quot;10.7 A comprehensive review of SWIFT operations vis-\n                                a-vis observations of various auditors and actionable items by\n                                3010912016 (Action ITO and IBD).&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_168\">                                       It is pertinent to note here that PNB was specifically\n                                asked to submit a Current Status Report vis-a-vis those\n                                paragraphs wherein PNB in its Compliance/Action Taken\n                                Report of July 2016 had remarked by specifying a target date\n                                for compliance. The Current Status Report submitted by PNB on\n                                27.06.2017 did not consist of the status report on the afore\n                                stated 10.7 paragraph of the IT exam report and the IT\n                                Compliance/Action taken report of 08 .07.20 16. Thus, in this\n                                instance PNB was guilty of the offence of wilful omission to\n                                make a material statement, also punishable uls 46 of the Act.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_169\">                                       8. The Petitioner contends that the summoning order of\n                                magistrate is also silent about which sub-section of<a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_220\"> s.46</a> of the\n                                Act it is summoned to answer the charge. This is a frivolous\n                                objection as extensive facts regarding the commission of the\n                                offence are set out in the Complaint, copy of which has been\n                                served upon Petitioners as per the provision of s.204(3). The Ld.\n                                Magistrate has also passed a detailed order setting out reasons\n                                for issuing of summons and these also set out the facts that\n                                constitute the offences for which the accused was summoned.\n                                The summoning order also discloses that it has been issued for\n                                offences uls 46 of the Act. From a combined reading of\n                                averments In the complaint, the speaking order issuing the\n                                summons itself, the accused have complete notice that the\n                                offences alleged against them are those prescribed and\n                                punishable under<a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_221\"> s.46(1)</a> of the Act.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_170\">                                9. In any event, it is settled law that the summoning order need\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_387\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_388\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_389\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                        74</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_390\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_391\">18:28</span>\n                                 not contain the precise section for which the accused are\n                                summoned. In a warrant triable case instituted other than on a\n                                police report i.e., on a complaint as in the present case, the\n                                precise section of the <a href=\"/doc/1569253/\" id=\"a_222\">IPC</a> with which the accused is charged is\n                                at the stage when charges are to be framed, which is u/s 246 in\n                                chapter XIX-B as is evident from the law <a href=\"/doc/1922701/\" id=\"a_223\">laid down in</a> the\n                                following cases:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_98\">                                <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"716370\" id=\"span_392\">(i) In <a href=\"/doc/716370/\" id=\"a_224\">Dy. Chief Controller of Imports &amp; Exports Vs. Roshanlal\n                                Agarwal &amp; Ors</a> &quot; SCC 139 the Hon&#x27;ble Supreme Court\n                                considered an order passed by the Special Court taking\n                                cognizance of an offence u/s 5 of the Import and Export Control\n                                Act and other offences under the <a href=\"/doc/1569253/\" id=\"a_225\">IPC</a>. The order passed by the\n                                Ld. Magistrate read as under: In paragraphs 8 and 9 it held the\n                                following:</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_99\">                                &quot;8 . ... &#x27;Cognizance taken. Register the case. Issue summons to\n                                the accused.&#x27;\n                                The High Court had quashed the order on the ground that it did\n                                not disclose that the magistrate had even perused the complaint\n                                or even applied his mind before taking cognizance. The\n                                Supreme Court set aside the order of the High Court on the\n                                ground that:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_100\">                                &quot;9. In determining the question whether any process is to be\n                                issued or not, what the Magistrate has to be satisfied is whether\n                                there is sufficient ground for proceeding and not, whether there\n                                is sufficient for conviction. Whether the evidence is adequate for\n                                supporting the conviction can be determined only at the trial\n                                and not at the stage of inquiry. At the stage of issuing the\n                                process to the accused, the Magistrate is not required to record\n                                reasons. &quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_171\">                                <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1310346\" id=\"span_393\">(ii) The High Court of Bombay in <a href=\"/doc/1310346/\" id=\"a_226\">Charanjit Singh v. Assistant\n                                Collector of Central Excise</a> 1989 SCCOnline Bom 505 held\n                                that:</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_172\">                                &quot;10 .... Only because the sections under which the learned\n                                Magistrate was proceeding had not been mentioned in the order\n                                of issue of process, it would not follow that there was non-\n                                application of mind .... What exact charge could be framed\n                                against the accused is a mailer which would arise for decision\n                                only at a later stage after evidence is recorded. this being a\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_394\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_395\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_396\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                         75</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_397\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_398\">18:28</span>\n                                 warrant case.&quot;\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_173\">                                (iii) The High Court of Punjab and Haryana in MurariLal Goel\n                                v Sukhcharan Singh Bajwa 1989 SCCOnline P&amp;H 309 held\n                                that:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_101\">                                &quot;4. It is not necessary to mention any particular offence or\n                                offences in the summoning order. Where certain offences are\n                                mentioned as in this case it cannot be taken to mean that the\n                                accused is discharged or absolved of the remaining offences.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_174\">                                <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1814230\" id=\"span_399\">10. The Counsel for the Petitioner Bank stated that he does not\n                                seek quashing of entire proceedings but only seeks a remand to\n                                trial court for fresh consideration. The Hon&#x27;ble Supreme in <a href=\"/doc/1814230/\" id=\"a_227\">U.P.\n                                Pollution Control Board vs. Mohan Meakins Ltd &amp; Ors</a> (2000)\n                                3 SCC 745 dealt with a situation where a Sessions Court in\n                                revision quashed process issued by Magistrate and remanded\n                                the matter to him for reconsideration. In paragraph 7 of <a href=\"/doc/1814230/\" id=\"a_228\">the said\n                                judgment</a>, the court held that:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_102\">                                &quot;it is unfortunate that the Sessions Judge himself did not look\n                                into the complaint at that stage to form his own opinion whether\n                                process could have been issued by the Chief Judicial Magistrale\n                                on the basis of the averments contained in the complaint.\n                                instead the Sessions Judge relegated the work to the trial\n                                Magistrale for doing the exercise over again.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_175\">                                12. lt is respectfully submitted that if this Hon&#x27; ble Court has\n                                any doubt whether the complaint discloses an offence, the\n                                proper course would be to form its own opinion whether process\n                                could have been issued by the magistrate on the basis of the\n                                averments contained in the complaint instead of relegating the\n                                work to the trial Magistrate for doing the exercise over again.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_176\">                                13. Moreover, the factual averments in the complaint relied\n                                upon by the Ld. Magistrate in his order issuing process disclose\n                                an offence under <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_229\">section 46(1)</a> of the Act only and cannot and\n                                do not disclose the offences under sub sections (2) (3) and (4)\n                                ofs.46 of the Act.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_177\">                                14. <a href=\"/doc/1638992/\" id=\"a_230\">Section 46(2)</a> punishes the failure by any person &quot;to\n                                produce any book. Account or other document or to furnish any\n                                statement or information which Under subsection (2) of 35 it is\n                                his duty to produce or furnish or to answer any question\n                                relating to the business of a banking company, which is asked\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_400\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_401\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_402\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                        76</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_403\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_404\">18:28</span>\n                                 by an officer making an inspection or scrutiny under that\n                                section.&quot; The various acts/omission complained of in the instant\n                                Complaint filed by the RBI do not consist of a failure to produce\n                                any book, account or other document or a failure to furnish any\n                                statement or information. The acts complained of in the\n                                Complaint are those, which constitute the wilful making of a\n                                statement which is false in a material particular knowing it to\n                                be false and in the one instance of wilfully omitting to make a\n                                material statement. Further, <a href=\"/doc/1638992/\" id=\"a_231\">Section 46(2)</a> of the Act does not\n                                punish the wilful making of a false statement in a material\n                                particular or wilfully omitting to make a material statement; it\n                                simpliciter publishes any failure to produce any book, account\n                                or other document or a failure to furnish any statement or\n                                information, which it is its duty to produce or to answer any\n                                question. Thus for the purpose of<a href=\"/doc/1638992/\" id=\"a_232\"> s.46(2)</a>, it is irrelevant if a\n                                stamen is wilfully false in a material particular or there is a\n                                wilful omission to make a material statement. <a href=\"/doc/1638992/\" id=\"a_233\">Section 46(2)</a>\n                                punishes the failure to do any of the Acts mentioned in the sub-\n                                section irrespective of mens rea and irrespective of the\n                                materiality of what is required to be furnished. That is why the\n                                offence<a href=\"/doc/1638992/\" id=\"a_234\"> u/s 46(2)</a>, being an offence of strict liability is\n                                punishable with fine only whereas the offence under <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_235\">section\n                                46(1)</a> requiring mens rea is punishable with imprisonment up to\n                                3 years and or with fine.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_178\">                                15. Moreover, <a href=\"/doc/1638992/\" id=\"a_236\">section 46(2)</a> requires that the failure to perform\n                                the various acts prescribed in the sub section must be in the\n                                course of an inspection under sub section (2) of<a href=\"/doc/222598/\" id=\"a_237\"> s.35</a> of the Act.\n                                In the instant case, the RBI had already completed the\n                                inspection, which it was empowered to carry out under <a href=\"/doc/222598/\" id=\"a_238\">section\n                                35</a> of the Act, by February 2016. The Information sought by the\n                                RBI from the Accused including the Petitioner Bank (PNB) and\n                                to which the accused wilfully gave also answers to or wilfully\n                                omitted to make a material statement in response thereto, was\n                                not in the course of an inspection. This information was sought\n                                in June 2016, much post the inspection carried out by the RBI\n                                and the false statement/wilful omissions were made from July\n                                2016 till June 2017. Thus, in any view of the matter the offences\n                                alleged in the Complaint could never be those prescribed under\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_405\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_406\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_407\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                        77</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_408\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_409\">18:28</span>\n                                 <a href=\"/doc/1638992/\" id=\"a_239\">section 46(2)</a> of the Act.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_179\">                                16. <a href=\"/doc/1363098/\" id=\"a_240\">Section 46(3)</a> is ex-facie in-applicable as that section\n                                punishes the receipt by a banking company of any deposit in\n                                contravention of an order under <a href=\"/doc/509759/\" id=\"a_241\">section 35(4)(a)</a> of the Act. The\n                                Complaint in the instant case is manifestly not concerned with\n                                the receipt by PNB of any deposit in contravention of an order\n                                under the said <a href=\"/doc/509759/\" id=\"a_242\">section 35(4)(a)</a> of the Act.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_180\">                                17. Finally, the instant Complaint is also ex facie not concerned\n                                with a contravention of the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_243\">section 46(4)</a> of the\n                                Act. Firstly, sub-section (4) of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_244\">section 46</a> commences with the\n                                words &quot;if any other provision of this act is contravened&quot;, the\n                                words &quot;any other provision&quot; meaning other than the provisions\n                                of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_245\">section 46</a> (I ) (2) and (3). In other words<a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_246\"> s.46(4)</a> does not\n                                deal with the offence of &quot; wilfully making a statement which is\n                                false in material particular or wilfully omitting to make a\n                                material statement.&quot; Moreover, the contraventions which\n                                section 46(4) deals with are expressly set out in clauses (i) and\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_181\">                                (ii) of sub section (4) of<a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_247\"> s.46</a>. It is manifestly clear that the\n                                instant Complaint is not concerned with the noncompliance with\n                                any requirement of the act, order, rule or direction made or\n                                condition imposed thereunder as prescribed by clause (i) of sub\n                                section (4) of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_248\">section 46</a> of the Act nor is it concerned with\n                                carrying out the terms of, or the obligations under, a scheme\n                                sanctioned under sub-section (7) of <a href=\"/doc/1829498/\" id=\"a_249\">section 45</a> as prescribed by\n                                clause (ii) of sub section (4) of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_250\">section 46</a> of the Act.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_182\">                                18. The upshot is that the Complaint and the Summoning order\n                                charged and summoned the accused with infringing the\n                                provisions containing in <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_251\">section 46(1)</a> of the Act only. The\n                                averments in the Complaint were not even remotely concerned\n                                with the provisions of sub-sections (2) (3) and (4) of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_252\">section 46</a>\n                                of the Act. The contentions of PNB are wilfully misconceived\n                                and the Complaint and the Summoning Order have been\n                                deliberately misconstrued.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_183\">                          100. Significantly, the respondent no.2 submitted through its written\n                          submissions whilst placing reliance on the response filed by the PNB i.e. the\n                          petitioner to the present petition to para 3.5 of the IT Report communicated\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_410\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_411\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_412\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                              78</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_413\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_414\">18:28</span>\n                           on 06.06.2016 by the RBI to the petitioner, that the PNB again reiterated to\n                          the effect:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_103\">                                 &quot;Bank has a core banking solution which takes care of its\n                                 main business requirements. All applications around CBS have\n                                 been integrated with STP except Treasury. The RBI thus submitted that the current status report submitted that\n                          the PNB thought that it was asked to submit a current status report vis-a-vis\n                          those paragraphs wherein PNB in its Compliance/Action Taken Report of\n                          July, 2016 and had remarked by specifying a target date for compliance and\n                          that in fact the current status report submitted by PNB on 27.06.2017 did not\n                          consist of the status report on the aforesaid para 10.7 of the IT exam report\n                          and the IT Compliance/Action taken report of 08.07.2016 and that the\n                          respondent no.2 thus contended that the PNB was guilty of the offence of\n                          wilful omission to make a material statement and also punishable under\n                          <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_254\">Section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_193\">                          105.     The respondent no.2 has thus submitted that the summoning order\n                          of the Magistrate specifies the grounds that constitute the offence for which\n                          the accused was summoned. Inter alia the respondent no.2 submitted that\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_425\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_426\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_427\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                              81</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_428\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_429\">18:28</span>\n                           extensive facts regarding the commission of the offence have been set out\n                          by the complainant i.e. the respondent no.2.
