Inter alia the respondent no.2 submitted that the factual averments in\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_431\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_432\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_433\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                              82</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_434\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_435\">18:28</span>\n                           the complaint relied upon by the learned Magistrate in the order issuing\n                          process disclose an offence under <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_258\">Section 46(1)</a> of the Banking Regulation\n                          Act, 1949 only and cannot and do not disclose the offences under sub\n                          sections (2) (3) and (4) ofs.46 of the <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_259\">Banking Regulation Act, 1949</a>.\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_196\">                          108.    The respondent no.2 through its written submissions as already\n                          adverted to hereinabove has categorically submitted that the complaint and\n                          the summoning order, summoned the accused with infringing the provisions\n                          contained in <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_260\">Section 46(1)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 only and\n                          that the averments in the complaint were not even remotely concerned with\n                          the provisions of sub-sections (2) (3) and (4) of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_261\">Section 46</a> of the Banking\n                          Regulation Act, 1949. The respondent no.2 has further submitted that the\n                          contentions of the PNB are wilfully misconceived and the complaint and the\n                          summoning order have been deliberately misconstrued.\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_197\">                          109.    As regards the contention raised on behalf of the petitioner/PNB that\n                          in terms of <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_262\">Section 47A(4)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949, which\n                          reads to the effect:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_110\">                                 &quot;No complaint shall be filed against any banking company in any\n                                 court of law in respect of any contravention or default in respect\n                                 of which any penalty has been imposed by the Reserve Bank\n                                 under this section.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_198\">                          and the consequential submissions made by the PNB that the contents in the\n                          instant case filed by the respondent no.2, the RBI was bad in law in terms of\n                          <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_263\">Section 47A(4)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949, it was submitted by\n                          the RBI that the same was wholly misconceived.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_199\">                          110. It was submitted thus by the respondent no.2 to the effect:-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_111\">                                 &quot;It is respectfully submitted that this contention is totally\n                                 misconceived. The Petitioner Bank has annexed to its petition\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_436\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_437\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_438\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                              83</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_439\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_440\">18:28</span>\n                                  before this court, both the Show Cause Notice (&quot;SC&quot;) in respect\n                                 of the penalty imposed and the Order imposing the penalty. The\n                                 SCN and the Order both reflect that the proceedings were\n                                 initiated and the final order of penalty imposed on account of\n                                 violation by the Petitioner Bank of the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_264\">section\n                                 46(4)</a> of the Act. In particular, the SCN initiating penalty\n                                 proceedings and the Order imposing the penalty after hearing\n                                 the Petitioner Bank, both disclose that the proceedings were\n                                 concerned for a violation by Petitioner bank of <a href=\"/doc/5624913/\" id=\"a_265\">section 46(4)(i)</a>\n                                 for non-compliance with and contravention of directions\n                                 contained       in    RBI      circular     Ref.     DBS      (CO)\n                                 CSITE/4493/31.01.015/2017-18 dated 20.2.2018. The bar U/S\n                                 47A(4) manifestly cannot apply to the instant complaint filed by\n                                 the RBI as that bar applies &quot;in respect of any contravention or\n                                 default in respect of which any penalty has been imposed by the\n                                 Reserve Bank under this section&quot;. The words - &quot;under this\n                                 section&quot; refer to Section 47 A whose title reads &quot;Power of\n                                 Reserve bank to impose penalty. Section 47A(1) expressly\n                                 empowers Reserve Bank to impose a penalty for a contravention\n                                 or a default of the provisions of sub section (2), (3) or (4) of\n                                 <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_266\">section 46</a> of the Act. The section does not contemplate the\n                                 imposition of any penalty by the RB[ for a contravention of\n                                 <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_267\">section 46(1)</a> of the Act. The penalty imposed on Petitioner\n                                 Bank as aforestated u/s 47A is one for, a violation of the\n                                 provisions of <a href=\"/doc/5624913/\" id=\"a_268\">section 46(4)(i)</a>. The Complaint filed by the RBI is\n                                 for the offence under <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_269\">section 46(1)</a> of the Act of wilfully making\n                                 a false statement in a material particular and wilfully omitting\n                                 to make a material statement. These acts are not within the\n                                 scope of <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_270\">section 46(4)</a> for which the penalty has been\n                                 undeniably imposed. On the other hand, <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_271\">section 46(1)</a> is totally\n                                 unconcerned with non-compliance or contravention of a RBI\n                                 circular, which in the penalty proceedings pertain to the RBI\n                                 circular dated 20.2.20 18. In short, the penalty has been\n                                 imposed for a violation of<a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_272\"> s.46(4)</a> of the Act and the Complaint\n                                 is filed for an altogether different and distinct contravention<a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_273\"> u/s\n                                 46 (1)</a> of the Act. Ex-facie the bar u/s 47A(4) does not apply to\n                                 the Complaint, which is the subject matter of the present\n\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_441\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_442\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_443\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                           84</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_444\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_445\">18:28</span>\n                                  proceedings and the contentions of the Petitioner Bank in this\n                                 respect are liable to be dismissed as ex-facie untenable.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_200\">                                                          ANALYSIS\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_201\">                          111. On consideration of the entire available record and rival pleas\n                          addressed on behalf of either side, this Court is of the considered view that\n                          the impugned order dated 11 .3 2019 of the Learned MM 05PHC in CC\n                          number 16711 of 2018 which reads to the effect:-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_112\">                                  &quot;Present complainant<a href=\"/doc/444619/\" id=\"a_274\"> u/s 200</a> Cr.P.C has been filed on behalf\n                                  of Reserve Bank of India. As per case of complainant,\n                                  complainant corporation is a statutory corporation and as per\n                                  <a href=\"/doc/222598/\" id=\"a_275\">Section 35</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949, complainant\n                                  is vested with the power to cause an inspection by one or more\n                                  of its officers of any banking company and its books and\n                                  accounts. The averments in the\n                             complaint of the complainant indicate that one of the crucial observations\n                             made in the IT examination report were vide 3.5 to the effect:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_116\">                                 &quot;3.5 Bank had not planned or envisioned implementation of\n                                 Single Enterprise Application as it had 80 applications\n                                 consisting of in-house developed purchased and outsourced\n                                 activities CBS was not integrated in automated fashion with\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_461\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_462\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_463\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                              88</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_464\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_465\">18:28</span>\n                                  various critical applications i.e. no Straight through\n                                 Processing (STP) was envisaged and the middleware used\n                                 involved manual interventions giving rise to various risks.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_203\">                             to which accused number 9 on behalf of accused No.1 i.e. the present\n                             petitioner submitted allegedly falsely to the effect :\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_117\">                               &quot;Bank has a core banking solution which takes care of its main\n                               business requirements. All applications around CBS have been\n                               integrated with STP except Treasury. The observation may be\n                               treated as complied.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_204\">                          112. The impugned order observes inter alia to the effect that as alleged by\n                          the complainant Corporation apart from a false statement in the compliance /\n                          action taken report sent on 8.7.2016 by the petitioner herein which reply was\n                          prepared, authorized and personally approved by accused nos.2,3,6,7,8 and 9\n                          and was allegedly evident from in the internal documentary exchanges dated\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_205\">                          4. 10.16 between the proposed accused persons which alleged false\n                          statement was made again on 27.6.2017 in response to a status report sought\n                          by the Deputy General Manager, Department of Banking supervision of the\n                          complainant Corporation vide an email dated 21.6.2017 the petitioner who\n                          had assured compliance by a target date, rather submitted a response\n                          indicating that the targets were yet to be achieved and the PNB again\n                          reiterated the response submitted by it vide email dated 8.7.16.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_206\">                          113. The impugned order takes into account the contentions raised by the\n                          complainant that the accused persons not only made a false statement in\n                          compliance/ action taken report but also wilfully made false statements\n                          despite being aware of the risks and repercussions of failing to integrate\n                          SWIFT with CBS and introducing STP between SWIFT messaging and\n                          CBS and that even in relation to the confidential circular issued on or about\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_466\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_467\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_468\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                              89</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_469\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_470\">18:28</span>\n                           03.08.2016 by the complainant to all scheduled commercial banks including\n                          the petitioner whereby, the banks were advised to strengthen the controls\n                          around the operating environment for fund transfers through SWIFT or\n                          similar interfaces and banks were advised to minimize the practice of direct\n                          creation of payment messages in the SWIFT environment with routing the\n                          same through CBS, for which discussions were held in a special meeting\n                          held on 19.08.2016 by various concerned divisions of the petitioner and\n                          accused no.11 being the convener of the meeting, pursuant thereto, the\n                          accused no. 5 under his signature, vide letter dated 19.08.2016 gave the false\n                          information to complainant corporation.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_207\">                          114.     The impugned order of the learned trial Court takes into account the\n                          submissions that have been made by the complainant through the complaint\n                          that on 25.11.2016, the complainant corporation again issued a confidential\n                          circular dated 31.01.2015, which was sent in a password protected form to\n                          the accused no.1 i.e. the petitioner herein and a letter dated 27.10.2016 in\n                          the form of questionnaire as issued by complainant corporation and response\n                          to the same sought and received from the petitioner, qua which, the\n                          petitioner gave false and misleading answer which was approved by the\n                          accused no. 4, 5,10 and 11 on behalf of the accused no. 1which aspects were\n                          taken into account by the learned trial Court at the order of summoning of\n                          the petitioner and other accused persons.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_208\">                          115. The contention raised by the complainant corporation that it\n                          discovered the falsity of the information provided by the proposed accused\n                          persons while scrutinizing the Brady House Branch, Mumbai of the accused\n                          no.1 and that it was found that information furnished on behalf of the\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_471\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_472\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_473\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                               90</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_474\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_475\">18:28</span>\n                           petitioner in last few years are completely false and that the proposed\n                          accused persons had wilfully and deliberately made false statement in utter\n                          disregard to their statutory obligation under <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_284\">Section 46</a> of the Banking\n                          Regulation Act, 1949 were taken into account by the learned trial Court at\n                          the time of the passing of the summoning order.\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_209\">                          116. The contention raised on behalf of the complainant through the\n                          complaint that during the IT examination conducted on behalf of the\n                          complainant at the premises of the petitioner, it was observed that the core\n                          banking system was not integrated with many critical applications and there\n                          was no online integration of SWIFT with CBS and that the accused persons\n                          had deliberately furnished false and misleading statements to the\n                          complainant corporation, which was done with common intention and in\n                          connivance with each other were taken into account by the learned trial\n                          Court observing thus that a case was made out in view of the complaint an\n                          documents on record for summoning of all the proposed accused persons in\n                          respect of the offence under <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_285\">Section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation Act,\n                          1949.\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_210\">                          117. Taking the observations in the impugned order into account, the\n                          contention raised on behalf of the petitioner, the PNB that the petitioner has\n                          been gravely prejudiced by the impugned order, which does not detail any\n                          reason for summoning the petitioner under <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_286\">Section 46</a> of the Banking\n                          Regulation Act, 1949 and does not spell out which particular subsection of\n                          <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_287\">Section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 has been invoked for which\n                          alleged offence, i.e. the petitioner and other accused persons have been\n                          summoned, - cannot be accepted.\n</p>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_476\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_477\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_478\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                               91</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_479\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_480\">18:28</span>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_211\">                           118. It is essential to observe that <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_288\">Section 46(1)</a> of the Banking Regulation\n                          Act, 1949, which reads to the effect:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_118\">                                  &quot;(1) Whoever in any return, balance-sheet or other document\n                                  1[or in any information required or furnished] by or under or\n                                  for the purposes of any provision of this Act, wilfully makes a\n                                  statement which is false in any material particular, knowing it\n                                  to be false, or wilfully omits to make a material statement,\n                                  shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term which may\n                                  extend to three years and shall also be liable to fine.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_212\">                          specifically imposes penalties and imprisonment in relation to wilful\n                          making a statement, which is false in any material particular knowing it to\n                          be false or wilful omitting to make material statement and that thus the\n                          contention that the petitioner seeks to urge through submissions made\n                          through the present petition, written submissions filed on behalf of the\n                          petitioner and oral submissions made by the learned senior counsel for the\n                          petitioner to the effect that the complaint filed by the complainant is\n                          probably a complaint under <a href=\"/doc/1638992/\" id=\"a_289\">Section 46(2)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act,\n                          1949 or under <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_290\">Section 46(4)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949, too\n                          cannot be countenanced presently.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_213\">                          119. This is so in as much as <a href=\"/doc/1638992/\" id=\"a_291\">Section 46(2)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act,\n                          1949 relates to the non-production of any book, account or other document\n                          or to furnish any statement or information in terms of the <a href=\"/doc/1942139/\" id=\"a_292\">Section 35(2)</a> of\n                          such enactment or not answering to the question relating to the business of a\n                          banking company and does not relate to wilfully making false statement in\n                          relation to any material particular knowing it to be false or wilfully omitting\n                          to make material statement, which falls within the ambit of <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_293\">Section 46(1)</a> of\n                          the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.\n</p>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_481\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_482\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_483\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                                92</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_484\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_485\">18:28</span>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_214\">                           120. As regards the contention raised on behalf of the petitioner that the\n                          allegations made in the complaint would fall within the ambit of <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_294\">Section\n                          46(4)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949, it is essential to observe that\n                          <a href=\"/doc/78403415/\" id=\"a_295\">Section 46(4)(ii)</a> is clearly inapplicable in as much as it is related to carrying\n                          out the terms of, or the obligations under, a scheme sanctioned under sub-\n                          section (7) of the said enactment.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_215\">                          121. As regards <a href=\"/doc/5624913/\" id=\"a_296\">Section 46(4)(i)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949\n                          which makes contravention of any other provision of this Act or if any\n                          default is made in complying with any requirement of this Act or of any\n                          order, rule or direction made or condition imposed there under or by the\n                          very words if any other provision at the outset under <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_297\">Section 46(4)</a> of the\n                          Banking Regulation Act, 1949 make it apparent that it relates to violation\n                          and non-compliance and default in cases other than those described in\n                          <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_298\">Section 46(1)</a>, <a href=\"/doc/1638992/\" id=\"a_299\">46(2)</a>, <a href=\"/doc/1363098/\" id=\"a_300\">46(3)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_216\">                          122. In these circumstances, it cannot be said that the contents of the\n                          complaint that were put forth by the complainant corporation i.e. the\n                          respondent no.2 and the impugned order dated 11.03.2019 of the learned\n                          trial Court did not spell out the alleged commission of the offence\n                          punishable under <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_301\">Section 46(1)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 against\n                          the petitioner herein. The contention thus raised through the written\n                          submissions made by the petitioner at A, B and E as adverted to\n                          hereinabove, cannot be accepted.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_217\">                          123. As regards the contention raised through the written submissions at\n                          Ground D raised by the petitioner, which reads to the effect:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_119\">                               &quot;D. THE IMPUGNED SUMMONING ORDER SUFFERS FROM\n                               A TOTAL NON - APPLICATION OF MIND AND DESERVES TO\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_486\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_487\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_488\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                                  93</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_489\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_490\">18:28</span>\n                                BE SET ASIDE ON THIS SHORT GROUN D:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_120\">                               1. The summoning order passed by the Learned Trial Court is a\n                               mere recapitulation of the allegations made by Respondent No.2 in\n                               its complaint and does not show any application of mind by the\n                               Learned Trial Court. It is a settled position of law that a\n                               summoning order must reflect the due application of mind and the\n                               Magistrate must satisfy himself that there is sufficient basis for\n                               proceeding against the accused. The absolute absence of\n                               application of mind in the impugned summoning order is also\n                               demonstrated by the fact that akin to criminal complaint filed by\n                               Respondent No. 2, even in the impugned summoning order, there is\n                               no indication as to under which sub-section of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_302\">section 46</a> of the\n                               1949 Act, Petitioner has been summoned under.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_121\">                               2. Summons issued by a criminal court are equally important and\n                               the summons issued by the Learned Trial Court (Annexure-P12 @\n                               pg. 65 of Petition) to Petitioner, in the present case, refer to\n                               <a href=\"/doc/1823824/\" id=\"a_303\">section 138</a> of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, which shows\n                               the lack of application of mind by the Learned Metropolitan\n                               Magistrate.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_218\">                          that the impugned summoning order suffers from a total non-application of\n                          mind and deserves to be set aside, taking into account the factum that the\n                          process had been issued under <a href=\"/doc/1823824/\" id=\"a_304\">Section 138</a> of the Negotiable Instruments\n                          Act, 1881, undoubtedly the process in the instant case issued is one under\n                          <a href=\"/doc/1823824/\" id=\"a_305\">Section 138</a> of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 and issuance of the\n                          same is extremely disturbing. However, the impugned order specifies the\n                          offence for which the petitioner has been summoned and the petitioner is\n                          aware of the contents of the impugned order.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_219\">                          124. It is the cardinal principal of jurisprudence that procedures are the\n                          handmaidens of justice and that justice per se when sought or sought to be\n                          invoked cannot be thwarted by procedural irregularities when no prejudice\n                          has been caused to the accused.\n</p>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_491\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_492\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_493\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                           94</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_494\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_495\">18:28</span>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_220\">                           125. It is, however, directed hereinabove that the learned trial Court\n                          ought to have ensured and that the Ld. Trial Courts shall henceforth\n                          ensure that the process issued to the accused persons is verified before\n                          issuance thereof so that hyperbolic errors of the kind as in the instant\n                          case of issuance of a process under <a href=\"/doc/1823824/\" id=\"a_306\">Section 138</a> of the Negotiable\n                          Instruments Act, 1881 in place of a process for summons of the accused\n                          under <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_307\">Section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 is not repeated in\n                          future.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_221\">                          126. Copy of this order with these directions be also sent to the learned\n                          trial Court which has passed the impugned order and issued the process in\n                          relation thereto. The learned District &amp; Sessions Judges of all Districts in\n                          Delhi/ New Delhi shall ensure that they put in place a procedure in their\n                          Registries to ensure issuance of the summons in terms of the summoning\n                          orders. Copy of these directions be also sent to all District &amp; Sessions\n                          Judges of New Delhi and Delhi through the learned Registrar General of this\n                          Court for compliance.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_222\">                          127. As regards the contention raised on behalf of the petitioner that in\n                          terms of <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_308\">Section 47A (4)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949, which reads\n                          to the effect:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_122\">                                 &quot;No complaint shall be filed against any banking company in any\n                                 court of law in respect of any contravention or default in respect\n                                 of which any penalty has been imposed by the Reserve Bank\n                                 under this section.&quot;,\n                          and that there is a complete embargo to any complaint being filed against the\n                          petitioner in any Court of law in respect of any contravention or default in\n                          respect of which any penalty has been imposed by the Reserve Bank under\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_496\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_497\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_498\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                              95</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_499\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_500\">18:28</span>\n                           <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_309\">Section 47A</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 and thus the contention\n                          raised by the petitioner to the effect that the allegations made in the criminal\n                          complaint are similar to allegations that were made in the show cause notice\n                          dated 23.08.2018, qua which, vide a letter dated 25.03.2019 of the\n                          committee of the Executive Directors of the RBI, a penalty of Rs.2 crores\n                          had been imposed on the petitioner Bank and which has since been\n                          deposited also by the petitioner, the complaint filed by the complainant was\n                          barred by <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_310\">Section 47A(4)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 cannot be\n                          accepted for the reasons that <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_311\">Section 47A(4)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act,\n                          1949 referred to hereinabove puts an embargo on the institution of a\n                          complaint against any banking company in any Court of law in respect of\n                          any contravention and default in respect of any penalty as has been imposed\n                          by the RBI under &quot;this Section&quot; i.e. <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_312\">Section 47A</a> of the Banking Regulation\n                          Act, 1949.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_223\">                          128. <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_313\">Section 47A(1)(a)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 relates to\n                          imposition of penalty by the RBI on the banking company where the\n                          contravention or default is of the nature referred to in subsection (2) of\n                          <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_314\">Section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949. Subsection 3 of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_315\">Section 46</a>\n                          of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 reads to the effect:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_123\">                               &quot;(3) If any deposits are received by a banking company in\n                               contra-vention of an order under clause (a) of sub-section (4) of\n                               <a href=\"/doc/222598/\" id=\"a_316\">section 35</a>, every director or other officer of the banking\n                               company, unless he proves that the contravention took place\n                               without his knowledge or that he exercised all due diligence to\n                               prevent it shall be deemed to be guilty of such contravention and\n                               shall be punishable with a fine which may extend to twice the\n                               amount of the deposits so received.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_224\">                          and thus relates to receipt of deposits by a banking company in\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_501\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_502\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_503\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                                 96</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_504\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_505\">18:28</span>\n                           contravention of an order under <a href=\"/doc/509759/\" id=\"a_317\">Section 35 (4)(a)</a> of the Banking Regulation\n                          Act, 1949 and does not relate to an offence prescribed and made punishable\n                          for furnishing of false information, statement or wilful omission to make\n                          true statement, which falls within the ambit of <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_318\">Section 46(1)</a> of the Banking\n                          Regulation Act, 1949.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_225\">                          129. Likewise in terms of <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_319\">Section 47A(c)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act,\n                          1949, the penalty is imposable by the RBI where there is a contravention or\n                          default specifically of the nature referred to in <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_320\">Section 46(4)</a> of the Banking\n                          Regulation Act, 1949.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_226\">                          130. <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_321\">Section 46(4)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 as already\n                          interpreted hereinabove specifies also that <a href=\"/doc/107190128/\" id=\"a_322\">Section 46(4)</a> of the Banking\n                          Regulation Act, 1949 relates to contravention or default made qua any other\n                          provision being flouted other than those prescribed in <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_323\">Section 46 (1)</a>, <a href=\"/doc/1638992/\" id=\"a_324\">46 (2)</a>,\n                          <a href=\"/doc/1363098/\" id=\"a_325\">46(3)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949. Thus the contention raised on\n                          behalf of the petitioner that in view of the imposition of the penalty on the\n                          petitioner vide a letter dated 25.08.2019 by the committee of the Executive\n                          Directors of the RBI, the same places an embargo on the institution on the\n                          complaint bearing No.CC 16711/2018 by the RBI i.e. the respondent no.2 to\n                          the complaint of the said case CC No.16711/2018 is unacceptable and\n                          devoid of merits.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_227\">                          131. Thus, taking into account the submissions that have been made on\n                          behalf of either side, the entire available records with allegations levelled\n                          against the petitioner by the RBI in relation to the alleged false statements\n                          made by the PNB qua the alleged commission of the offence punishable\n                          under <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_326\">Section 46(1)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 by the petitioner\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_506\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_507\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_508\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                               97</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_509\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_510\">18:28</span>\n                           through the contents made in the complaint as set forth through the written\n                          submissions dated 16.11.2019 specifically para 7 thereof, which reads to the\n                          effect:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_124\">                                     &quot;7. (i) The first instance of PNB furnishing false\n                                     information to the RBI was on 08.07.2016. Pursuant to an\n                                     inspection carried out by it in February 2016 of all aspects\n                                     of PNB&#x27;s Information Technology (IT) systems, the bank\n                                     prepared a comprehensive report and forwarded a copy of\n                                     the same to PNB vide email dated 06.06.2016. One of the\n                                     crucial observations made in the IT examination report was\n                                     in paragraph3.5 of the report, which is reproduced below:-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_125\">                                     &quot;3.5 Banks had not planned or envisioned\n                                     implementation of Single Enterprise Application as it had\n                                     80 applications consisting of in-house developed.\n                                     purchased and outsourced activities. CBS was not\n                                     integrated in automated fashion with various critical\n                                     applications i.e., no Straight Through Processing (STP)\n                                     was envisaged and the middleware used involved manual\n                                     interventions giving rise to various risks.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_228\">                                     Through the said mail PNB was called on to submit a\n                                     Compliance Report inter alia on the above observation.\n                                     PNB&#x27;s response to RBI vide letter dated 08.07.2016\n                                     furnished false information regarding its compliance with\n                                     the observations cited above. PNB&#x27;s response to\n                                     paragraph 3.5 of the IT Exam/Report is reproduced\n                                     below:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_126\">                                     &quot;Bank has a core banking solution which takes care of its\n                                     main business requirements. All applications around CBS\n                                     have been integrated with STP except Treasury. The\n                                     observations may be treated as complied&#x27;\n                                     This assertion by PNB was subsequently discovered by\n                                     RBI to be totally false in January 2018 onwards when it\n                                     received a series of Fraud Monitoring Reports (&quot;FMRs&quot;)\n                                     from PNB. In column 9c of these FMRs titled &quot;modus\n                                     operandi&quot;, the report stated as under:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_127\">                                     &quot;9c. Issuance of Letters of Undertaking (LOU) by SWIFT\n                                     without obtaining approval of the Authority, without\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_511\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_512\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_513\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                             98</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_514\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_515\">18:28</span>\n                                      necessary applications. documents and without making\n                                     entries in the CBS system&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_229\">                                     Thus, while PNB&#x27;s compliance response on 08.07.2016\n                                     informed RBI that &quot;all applications around CBS have\n                                     been integrated with STP except Treasury&quot; the FMRs as\n                                     above reported that the Modus Operandi of the fraud was\n                                     facilitated by Issuance of LOUs by SWIFT inter-alia\n                                     without necessary applications.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_230\">                                     (ii) On 21.06.2017 the Dy. General Manager of Department\n                                     of Banking Supervision of RBI vide an email also called\n                                     upon PNB to submit a Current Status on those paras of its\n                                     IT report communicated on 06.06.2016 where the PNB had\n                                     assured compliance by a target date. In response, PNB filed\n                                     not just a response to compliance by it which remained to\n                                     be carried out by a target date but furnished a response to\n                                     every paragraph of the said report of RBI. In response to\n                                     paragraph 3.5 of the IT report, PNB again reiterated that:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_128\">                                     &quot;Bank has a core banking solution which takes care of its\n                                     main business requirements. All applications around CBS\n                                     have been integrated with STP except Treasury. The\n                                     observations may be treated as complied&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_231\">                                     This was a reiteration of the earlier falsehood\n                                     communicated to RBI on 08.07.2016, which as aforestated\n                                     was found to be manifestly false by column 9c of the FMRs\n                                     submitted to it by PNB from January 20 18 onwards. In\n                                     other words, the offence of furnishing the same false\n                                     information was committed on two separate occasions -\n                                     08.07.2016 and 27.06.2017.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_232\">                                     (iii) On 03.08.2016 RBI sent a confidential letter to all\n                                     scheduled commercial banks including PNB. Paragraph 3\n                                     of the said letter stated as follows:-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_129\">                                     &quot;You are also advised to get the SWIFT infrastructure\n                                     comprehensively audited immediately for malicious\n                                     software script/activities. Appropriate steps may be taken to\n                                     rectify malicious activity and to patch vulnerabilities, if\n                                     any, in the SWIFT IT infrastructure. In the meantime, you\n                                     may take steps to ensure strict vigil on the transactions\n                                     made through your SWIFT infrastructure.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_516\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_517\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_518\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                         99</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_519\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_520\">18:28</span>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_233\">                                      PNB replied to RBI&#x27;s said letter of 03.08.2016 vide letter\n                                     dated 19.08.20 16. Its response to the said paragraph 3 of\n                                     the RBI letter was as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_130\">                                     &quot;We confirm SWIFT best practices are being adopted in the\n                                     Bank by puffing in place a control mechanism at source\n                                     level to carry out additional checks and balances before\n                                     transmission of financial messages takes place. Swift\n                                     system Configuration is in line with SWIFT protocol and all\n                                     patches have been updated to mitigate the cyber security\n                                     risk. &quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_234\">                                     The FMRs of 20 18 disclosed that far from adopting SWIFT\n                                     best practices &quot;by putting in place a control mechanism at\n                                     source level to carry out additional checks and balances\n                                     before transmission of financial messages takes place&quot; by P\n                                     B as informed in its reply dated 19.08.2016, Letters of\n                                     Undertakings (LOUs) were issued by the Brady House\n                                     branch of the bank, as per column 9c of the FMRs &quot;without\n                                     obtaining approval of the Authority, without necessary\n                                     applications, documents and without making entries in the\n                                     CBS 5ystem&#x27;. However.\n                                     reconciliation to verify that all outward messages\n                                     transmitted by the bank are captured in CBS is being done\n                                     by A D branches&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_236\">                                     The statement was again found to be false by FMRs of 2018\n                                     as aforestated column 9c dealing with modus operandi of\n                                     the fraud categorically disclosed that Letters of\n                                     Undertaking by SWIFT were issued without making any\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_521\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_522\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_523\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                       100</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_524\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_525\">18:28</span>\n                                      entries in the CBS (Core Banking System).\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_237\">                                     v) The final instance consists not of a making false\n                                     statement but of the alternative offence U/S 46(1) of the Act,\n                                     i.e., willful omission to make a material statement. RBI vide\n                                     its email dated 21.06.2017 had sought from PNB. A\n                                     Current Status report on those paras of its IT Compliance\n                                     Report communicated on 06.06.2016 where it had assured\n                                     compliance by a target date. However, patching status of other components of\n                                     SWIFT infrastructure like OS/Data base server. Network\n                                     devices enabling SWIFT messaging platform was not\n                                     available. There was 110 online integration of SWIFT with\n                                     CBS, Treasury, EDMS or Overseas systems. Bank also\n                                     confirmed that no security incidents have been reported in\n                                     the SWIFT operations for the last two years .. .. &quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_238\">                                     In response to paragraph 10.7 of the IT Exam Report of\n                                     06.06.2016, PNB in its IT Compliance/Action Taken Report\n                                     of 08 .07.20 16 had responded with the following:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_134\">                                     &quot;10.7 A comprehensive review of SWIFT operations vis-a-\n                                     vis observations of various auditors and actionable items\n                                     by 3010912016 (Action ITO and IBD).&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_239\">                                     It is pertinent to note here that PNB was specifically asked\n                                     to submit a Current Status Report vis-a-vis those\n                                     paragraphs wherein PNB in its Compliance/Action Taken\n                                     Report of July 2016 had remarked by specifying a target\n                                     date for compliance. The Current Status Report submitted\n                                     by PNB on 27.06.2017 did not consist of the status report\n                                     on the afore stated 10.7 paragraph of the IT exam report\n                                     and the IT Compliance/Action taken report of 08 .07.20 16.\n                                     Thus, in this instance PNB was guilty of the offence of\n                                     wilful omission to make a material statement, also\n                                     punishable uls 46 of the Act.&quot;\n</p>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_526\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_527\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_528\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                          101</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_529\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_530\">18:28</span>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_240\">                              and taking into account also the reply of the petitioner dated 08.07.2016\n                             to the RBI, which reads to the effect:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_135\">                                 &quot;Bank has a core banking solution which takes care of its main\n                                 business requirements. the effect that Letters of Undertakings (LOUs)\n                          were issued by the Brady House branch of the bank as per column 9c of the\n                          FMRs without obtaining approval of the authority, without necessary\n                          applications, documents and without making entries in the CBS system and\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_536\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_537\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_538\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                          103</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_539\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_540\">18:28</span>\n                           thus having furnished false information to the RBI, are aspects which are\n                          germane and essential to be looked into by the learned trial Court at the time\n                          of consideration of framing of the charges of allegations against the\n                          petitioner and other accused persons.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_248\">                          <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1814230\" id=\"span_541\">132. In the circumstances, thus it is held that the impugned order has to be\n                          sustained qua the summoning of the petitioner for the commission of an\n                          offence punishable under <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_327\">Section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949,\n                          which apparently in the circumstances of the instant case and the entire\n                          available record i.e. complaint of the complainant and evidence led by the\n                          complainant relates to the alleged commission of the offence punishable\n                          under <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_328\">Section 46(1)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949. This is so and as\n                          <a href=\"/doc/1814230/\" id=\"a_329\">laid down by</a> the Hon\u201fble Supreme Court in <a href=\"/doc/1814230/\" id=\"a_330\">U.P. Pollution Control Board\n                          vs. Mohan Meakins Ltd &amp; Ors</a> (2000) 3 SCC 745, it is not considered\n                          appropriate to accept the contention raised by the petitioner of remanding\n                          the matter to the Magistrate for doing the exercise again for re-consideration\n                          to invoke any specific provision of <a href=\"/doc/1747434/\" id=\"a_331\">Section 46</a> of the Banking Regulation\n                          Act, 1949 in as much as, as already observed hereinabove, the offence\n                          alleged to have been committed by the petitioner as alleged by the RBI\n                          through the complaint and brought forth through the impugned summoning\n                          order relates to the alleged commission of offence punishable under <a href=\"/doc/1888484/\" id=\"a_332\">Section\n                          46(1)</a> of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_249\">                          133. As regards the contention raised on behalf of the petitioner that the\n                          examination of Sh.Tarun Kumar Singh, General Manager, Department of\n                          Banking Supervisor, RBI could not have been exempted under <a href=\"/doc/444619/\" id=\"a_333\">Section 200</a>\n                          of the Cr.P.C., 1973 as he is not a \u201epublic servant\u201f, taking into account the\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_542\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_543\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_544\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                               104</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_545\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_546\">18:28</span>\n                           factum that the RBI is an instrumentally of the State, the role of Sh.Tarun\n                          Kumar Singh, is General Manager falls within the ambit of being a \u201epublic\n                          servant\u201f in terms of <a href=\"/doc/444619/\" id=\"a_334\">Section 200</a> of the Cr.P.C., 1973, and thus the\n                          contention of the petitioner that Sh.Tarun Kumar Singh having not been\n                          examined, the impugned order dated 11.03.2019 is liable to be set aside,\n                          cannot be accepted.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_250\">                          134. In the circumstances, the petition CRL.M.C.2696/2019 is thus\n                          dismissed, and the order dated 20.05.2019 staying the Trial Court\n                          proceedings is set aside. CRL.M.A.31239/2019 filed by the respondent no.2\n                          is disposed of accordingly.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_251\">                          135. Nothing stated hereinabove shall however amount to any expression\n                          on the merits or demerits of the trial that may take place.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_252\">                                                                               ANU MALHOTRA, J\n                          JUNE 25, 2021/sv/vm\n\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_547\">Signature Not Verified</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_548\">Digitally Signed</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_549\">By:SUMIT GHAI             CRL.M.C. 2696/2019                                                            105</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_550\">Signing Date:16.07.2021</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_551\">18:28</span>\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_552\"> </span>",

RISHI HIREMATH @ MUNNA\n      S/O BASAVARAJA HIREMATH\n      AGED ABOUT 37 YEARS\n      COMMISISON AGENT IN COMPUTERS\n      R/O ASAR ONI\n      OLD HUBLI, HUBLI           ...APPELLANTS\n\n\n        (By Sri HASHMATH PASHA, ADVOCATE\n               FOR APPELLANT 1 TO 14,\nAPPEAL AGAINST APPELLANT 15 IS ABATED VIDE ORDER\n                  DATED 28.3.2012)\n                               7\n\n\n\n\nAND:\n\nSTATE OF KARNATAKA BY\nJAGAJEEVANRAMNAGAR\nPOLICE STATION\nBANGALORE CITY\nINVESTIGATED BY DEPUTY\nSUPERINTENDENT OF POLICE\nSPECIAL ENQUIRY SQUAD\nC.O.D, BANGALORE\nREP BY\nSTATE PUBLIC PROSECUTOR                    ...RESPONDENT\n\n  (By Sri H N NILOGAL, SPECIAL PUBLIC PROSECUTOR)\n\n\n     THIS CRL.APPEAL IS FILED UNDER SECTION 374(2)\nCR.P.C BY THE ADVOCATE FOR THE APPELLANTS\nPRAYING TO SET ASIDE THE JUDGMENT AND ORDER OF\nCONVICTIONS DATED 21.11.2008 AND SENTENCES\nDATED 29.11.2008 PASSED IN S.C.NO.696/05, ON THE\nFILE OF 34TH ADDL. CITY CIVIL AND SESSIONS JUDGE\nAND P.O SPL.COURT FOR THE TRIAL OF CHURCH BOMB\nBLAST CASES, CENTRAL PRISON PREMISES, BANGALORE.\n\n\n     THESE CRL.RC AND CRL.As HAVING BEEN HEARD\nAND RESERVED FOR JUDGMENT, COMING ON FOR\nPRONOUNCEMENT       OF   JUDGMENT     THIS DAY,\nN. KUMAR J., DELIVERED THE FOLLOWING:\n\n\n                      JUDGMENT\n</pre>\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_1\">       A series of bomb blasts at Churches situate at various\n\nplaces rocked the State of Karnataka in the year 2000\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_1\">                               8</span>\n\n\n\n\nattempting to disturb the normal tranquility of the civilized\n\nsociety and the peace loving people of Karnataka who believe\n\nin co-existence of all religious denominations. The Karnataka\n\nGovernment constituted a Special Court for trial of these\n\ncases numbering four, in Bangalore.       These Appeals and\n\nReference arise out of the bomb blast that took place at St.\n\nPeter and Paul Church situated at JJ Nagar Main Road,\n\nBangalore, on 09.07.2000 at 10.15. p.m.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_2\">2.          Crl. A. No.1203/2008 is preferred by A.10 and\n\nCrl. A. No.24/2009 is preferred by A.7, A.8, A.9, A.11, A.12,\n\nA.13, A.18, A.19, A.20, A.21, A.22, A.24, A.25, A.26 and\n\nA.27.   against   the   judgment   and   sentence   passed   in\n\nS.C.No.696/2005 in Crime No.113/2000, by the 34th Addl.\n\nCity Civil &amp; Sessions Judge and Presiding Officer., Special\n\nCourt for the trial of Church Bomb Blast Cases, Central\n\nPrison Premises, Bangalore, convicting the accused under\n\n<a href=\"/doc/729076/\" id=\"a_1\">Section 235(2)</a> Cr.P.C for offences under Section 120-B read\n\nwith <a href=\"/doc/1949191/\" id=\"a_2\">Sections 121-A</a>, <a href=\"/doc/1641007/\" id=\"a_3\">124-A</a> and <a href=\"/doc/345634/\" id=\"a_4\">153-A</a> IPC, and for other\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_2\">                                 9</span>\n\n\n\n\noffences, for bomb blast which took place at St. Peter and\n\nPaul Church situated at JJ Nagar Main Road, Bangalore.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_3\">3.          The State has preferred Crl.R.C. No.3/2008\n\nseeking confirmation of death sentence of A.10. Therefore,\n\nall these appeals are taken up for consideration together and\n\ndisposed of by this common order.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_4\">\nBACK GROUND\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_5\">4.          Accused    are    the       members   of   Deeendar\n\nAnjuman Association having its head office at Asifnagar,\n\nHyderabad in the State of Andhra Pradesh and branches\n\nat various places in that State as well as Karnataka, Goa,\n\nMaharashtra and other States.            Late Hazrath Moulana\n\nSyed     Siddique     Kibla         @     Siddique     Deendar\n\nChannabasaveshwara, the founder of Deendar Anjuman\n\nwas born on 07th June 1886 to a remarried widow at\n\nBalampet near    Gurimitkal in Gulbarga District. He had\n\nprimary education at          Hirapura Village in Gulbarga\n\nDistrict, studied Kannada, English,          Urdu, Arabic and\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_3\">                                10</span>\n\n\n\n\nother languages and higher education from Mohammadan\n\nCollege, Madras. After graduation, he practised as an\n\nOphthalmologist for some days in Bijapur District. He\n\nstudied Ramayana, Mahabharatha, Bible, Quran, Vachana\n\nSahitya and       other literature   pertaining    to   different\n\nreligions    in India and was         closely associated with\n\nLingayath Community and its literature.           Being a good\n\norator with knowledge of different religions, he was able to\n\nestablish social contact with people of all religions in that\n\narea.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_6\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_7\">5.            Siddique      Deendar       Channabasaveshwara\n\nprofessed that he was a believer of &#x27;Eka Devo Bhava and\n\n&#x27;Sarva Dharma&#x27;, respect for all religions and that everyone\n\nshould      believe   in   &#x27;Ekodharma,     Ekojagadguru       and\n\nEkojagadeeshwara&#x27;. Believing this, the gullible people blindly\n\naccepted him as their Guru and participated in all the\n\nmeetings. This was the fa\u00e7ade he adopted to fool the public\n\nat large, but in reality, Eko Jagadeeshwara meant Allah, Eko\n\nJagadguru - Mohammad Paigambar and Eko Dharma -\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_4\">                                  11</span>\n\n\n\n\nIslam;   and        Siddique   Deendar     Channabasaveshwara&#x27;s\n\nhidden agenda was to Islamise the whole world including\n\nIndia.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_8\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_9\">6.             To   achieve    this   object,   Siddique   Deendar\n\nChannabasaveshwara came out with the theory that the\n\nancestors of Lingayaths originally belonged to Arab Country\n\nand resided near Egypt. When Egypt was about to flourish,\n\nthey migrated to South India and were called &#x27;Dravidians&#x27;.\n\n2000 years ago, rivers used to flow with red blood of cattle\n\nkilled by the Hindus for consuming the meat and if a\n\nBrahmin did not eat meat, he would be an outcast. However,\n\nthe Lingayaths were made to give up eating beef and chant\n\n&#x27;Hari Hari Sita Rama&#x27; instead of their mahamantra &#x27;Hara\n\nHara Mahadeva&#x27; by the Aryan race that ruled the land.\n\nJagadguru Shankaracharya was responsible for breeding\n\njealousy among different communities and therefore cannot\n\nbe called a Jagadguru. Idol worshipping was started because\n\nof Jagadguru Shankaracharya whereas even the Bible says\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_5\">                                     12</span>\n\n\n\n\nthat the idol worshippers should be killed. Therefore\n\nMohammad Paigambar alone can be the Jagadguru.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_10\">7.           To propagate the above ideas and to achieve his\n\nobject     and       hidden      agenda,        Siddique     Deendar\n\nChannabasaveshwara wrote books titled &#x27;Lingayata&#x27; in\n\nKannada,     &#x27;Pancha       Shanti     Marga&#x27;,     &#x27;Sandhyavandana\n\nMahasankalpa&#x27;,       Hindu     Muslim     Ikamathya&#x27;,      Zmama-Ul-\n\nJihad&#x27; and &#x27;Jagadguru&#x27;. He also got printed, published and\n\ncirculated       various      books      interpreting      Ramayana,\n\nMahabharatha, Bible, Quran and Vachana Sahitya and\n\nother literature interpreting them in his own way, in many\n\nlanguages including Kannada, English, Urdu, Arabic, Tamil\n\nand Telugu. Siddique Deendar Channabasaveshwar not only\n\nclaimed to be the reincarnation of Channabasaveshwara, the\n\nLingayath    saint    but     declared     himself   as    Jagadguru\n\nChannabasaveshwara with an intention to establish contact\n\nwith Lingayaths in the states of Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh,\n\nGoa and Maharashtra. His main object was to convert\n\npersons following other religions to Islam so that he could\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_6\">                               13</span>\n\n\n\n\nbecome the Jagadguru and rule entire India and Islam\n\nwould be the only religion followed in India.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_11\">8.          Siddique Deendar Channabasaveshwara and his\n\nfollowers, 16 in number, were prosecuted for endangering\n\npublic   peace   by preaching      new   religion   and   thereby\n\npromoting hatred and enemity between people belonging to\n\ndifferent communities. After enquiry, the District Magistrate,\n\nDharwad gave a verdict in favour of the prosecution and\n\nordered that the accused should execute personal bond for\n\nRs.100/- with a surety for the likesum and to maintain good\n\nbehaviour for one year.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_12\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_13\">9.          After liberation of Hyderabad State, the Agent-\n\nGeneral of India in Hyderabad State, Dr.K.M.Munshi, during\n\nhis stay at Hyderabad personally observed the activities of\n\nDeendar Anjuman Organization and its founder and has\n\nrecorded thus at pages 40 and 41 of his autobiography- &quot;The\n\nEnd of an Era: Hyderabad Memoirs&quot;:\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_7\">                             14</span><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_14\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_15\">&quot;The     Deendars        however        remained      active\nproselytizers, though by January 1948 their\ninfluence diminished. The head of the religious\nsect styled himself as &#x27;Hazarat Moulana Siddique\nDeendar Channa Basaveshwar Qible&#x27;. He posed\nas the avatar of Channa Basaveshwara, the\nancient founder of the Lingayath sect of the\nHindus and claimed to have the same divine\nmarks on his body as that saint. Four Siddique&#x27;s\nlieutenants also declared themselves to be the\nHindu     divinities      viz.,     Vyas,     Narasimha,\nSrikrishna and Veerabhadra. Siddique had his\nheadquarters      at     Hyderabad.         His   followers\nreckoned to be 500 strong, had no ostensible\nmeans of livelihood. When the situation in a\nvillage grew tense, they led the Muslims against\nHindus and set out to loot the possession of the\nHindus. When they set out to loot the possession\nof the Hindus, they dressed as Razakars. At one\ntime Siddique even ventured to capture Hampi,\nthe    ruined   capital    of     the   vanished    empire\nVijayanagar, to recover buried treasure. Deendar\nSiddiuqe was represented as shooting lions,\ntigers, leopards and foxes, all of which had the\nshape     of    Sikhs,    Hindus,        Christians    and\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_8\">                                15</span>\n\n\n\n\n      Lingayaths. He also declared Jihad, a religious\n      war against the shrines of the Hindus and issued\n      a public appeal for one lakh volunteers and a\n      loan of Rs.5,00,000/- for the purpose&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_16\">\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_17\">10.         Siddique Deendar Channabasaveshwara had\n\nfour wives by name (1) Zainabi at Ramdurg, (2) Tajdar\n\nBegum at Bangalore, (3) Hazra Begum at Ramdurg and (4)\n\nFathima   Begum    at   Bangalore.     He   renamed       them   as\n\nGowramma,       Gangeshwari,        Parvathi     and      Kalamma\n\nrespectively. After death, the 1st and 3rd wives Zainabi and\n\nHazra Begum who were the full sisters, were buried at\n\nBhatakurki Village in Ramdurg Taluk of Belgaum District in\n\nKarnataka. The 2nd wife Tajdar Begum died at Mardan in\n\nPakistan. Fathima Begum, the 4th wife died in Asifnagar,\n\nHyderabad, Andhra Pradesh and was buried at Deendar\n\nAnjuman    at   Asifnagar.     Hazarath        Siddique   Deendar\n\nChannabasaveshwara died on 4th April 1952 and his body\n\nwas also buried at Asifnagar in Hyderabad and a tomb was\n\nbuilt in his memory.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_9\">                             16</span><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_18\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_19\">11.         A-1, Zia-Ul-Hassan, who later became the Guru\n\nof Deendar Anjuman Ashram is the son of Siddique Deendar\n\nChannabasaveshwara and migrated to Pakistan with his\n\nseven sons (1) Khaleel Pasha, (2) Syed Javed Hasan, (3)\n\nSayed Javed Pasha, (4) Syed Asif, (5) Syed Khalid Hasan, (6)\n\nSyed Abid Pasha and (7) Syed Adil Pasha. Zia-Ul-Hassan&#x27;s\n\ndaughter Sameena is married to Abdul Saleel Choudhry, the\n\nSecretary of Deendar Anjuman Ashram and they are\n\nresiding in the house situated within the ashram premises\n\nin Hyderabad.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_20\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_21\">12.         During 1998 A-1 Zia-Ul-Hassan had been to\n\nMecca and Madina, where he is said to have got the\n\nprophecy that India would become an Islamic country within\n\ntwo years, a dream of Deendar Anjuman, that there would\n\nbe a war between India and Pakistan, that north India would\n\nsuffer 70 to 75% loss and all the arms and ammunitions\n\nwould be exhausted. Then he would enter India with nine\n\nlakh followers (pathans). Soon after setting foot on Punjab,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_10\">                               17</span>\n\n\n\n\nhis followers would create havoc in southern India, paving\n\nway for his easy entry into that part too.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_22\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_23\">13.         The organisers of Deendar Anjuman Ashram, to\n\nattract young Muslims into achieving their object held secret\n\nmeetings every year after celebration of Urs in the month of\n\nRajab and motivated young people to undergo training in\n\nJihad, Nifaaq and Siria activities. Accordingly many went to\n\nPakistan and took training in the preparation of explosive\n\nsubstances, bombs etc., blasting of bombs and also\n\noperating arms and ammunitions. Further the followers of\n\nDeendar Anjuman were angered by the conversion of poor\n\nMuslims to Christianity by the Missionaries and therefore\n\nconspired   to   create   disharmony,    feeling   of   enemity,\n\nhatredness and ill-will between the two major communities\n\nin India i.e., the Hindus and the Christians, so that India\n\nwould face the wrath of Western world and could no longer\n\nproject itself as a Secular nation. In other words, they\n\nconspired to make Christian dominated Western Countries\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_11\">                                18</span>\n\n\n\n\nto condemn India and create hatredness towards the Govt.\n\nestablished by law in India.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_24\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_25\">14.         In furtherance of the above object, accused\n\npersons blasted several Churches in Andhra Pradesh,\n\nKarnataka and Goa and we are concerned only with the four\n\nblasts that took place in the State of Karnataka, of them one\n\nwas accidental.   The first blast took place on 08.06.2000 at\n\nSt. Ann&#x27;s Catholic Church, Wadi, Chittapur Taluk, Gulbarga\n\nDistrict at about 6:30 a.m., where again one more blast took\n\nplace at 9:30 a.m. The second blast took place on\n\n08.07.2000 at St. Luthern Church in Hubli between\n\n3:30 a.m. and 4:30 a.m. The third bomb blast was on\n\n09.07.2000 at 10:15 p.m. at St. Peter and Paul Church on\n\nJ.J.Nagar Main Road, Bangalore Before that, a Maruti Van\n\nbearing registration No.GA-01/U-2786, carrying bombs for\n\nbeing planted in some other church exploded at about 8.30\n\np.m. near Minerva Mills killing A-14 and A-15 and causing\n\ngrievous injuries to A-10.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_12\">                              19</span><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_26\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_27\">15.         Consequent to the above, the police stations at\n\nWadi, Keshavapura in Hubli, J.J. Nagar and Magadi Road in\n\nBangalore registered case in Cr.No.77/2000, 87/2000,\n\n113/2000 and 290/2000 respectively. During investigation,\n\nbesides floppies, C.Ds, Credit cards, pass books, passports\n\netc., the Investigating Officers seized the literature, books\n\nand pamphlets containing the objects and activities of\n\nDeendar Anjuman Ashram, from the house and office\n\npremises of the accused persons.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_28\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_29\">16.         In all these cases, after investigation charge\n\nsheets were laid against the accused persons for offences\n\npunishable U/Ss. 121, 121-A, 124-A, 153-A, 295, 436, 427\n\nr/w <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_5\">Sec.120-B</a> IPC and also Ss. 3, 4 and 5 of <a href=\"/doc/1568384/\" id=\"a_6\">Explosive\n\nSubstances Act, 1908</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1335368/\" id=\"a_7\">Sec. 5</a> r/w <a href=\"/doc/176528/\" id=\"a_8\">Sec.9-B</a> of Explosives\n\nAct, 1884. On committal by the learned Magistrate, these\n\nwere numbered by the court of Sessions as S.C.570/2003,\n\n580/2003, 696/2005 and 423/2001 respectively and the\n\nState of Karnataka constituted a Special Court to try the\n\nsame.
