Andhra Pradesh and Goa also prosecuted the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_13\">                                  20</span>\n\n\n\n\naccused in connection with blasting of the Churches in\n\nthose States.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_30\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_31\">17.            During   trial,   witnesses   were   examined,\n\ndocuments were exhibited and material objects were marked\n\nby the prosecution in each case and the details of the same\n\nis as below:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1\">(1)   In S.C.No.696/2005, 64 witnesses were examined as\n\n      P.W.1 to P.W.64 and 262 documents were marked as\n\n      Ex.P1 to Ex.P262 and 80 material objects were\n\n      admitted as M.O. 1 to M.O.80.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_2\">(2)   In   S.C.No.580/2003        c/w.   S.C.No.579/2003   96\n\n      witnesses were examined as P.W.1 to P.W.96 and 244\n\n      documents were marked as Ex.P1 to Ex.P244 and 80\n\n      material objects were admitted as M.O.1 to M.O.80.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_3\">(3)   In S.C.No.570/2003, 61 witnesses were examined as\n\n      P.W.1 to P.W.61 and 161 documents were marked as\n\n      Ex.P1 to Ex.P161 and 40 material objects were\n\n      admitted as M.O.1 to M.O.40.\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_14\">                               21</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_4\">(4)   In S.C. No.423 of 2001, 67 witnesses were examined\n\n      as P.W.1 to P.W.67 and 298 documents were marked\n\n      as Ex.P1 to Ex.P298 and 212 material objects were\n\n      admitted as M.O.1 to M.O.212.\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_32\">18.         Sahabzada Zia Ul Hassan @ Zia Ul Hassan @\n\nHazarath, S/o Late Hazarath Moulana Syed Siddique\n\nHussain who is a common accused in all these cases and his\n\nfour sons Syed Zahed Ul Hassan @ Zahed Pasha, Syed\n\nKhalid Pasha @ Khalid, Syed Shabihul Hassan @ Syed Adil\n\nPasha @ Adil and Khaleel Pasha @ Khaleel who are also the\n\naccused,   are   residents   of   Mardan    in   Pakistan   but,\n\nabsconding. Syed Wahab S/o Late Syed Ismail, a resident of\n\nVijayawada in Andhra Pradesh and Sheik Ameer Ali @\n\nAmeer Ali, S/o Moulana are also absconding. Therefore,\n\ncases against these accused have been split up.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_33\">19.         A-14-    Mohammed       Zakir    @     Zakir,   S/o\n\nMohammed Burhanuddin a resident of Deendar Anjuman\n\nAshram, Asif Nagar, Hyderabad and A-15 Mohammed Fida\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_15\">                               22</span>\n\n\n\n\nUr Rahaman Siddique @ Siddique, S/o Mohammed Zia Ur\n\nRahaman @ Mohsin Chitradurgi of Somappa Block, Church\n\nStreet, J.C.Nagar, Bangalore died in Maruti Van bomb blast\n\nthat took place near Minerva Circle, Bangalore. One of the\n\nmain accused in all these cases Syed Khaliquzama @\n\nKhaliquzama, a resident of Nuzvid, Krishna District in\n\nAndhra Pradesh died during trial before the Special Court\n\nand another accused by name Rishi Hiremath @ Munna, a\n\nresident of Asar Oni in Old Hubli died during the pendency\n\nof the appeals. As such the criminal proceedings against the\n\nabove accused have stood abated.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_34\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_35\">20.          Upon consideration of the material on record\n\nand after hearing the arguments, the Special Court passed\n\nseparate orders in all the four cases and pronounced the\n\njudgment on the same day convicting all the accused in all\n\nthe   four   cases   except   A.23-Mohd.    Riyazuddin    in\n\nS.C.696/2005, A-6- Mohd. Jaffar in S.C.No.579/2003 c/w\n\n580/2003 and A.1- Syed Hamir Hamza and A.2- Ismail\n\nAkkis in S.C.No.570/2003, who were acquitted.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_16\">                               23</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_36\">21.         In S.C.No.696/2005, A.10 was imposed death\n\nsentence.   A.1 and A.5 were imposed death sentence in\n\nS.C.No.579/03     c/w     580/2003   and   A.3   to   A.7   in\n\nS.C.No.570/03 for the offence under <a href=\"/doc/786750/\" id=\"a_9\">Section 121</a> IPC. Other\n\naccused were sentenced to life imprisonment.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_37\">22.         The Sessions Court, as required U/s 366\n\n<a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_10\">Cr.P.C</a>., submitted the proceedings to the High Court for\n\nconfirmation of death sentence in Crl.R.C. No.3, 4 and 6 of\n\n2008, in all the cases.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_38\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_39\">23.         The accused who were convicted in all these\n\nappeals have preferred appeals against the judgment of the\n\nSessions Court.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_40\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_41\">24.         Crl.A.No.1202/08 arising from the judgment of\n\nconviction and sentence in S.C.No.423/01 was allowed with\n\nrespect to A-8, A-9 and A-17. In so far as the first appellant\n\ntherein (A-1) is concerned the appeal was allowed in part by\n\nsetting aside the conviction and sentence passed against him\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_17\">                             24</span>\n\n\n\n\nfor the offence punishable U/Ss. 121, 120-B r/w Ss.121-A,\n\n124-A, 153-A and 304 Part II of IPC and Sec.3 of the\n\nExplosive Substances Act, 1908; he was convicted for a\n\nlesser offence punishable U/s 304-A <a href=\"/doc/1569253/\" id=\"a_11\">IPC</a> and was sentenced\n\nto undergo RI for two years.      Further the judgment of\n\nconviction for the offence U/Ss. 337 and 427 <a href=\"/doc/1569253/\" id=\"a_12\">IPC</a>, <a href=\"/doc/1335368/\" id=\"a_13\">Sec. 5</a> of\n\nthe Explosive Substances Act, 1908 and <a href=\"/doc/161735826/\" id=\"a_14\">Sec.9(1)(b)</a> of the\n\nExplosives Act, 1884 read with Rule 5 of the Explosives\n\nRules 1983 is confirmed. The sentence imposed by the\n\nSessions Court on the first accused for the said offence was\n\nalso confirmed and it was ordered to run concurrently; and\n\nwas also given the benefit of set off for the period of\n\ndetention he had already undergone.    It is submitted that\n\nagainst the said judgment, the State has preferred an appeal\n\nto the Supreme Court and special leave to appeal has been\n\ngranted by an order dated 03.02.2014 and the appeal is\n\nregistered as Criminal Appeal Nos.351-353/2014 and the\n\nsame is pending consideration.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_18\">                             25</span><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_42\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_43\">25.         We have heard the remaining appeals together\n\nas they involve common question of law, but separate\n\njudgments are rendered after appreciation of the evidence\n\nadduced in each case independently and pronounced\n\nsimultaneously today.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_44\">\nFACTS IN S.C.No.696 OF 2005\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_45\">26.         According to the prosecution, to achieve the\n\nobject as narrated earlier, A.1 conspired with his sons A.2,\n\nA.3, A.4 and A.5 held meeting at Deendar Anjuman Ashram,\n\nHyderabad on 20.10.1999 and this was followed by several\n\nmeetings at other places including the house of A-10 at\n\nVarthur, A-11 at Vijayanagar, Bangalore and deceased A-\n\n14&#x27;s father-in-law&#x27;s house at Ganganagar and deceased A-\n\n15&#x27;s father&#x27;s house at J.C. Nagar, Bangalore. A-10, A-11, A-\n\n19 and A-20 in furtherance of such conspiracy prepared\n\nbombs in the house of A-19 Sheik Hashim Ali at Hyderabad,\n\ntransported the same to Bangalore through A-14.         A-9\n\nnamely Syed Abdul Khadar Jilani, who was specially trained\n\nin preparing time bombs, prepared the bombs and later\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_19\">                              26</span>\n\n\n\n\ntransported to various places. A-10 went to the house of A-\n\n11 situated at Vijayanagar, Bangalore along with deceased\n\nA-14 and A-15 in his Maruti Van bearing registration No.GA-\n\n01-U-2786 and took delivery of highly explosive substances\n\nand bombs including IED on 09.07.2000 and planted the\n\nsame near the rear window of St. Peter and Paul Church\n\nsituated at JJ Nagar Main Road, Bangalore around 7.30 pm\n\nto 9.00 pm. As per the time set by the accused, the bombs\n\nexploded at 10.15 pm causing heavy damage to the church\n\nbuilding. This caused widespread disaffection in the minds of\n\nthe Christians and they resorted to large scale destruction of\n\npublic property in retaliation; 17 vehicles were damaged in\n\nthat incident. The situation turned very tense warranting\n\ndeployment of additional forces.    Crime No.114/2000 was\n\nregistered with respect to the incident of stone pelting,\n\ncausing damage to the property and life.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_46\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_47\">27.         On 10.07.2000 at about 2.25 am, Father\n\nTomeyar, the Priest of St. Peter and Paul Church gave First\n\nInformation as per Ex.P-1 and J.J.Nagar Police registered\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_20\">                                  27</span>\n\n\n\n\nCr.No.113/2000 for the offence U/Ss. 3 and 4 of <a href=\"/doc/1568384/\" id=\"a_15\">Explosive\n\nSubstances Act</a> r/w <a href=\"/doc/222396/\" id=\"a_16\">Section 427</a> IPC. After completion of\n\ninvestigation, the Investigating Officer obtained necessary\n\nsanction from the Government of Karnataka to prosecute the\n\naccused persons as required U/s 196 <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_17\">Cr.P.C</a>. and also the\n\nsanction   from   the   Police   Commissioner,        Bangalore   to\n\nprosecute them for the offence punishable under <a href=\"/doc/1568384/\" id=\"a_18\">Explosive\n\nSubstances Act and Explosives Act</a>, submitted the final\n\nreport   on   19.03.2001   before     the   learned    3rd   ACMM,\n\nBangalore, against 29 accused persons showing A-1 to A-5,\n\nA-16 and A-29 as absconding and A-6, A-14 and A-15 as\n\ndead, for the offence punishable U/Ss. 124-A, 153-A, 295,\n\n436, 427 <a href=\"/doc/1569253/\" id=\"a_19\">IPC</a> r/w <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_20\">Sec.120-B</a> IPC and Ss.3, 4 and 5 of the\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1568384/\" id=\"a_21\">Explosive Substances Act, 1908</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1335368/\" id=\"a_22\">Sec.5</a> r/w <a href=\"/doc/176528/\" id=\"a_23\">Sec.9-B</a> of\n\nExplosives Act, 1884. This was registered as C.C.6264/01\n\nand committal order was passed only in respect of A-17 and\n\nA-28 by splitting up the case against other accused who,\n\nwere in custody in connection with the criminal case on the\n\nfile of the Sessions Court at Hyderabad. The case against A-\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_21\">                              28</span>\n\n\n\n\n17 and A-28 was registered as S.C.No.425/2001 and made\n\nover to the Special Court for trial of Church Bomb Blast\n\nCase. After trial, S.C.425/2001 ended in the acquittal of A-\n\n17 and A-28.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_48\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_49\">28.         In the split up case C.C.No.6678/01 warrants\n\nwere issued against the remaining accused by the 3rd\n\nACMM, Bangalore pursuant to which the presence of A-7 to\n\nA-13 and A-18 to A-27 was secured. After complying with the\n\nrequirement of <a href=\"/doc/1613898/\" id=\"a_24\">Section 207</a> Cr.P.C. and by once again\n\nsplitting up the case against absconding A-1 to A-5, A-16\n\nand A-29, this case too was committed U/s 209 <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_25\">Cr.P.C</a>. to\n\nthe Sessions Court by order dated 04.07.2005. On receipt of\n\nrecords, the Principal City Civil &amp; Sessions Court, Bangalore\n\nCity, registered the case in S.C.No.696/05 and made it over\n\nto the Special Court.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_50\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_51\">29.         A-7 to A-13 and A-18 to A-27 were produced\n\nbefore the Special Court on 12.09.2005 and as per their\n\nrequest, once again copies of the prosecution papers were\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_22\">                             29</span>\n\n\n\n\nfurnished to them. Thereafter the Public Prosecutor and the\n\nCounsel appearing for the accused were heard on the\n\nquestion of framing of charges. Since there was no objection\n\nto frame the charges, on 26.09.2005,    charge came to be\n\nframed against A-7 to A-13 and A-18 to A-27 for the offence\n\npunishable U/Ss. 124-A, 153-A, 295, 436 r/w <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_26\">Sec.120-B</a>\n\nIPC, <a href=\"/doc/1335368/\" id=\"a_27\">Sec.5</a> r/w <a href=\"/doc/176528/\" id=\"a_28\">Sec.9-B</a> of Explosives Act, 1884 r/w <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_29\">Sec.120-\n\nB </a>IPC and Ss. 3 and 5 of the <a href=\"/doc/1568384/\" id=\"a_30\">Explosive Substances Act, 1908</a>\n\nr/w <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_31\">Sec.120-B</a> IPC, read over and explained to them; A-7 to\n\nA-13 and A-18 to A-27 pleaded not guilty and claimed to be\n\ntried.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_52\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_53\">30.         The prosecution examined 64 witnesses as P.W1\n\nto PW64 and got marked 262 documents as Ex.P-1 to P-262;\n\n80 material objects are also marked for the prosecution as\n\nM.O.1 to 80. After the prosecution closed its side, accused\n\nA-7 to A-13 and A-18 to A-27 were examined as required U/s\n\n313 <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_32\">Cr.P.C</a>. and all the incriminating evidence was denied by\n\nthem in toto. However, A-7 to A-13 and A-18 to A-27 did not\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_23\">                              30</span>\n\n\n\n\nchoose to lead oral or documentary evidence in support of\n\ntheir defence.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_54\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_55\">31.         At the time of arguments, the learned Special\n\nPublic Prosecutor made an application U/s 216 <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_33\">Cr.P.C</a>, for\n\nframing of additional charges for the offence punishable\n\nU/Ss.121 and 121-A <a href=\"/doc/1569253/\" id=\"a_34\">IPC</a>. After hearing the prosecution and\n\nthe counsel appearing for A-7 to A-13 and A-18 to A-27, the\n\nSpl. Court framed additional charges on 12.12.2007, read\n\nover and explained the same to A-7 to A-13 and A-18 to A-27\n\nfor which they pleaded not guilty and claimed to be tried.\n\nNeither the prosecution nor the accused persons chose to\n\nadduce additional evidence in respect of the additional\n\ncharges. However PW36, PW42, PW43, PW54 and PW64 were\n\nrecalled and further cross examined by A-7 to A-13 and A-18\n\nto A-27.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_56\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_57\">32.         The Trial Court, on consideration of the material\n\non record convicted A-7 Syed Hasanuzama, A-8 Syed\n\nShamsuzama, A-9 Syed Abdul Khadar Jilani, A-10 Syed\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_24\">                              31</span>\n\n\n\n\nMohammed Ibrahim, A-11 Abdul Rahaman Sait, A-12\n\nAmanath Hussain Mulla, A-13 Mohammed Siddique, A-18\n\nSheik   Fardeen   Vali,   A-19    Sheik   Hashim   Ali,   A-20\n\nMohammed Farooq Ali, A-21 Sheik Abdul Habeeb, A-22 -\n\nMohammed Gayasuddin, A-24         Mohammed Sharfuddin, A-\n\n25 - Meera Sab, A-26 - Syed Muniruddin Mulla and A-27 -\n\nRishi Hiremath, for the offence U/s 120-B r/w Ss.121-A,\n\n124-A and 153-A <a href=\"/doc/1569253/\" id=\"a_35\">IPC</a> and sentenced them to            undergo\n\nimprisonment for life. A-10 Syed Mohammed Ibrahim, is also\n\nconvicted for the offence U/s 120-B r/w <a href=\"/doc/1949191/\" id=\"a_36\">Section 121-A</a> of IPC\n\nand sentenced to death subject to confirmation by this\n\nCourt. A-10 was also convicted for an offence under <a href=\"/doc/1056565/\" id=\"a_37\">Section\n\n3</a> of the Explosive Substances Act, 1908 and under Rule 5 of\n\nthe Explosive Substances Rules, 1983 read with <a href=\"/doc/176528/\" id=\"a_38\">Section 9-B</a>\n\nof the Explosives Substances Act, 1884.     Against this the\n\naccused have preferred the appeal.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_58\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_59\">33.        It is in this background, we have to find out\n\nwhether the appellants have made out sufficient grounds for\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_25\">                                  32</span>\n\n\n\n\ninterfering with the order of conviction and sentence passed\n\nby the trial court.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_60\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_61\">34.          Sri.Hasmath Pasha, learned counsel for the\n\naccused-appellants has urged the following grounds:\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_5\">      (1)    The accused persons, except A-13, were\n             are all tried for the offence of criminal\n             conspiracy     in    S.C.95    of    2001   at\n             Hyderabad, convicted and sentenced and\n             they could not have been tried again for the\n             same offence and it offends Article 20(2) of\n             the Constitution of India as well as section\n             300 of Cr.P.C.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_62\">      (2)    The sanction accorded by the Government\n             for prosecuting the accused for an offence\n             U/s 124-A <a href=\"/doc/1569253/\" id=\"a_39\">IPC</a> is invalid. Similarly, the\n             sanction accorded by the Commissioner of\n             Police for an offence U/s 3 of the <a href=\"/doc/1568384/\" id=\"a_40\">Explosive\n             Substances Act, 1908</a> is also invalid.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_63\">      (3)    That     no   sanction    is   accorded     for\n             prosecuting the accused for offence U/s\n             121-A of <a href=\"/doc/1569253/\" id=\"a_41\">IPC</a> as such, the entire trial is\n             vitiated for want of jurisdiction.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_26\">                               33</span><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_64\">\n\n\n\n\n(4)   The entire case of the prosecution revolves\n      around     three    aspects:         (a)   Confession\n      statement; (b) Evidence of an Accomplice;\n      and (c) Evidence of an eye witness. While\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_65\">      (a) and (b) are inadmissible in evidence, (c)\n      is not trustworthy.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_66\">(5)   Identification     of    the        accused    in   the\n      identification parade is vitiated as the\n      parade conducted is contrary to law.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_67\">(6)   The original confession statements and the\n      mahazars drawn were not produced but\n      only the copies were produced. As such the\n      secondary        evidence            adduced        was\n      inadmissible in evidence.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_68\">(7)   The statement of accused to the police,\n      which led to discovery of the material\n      objects involved in the offence alone is\n      admissible. The condition precedent for\n      application of the said rule is, police should\n      not be aware of the places and the material\n      objects seized. If they were aware of the\n      material    objects          even    before    seizure,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_27\">                               34</span>\n\n\n\n\n            <a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_42\">Section 27</a> of the Evidence Act would have\n            no application.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_69\">      (8)   The panch witnesses to seizure mahazars\n            have   all   turned    hostile   and   therefore\n            seizure is not proved.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_70\">\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_71\">35.         Per contra, the learned Special Public Prosecutor\n\nSri H. N. Nilogal argued that the sanction accorded is legal\n\nand valid. Since additional charge was framed under <a href=\"/doc/1949191/\" id=\"a_43\">Section\n\n121A</a> of the Indian Penal Code on the same set of facts, no\n\nfurther sanction in law was required. The confession\n\nstatement is in the handwriting of the accused; it was given\n\nwithout any coercion or force and he has admitted the same.\n\nThough an attempt was made to retract after a period of 2\u00bd\n\nyears, the Court below was justified in acting on the\n\nconfessional statement. The witness whom the accused are\n\nterming as an accomplice is not an accomplice but an\n\nindependent witness.       Evidence of this witness clearly\n\nestablishes the charges levelled against the accused and\n\ntherefore, the Special Court was justified in acting on the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_28\">                              35</span>\n\n\n\n\nsaid evidence. Insofar as the evidence of the eye witness is\n\nconcerned, his evidence is quite natural. It is not disputed\n\nthat he was near the place of incident. He has identified the\n\naccused and therefore, the Court below was justified in\n\naccepting his evidence and acting on the same. Though some\n\nof the witnesses have turned hostile, all of them have not\n\nturned hostile. A portion of the evidence of those witnesses\n\nwho turned hostile corroborates other available evidence that\n\nthese accused were the members of Deendar Anjuman\n\norganization and are all related. In so far as panch witnesses\n\nare concerned, though some of them have turned hostile,\n\ninvestigating officers are examined to prove the seizure\n\nmahazar.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_72\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_73\">36.         Insofar as the production of secondary evidence\n\nis concerned, it was submitted by the learned Spl.P.P. that\n\nfour cases were tried by the same Court. As originals were\n\nproduced in one case, certified copies     were produced in\n\nother cases. The originals were always available in the Court\n\nfor inspection by the accused persons and the prosecution,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_29\">                                36</span>\n\n\n\n\nas such no prejudice was caused to the accused. Under\n\nthese circumstances, secondary evidence is admissible and\n\nthe Court below was justified in acting on the same. It is true\n\nthat to attract <a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_44\">Section 27</a> of the Evidence Act the statement\n\nof the accused should result in discovery of fact. Since some\n\nof the material objects were seized on such statement, of the\n\naccused, the part which led to discovery of the fact that the\n\naccused knew where the incriminating materials were found,\n\nbecomes relevant and admissible in evidence. Even otherwise\n\nthe accused persons leading the police to a place they\n\nalready   knew   itself   would     not   render   the     evidence\n\ninadmissible.     The     learned    Trial   Judge,   on    proper\n\nappreciation of the entire evidence has rightly convicted the\n\naccused persons and the same does not call for any\n\ninterference.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_74\">\nPOINTS FOR CONSIDERATION\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_75\">37.         From the aforesaid material on record and the\n\narguments of the learned counsel for the parties, the\n\nfollowing points arise for our consideration in this appeal:\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_30\">                        37</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_6\">1.   Whether the Trial against the accused in this\n     appeal amounts to second trial and is hit by\n     <a href=\"/doc/741791/\" id=\"a_45\">Section 300</a> of the Cr.P.C. on the principles of\n     Double Jeopardy?\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_7\">2.   Whether the sanction accorded for prosecuting the\n     accused for the offence under <a href=\"/doc/345634/\" id=\"a_46\">Section 153A</a>, <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_47\">120B</a>\n     <a href=\"/doc/1392445/\" id=\"a_48\">124</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1641007/\" id=\"a_49\">124A</a> of the IPC is invalid?\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_8\">3.   Whether the prosecution of the accused for\n     offence under <a href=\"/doc/786750/\" id=\"a_50\">Section 121</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1949191/\" id=\"a_51\">121A</a> of IPC\n     without sanction is one without jurisdiction?\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_9\">4.   Whether    the    sanction        accorded    by     the\n     Commissioner of Police for the offences under\n     <a href=\"/doc/339028/\" id=\"a_52\">Section 3</a> of the Explosives Act is invalid?\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_10\">5.   Whether the confessional statement of A.7 is\n     admissible in evidence?\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_11\">6.   Whether    the   evidence    of    P.W.54    could   be\n     construed as evidence of an accomplice and is\n     inadmissible for want of corroboration?\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_12\">7.   Whether the evidence of P.W.3 - eye witness in\n     the case is vitiated because of the defect in the\n     identification parade conducted?\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_31\">                              38</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_13\">      8.    Whether the seizure is not proved as all the\n            Mahazar witnesses have turned hostile?\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_14\">      9.    Whether <a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_53\">Section 27</a> of the Evidence Act has no\n            application as the police were aware of the places\n            and the material objects seized?\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_15\">      10.   Whether the production of the copies i.e., the\n            secondary evidence in respect of the Mahazars\n            and confessional statement of A.7 is inadmissible\n            in evidence as original was not produced?\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_16\">      11.   Whether the order of conviction passed by the\n            Trial Court is based on legal evidence and is\n            sustainable in law?\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_17\">      12.   Whether the death sentence imposed on A.10\n            requires to be confirmed?\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_76\">38.         The charges levelled against the accused can be\n\nbroadly classified as charges regarding conspiracy, waging\n\nwar, sedition, creating disharmony between sections of the\n\npublic and contravention of the provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/1568384/\" id=\"a_54\">Explosives\n\nAct and Explosive Substances Act</a>. For a proper appreciation\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_32\">                               39</span>\n\n\n\n\nof the facts and application of the law to the said facts, it is\n\nnecessary to have a clear understanding of the law.\n\nTherefore, firstly we have to look to the law on these aspects.\n\n\nCONSPIRACY\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_77\">39.         What is the meaning of the term &quot;conspiracy&quot;?\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_78\">      In HALSBURY&#x27;S LAWS of England (vide 4th Edn.\n\nVol.11, page 44, page 58), the ENGLISH LAW as to\n\nconspiracy has been stated thus:\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_79\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_18\">      &quot;58. Conspiracy consists in the agreement of two\n      or more persons to do an unlawful act, or to do a\n      lawful act by unlawful means. It is an indictable\n      offence at common law, the punishment for which\n      is imprisonment or fine or both in the discretion of\n      the Court.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_19\">           The essence of the offence of conspiracy is\n      the fact of combination by agreement. The\n      agreement may be express or implied, or in part\n      express and in part implied. The conspiracy\n      arises and the offence is committed as soon as\n      the agreement is made; and the offence continues\n      to be committed so long as the combination\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_33\">                               40</span>\n\n\n\n\n      persists, that is until the conspiratorial agreement\n      is terminated by completion of its performance or\n      by abandonment or frustration or however, it may\n      be. The actus reas in a conspiracy is the\n      agreement to execute the illegal conduct, not the\n      execution of it. It is not enough that two or more\n      persons pursued the same unlawful object at the\n      same time or in the same place; it is necessary to\n      show a meeting of minds, a consensus to effect\n      an   unlawful   purpose.     It   is   not,   however,\n      necessary that each conspirator should have\n      been in communication with every other.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_80\">40.         The ENGLISH LAW on this matter is well\n\nsettled. RUSSELL on crime (12 Ed.Vol.I, p.202) may be\n\nusefully noted:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_20\">            The gist of the offence of conspiracy then\n      lies, not in doing the act, or effecting the purpose\n      for which the conspiracy is formed, nor in\n      attempting to do them, nor in inciting others to do\n      them, but in the forming of the scheme or\n      agreement between the parties, agreement is\n      essential. Mere knowledge, or even discussion, of\n      the plan is not, per se, enough.&quot;\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_34\">                               41</span>\n\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_81\">41.         In America, the concept of criminal conspiracy is\n\nno different and AMERICAN JURISPRUDENCE, 2nd Edn.,\n\nVol.16, Page 129, defines conspiracy as below:\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_21\">      &quot;A conspiracy is said to be an agreement between\n      two or more persons to accomplish together a\n      criminal or unlawful act or to achieve by criminal\n      or unlawful means an act not in itself criminal or\n      unlawful ... The unlawful agreement and not its\n      accomplishment is the gist or essence of the crime\n      of conspiracy.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_82\">42.         DR. SHRI HARI SINGH GOUR in his well known\n\n&#x27;COMMENTARY ON PENAL LAW OF INDIA&#x27;, (Vol.2, 11th\n\nedn., p.1138) summed up the legal position in the following\n\nwords:\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_83\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_22\">      &quot;In order to constitute a single general conspiracy\n      there must be a common design. Each conspirator\n      plays his separate part in one integrated and\n      united effort to achieve the common purpose.\n      Each one is aware that he has a part to play in a\n      general conspiracy though he may not know all\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_35\">                               42</span>\n\n\n\n\n      its secrets or the means by which the common\n      purpose is to be accomplished. The evil scheme\n      may be promoted by a few, some may drop out\n      and some may join at a later stage, but the\n      conspiracy continues until it is broken up. The\n      conspiracy may develop in successive stages.\n      There may be general plan to accomplish the\n      common design by such means as may from time\n      to time be found expedient.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_84\">43.         Section 120-A and 120-B were brought on the\n\nstatute book by way of amendment to <a href=\"/doc/1569253/\" id=\"a_55\">IPC</a> in 1913. The\n\nStatement of Objects and Reasons to the amending Act\n\nreveals that the underlying purpose was to make a mere\n\nagreement to do an illegal act or an act which is not illegal by\n\nillegal means punishable under law. Before these sections\n\nwere added to the Penal Code, conspiracy to commit offences\n\nwas not punishable. It became punishable as abetment if the\n\noffence was actually committed. This is evident from the\n\nlanguage of Section 107 which defines &quot;abetment.&quot; By the\n\namendment of the Penal Code by addition of ss. 120-A and\n\n120-B, the Legislature brought the law in India in line with\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_36\">                                     43</span>\n\n\n\n\nthe English law by making the overt act unessential when\n\nthe conspiracy is to commit any punishable offence. It made\n\nconspiracy itself punishable as a distinct offence, apart from\n\nthe object of conspiracy which, if carried, out, would itself\n\namount to an offence.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_85\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_86\">44.             <a href=\"/doc/591631/\" id=\"a_56\">Section     120-A</a>      of      IPC    defines     criminal\n\nconspiracy. It says:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_23\">      120A. Definition of criminal conspiracy.--\n      When two or more persons agree to do, or cause\n      to be done,--\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_24\">      (1) an illegal act, or\n      (2) an act which is not illegal by illegal means,\n      such an agreement is designated a criminal\n      conspiracy:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_25\">      Provided that no agreement except an agreement\n      to commit an offence shall amount to a criminal\n      conspiracy       unless       some    act      besides     the\n      agreement is done by one or more parties to such\n      agreement in pursuance thereof.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_26\">      Explanation.--It          is   immaterial       whether     the\n      illegal    act   is   the     ultimate    object   of     such\n      agreement, or is merely incidental to that object.\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_37\">                                44</span>\n\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_87\">45.          Under <a href=\"/doc/704346/\" id=\"a_57\">section 43</a> of the IPC, an act would be\n\nillegal if it is an offence or if it is prohibited by law. <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_58\">Section\n\n120-B</a> IPC which prescribes the punishment to be imposed\n\non a party to a criminal conspiracy reads as under:-\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_27\">       120B. Punishment of criminal conspiracy.-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_28\">      (1)    Whoever is a party to a criminal conspiracy\n      to commit an offence punishable with death,\n      imprisonment for life or rigorous imprisonment for\n      a term of two years or upwards, shall, where no\n      express provision is made in this Code for the\n      punishment of such a conspiracy, be punished in\n      the same manner as if he had abetted such\n      offence.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_29\">      (2)    Whoever is a party to a criminal conspiracy\n      other than a criminal conspiracy to commit an\n      offence    punishable    as   aforesaid    shall   be\n      punished with imprisonment of either description\n      for a term not exceeding six months, or with fine\n      or with both.\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_38\">                                45</span>\n\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_88\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"342903\" id=\"span_39\">46.         The Apex Court in <a href=\"/doc/342903/\" id=\"a_59\">E.G. BARSAY V. STATE OF\n\nBOMBAY</a> reported in AIR 1961 SC 1762, held as under:</span>\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_30\">       &quot;The gist of the offence is an agreement to break\n       the law. The parties to such an agreement will\n       be guilty of criminal conspiracy, though the\n       illegal act agreed to be done has not been done.\n       So too, it is an ingredient of the offence that all\n       the parties should agree to do a single illegal act.\n       It may comprise the commission of a number of\n       acts. Under <a href=\"/doc/704346/\" id=\"a_60\">Section 43</a> of the Indian Penal Code,\n       an act would be illegal if it is an offence or if it is\n       prohibited by law.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_89\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1378876\" id=\"span_40\">47.         The Supreme Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/1378876/\" id=\"a_61\">YASH PAL\n\nMITTAL V. STATE OF PUNJAB</a> reported in (1977) 4 SCC\n\n540 laid as follows at page 543, para 9.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_90\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_31\">       &quot;The very agreement, concert or league is the\n       ingredient of the offence. It is not necessary that\n       all the conspirators must know each and every\n       detail of the conspiracy as long as they are co-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_32\">       participators    in   the    main    object   of   the\n       conspiracy. There may be so many devices and\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_41\">                              46</span>\n\n\n\n\n       techniques adopted to achieve the common goal\n       of the conspiracy and there may be division of\n       performances in the chain of actions with one\n       object to achieve the real end of which every\n       collaborator must be aware and in which each\n       one of them must be interested. There must be\n       unity of object or purpose but there may be\n       plurality of means sometimes even unknown to\n       one   another,   amongst     the   conspirators.   In\n       achieving the goal several offences may be\n       committed by some of the conspirators even\n       unknown to the others. The only relevant factor\n       is that all means adopted and illegal acts done\n       must be and purported to be in furtherance of\n       the object of the conspiracy even though there\n       may be sometimes misfire or overshooting by\n       some of the conspirators.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_91\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"620904\" id=\"span_42\">48.          The Apex Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/620904/\" id=\"a_62\">MOHD. USMAN\n\nMOHAMMAD HUSSAIN MANIYAR AND ORS. V. STATE OF\n\nMAHARASHTRA</a> reported in (1981) 2 SCC 443, held that\n\nfor an offence under <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_63\">Section 120B</a> IPC, the prosecution need\n\nnot necessarily prove that the conspirators expressly agreed\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_43\">                               47</span>\n\n\n\n\nto do or cause to be done the illegal act, the agreement may\n\nbe proved by necessary implication.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_92\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_93\">49.         Judge   Learned   Hand,    in   VAN   RIPER    VS.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_94\">UNITED STATES (13 F 2d. 961) said of conspiracy as\n\nfollows:\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_95\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_33\">      &quot;When men enter into an agreement for an\n      unlawful end, they become ad hoc agents for one\n      another and have made a partnership in crime.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_96\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"928322\" id=\"span_44\">50.         We may usefully refer to the case of <a href=\"/doc/928322/\" id=\"a_64\">AJAY\n\nAGARWAL VS. UNION OF INDIA AND ORS</a> reported in JT\n\n1993 (3) SC 203 where it was held as under:</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_97\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_34\">      &quot;8.....It is not necessary that each conspirator\n      must know all the details of the scheme nor be a\n      participant at every stage.   It is necessary that\n      they should agree for design or object of the\n      conspiracy.   Conspiracy is conceived as having\n      three elements: (1) agreement; (2) between two or\n      more persons by whom the agreement is effected;\n      and (3) a criminal object, which may be either the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_45\">                         48</span>\n\n\n\n\nultimate aim of the agreement, or may constitute\nthe means, or one of the means by which that aim\nis to be accomplished. It is immaterial whether\nthis is found in the ultimate objects. The common\nlaw definition of &#x27;criminal conspiracy&#x27; was stated\nfirst by Lord Denman in Jones&#x27; case that an\nindictment   for   conspiracy   must    &quot;charge   a\nconspiracy to do an unlawful act by unlawful\nmeans&quot; and was elaborated by Willies, J. on\nbehalf of the judges while referring the question\nto the House of Lords in Mulcahy v. Reg and\nHouse of Lords in unanimous decision reiterated\nin Quinn v. Leathem:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_35\">    &#x27;A conspiracy consists not merely in the\nintention of two or more, but in the agreement of\ntwo or more, to do an unlawful act, or to do a\nlawful act by unlawful means. So long as such a\ndesign rest in intention only, it is not indictable.\nWhen two agree to carry it into effect, the very\nplot is an act in itself, and the act of each of the\nparties, promise against promise, actus contra\nactum, capable of being enforced, if lawful; and\npunishable if for a criminal object, or for the use\nof criminal means.&#x27;\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_46\">                                 49</span>\n\n\n\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_98\">51.          <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"194120\" id=\"span_47\">In the case of <a href=\"/doc/194120/\" id=\"a_65\">STATE VS. NALINI</a> reported in\n\n1999 (5) SCC 253, S.S.M. QUADRI, J, after a survey of case\n\nlaw,   the   Apex    Court    made    the   following   pertinent\n\nobservations: (at paragraph 662)\n\n\n             &quot;In reaching the stage of meeting of minds,\n       two or more persons share information about\n       doing an illegal act or a legal act by illegal means.\n       This is the first stage where each is said to have\n       knowledge of a plan for committing an illegal act\n       or a legal act by illegal means. Among those\n       sharing the information some or all may form an\n       intention to do an illegal act or a legal act by\n       illegal means. Those who do form the requisite\n       intention would be parties to the agreement and\n       would be conspirators but those who drop out\n       cannot be roped in as collaborators on the basis\n       of mere knowledge unless they commit acts or\n       omissions from which a guilty common intention\n       can be inferred. It is not necessary that all the\n       conspirators should participate from the inception\n       to the end of the conspiracy; some may join the\n       conspiracy after the time when such intention\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_48\">                                      50</span>\n\n\n\n\n      was first entertained by any one of them and\n      some others may quit from the conspiracy. All of\n      them cannot but be treated as conspirators.</span>\n</p><pre id=\"pre_2\">      Where    in        pursuance     of     the   agreement       the\n      conspirators        commit     offences       individually     or\n</pre><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_99\">      adopt illegal means to do a legal act which has a\n      nexus to the object of conspiracy, all of them will\n      be liable for wsuch offences even if some of them\n      have not actively participated in the commission\n      of those offences.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_100\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_101\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"108787168\" id=\"span_49\">52.         The Apex Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/108787168/\" id=\"a_66\">&#x27;DEVENDER PAL\n\nSINGH v. STATE OF NCT OF DELHI</a>&#x27; reported in 2002 SCC\n\n(Crl) 978 has held as under:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_36\">       40. The elements of a criminal conspiracy have\n       been    stated       to   be:    (a)    an    object   to    be\n       accomplished, (b) a plan or scheme embodying\n       means        to    accomplish        that    object,   (c)   an\n       agreement or understanding between two or\n       more of the accused persons whereby they\n       become definitely committed to cooperate for the\n       accomplishment of the object by the means\n       embodied in the agreement, or by any effectual\n       means, (d) in the jurisdiction where the statute\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_50\">                          51</span>\n\n\n\n\nrequired an overt act. The essence of a criminal\nconspiracy is the unlawful combination and\nordinarily the offence is complete when the\ncombination is framed. From this it necessarily\nfollows that unless the statute so requires, no\novert act need be done in furtherance of the\nconspiracy,    and    that     the   object   of    the\ncombination need not be accomplished, in order\nto constitute an indictable offence. Law making\nconspiracy a crime is designated to curb\nimmoderate power to do mischief which is\ngained by a combination of the means.              The\nencouragement        and      support    which      co-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_102\">conspirators give to one another rendering\nenterprises possible which, if left to individual\neffort, would have been impossible, furnish the\nground for visiting conspirators and abettors\nwith condign punishment.           The conspiracy is\nheld to be continued and renewed as to all its\nmembers       wherever       and     whenever      (See\nAmerican Jurisprudence, Vol.II, Section 23,\np.559) For an offence punishable under Section\n120-B, the prosecution need not necessarily\nprove that the perpetrators expressly agree to\ndo or cause to be done an illegal act; the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_51\">                                  52</span>\n\n\n\n\n       agreement      may   be    proved      by    necessary\n       implication. Offence of criminal conspiracy has\n       its foundation in an agreement to commit an\n       offence. A conspiracy consists not merely in the\n       intention of two or more, but in the agreement of\n       two or more to do an unlawful act by unlawful\n       means.    So long as such a design rests in\n       intention only, it is not indictable.       When two\n       agree to carry it into effect, the very plot is an\n       act in itself, and an act of each of the parties,\n       promise against promise, actus contra actum,\n       capable of being enforced, if lawful, punishable\n       if for a criminal object or for use of criminal\n       means.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_103\">\n\nWHETHER CONSPIRACY IS A CONTINUING OFFENCE?\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_104\">53.          In HALSBURY&#x27;S LAW OF ENGLAND, third\n\nedition, vol. 10, page 327, para 602, while dealing with\n\ncontinuing offence it was stated as under:\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_37\">      &quot;A criminal enterprise may consist of continuing\n      act which is done in more places than one or of a\n      series of acts which are done in several places. In\n      such   cases,   though     there   is   one    criminal\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_52\">                                  53</span>\n\n\n\n\n      enterprise, there may be several crimes, and a\n      crime is committed in each place where a\n      complete criminal act is performed although the\n      act may be only a part of the enterprise.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_105\">54.            Conspiracy   to   commit    crime     by   itself   is\n\npunishable as a substantive offence and every individual\n\noffence committed pursuant to the conspiracy is separate\n\nand distinct offence to which individual offenders are liable\n\nto punishment, considering their overt acts, independent of\n\nthe conspiracy. The agreement does not come to an end with\n\nits making, but would endure till it is accomplished or\n\nabandoned or proved abortive. Being a continuing offence, if\n\nany acts or omissions which constitute an offence are done\n\nthe   conspirators   continue    to   be   parties   to   the   said\n\nconspiracy. The agreement continues in operation and\n\ntherefore in existence until it is discharged or terminated by\n\ncompletion of its performance or by abandonment or\n\nfrustration.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_53\">                                   54</span><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_106\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_107\">55.         Lord Pearson explaining the meaning of the term\n\nconspiracy has held that:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_38\">      &quot;a conspiracy involved an agreement express or\n      implied.    A conspiratorial agreement is not a\n      contract,   not   legally    binding    because      it    is\n      unlawful. But as an agreement it has its three\n      stages, namely, (1) making or formation; (2)\n      performance or implementation; (3) discharge or\n      termination. When the conspiratorial agreement\n      has been made, the offence of conspiracy is\n      complete,   it    has    been    committed,    and        the\n      conspirator can be prosecuted even though no\n      performance had taken place. But the fact that\n      the offence of conspiracy is complete at the stage\n      does not mean that the conspiratorial agreement\n      is finished with.       It is not dead.   If it is being\n      performed, it is very much alive. So long as the\n      performance continues, it is operating, it is being\n      carried out by the conspirators,              and    it is\n      governing or at any rate influencing their conduct.\n      The   conspiratorial        agreement     continues        in\n      operation and therefore in existence until it is\n      discharged (termination) by completion of its\n      performance or by abandonment or frustration or,\n      however, it may be.&quot;\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_54\">                                  55</span>\n\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_108\">56.          Conspiracy by itself is a substantive offence and\n\nis distinct from the offence to be committed in respect of\n\nwhich the conspiracy is entered into. That the general\n\nconspiracy is distinct from number of separate offences\n\ncommitted while executing the offence of conspiracy. Each\n\none of the act constitutes a separate offence punishable,\n\nindependent of the conspiracy. The traditional concept of\n\nsingle agreement can also accommodate the situation where\n\na well-defined group conspires to commit multiple crimes, so\n\nlong as all these crimes are the objects of the same\n\nagreement or continuous conspiratorial relationship, and the\n\nconspiracy continues to subsist though it was entered in the\n\nfirst instance.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_109\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_110\">57.          Therefore,   what    follows   from   the   aforesaid\n\njudgments is that Criminal conspiracy is an agreement, by\n\ntwo or more persons to do, or cause to be done, an illegal act\n\nor an act, which is not illegal, by illegal means. The\n\nagreement is the gist of the offence. The offence of criminal\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_55\">                              56</span>\n\n\n\n\nconspiracy is of a technical nature and the essential\n\ningredient of the offence is the agreement to commit an\n\noffence. &quot;A conspiracy&quot; consists, not merely in the intention\n\nof two or more but in the agreement of two or more to do an\n\nunlawful act or to do a lawful act by unlawful means. So\n\nlong as such a design rests in the intention only it is not\n\nindictable. The entire agreement must be viewed as a whole\n\nand it has to be ascertained as to what in fact the\n\nconspirators intended to do or the object they wanted to\n\nachieve. Consequently, even if the acts done by a conspirator\n\nin furtherance of the criminal conspiracy do not strictly\n\namount to offence, he is liable to be convicted under Section\n\n120-B. The gist of the offence is the bare engagement and\n\nassociation to break the law, whether any act be done in\n\npursuance or furtherance thereof by the conspirators or not.\n\nIn order to constitute a single general conspiracy, there must\n\nbe a common design and common intention of all to work in\n\nfurtherance of the common design. Each conspirator plays\n\nhis separate part in one integrated and united effort to\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_56\">                                57</span>\n\n\n\n\nachieve the common purpose. Each one is aware that he has\n\na part to play in a general conspiracy though he may not be\n\nknowing all its secrets or the means by which the common\n\npurpose is accomplished. It is not necessary that all the\n\nconspirators must know each and every detail of the\n\nconspiracy as long as they are co-participants in the main\n\ntheme and object of the conspiracy. There must be unity of\n\nobject or purpose but there may be plurality of means\n\nsometimes even unknown to one another. The only relevant\n\nfactor is that all means adopted and illegal acts done must\n\nbe and purported to be in furtherance of the object of the\n\nconspiracy. The evil scheme may be promoted by a few, some\n\nmay drop out and some may join at a later stage, but the\n\nconspiracy continues until it is broken up. The conspiracy\n\nmay develop in successive stages. There may be a general\n\nplan to accomplish the common design by such means as\n\nmay from time to time be found expedient.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_111\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_112\">58.        The offence of criminal conspiracy under Section\n\n120-A is a distinct offence.    The conspiracy to commit a\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_57\">                                58</span>\n\n\n\n\ncrime and the crime itself are two different offences.\n\nConspiracy precedes the commission of crime and is\n\ncomplete before the crime is attempted or completed. The\n\nvery agreement, concert or league is the ingredient of the\n\noffence. The offence of criminal conspiracy is complete as\n\nsoon as two or more persons agree to do or cause to be done\n\nan illegal act, or an act which is not illegal by illegal means.\n\nIt is immaterial whether the illegal act is the ultimate object\n\nof such an agreement or is merely, incidental to that object.\n\nThe agreement in itself is enough to constitute the offence.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_113\">59.            Although a mere agreement to do an illegal act\n\nor a legal act by illegal means is itself a conspiracy, the\n\nconspiracy is not concluded directly and the agreement is\n\nmade in the sense that the offence is once and for all\n\nconstituted.    A criminal conspiracy may persist as long as\n\nthe persons constituting it continue to act in accord in\n\nfurtherance of their objects. An agreement, to do an illegal\n\nact, which amounts to a conspiracy will continue as long as\n\nthe members of the conspiracy remain in agreement and as\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_58\">                               59</span>\n\n\n\n\nlong as they are acting in accord and in furtherance of the\n\nobject for which they entered into the agreement. Although\n\nconspiracy is committed as soon as the agreement for the\n\n&#x27;unlawful&#x27; object is made, it is clear that conspiracy is a\n\ncontinuing   offence   and   is   committed   not   only   when\n\nagreement is first reached but also as long as the agreement\n\nto effect the unlawful object subsists or continues. A\n\nconspiracy thus, is a continuing offence and continues to\n\nsubsist and committed wherever one of the conspirators\n\ndoes an act or series of acts. So long as its performance\n\ncontinues, it is a continuing offence till it is executed or\n\nrescinded or frustrated by choice or necessity irrespective of\n\nthe time limit. A crime is complete as soon as the agreement\n\nis made, but it is not a thing of the moment. It does not end\n\nwith the making of the agreement. It will continue so long as\n\nthere are two or more parties to it intending to carry into\n\neffect the design. Its continuance is a threat to the society\n\nagainst which it was aimed at and would be dealt with as\n\nsoon as that jurisdiction can properly claim the power to do\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_59\">                                  60</span>\n\n\n\n\nso. Thus if the object of the conspiracy is accomplished and\n\nthe conspirators disband themselves, the conspiracy comes\n\nto an end. So also would be the case if the conspiracy is\n\nrouted as a result of police action and the conspirators are\n\ntaken into custody.        It should also be borne in mind that\n\neven after the conspirators released from the custody they\n\nmay renew their conspiracy if the object is still not received.\n\n\n\nEVIDENCE OF CONSPIRACY\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_114\">60.         There is no difference between the mode of\n\nproof of the offence of conspiracy and that of any other\n\noffence, it can be established by direct or circumstantial\n\nevidence.   Privacy        and   secrecy     are    pre-dominant\n\ncharacteristics   of   a    conspiracy     rather   than   a   loud\n\ndiscussion in an elevated place open to public view.\n\nGenerally, a conspiracy is hatched in secrecy and it may\n\nbe difficult to adduce direct evidence of the same. Direct\n\nevidence in proof of a conspiracy is seldom available. It is\n\nnot always possible to give affirmative evidence about the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_60\">                                61</span>\n\n\n\n\ndate of the formation of the criminal conspiracy, about the\n\npersons who took part in the formation of the conspiracy,\n\nabout the object which the objectors set before themselves\n\nas the object of conspiracy, and about the manner in\n\nwhich the object of conspiracy is to be carried out. All this\n\nis necessarily a matter of inference. The prosecution will\n\noften rely on evidence of acts of various parties to infer\n\nthat they were done in consequence of their common\n\nintention. The prosecution will also more often rely upon\n\ncircumstantial    evidence.    The    conspiracy    can     be\n\nundoubtedly      proved   by   such   evidence     direct   or\n\ncircumstantial. But the Court must enquire whether the\n\ntwo persons are independently pursuing the same end or\n\nthey have come together to the pursuit of the unlawful\n\nobject. The former does not render them conspirators, but\n\nthe latter does. It is however, essential that the offence of\n\nconspiracy requires some kind of physical manifestation of\n\nagreement. The express agreement, however, need not be\n\nproved. Nor actual meeting of two persons is necessary.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_61\">                              62</span>\n\n\n\n\nNor it is necessary to prove the actual words of\n\ncommunication. The evidence as to transmission of\n\nthoughts sharing the unlawful design may be sufficient\n\nunder given set of circumstances of an individual case.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_115\">61.         In order to prove a criminal conspiracy which is\n\npunishable under Section 120-B there must be direct or\n\ncircumstantial   evidence   to   show   that   there   was   an\n\nagreement between two or more persons to commit an\n\noffence. It is to be proved largely on the inferences drawn\n\nfrom illegal act or omissions committed by the conspirators\n\nin pursuance of common design- which has been properly\n\nproved. Conspiracy may be proved by necessary implication.\n\nThe direct evidence will be seldom forthcoming and it is,\n\ntherefore, necessary to look at the circumstances to see\n\nwhether a conspiracy actually existed which is largely\n\ninferential. Of sheer necessity this section has to be read in\n\nconjunction with <a href=\"/doc/1946503/\" id=\"a_67\">Section 10</a> of the Indian Evidence Act.\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1946503/\" id=\"a_68\">Section 10</a> of the Evidence Act introduces the doctrine of\n\nagency and if the conditions laid down therein are satisfied,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_62\">                                    63</span>\n\n\n\n\nthe   act   done   by   one   is    admissible   against   the   co-\n\nconspirators. But this section will come into play only when\n\nthe Court is satisfied that there is reasonable ground to\n\nbelieve that two or more persons have conspired together to\n\ncommit an offence or an actionable wrong, that is to say,\n\nthere should be a prima facie evidence that a person was a\n\nparty to the conspiracy before his acts can be used against\n\nhis co-conspirators. Once such a reasonable ground exists,\n\nanything said, done or written by one of the conspirators in\n\nreference to the common intention, after the said intention\n\nwas entertained is relevant against the others not only for\n\nthe purpose of proving the existence of the conspiracy but\n\nalso for proving that the other person was a party to it.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_116\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"374135\" id=\"span_63\">62.          The Patna High Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/374135/\" id=\"a_69\">STATE OF\n\nBIHAR V. PARAMHANS</a> reported in 1986 Pat LJR 688 held\n\nthat Conspiracy can be proved by circumstances and other\n\nmaterials. It states thus:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_39\">        <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"702724\" id=\"span_64\">&quot;To establish a charge of conspiracy knowledge\n        about indulgence in either an illegal act or a\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_65\">                              64</span>\n\n\n\n\n       legal act by illegal means is necessary. In some\n       cases, intent of unlawful use being made of the\n       goods or services in question may be inferred\n       from the knowledge itself. This apart, the\n       prosecution has not to establish that a particular\n       unlawful use was intended, so long as the goods\n       or service in question could not be put to any\n       lawful use.   Finally, when the ultimate offence\n       consists of a chain of actions, it would not be\n       necessary for the prosecution to establish, to\n       bring home the charge of conspiracy, that each\n       of the conspirators had the knowledge of what\n       the collaborator would do, so long as it is known\n       that the collaborator would put the goods or\n       service to an unlawful use. (See: <a href=\"/doc/702724/\" id=\"a_70\">State of\n       Maharashtra v. Som Nath Thapa [JT</a> 1996 (4) SC\n       615]).&quot;</span>\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_117\">63.         <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"667073\" id=\"span_66\">In <a href=\"/doc/667073/\" id=\"a_71\">KEHAR SINGH AND ORS. V. THE STATE\n\n(DELHI ADMINISTRATION</a>) reported in AIR 1988 SC 1883\n\nAT P. 1954, the Apex Court observed as under:</span>\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_40\">            &quot;275. Generally, a conspiracy is hatched in\n      secrecy and it may be difficult to adduce direct\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_67\">                                  65</span>\n\n\n\n\n      evidence of the same. The prosecution will often\n      rely on evidence of acts of various parties to infer\n      that they were done in reference to their common\n      intention. The prosecution will also more often\n      rely upon circumstantial evidence. The conspiracy\n      can be undoubtedly proved by such evidence\n      direct or circumstantial. But the court must\n      enquire     whether       the     two    persons    are\n      independently pursuing the same end or they\n      have come together in the pursuit of the unlawful\n      object.   The    former    does    not   render    them\n      conspirators, but the latter does. It is, however,\n      essential that the offence of conspiracy required\n      some      kind   of   physical      manifestation    of\n      agreement. The express agreement, however,\n      need not be proved. Nor actual meeting of the two\n      persons is necessary. Nor it is necessary to prove\n      the actual words of communication. The evidence\n      as to transmission of thoughts sharing the\n      unlawful design may be sufficient.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_118\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1607630\" id=\"span_68\">64.          The Apex Court, in the case of <a href=\"/doc/1607630/\" id=\"a_72\">&#x27;NAZIR KHAN &amp;\n\nORS. v. STATE OF DELHI</a>&#x27; reported in 2003 SCC (Crl)\n\n2033 has held as under:</span>\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_69\">                                 66</span><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_119\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_41\">       &quot;15. xxxx\n\n       No doubt, in the case of conspiracy there cannot\n       be any direct evidence. The ingredients of\n       offence are that there should be an agreement\n       between persons who are alleged to conspire\n       and the said agreement should be for doing an\n       illegal act or for doing by illegal means an act\n       which itself may not be illegal. Therefore, the\n       essence of criminal conspiracy is an agreement\n       to do an illegal act and such an agreement can\n       be proved either by direct evidence or by\n       circumstantial evidence or by both, and it is a\n       matter     of   common    experience   that   direct\n       evidence to prove conspiracy is rarely available.\n       Therefore, the circumstances proved before,\n       during and after the occurrence have to be\n       considered to decide about the complicity of the\n       accused.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_120\">65.        In DEVENDRANATH PAL SINGH&#x27;S case the\n\nApex Court held as under:\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_70\">                                  67</span><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_121\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_42\">      &quot;No doubt, in the case of conspiracy there cannot\n      be any direct evidence. The ingredients of offence\n      are that there should be an agreement between\n      persons who are alleged to conspire and the said\n      agreement should be for doing an illegal act or for\n      doing by illegal means an act which itself may\n      not be illegal. Therefore, the essence of criminal\n      conspiracy is an agreement to do an illegal act\n      and such an agreement can be proved either by\n      direct evidence or by circumstantial evidence or\n      by both, and it is a matter of common experience\n      that direct evidence to prove conspiracy is rarely\n      available. Therefore, the circumstances proved\n      before, during and after the occurrence have to be\n      considered to decide about the complicity of the\n      accused.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_122\">66.            The   essential   ingredient   of    the   offence   of\n\ncriminal conspiracy is the agreement to commit an offence.\n\nIn a case where the agreement is for accomplishment of an\n\nact which by itself constitutes an offence, then in that event\n\nno overt act is necessary to be proved by the prosecution\n\nbecause   in    such    a   situation,   criminal    conspiracy     is\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_71\">                              68</span>\n\n\n\n\nestablished by proving such an agreement. Where the\n\nconspiracy alleged is with regard to commission of a serious\n\ncrime of the nature contemplated in Section 120B read with\n\nthe proviso to sub-section (2) of Section 120A, then in that\n\nevent mere proof of an agreement between the accused for\n\ncommission of such a crime alone is enough to bring about a\n\nconviction under Section 120B and the proof of any overt act\n\nby the accused or by any one of them would not be\n\nnecessary. The provisions, in such a situation, do not require\n\nthat each and every person who is a party to the conspiracy\n\nmust do some overt act towards the fulfillment of the object\n\nof conspiracy. The essential ingredient being an agreement\n\nbetween the conspirators to commit the crime and if these\n\nrequirements and ingredients are established, the act would\n\nfall within the trappings of the provisions contained in\n\nsection 120B. Conspiracies are not hatched in the open, by\n\ntheir nature, they are secretly planned, they can be proved\n\neven by circumstantial evidence, the lack of direct evidence\n\nrelating to conspiracy has no consequence.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_72\">                                  69</span>\n\n\n\n\nWAGING WAR (<a href=\"/doc/786750/\" id=\"a_73\">Section 121</a> IPC)\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_123\">            121. Waging, or attempting to wage\n            war,    or     abetting      waging       of     war,\n            against       the    Government         of     India.-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_124\">            Whoever,       wages         war      against     the\n            Government of India, or attempts to wage\n            such war, or abets the waging of such war,\n            shall    be       punished     with     death,      or\n            imprisonment for life and shall also be\n            liable to fine.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_125\">\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_126\">67.         The concept of war embodied in <a href=\"/doc/786750/\" id=\"a_74\">Section 121</a> IPC\n\nis not to be understood in the international law sense of inter\n\ncountry war involving military operations by and between\n\ntwo or more hostile countries. <a href=\"/doc/786750/\" id=\"a_75\">Section 121</a> IPC is not meant\n\nto punish prisoners of war of a belligerent nation.                  Apart\n\nfrom the legislative history of the provision and the\n\nunderstanding of the expression by various High Courts\n\nduring the pre-independence days, the Illustration to Section\n\n121 itself makes it clear that &#x27;war&#x27; contemplated by Section\n\n121 is not conventional warfare between two nations. The\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_73\">                                 70</span>\n\n\n\n\norganizing or joining an insurrection against the Government\n\nof India is also a form of war. &quot;Insurrection&quot; as defined in\n\ndictionaries and as commonly understood connotes a violent\n\nuprising by a group directed against the Government in\n\npower or the civil authorities. &quot;Rebellion, revolution and civil\n\nwar&#x27; are progressive stages in the development of civil\n\nunrest, the most rudimentary form of which is insurrection.\n\nUnlawful assemblies, riots, insurrections, rebellions, levying\n\nof war are offences which run into each other and not\n\ncapable of being marked off by perfectly definite boundaries.\n\nAll of them have in common one feature, namely, that the\n\nnormal tranquillity of a civilized society is, in each of the\n\ncases mentioned, disturbed either by actual force or at least\n\nby the show and threat of it.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_127\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_128\">68.         The offence of waging war was inserted in the\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1569253/\" id=\"a_76\">Indian Penal Code</a> to accord with the concept of levying war\n\nin the English Statutes of treason, the first of which dates\n\nback to 1351 A.D.     We should, therefore, understand the\n\nexpression &quot;wages war&quot; occurring in <a href=\"/doc/786750/\" id=\"a_77\">Section 121</a> IPC broadly\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_74\">                              71</span>\n\n\n\n\nin the same sense in which it was understood in England\n\nwhile dealing with the corresponding expression in the\n\nTreason Statute. However, we have to view the expression\n\nwith the eyes of the people of free India and we must\n\nmodulate and restrict the scope of observations too broadly\n\nmade in the vintage decisions so as to be in keeping with the\n\ndemocratic    spirit   and   the   contemporary    conditions\n\nassociated with the working of our democracy.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_129\">69.          The most important is the intention or purpose\n\nbehind the defiance or rising against the Government. The\n\nintention and purpose of the war-like operations directed\n\nagainst the Governmental machinery is an important\n\ncriterion. If the object and purpose is to strike at the\n\nsovereign authority of the Ruler or the Government to\n\nachieve a public and general purpose in contra-distinction to\n\na private and a particular purpose; it is an important indicia\n\nof waging war. Of course, the purpose must be intended to\n\nbe achieved by use of force, arms and by defiance of\n\nGovernment troops or armed personnel deployed to maintain\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_75\">                               72</span>\n\n\n\n\npublic tranquility. The number of force, the manner in which\n\nthey are arrayed, armed or equipped is immaterial. Even a\n\nlimited number of persons who carry powerful explosives\n\nand missiles without regard to their own safety can cause\n\nmore devastating damage than a large group of persons\n\narmed with ordinary weapons or fire arms. Then, the other\n\nsettled proposition is that there need not be the pomp and\n\npageantry usually associated with war such as the offenders\n\nforming themselves in battle-line and arraying in a war like\n\nmanner. Even a stealthy operation to overwhelm the armed\n\nor other personnel deployed by the Government and to attain\n\na commanding position by which terms could be dictated to\n\nthe Government might very well be an act of waging war.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_130\">70.         Even if the conspired purpose and objective falls\n\nshort of installing some other authority or entity in the place\n\nof an established Government, it does detract from the\n\noffence of waging war. There is no warrant for such\n\ntruncated interpretation.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_76\">                               73</span><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_131\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_132\">71.          Section 121 of the I.P. Code embraces every\n\ndescription of war whether by insurrection or invasion. The\n\ntrue criterion is the purpose or intention with which the\n\ngathering assembled. The object of the gathering must be to\n\nattain by force and violence, an object of a general public\n\nnature thereby striking directly against the Government&#x27;s\n\nauthority.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_133\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_134\">72.          The concept of war embodied in <a href=\"/doc/786750/\" id=\"a_78\">Section 121</a> of\n\nthe Indian Penal Code has been the subject matter of various\n\ndecisions.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_135\">      The observations of LORD HOLT, C. J. in a case\n\nreported in HOLT&#x27;S REPORTS (1688-1700) at 681-682\n\nreads as under:-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_136\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_43\">      &quot;Holt L. C.J. in Sir John Friend&#x27;s case says, &#x27;if\n      persons do assemble themselves and act with\n      force in opposition to some law which they think\n      inconvenient, and hope thereby to get it repealed,\n      this is a levying war and treason&quot;. &quot;I tell you the\n      joint opinion of us all, that, if this multitude\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_77\">                               74</span>\n\n\n\n\n      assembled with intent, by acts or force and\n      violence, to compel the legislature to repeal a law,\n      it is high treason&quot;. The question always is,\n      whether the intent is, by force and violence, to\n      attain an object of a general and public nature, by\n      any instruments; or by dint of their numbers&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_137\">73.         The speech of LORD MANSFIELD, CJ addressed\n\nto the Jury in LORD GEORGE GORDON&#x27;S CASE (1781) is\n\noften quoted to unfold the meaning of the expression &#x27;levying\n\nwar against the King&#x27;. To quote the words of Mansfield, C.J.:\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_44\">            &quot;There are two kinds of levying war: one\n      against the person of the King: to imprison, to\n      dethrone, or to kill him; or to make him change\n      measures, or remove counsellors : the other,\n      which is said to be levied against the majesty of\n      the King or, in other words, against him in his\n      regal capacity; as when a multitude rise and\n      assemble to attain by force and violence any\n      object of a general public nature; that is levying\n      war against the majesty of the King; and most\n      reasonably so held, because it tends to dissolve\n      all the bonds of society, to destroy property, and\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_78\">                                   75</span>\n\n\n\n\n      to overturn Government ; and by force of arms, to\n      restrain the King from reigning, according to law&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_45\">              &quot;No amount of violence, however great, and\n      with whatever circumstances of a warlike kind it\n      may be attended, will make an attack by one\n      subject on another high treason. On the other\n      hand,        any   amount    of   violence,   however\n      insignificant, directed against the King will be\n      high treason, and as soon as violence has any\n      political objects, it is impossible to say that it is\n      not directed against the king, in the sense of\n      being armed opposition to the lawful exercise of\n      his power&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_138\">74.           In    1820   LORD    PRESIDENT        HOPE   in   his\n\nsumming up speech to the jury in REX VS. ANDREW\n\nHARDIE, (1820, 1 STATE TRIALS N.S., 610) explained the\n\ndistinction between levying a war and committing a riot in\n\nthe following words:\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_139\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_46\">      &quot;Gentlemen, it may be useful to say a few words\n      on the distinction between levying war against\n      the King and committing a riot. The distinction\n      seems to consist in this, although they may often\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_79\">                           76</span>\n\n\n\n\nrun very nearly into each other.
