In    fact,   when    the   said   Siddique   Deendar\n\nChennabasaveshwara preached his religion, the British\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_147\">                                   142</span>\n\n\n\n\nGovernment initiated proceedings against him under <a href=\"/doc/1089903/\" id=\"a_94\">Section\n\n108</a> of the Criminal Procedure Code in the Court of District\n\nMagistrate, Dharwad for endangering the public peace by\n\npreaching a new religion in such a way as to promote\n\nfeelings   of     hatred    and     enimity    between        different\n\ncommunities. The learned Magistrate held that all the ten\n\naccused intentionally disseminate or attempt to disseminate\n\nmatter, the publication of which is likely to result in feelings\n\nof hatred between different classes of his Majestys&#x27; subject.\n\nTherefore he ordered Siddiq Deendar Channabasaveshwara\n\nto give his personal surety in Rs.100/- together with one\n\nanother surety in Rs.100/- and that he will be of good\n\nbehaviour for one year.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_223\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_224\">109.            This order was passed on 5th July, 1934.           The\n\nsaid order reads as under:\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_225\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_152\">                  &quot; In the Court of the District Magistrate\n                              Dharwar\n\n                Order under <a href=\"/doc/6649109/\" id=\"a_95\">Sec. 108</a> C.P.C. against\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_148\">                        143</span>\n\n\n\n\n     (1)    Siddiq Deendar Channa\n            Bashweshwar\n     (2)    Syeb Hamiduddin W.D. Syed\n            Hussain\n     (3)    Syed Kassim W.D. Syed Budan\n     (6)    Abdul Gaffar W.D. Mohamed Saheb\n            of Hubli\n     (7)    Mohamad Azimuddin W.D. Khaja\n            Hussain\n     (8)    Syed Mohamad Hussain W.D. Syed\n            Mohamad Mastan Saheb\n     (9)    Hazrat Saheb W.D. Dawala Saheb\n            Mulgund of Gadag\n     (10)   Abdul Jabbar W.D. Syed Azim Saheb\n     (16)   Raja Saheb W.D. Hussain Saheb\n            Alias Patel Saheb of Asundi of Gadag\n     (17) Syed Rasool W.D. Mohamad Hashim\n            Katgari Belgaum\n\n     The ten respondents are the remnant of a\nparty of 19 persons against whom Mr. Gudi Sub-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_226\">Inspector of Police Dharwar laid information in\nmy court on May 23rd that they were endangering\nthe public peace by preaching a new religion in\nsuch a way as to promote feelings of hatred and\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_149\">                         144</span>\n\n\n\n\nenimity    between      different      communities.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_227\">Accordingly I passed an order under<a href=\"/doc/129687811/\" id=\"a_96\"> S.112</a>, <a href=\"/doc/6649109/\" id=\"a_97\">108</a>,\nCPC requiring respondent No.1, their leader, to\nshow cause why be should not be bound over in\nRs.200 with one other security in the same\namount to be of good behaviour for one year.\nWhile I called on the rest to show cause why their\nleader to show cause why he should not be\nbound over in Rs.200 with one other security in\nthe same amount to be of good behaviour for one\nyear. While I called on the rest to show cause\nwhy they should not be bound over to be of good\nbehaviour for the same length of time but in\nRs.100 with one security in the like amount. On\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_150\">June 5th I discharged respondents Nos.12, 13</span>\nand 14 and on June 7th Nos.4, 5, 11, 15, 18 and\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_228\">19.\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_229\">      2.     It is common ground to both the\nprosecution and the defence that the remaining\nrespondents are the followers of respondent No.1\nwho was himself Hazrat Moulana Siddiq Deendar\nChenna Bashweshwar and who is a native of the\nNizam&#x27;s    Dominions.    He   claims     to   be    a\nreincarnation of   Basav, the founder of           the\nLingayat religion who lived from about 1100 to\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_151\">                         145</span>\n\n\n\n\n1160 A.D.     It appears that he relies on certain\nmarks on his hands to prove his claim to be this\npersonage, although he claims to be the Avatar of\nthis Lingayat Saint. He also claims to be devout\nMohamadan and to have found the way to\nreconcile    Hinduism   and    Mohammaddanism.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_230\">Hence he and his followers use Hindu names as\nwell as Mohaamadan names, and wear pink\ncloth   of   Hindu   Sadhus   out   into   robes   of\nMohammadan style.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_231\">        3.    From this summary of the teaching of\nthe first respondent, it will be clear that he needs\nto be a very tactful and discreet preacher if he is\nto avoid arousing the hostility of Lingayats in\nparticular of Hindus in general and as well of\northodox Mohammadans are likely to be annoyed\nonly because the doctrine of reincarnation is\nunknown to orthodox Mohammadan theology.\nThe Langayats are likely to be intensely annoyed\nbecause even if as the respondents say, their\nbards, and some of their hypocryphal writers\nhave prophesied the return of Basava yet they\ncannot but repudiate the idea that their founder\nshould now be reincarnated as a Mohammaden;\nwhile Hindus in General are not likely to approve\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_152\">                         146</span>\n\n\n\n\nof the idea that the man who unites Hindusim\nand Mohammadanism can yet assert that he\nremains a devout Mohammadan.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_232\">      4.     The prosecution alleges that so far\nfrom the respondents and his followers being\ndiscreet and tactual missionaries, they are fire\nbrands far from dissimulating their contempt for\nHindusim. They, it is alleged, occasionally give\nvent to it in a way that is bound to result in\nbreaches of the public peace. The respondents on\nthe other hand assert that their leader in\nparticular and themselves as well, are models of\nsweet reasonableness and never give occasion for\noffence to the followers of any religion.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_233\">      5.     The prosecution evidence consists of\n      (1)    evidence of two occasions in the\nDharwar District when breaches of peace might\neasily have followed the preaching of the principle\nrespondent No.1 on December 26th 1930 in\nDharwar. When the police had to disperse by\nforce a large crowd which had surrounded the\nrespondents. After which at the request of the\nDistrict Magistrate the respondents left Dharwar.\nAnd\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_153\">                             147</span>\n\n\n\n\n        (2)       On May 20th 1934 at Hubli when the\nfirst respondent in a lecture at a place open to the\npublic remarked after pointing out the superior\nprotection which God has given the Kaba at\nMecca        as   compared      with   the    temples   of\nHindusim,         said   that    the   Arya    Samajists\npropagated their religion by prostituting their\nwives and daughters. Luckily there was only one\nHindu there and his protests were easily quitted\nand no breach of the peace occurred;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_234\">        6.        The prosecution further give evidence\n(2) of the many occasions which the authorities\nresponsible for the public peace have taken action\nto restrain the respondents from preaching in\npublic in this District. In every year from and\nincluding 1930 written notices of some sort have\ncut short the preaching of respondents Nos.1 and\n3, 8 and 16 have also had notices served on them\nin this district every year since 1930. No.17 has\nhad a notice served on him in this District before\nthis year. In addition similar action has had to be\ntaken against the respondent and his party in\nMysore and in Hyderabad State.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_154\">                           148</span><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_235\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_236\">      7 (3) The prosecution have also called as\nwitnesses both an individual Mohammadan one\nof several who signed a petition to me asking that\nthe   respondents      should   be   restrained   from\nspeaking in public. And an individual Lingayat\nwho deposed the annoyance caused to followers\nof their respective religions by the words of the\nrespondents.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_237\">      8.        In reply the respondents over (I) that\na breach of the peace has never actually followed\ntheir preaching. (2) The first respondent has\nfurther produced in order to prove that he is an\northodox Mohamaddan and that his preaching\nought not to annoy Mohammaddans, a Fitman\nfrom H.E.H the Nizam dated 1336 A.N. (A.D.\n1928) to the effect that he was to get an\nallowance of Fifty rupees a month so long as he\ntoured in the Hyderabad State and preached as\nhe went. He was to submit reports of his sermons\nto H.E.H. (the allowance has since been stopped)\ngovernment. (3) The defence have also called\nindividual witness including a Lingayat and two\nHindus who swear that their sermons are\ninoffensive and do not provoke the followers of\nany religion.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_155\">                         149</span><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_238\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_239\">      9.     Of the two opposing views of the\nactivities of the accused. I have no hesitation in\naccepting that the prosecution as correct. It is\nprobably true that on special occasions as for\ninstance    at   a   conference   of   religions   the\nrespondents may be moderate and entirely\nreasonable. On such an occasion respondent No.1\ncertainly would not make the offensive suggestion\nabout Arya Samajists which I have quoted in my\nfilth paragraph Probably indeed, he would not\nhave made use of that phrase on May 20th at\nHubli had he seen any Hindus in his audience.\nBut at the same time I believe that respondent\nNo.1 did utter these words on May 20th , and that\nthey express a contempt for educateD 4 Hindus\nwhich he really fees and which when he gets\nexcited he might be unable to conceal.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_240\">      10.    In Court, respondent No.1 and his\nfollowers have been uniformly courteous to me,\nBut I formed the opinion that they are over-apt to\ninsist on their rights, that they are not very\nscrupulous as to how they induce their witness to\nsuppress an inconvenient opinion. And that in\nshort they are a rather hot tampered body of men,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_156\">                        150</span>\n\n\n\n\nthough in court they had their tempers well under\ncontrol. I believe that they have not the kind of\ntemperament necessary in those who wishs to\npreach an offensive doctrine       without giving\noffence in face. I should suppose that if no breach\nof the peace has over resulted from their sermons,\nit is largely because as soon as the police see\nthem coming and hear what they are going to talk\nabout, they take special precautions to maintain\nthe public peace.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_241\">      11.   I therefore find that all the ten\naccused intentionally disseminate or attempt to\ndisseminate matter, the publication of which is\nlikely to result in feelings of hatred     between\ndifferent classes of his Majestys&#x27; subjects. And I\ntherefore order the first respondent to give his\npersonal surety in Rs.100 together with one\nanother surety in Rs.100 that he will be of good\nbehaviour for one year. And I direct the remaining\nnine accused each to give his personal security in\nFifty rupees together with one other security in\nfifty rupees that they will be of good behaviour for\none year.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_157\">                                151</span><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_242\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_243\">              12.   The police prosecutor R.S. Talang\n       presented his case ably. It was also good of him\n       not to object the extreme latitude which I allowed\n       the first respondent both in examining his defence\n       witnesses and in speaking for nearly three fours\n       in his own defence.</p><pre id=\"pre_5\">\n\n\n       Dharwar\n       July 5th 1934                       (Sd) L.N. Brown\n                                           D. M Dharwar&quot;\n\n\n110.          Subsequently,    after    independence,       Sri   K.M.\n\nMunshi,    Agent-General      of   India    during    his    stay   at\n\n</pre><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_244\">Hyderabad had personally observed the activities of this\n\nDeendar Anjuman Organisation and its founder and made a\n\nrecord in his autobiography namely &quot;The End of an Era\n\n[Hyderabad Memoirs]&quot;, about the contents of the which book\n\njudicial notice can be taken according to <a href=\"/doc/1289712/\" id=\"a_98\">Section 57(13)</a> of\n\nIndian Evidence Act. The contents of pages 40 and 41 of the\n\nsaid book is extracted as below:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_153\">       &quot;The   Deendars,      however,      remained    active\n       proselytizers, though, by January, 1948, their\n       influence had become limited.\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_158\">                          152</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_154\">The head of this religious sect styled himself\n&#x27;Hazarat    Moulan      Siddiq   Deendar    Channa\nBasaveswar Qible&#x27;. He posed as the avatar of\nChanna Basaveswar, the ancient founder of the\nLingayat sect of the Hindus and claimed to have\nthe same divine marks on his body as that saint.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_155\">Four   of   Siddiq&#x27;s   lieutenants   also   declared\nthemselves to be the Hindus divinities, Vyas, Sri\nKrishna, Narasimha and Veerabhadra. Of course\nthe Nizam had a secure place in the pantheon;\nhe was Dharmaraja, the god of righteousness of\nthe Hindu scriptures.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_156\">Siddiq had his headquarters in Hyderabad. His\nfollowers, reckoned to be five hundred strong,\nhad no ostensible means of livelihood. They wore\nthe green turban of Muslim divines, the saffron\nrobe of Hindu sadhus and beard in the style of\nthe Sikhs. When the situation in a village grew\ntense, they led the Muslims against the Hindus.\nWhen they set out to loot the possessions of the\nHindus, they dressed as Razakars.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_157\">The exploits of Siddiq were reminiscent of a\nforgotten age. At one time he even started to\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_159\">                          153</span>\n\n\n\n\ncollect an army for the purpose of capturing\nHampi, the ruins of the capital of the vanished\nempire of Vijayanagar, to recover its buried\ntreasure.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_158\">His attacks on the Hindus were characterized by\nneither taste nor self-restraint. In the religious\nliterature   of   the   Deendars,      Siddiq   was\nrepresented as shooting lions, tigers, leopards\nand foxes, all of which had the shape of Sikhs,\nHindus, Christians and Lingayats. One of their\nbooks, Awanul-Nas, contained the following\nexhortation:-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_159\">My Muslim brethren! The Quran has taught you\nonly one thing: that is, to change the country in\nwhich you live into Pakistan; in other words, to\ncompel others to drink of the waters of the\nQuran-e-Masjid. A bowl containing one-quarter\nmilk and three-quarters dung cannot be called\nclean. Whether it is Arabistan, or Turkestan or\nAfghanistan, so long as it contains Kafiristan in\nits territory, it cannot be called Pakistan.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_160\">Siddiq   began    to    pursue   his   proselytizing\nactivities vigorously. He also declared a jihad [a\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_160\">                                154</span>\n\n\n\n\n       religious war] against the shrines of the Hindus\n       and issued a public appeal for one lakh\n       volunteers and a loan of Rs. 5,00,000 for the\n       purpose. No step was taken by the authorities\n       against him. The Hindus, thereupon, on January\n       10, 1932, submitted a petition to the Nizam to\n       check his activities. When the opposition became\n       too strong, the Nizam&#x27;s Government imposed\n       some kind of restriction on Siddiq&#x27;s activities, but\n       till 1948 he continued to function, through on a\n       very limited scale. The Nizam&#x27;s Government then\n       imposed some restriction on his activities.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_161\">       Because he claimed to be a reincarnated Hindu\n       saint, Siddiq was thoroughly disliked by the\n       fanatic Razvi, who also considered him a\n       potential   rival.   Meanwhile,      although    the\n       Razakars    looked    upon    the   Deendars    with\n       contempt, they tolerated them as convenient\n       allies for the terrorization of the Hindus.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_245\">111.         Therefore      from    the    aforesaid   undisputed\n\nmaterial, at an undisputed point of time, it is clear that the\n\nintention behind this organization and its movement is to\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_161\">                                155</span>\n\n\n\n\nIslamize the whole of India, to preach that Allah is the only\n\nGod and Hazarath Mohammad Paigamber is only the\n\nJagadguru and to make all the people who are inhibitants of\n\nIndia to accept this view and to achieve the said purpose,\n\nthey declared religious war.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_246\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_247\">112.        It is in this background, we have to appreciate\n\nthe legal and factual issues that arise for consideration in\n\nthese appeals.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_248\">\nLEGAL ISSUES\nRe: POINT No.1\nDOUBLE JEOPARDY\n\n(<a href=\"/doc/17858/\" id=\"a_99\">ARTICLE 20(2)</a> OF THE CONSTITUTION AND SECTION\n300(1) OF CR.P.C.)\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_249\">113.        Before framing the charges against accused in\n\nthis case, it was contended that they were already tried and\n\nconvicted for the offence of conspiracy by the Special Court\n\nat Hyderabad in S.C. No. 95/2001.        Therefore, the trial\n\nbefore the Special Court at Bangalore against the same\n\naccused for the same offence amounted to second trial which\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_162\">                              156</span>\n\n\n\n\nis hit by <a href=\"/doc/741791/\" id=\"a_100\">Section 300</a> Cr.P.C. on the principles of double\n\njeopardy.   After   considering   the   said   contention,   vide\n\nconsidered order dated 18.7.2005, the trial Court held that\n\nthe trial was not hit by <a href=\"/doc/741791/\" id=\"a_101\">Section 300</a> Cr.P.C in spite of the\n\nprevious trial conducted against the present accused and\n\nothers by the Special Court at Hyderabad. Notwithstanding\n\nthe said finding, this issue was again raised in the trial\n\nCourt at the time of arguments on merits and it was\n\nanswered in the negative by the Trial Court.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_250\">114.        Even in this appeal, the accused urged before us\n\nthat except A.13, others were accused were tried in\n\nS.C.No.95/01 on the file of the Additional Metropolitan\n\nSessions Judge for trial of Jubilee Hills car bomb blast,\n\nHyderabad for similar offences. After trial, these 16 accused\n\nwere convicted in that case and they have undergone\n\nimprisonment. Therefore they could not be tried again for the\n\nsame offence, i.e. the offence of conspiracy as it would offend\n\n<a href=\"/doc/17858/\" id=\"a_102\">Article 20(2)</a> of the Constitution of India read with <a href=\"/doc/279174/\" id=\"a_103\">Section\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_163\">                              157</span></a>\n\n\n\n\n<a href=\"/doc/741791/\" id=\"a_104\">300</a> of Cr.P.C. To answer this point, it is necessary to refer\n\nto that Hyderabad case.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_251\">\nHYDERABAD CASE\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_252\">115.          50   accused   were   charge   sheeted   in   SC\n\nNo.95/2001 on the file of the Additional Metropolitan\n\nSessions Judge for trial of Jubilee Hills Car Bomb Blast\n\nCase-Cum-Additional Family Court, Hyderabad, for the\n\noffences under <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_105\">Sections 120B</a>, <a href=\"/doc/1641007/\" id=\"a_106\">124A</a>, <a href=\"/doc/857209/\" id=\"a_107\">153(A)</a>, <a href=\"/doc/305995/\" id=\"a_108\">295</a>, <a href=\"/doc/305995/\" id=\"a_109\">295(A)</a>,\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1064219/\" id=\"a_110\">296</a>, <a href=\"/doc/455468/\" id=\"a_111\">307</a>, <a href=\"/doc/724142/\" id=\"a_112\">324</a>, <a href=\"/doc/1133601/\" id=\"a_113\">325</a>, <a href=\"/doc/1540253/\" id=\"a_114\">326</a>, <a href=\"/doc/1119707/\" id=\"a_115\">395</a>, <a href=\"/doc/1517318/\" id=\"a_116\">436</a> IPC and <a href=\"/doc/1056565/\" id=\"a_117\">Sections 3</a>, <a href=\"/doc/895566/\" id=\"a_118\">4</a>, <a href=\"/doc/1335368/\" id=\"a_119\">5</a>\n\nand <a href=\"/doc/1539301/\" id=\"a_120\">6</a> of Explosive Substances Act and Section 25(1-A) and\n\n<a href=\"/doc/244673/\" id=\"a_121\">Section 27</a> of Arms Act, of them sever accused were\n\nabsconding.    Therefore, only 43 accused were tried for the\n\naforesaid offences. In the said case as may as 30 charges\n\nwere framed.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_253\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_254\">116.          The judgment in the Hyderabad case discloses\n\nthat, since there were individual overt acts alleged against\n\nsome of the accused, charges under Sections 324, 336, 307,\n\n395 were framed and separate cases were registered and the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_164\">                                158</span>\n\n\n\n\nCourt opined that these aspects could be gone into in\n\nindividual case.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_255\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_256\">117.         Therefore, though 30 charges were framed in\n\nSC95/01 no finding was recorded in respect of 27 charges\n\nand only in respect of 3 charges finding was recorded and\n\nthe accused were found guilty in respect of only charges 1 to\n\n3, which are as under:\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_257\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_162\">   1. That you A-8 to A-20, A-24 to A29, A-31 to A-45\n       and   A-50     being   members     of   the   Deendar\n       Anjuman on or about i.e., from November, 1999 to\n       May, 2001 attended several secret meetings i.e.,\n       a meeting on 20-10-1999 at Deendar Anjuman\n       Office, Asifnagar, Hyderabad; a meeting on 23-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_258\">       11-1999 at Ashoka Lodge, Hyderabad; meetings\n       held in the flat of Hashim for 9 times from 24-11-\n       1999 and a meeting held on 27-5-2000 in the\n       house of Jaleel Chowdhary; a meeting on 24-5-\n       2000 in the house of Khaliq-us-Zama at Nuzvid; a\n       meeting in May, 2000 in the mango grove at\n       Nuzvid   and    a   meeting   in   D.A.R.     office   at\n       Vizianagrram and agreed with Saheb Jada Syed\n       Zia Ul Hasan, Syed Fayyaz U1 Hassan @ Khaleel\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_165\">                               159</span>\n\n\n\n\n     Pasha, Syed Shabihul Hassan @ Adil Hassan,\n     Syed Zahed Ul Hassan @ Zahid Pasha; Syed\n     Khalid Hassan @ Khalid Pasha, Syed Hiazul\n     Hassan @     Abed Pasha; Javed Pasha who are\n     residents of Pakistan and with Zakir, Siddiq &amp;\n     Moinuddin to do illegal acts and other acts, to wit,\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_259\">     1)   acquisition    of   explosive   substance;   2)\n     disturbing religious congregations; 3) descration\n     of places of worship by planting LEDS; 4) sending\n     threatening letters to Christian missionaries and\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_260\">     5) committing dacoities, etc., and did those acts in\n     pursuance of the agreement, to cause disaffection\n     towards the Government established by law and\n     thereby committed an offence punishable under\n     <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_122\">Sec.120-B</a> of I.P.C. within my cognizance.\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_261\">2.   That you A-47 being a member of Deendar\n     Anjuman            Organisation in the months of\n     February, 2000 and April, 2000 and May, 2000\n     agreed with the persons detailed in charge No.1\n     to do an illegal act viz., supply of explosive\n     substances and that you supplied explosive\n     substances to A-30, A-46, A-48 and A-49 on\n     number of occasions in February, 2000 and April\n     2000 in pursuance of the agreement to cause\n     disaffection towards the Government established\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_166\">                                 160</span>\n\n\n\n\n        by law and that you attended a secret meeting\n        on 16-1-2000 between 1400 hours and 1900\n        hours in the Mango Grove at Nuzvid and that\n        you thereby committed an offence punishable\n        under <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_123\">Section 120-B</a> of I.P.C. and within my\n        cognizance.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_262\">3.     That you A-30, A-46, A-48 and A-49 being\n        members of Deendar Anjuman Organization, in\n        the months of February, 2000 and April 2000\n        and May, 2000 and on other dates agreed with\n        the persons detailed in charge No.1 to do illegal\n        acts, viz, solicit, purchase, transport explosive\n        substances and that you solicited, purchased\n        and   transported   explosive   substances      from\n        Kodad in pursuance of an agreement to cause\n        disaffection towards the Government established\n        by law and that you thereby committed an\n        offence punishable under <a href=\"/doc/1128948/\" id=\"a_124\">Section 120(B)</a> I.P.C.\n        and within my cognizance.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_263\">118.          Evidence   was     adduced      on   behalf   of   the\n\nprosecution to prove those charges. All the accused filed a\n\ncommon statement under <a href=\"/doc/767287/\" id=\"a_125\">Section 313</a> Cr.P.C stating that\n\nthe    Deendar   Anjuman       Organisation    was    founded    by\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_167\">                                161</span>\n\n\n\n\nSiddique Deendar Chenna Basaveshwara, Hyderabad in the\n\nyear 1924 with more than 200 disciples. The main objects of\n\nthe organization are Ekho Jagadeeswara (Belief in the Unity\n\nof God), Ekho Jagadguru (Belief in the oneness of world\n\nteacher), Sarva Avathara Satyaha (Belief in all the prophets\n\nof the world), Sarva Dharma Grandha Satyaha (Belief in all\n\nthe   revealed   scriptures   of   the    world)      and   Samata   -\n\nSammelana Pradhana (Belief in oneness and unity of all\n\nhuman beings irrespective of their caste, creed, varna,\n\nreligion).   They   further   stated     that   His    Holiness   was\n\nconducting International Religious Conferences in the Head\n\nOffice at Asifnagar and he demised in the year 1952. They\n\nfurther contended that they were not responsible for the acts\n\nattributed against them. The attrocities were committed by\n\nthe Bajarangdal and Vishwa Hindu Parishat and their parent\n\norganizations like R.S.S. &amp; Shiva Sena, on the minorities in\n\nvarious parts of the country and the same was published in\n\nthe news papers. They did not adduce any defence evidence.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_168\">                                162</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_264\">119.           Charges 1 to 3 primarily were, entering into\n\nagreement to do illegal acts and other acts, such as\n\nacquisition of explosive substance, disturbing religious\n\ncongregations, desecration of places of worship by planting\n\nLEDS, sending threatening letters to Christian missionaries\n\nand committing dacoities, etc. The question was the accused\n\ndid those acts in pursuance of the agreement to cause\n\ndisaffection towards the Government established by law and\n\nthereby committed an offence punishable under Sec.120-B\n\nof I.P.C.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_265\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_266\">120.           Therefore, the Court framed the following points\n\nfor determination in the said case :-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_267\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_163\">   1.       Whether there is criminal conspiracy among\n            the accused to foment communal disharmony\n            and in that connection whether they have\n            committed any specific overt acts?\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_164\">   2.       Whether the accused maliciously insulted the\n            religious belief, caused disturbance to the\n            religious assemblies?\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_169\">                               163</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_165\">  3.      Whether the accused committed deliberate\n\n          and malicious acts with intent to insult the\n\n          religion or intending to outrage the religious\n\n          feelings of any class?\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_268\">121.         The learned Judge, after considering the entire\n\nevidence and argument held as under : -\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_269\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_166\">             &quot;44. Considering the voluminous evidence\n       let in by the prosecution and basing on the legal\n       propositions, I am satisfied that the prosecution is\n       able to establish the continuation of link and\n       strived to produce the evidence to the maximum\n       extent and gathered information to prove the\n       offence under Section 120B I.P.C.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_167\">             Though the accused were not connected,\n       the prosecution is able to prove the explosions\n       and attempt to make an explosion in various\n       places of worship and religious meetings.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_168\">             There are specific cases for all the offences\n       though my learned predecessor framed as many\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_170\">                                  164</span>\n\n\n\n\n       as 30 charges in this case they are linked with\n       individual cases. The evidence placed by the\n       prosecution is well considered in those cases to\n       arrive at a conclusion, whether the accused\n       arrayed in that case were guilty or not, therefore\n       separate findings on those issues in this case are\n       not warranted as provided under Sec.300 Cr.P.C.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_169\">       45.   In view of my above discussion in the\n       above paras, I am satisfied that the prosecution is\n       able to prove the charges 1 to 3 for the offence\n       under <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_126\">Sec.120-B</a> IPC against all the accused who\n       are tried in this case.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_170\">             Finding is not recorded in respect of other\n       charges as they are directly connected with other\n       cases.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_171\">       46.   In the result, I find that the accused are\n       found guilty for the offence punishable under\n       Sec.120 I.P.C and they are convicted under\n       Section 235(2) Cr.P.C.\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_270\">122.         Thereafter, on hearing the accused on the\n\nquestion of sentence it was held as under : -\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_171\">                                165</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_172\">       &quot;50. In the result, A.8 to A.20, A.24 to A.41,\n       A.43 to A.50 are found guilt for the offence under\n       <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_127\">Section 120-B</a> I.P.C. and they are accordingly\n       convicted and sentenced to under go Rigorous\n       Imprisonment for 6 months each.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_173\">       51.   The period of detention undergone by the\n       accused to be set off against the sentences of\n       imprisonment as required under Section 428\n       Cr.P.C.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_174\">       52.   Since all the accused have completed the\n       said period they are directed to be set at liberty\n       forthwith if they are no longer required in any\n       other case. M.O.2 shall be returned to P.W.122,\n       M.O.3 shall be returned to P.W.121. M.Os. 86, 87,\n       89, 98-101 shall be confiscated to the State.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_175\">             M.Os.6 to 8, 81, 88 and 141 shall be\n       confiscated to the State.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_271\">123.         The conspiracy which gave raise to the said case\n\narose out of meeting, which was held within Andhra Pradesh\n\nat various places, i.e., one held on 20-10-1999 at Deendar\n\nAnjuman Office, Asifnagar, Hyderabad; on 23-11-1999 at\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_172\">                              166</span>\n\n\n\n\nAshoka Lodge, Hyderabad; and those held in the flat of\n\nHashim for 9 times from 24-11-1999 and a meeting held on\n\n27-5-2000 in the house of Jaleel Chowdhary; a meeting on\n\n24-5-2000 in the house of Khaliq-uz-Zama at Nuzvid; a\n\nmeeting in May, 2000 in the mango grove at Nuzvid and a\n\nmeeting in D.A.R. office at Vizianagram. All those meetings\n\nrelate to the offence committed within the State of Andhra\n\nPradesh as is clear from the charges framed in the said case.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_272\">124.        The offence of criminal conspiracy under Section\n\n120A is a distinct offence.        The agreement in itself\n\nconstitutes the offence. The offence is of a technical nature.\n\nThe conspiracy to commit a crime and the crime itself are\n\ntwo distinct and different offences. A conspiracy is complete\n\nas soon as the agreement is made or as soon as there is\n\nmeeting of minds and unity of purpose between the\n\nconspirators to do that illegal act or legal act by illegal\n\nmeans. But it is not a thing of the moment. It does not end\n\nwith the making of the agreement. It will continue so long as\n\nthere are two or more parties to it intending to carry into\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_173\">                               167</span>\n\n\n\n\neffect the design. Its continuance is a threat to the society\n\nagainst which it was aimed at.       Criminal conspiracy may\n\ncome into existence, and may persist and will persist so long\n\nas the persons constituting the conspiracy remain in\n\nagreement and so long as they are acting in accord, in\n\nfurtherance of the objects for which they entered into the\n\nagreement.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_273\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_274\">125.         Conspiracy is itself a substantive offence and is\n\ndistinct from the offence to commit which the conspiracy is\n\nentered into. General conspiracy is distinct from number of\n\nseparate offences committed while executing the offence of\n\nconspiracy. Each act constitutes separate offence punishable\n\nindependently    of   the   conspiracy.   Conspiracy   may   be\n\nconsidered to be a march under a banner and a person may\n\njoin or drop out in the march without the necessity of a\n\nchange in the text of the banner. Where a well defined group\n\nconspires to commit multiple crimes so long as all these\n\ncrimes are the objects of the same agreement or continuous\n\nconspiratorial relationship the conspiracy continues to\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_174\">                                  168</span>\n\n\n\n\nsubsist though it was entered in the first instance.             A\n\nconspiracy thus is a continuing offence and continues to\n\nsubsist and committed whenever one of the consiprators\n\ndoes an act or series of acts . So long as its performance\n\ncontinues, it is a continuing offence till it is executed or\n\nrescinded or frustrated by choice or necessity.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_275\">126.          Section 120B prescribes the punishment for\n\ncriminal conspiracy.        It is in two parts.   The first part is\n\ncontained in sub-section (1) of 120B. It provides who ever is\n\na party to a criminal conspiracy to commit an offence\n\npunishable with death, imprisonment for life or rigorous\n\nimprisonment for a term of 2 years or upwards, shall where\n\nno express provision is made in this code for the punishment\n\nof such a conspiracy, be punished in the same manner as if\n\nhe had abetted such offence.           In other words, this sub-\n\nsection deals with criminal conspiracy to commit certain\n\ntypes of offences which is committed in pursuance of a\n\nconspiracy.    If, in Code, punishment for this conspiracy is\n\nexpressly     prescribed,    then   the   punishment     for   such\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_175\">                                 169</span>\n\n\n\n\nconspiracy is according to such express provision. If there is\n\nno express provision made in the Code for the punishment\n\nfor such a conspiracy, the accused shall be punished in the\n\nsame manner as if he had abetted such offence.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_276\">127.            Sub-section   (2)   provides   if   the   criminal\n\nconspiracy committed does not fall within sub-section (1) of\n\nSection 120B then the punishment shall be imprisonment of\n\neither description for a term not exceeding 6 months or with\n\nfine or both.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_277\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_278\">128.            Therefore, when a punishment is imposed under\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_128\">Section 120B</a> of IPC, it is necessary to find out whether any\n\nexpress provision is made in this Code for the punishment of\n\nsuch a conspiracy. Then the punishment to be imposed for\n\nsuch conspiracy is according to the said express provision.\n\nIn the absence of such express provision, it is to be found\n\nout what is the offence committed by the accused in\n\npursuance to the offence of Criminal conspiracy. Then it has\n\nto be found out whether such an offence is, punishable with\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_176\">                               170</span>\n\n\n\n\ndeath or imprisonment for life or rigourous imprisonment for\n\na term of two years or upwards.       If the accused is found\n\nguilty of such an offence, he is not only liable to be\n\nsentenced for the said offence, he is also liable to be\n\nsentenced for the Criminal Conspiracy to commit such\n\noffence for the same period prescribed under the law.\n\nHowever, when the offence committed by the accused in\n\npursuance of such Criminal Conspiracy does not fall within\n\nany of the aforesaid offences, then only he shall be punished\n\nwith imprisonment of either description for the term not\n\nexceeding six months.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_279\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_280\">129.         <a href=\"/doc/655638/\" id=\"a_129\">Article 20</a> of the Constitution of India prohibits\n\nprosecution of a person who has been punished for the same\n\noffences earlier. It reads as under : -\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_281\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_176\">       &quot;20. Protection in respect of conviction for\n       offences.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_177\">             (1) No person shall be convicted of any\n       offence except for violation of the law in force at\n       the time of the commission of the act charged as\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_177\">                               171</span>\n\n\n\n\n       an offence, nor be subjected to a penalty greater\n       than that which might have been inflicted under\n       the law in force at the time of the commission of\n       the offence.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_178\">             (2) No person shall be prosecuted and\n       punished for the same offence more than once.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_179\">             (3) No person accused of any offence shall\n       be compelled to be a witness against himself.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_282\">130.         <a href=\"/doc/17858/\" id=\"a_130\">Article 20(2)</a> protects a person from being\n\n&quot;prosecuted and punished for the same offence more than\n\nonce&quot;. The question has to be answered as to whether the\n\npetitioners had previously been prosecuted and punished for\n\nthe same offence for which they are now being prosecuted.\n\nHowever, Section 300 of Cr. P.C reads as under:-\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_180\">       &quot;300. Person once convicted or acquitted not\n       to be tried for same offence.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_181\">             (1) A person who has once been tried by a\n       Court of competent jurisdiction for an offence and\n       convicted or acquitted of such offence shall, while\n       such conviction or acquittal remains in force, not\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_178\">                         172</span>\n\n\n\n\nbe liable to be tried again for the same offence,\nnor on the same facts for any other offence for\nwhich a different charge from the one made\nagainst him might have been made under sub-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_283\">section (1) of section 221, or for which he might\nhave been convicted under sub- section (2)\nthereof.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_284\">      (2) A person acquitted or convicted of any\noffence may be afterwards tried, with the consent\nof the State Government, for any distinct offence\nfor which a separate charge might have been\nmade against him at the former trial under sub-\nsection (1) of section 220.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_285\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_286\">      (3) A person convicted of any offence\nconstituted by any act causing consequences\nwhich, together with such act, constituted a\ndifferent offence from that of which he was\nconvicted, may be afterwards tried for such last-\nmentioned offence, if the consequences had not\nhappened, or were not known to the Court to\nhave happened, at the time when he was\nconvicted.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_179\">                           173</span><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_287\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_288\">        (4) A person acquitted or convicted of any\noffence     constituted    by   any    acts   may,\nnotwithstanding such acquittal or conviction, be\nsubsequently charged with, and tried for, any\nother offence constituted by the same acts which\nhe may have committed if the Court by which he\nwas first tried was not competent to try the\noffence with which he is subsequently charged.\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_289\">        (5) A person discharged under section 258\nshall not be tried again for the same offence\nexcept with the consent of the Court by which he\nwas discharged or of any other Court to which\nthe first- mentioned Court is subordinate.\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_290\">        (6) Nothing in this section shall affect the\nprovisions of <a href=\"/doc/87754/\" id=\"a_131\">section 26</a> of the General Clauses\nAct, 1897 , (10 of 1897 ) or of section 188 of this\nCode.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_291\">Explanation.- The dismissal of a complaint, or the\ndischarge of the accused, is not an acquittal for\nthe purposes of this section.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_180\">                                 174</span><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_292\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_293\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"640825\" id=\"span_181\">131.         The Apex Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/640825/\" id=\"a_132\">KOLLA VEERA\n\nRAGHAV RAO Vs. GORANTLA VENKATESWARA RAO &amp;\n\nANOTHER</a> repo rted in AIR 2011 SC 641, dealing with\n\nthese provisions held as under:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_182\">       5.    It may be noticed that there is a difference\n       between the language used in <a href=\"/doc/17858/\" id=\"a_133\">Article 20(2)</a> of the\n       Constitution of India and <a href=\"/doc/1311723/\" id=\"a_134\">Section 300(1)</a> of\n       Cr.P.C.. <a href=\"/doc/17858/\" id=\"a_135\">Article 20(2)</a> states:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_183\">       &quot;no person shall be prosecuted and punished for\n       the same offence more than once.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_294\">       6.    On the other hand, <a href=\"/doc/1311723/\" id=\"a_136\">Section 300(1)</a> of Cr.P.C.\n       States: &quot;300. Person once convicted or acquitted\n       not to be tried for the same offence-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_295\">             (1) A person who has once been tried by a\n       Court of competent jurisdiction for an offence and\n       convicted or acquitted of such offence shall, while\n       such conviction or acquittal remains in force, not\n       be liable to be tried again for the same offence,\n       nor on the same facts for any other offence for\n       which a different charge from the one made\n       against him might have been made under sub-\n       section (1) of section 221 or for which he might\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_182\">                                   175</span>\n\n\n\n\n       have     been    convicted     under    sub-section   (2)\n       thereof.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_296\">       7.              Thus, it can be seen that <a href=\"/doc/1311723/\" id=\"a_137\">Section\n       300(1)</a> of Cr.P.C. is wider than <a href=\"/doc/17858/\" id=\"a_138\">Article 20(2)</a> of the\n       Constitution.     While,     <a href=\"/doc/17858/\" id=\"a_139\">Article    20(2)</a>    of   the\n       Constitution only states that &#x27;no one can be\n       prosecuted and punished for the same offence\n       more than once&#x27;, <a href=\"/doc/1311723/\" id=\"a_140\">Section 300(1)</a> of Cr.P.C. states\n       that no one can be tried and convicted for the\n       same offence or even for a different offence but on\n       the same facts.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_297\">       8.              In the present case, although the\n       offences are different but the facts are the same.\n       Hence,      <a href=\"/doc/1311723/\" id=\"a_141\">Section   300(1)</a>     of    Cr.P.C.   applies.\n       Consequently, the prosecution under <a href=\"/doc/1436241/\" id=\"a_142\">Section 420</a>,\n       IPC was barred by <a href=\"/doc/1311723/\" id=\"a_143\">Section 300(1)</a> of Cr.P.C.&quot;\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_298\">132.          Section 300 has further widened the protective\n\nwings by debarring a second trial against the same accused\n\non the same facts even for a different offence if a different\n\ncharge against him for such offence could have been made\n\nunder Section 221(1) of the Code, or he could have been\n\nconvicted for such other offence under Section 221(2) of the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_183\">                                     176</span>\n\n\n\n\nCode. In this context it is useful to extract <a href=\"/doc/1096701/\" id=\"a_144\">Section 221</a> of the\n\nCriminal Procedure Code.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_299\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_184\">       &quot;221. Where it is doubtful what offence has been\n       committed-(1) If a single act or series of acts is of\n       such a nature that it is doubtful which of several\n       offences   the    facts     which   can   be    proved    will\n       constitute, the accused may be charged with having\n       committed all or any of such offences, and any\n       number of such charges may be tried at once; or he\n       may be charged in the alternative with having\n       committed some one of the said offences.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_185\">       (2)     If in such a case the accused is charged with\n       one offence, and it appears in evidence that he\n       committed a different offence for which he might\n       have been charged under the provisions of sub-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_300\">       section (1), he may be convicted of the offence which\n       he is shown to have committed, although he was not\n       charged with it.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_301\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_302\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"211441\" id=\"span_184\">133.           The      Calcutta    High   Court      in   the   case   of\n\n<a href=\"/doc/211441/\" id=\"a_145\">JITENDRA NATH GUPTA AND OTHERS vs EMPEROR</a> [AIR\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_185\">                             177</span>\n\n\n\n\n1937 CAL 99] dealing with Section 403 of the old Code held\n\nas under:-</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_186\">     <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"1463622\" id=\"span_186\">&quot;The question as to whether a particular trial is\n     barred by reason of previous prosecution ending\n     in conviction or acquittal is a question to be\n     determined on the facts and circumstances of a\n     particular   case:   one     of   the   tests   the\n     Commissioners of the Tribunal in the case before\n     us have observed in their judgment is whether\n     facts are the same or not; but the true test as has\n     been said in cases decided by this Court is not so\n     much whether the facts are the same in both\n     trials as whether the acquittal or conviction from\n     the first charge necessarily involves an acquittal\n     or conviction on the second charge: see <a href=\"/doc/1463622/\" id=\"a_146\">Ram\n     Sahay Ram v. Emperor</a> AIR 1921 Cal 181. It is\n     necessary to indicate here that the provisions\n     contained in Section 403, Criminal P.C., are\n     complete by themselves on the subject of the\n     effect of previous acquittal or convictions and no\n     question of exercise of inherent jurisdiction or the\n     application of the rule of res judicata arises\n     where there are specific provisions in the law.\n     The four sub-sections of Section 403 deal with\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_187\">                          178</span>\n\n\n\n\nfour different kinds of cases: (1) the case of one\nset of facts or omissions constituting one legal\noffence; (2) the case of one series of facts\ninvolving more than one offence; (3) the case of\none set of facts constituting more than one legal\noffence; and (4) a special case where a single act\nor set of acts has had a consequence unknown or\nhaving occurred since the first trial. The law as\nenacted, complete by itself, deals with the maxim\nthat &#x27;no man ought to be tried twice upon the\nsame facts&#x27; which must mean that a person\ncannot be tried a second time for an offence\nwhich is involved in the offence with which he\nwas previously charged. Keeping the above\nconsideration in view it must be held in the case\nbefore us that the contention as to the non-\nmaintainability   of    the   prosecution   must   be\noverruled as wholly without substance. The facts\nin the present prosecution under <a href=\"/doc/1949191/\" id=\"a_147\">Section 121-A</a>,\nI.P.C.,   and   those    involved   in   the   earlier\nprosecutions are not the same; some of the facts\nare no doubt common, but on the facts of the\nprevious trials a charge under <a href=\"/doc/1949191/\" id=\"a_148\">Section 121-A</a>,\nI.P.C., could not possibly have been framed or a\ntrial held on that charge. As the Commissioners of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_188\">                               179</span>\n\n\n\n\n       the Tribunal have observed, the offences with\n       which the accused were charged in the earlier\n       trial were some of the limits, the present charge\n       under <a href=\"/doc/1949191/\" id=\"a_149\">Section 121-A</a>, I.P.C., includes them, but\n       goes much beyond them. The facts of this case\n       therefore attract the operation not of Sub-section\n       (1) but of Sub-section (2) of Section 403, Criminal\n       P.C. The offence charged in the present case was\n       a distinct offence and could not possibly be\n       covered by a charge under <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_150\">Section 120-B</a>, I.P.C.&quot;</span>\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_303\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"102020658\" id=\"span_189\">134.         Again in the case of <a href=\"/doc/102020658/\" id=\"a_151\">STATE vs RAM KANWAR</a>\n\nreported in 1984 Cri.L.J.958, it was held as under:-</span>\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_187\">           &quot;13. Giving my thoughtful consideration to\n       the relevant provisions of the law as also the\n       aforesaid various authorities and especially the\n       Supreme    Court&#x27;s authority in Ranchhod Lal&#x27;s\n       case (supra), I am of the view that the separate\n       charges are to be normally framed for each\n       offence of criminal breach of trust or dishonest\n       misappropriation of money or defalcation of\n       accounts as is the general rule enunciated in\n       <a href=\"/doc/889533/\" id=\"a_152\">Section 218</a> Criminal Procedure Code but by way\n       of an exception the provision contained in Sub-\n       section (2) of Section 212 Criminal Procedure\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_190\">                          180</span>\n\n\n\n\nCode may be resorted to by the prosecution so as\nto specify the gross sum of the various items of\ncriminal breach of trust, misappropriation of\nmoney or defalcation of accounts and also the\ntwo terminal dates between which those offences\nare alleged to have been committed, without\nspecifying the particular items or exact dates on\nwhich those offences were committed, and the\ncharge as framed in respect of all those items\nshall be deemed to be charge of one offence\nwithin the meaning of <a href=\"/doc/1402105/\" id=\"a_153\">Section 219</a> Criminal\nProcedure Code provided that the time intervening\nthe two terminal dates does not exceed one year,\nand this provision of law is an enabling one for\nthe prosecution and cannot be converted into a\ndisabling one.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_188\">    Further      more,   <a href=\"/doc/741791/\" id=\"a_154\">Section   300</a>   Criminal\nProcedure Code enunciating the English rule of\nautrefois acquit and authrefoid convict applicable\nto criminal trials to also not applicable when\nrecourse is had by the prosecution to the\naforesaid exceptional rule contemplated by Sub-\nsection (2) of Section 212 when challan or charge-\nsheet is filed by the prosecution against the\naccused person in respect of various items of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_191\">                           181</span>\n\n\n\n\nmisappropriations defalcation, trial held resulting\nin conviction or acquittal and subsequent charge-\nsheet/challan is filed in respect of other items of\nmisappropriation     occurring    during   the        same\nperiod of the two terminal dates or within the\nsame year to which the items of the previous\ncharge-sheet pertained Even the conviction in the\nsubsequent trial though for the items of the same\nperiod or of the same year .to which the items of\nthe earlier charge-sheet pertained, would not be\nillegal)\n\n\n     (16) The well-known maxima &#x27;nemo debet bis\nvexari pro eadem causa&quot; (no person should be\ntwice vexed for the same offence) embodies the\nwell established Common Law rule that no one\nshould be put to peril twice for the same offence.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_304\">The principle which is sought to be incorporated\ninto <a href=\"/doc/741791/\" id=\"a_155\">Section 300</a> of the Criminal Procedure Code is\nthat no man should be vexed with more than one\ntrial for offences arising out of identical acts\ncommitted by him. When an offence has already\nbeen the subject of judicial adjudication, whether\nit ended in acquittal or conviction, it is negation of\ncriminal   justice   to   allow   repetition     of    the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_192\">                                    182</span>\n\n\n\n\n        adjudication in a separate trial on the same set of\n        facts.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_305\">\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_306\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1432787\" id=\"span_193\">135.             Though <a href=\"/doc/17858/\" id=\"a_156\">Article 20(2)</a> of the Constitution of India\n\nembodies a protection against second trial after a conviction\n\nfor the same offence, the ambit of the sub-article is narrower\n\nthan the protection afforded by Section 300 of the Procedure\n\nCode.       It    is   held   by   the   Apex   Court   in   <a href=\"/doc/1432787/\" id=\"a_157\">Manipur\n\nAdministration v. Thokehom Bira Singh</a>, AIR 1965 SC\n\n87 that &quot;if there is no punishment for the offence as a result\n\nof the prosecution, <a href=\"/doc/17858/\" id=\"a_158\">Article 20(2)</a> has no application&quot;. While\n\nthe sub-article embodies the principle of autrefois convict,\n\n<a href=\"/doc/741791/\" id=\"a_159\">Section 300</a> of the Criminal Procedure Code combines both\n\nautrefois convict and autrefois acquit.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_307\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_308\">136.             What follows from the aforesaid decisions is, to\n\nattract <a href=\"/doc/741791/\" id=\"a_160\">Section 300</a> Cr.P.C, the facts of the present case and\n\nthe charges framed for the offences should be the same in\n\nthe previous case already tried against the accused.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_194\">                               183</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_309\">137.        The plea of autrefois acquit arises when a person\n\nis tried again on the same offence or on the same facts for\n\nany other offence under conditions attracting Section 221.\n\nThe protection afforded by these words, &quot;nor on the same\n\nfacts for any other offence extends to different offences only\n\nwhen they are based on the same facts and fall within\n\nSection 221. The true test is not so much whether the facts\n\nare the same in both trials as whether the acquittal on the\n\nfirst charge necessarily involves an acquittal on the second\n\ncharge.   The principle of autrefois acquit is not applicable\n\nwhen the section though is the same in both the trials, the\n\nfacts are wholly different.    The principle is that when a\n\nperson can be tried for several offences at one trial, but has\n\nbeen tried only for a few, he should not be tried again for the\n\nother offences for which he could have been tried before.\n\nWhen a person is charged with six offences in respect of two\n\ntransactions which under <a href=\"/doc/1402105/\" id=\"a_161\">Section 219</a> of Cr.P.C could not be\n\ntried at one trial and the case was split up into two, acquittal\n\nin one case does not bar the trial of the other. If the offences\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_195\">                               184</span>\n\n\n\n\nwere distinct, there is no question as to the rule of double\n\njeopardy. Two different trials for distinct offences arising out\n\nof different transactions, the common object being the same\n\nan acquittal in one case does not bar conviction in another.\n\n&#x27;Distinct offence&#x27; means an offence entirely unconnected with\n\nthe former charge. Section 300(2) is no bar to subsequent\n\ntrial on the offence of conspiracy, though committed in the\n\ncourse of the same transaction in which the offence of\n\nmurders and dacoities were committed, if not known to\n\nprosecution at the time of previous trial.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_310\">138.        The principle of res judicata or issue of estoppel\n\nis different from the principle of double jeopardy or autrefois\n\nacquit in <a href=\"/doc/741791/\" id=\"a_162\">Section 300</a> Cr.P.C.       Where an issue of fact has\n\nbeen tried by a competent Court and a finding has been\n\nreached in favour of an accused, it would constitute res\n\njudicata against the prosecution, not as a bar to the trial and\n\nconviction of the accused for a different or a distinct offence\n\nbut as precluding the reception of evidence to disturb the\n\nfinding of fact when the accused is tried subsequently even\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_196\">                              185</span>\n\n\n\n\nfor a different offence which might be permitted by the terms\n\nof Section 300.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_311\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_312\">139.        The charge of conspiracy which was the subject\n\nmatter of criminal case before the Hyderabad Court makes it\n\nclear, that all the meetings which were held were within\n\nAndhra Pradesh. Charge No.1 in the said case gives the date\n\nof the meetings and the place of the meetings. Para 45 of the\n\njudgment in Hyderabad case extracted above, makes it clear\n\nthat the accused were not convicted for any of the offences\n\ncommitted in pursuance of the Criminal conspiracy.       The\n\nreason being the said offences were the subject matter of\n\nvarious cases pending in the Hyderabad Court and therefore,\n\nno finding was recorded insofar as those offences are\n\nconcerned. But the accused were convicted for the offence of\n\nCriminal Conspiracy, which itself is an independent offence\n\nand awarded the sentence as prescribed under Sub-\n\nSection(2) of <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_163\">Section 120B</a> of IPC.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_197\">                               186</span><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_313\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_314\">140.           Therefore, in the instant case, no charges are\n\nframed in respect of the main conspiracy held in the\n\nDeendar Anjuman Ashram on 20.10.1999, whereas, the\n\ncharges framed in this case are in respect of the renewed\n\nand continued conspiracies entered into in the territory of\n\nState of Karnataka which were held in various cplaces in\n\nBangalore, Chikkaballapur, Batakurki, Kokatnur, Hubli and\n\nso many other places. The offence of conspiracy is a\n\ncontinuing offence and if any member of the conspiracy acts\n\nin furtherance of the continued and renewed conspiracy then\n\nall the members of the conspiracy will become responsible\n\nfor that continued and renewed conspiracy and it constitutes\n\na separate offence and they are required to be punished for\n\nsuch offence. The crime that is committed in Karnataka\n\nemanated from the conspiracy, which was hatched at the\n\nsaid places.    The accused and their associates are said to\n\nhave secured explosive substances, prepared the bombs,\n\nplanted them in the Churches at Hubli, Wadi, Bangalore and\n\nthey were attempting to plant in some other places, blasted\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_198\">                                 187</span>\n\n\n\n\nthem with an intention to create disharmony in between the\n\nmajor religions of India, derail the Indian economy, create\n\nlawlessness and thereby Islamise the whole India.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_315\">141.         Therefore, the charges framed in this case are\n\npertaining to the conspiracies and other offences like 120-B,\n\n121, 121-A, 124-A, 153-A, 295, 427, 436 <a href=\"/doc/1569253/\" id=\"a_164\">IPC</a> and also under\n\nthe    provisions   of   the   <a href=\"/doc/1568384/\" id=\"a_165\">Explosive   Substances   Act   and\n\nExplosives Act</a>.     All these charges pertain to the offences\n\ncommitted by the accused within the State of Karnataka.\n\nThe same is not the subject matter of charge No.1 to 3 in the\n\ncase before the Hyderabad Court. In Hyderabad the charge\n\nwas not under Section 120, 121, 121A. Charge was under\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_189\">Section 120B and other offences. Therefore, as the accused\n\nin this case were not tried for the same offence in the earlier\n\nproceedings, neither <a href=\"/doc/17858/\" id=\"a_166\">Article 20 (2)</a> of the Constitution of\n\nIndia nor <a href=\"/doc/741791/\" id=\"a_167\">Section 300</a> of Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 is\n\nattracted to the facts of this case.   Therefore, the trial Court\n\nrightly held the trial in this case is not hit by the aforesaid\n\nprovision.\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_199\">                             188</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_190\">Re: POINT No.2 - SANCTION\n(SECTION 196 OF CR.P.C)\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_316\">142.        The accused were prosecuted for offences under\n\nSections 124A, 153A, 295, 436, 427 read with <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_168\">Section 120B</a>\n\nof IPC and also   under <a href=\"/doc/1056565/\" id=\"a_169\">Sections 3</a>, <a href=\"/doc/895566/\" id=\"a_170\">4</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1335368/\" id=\"a_171\">5</a> of the Indian\n\nExplosive Substance Act, 1908 as well as under <a href=\"/doc/1335368/\" id=\"a_172\">Section 5</a>\n\nread with <a href=\"/doc/176528/\" id=\"a_173\">Section 9B</a> of the Explosive      Act, 1884.    To\n\nprosecute an accused for an offence under Sections 153A\n\nfor criminal conspiracy, to commit such offence, previous\n\nsanction of the Central Government or of the State\n\nGovernment is necessary without which no Court shall take\n\ncognizance of such offence. Therefore, on 12.3.2001 PW-57\n\nthe IO submitted a request letter to IGP, COD, Bangalore, to\n\naccord sanction to prosecute 29 accused for the offences\n\nunder Sections 153A, 120B and 124A as required under\n\n<a href=\"/doc/967463/\" id=\"a_174\">Section 196</a> of Cr.P.C. Along with the said request a copy of\n\nthe FIR, mahazars, FSL reports and other connected records\n\nwere submitted to accord sanction to prosecute the accused\n\nin Crime No. 113/2000 of J.J. Nagar Police Station. After\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_200\">                                  189</span>\n\n\n\n\nexamining the records, the Government accorded sanction\n\nfor prosecution of the accused under <a href=\"/doc/967463/\" id=\"a_175\">Section 196</a> of Cr.P.C\n\nvide Order bearing OE27 MOH IB/2001 dated 17.3.2001\n\nwhich is marked as Ex.P61.             The list of the accused for\n\nwhose prosecution, the sanction was accorded was also\n\nenclosed. It is marked as Ex.P62.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_317\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_318\">143.        Ex.P61       bears     the       signature     of     one\n\nV.G.A.Ashirvadam, Under Secretary to the Government of\n\nKarnataka, Department of Home and Transport. A perusal\n\nof Ex.P61 discloses that the sanctioning authority took note\n\nof the fact that, on 9.7.2000 at 10.15 PM a bomb exploded in\n\nSt. Peter&#x27;s Pal Church situated in J.J. Nagar main Road.\n\nThe Priest of the Church, Father Tomeyar, lodged a\n\ncomplaint   with   the   jurisdictional      police.     The    police\n\nregistered a case against 29 accused in Crime No. 113/2000\n\nfor offences under <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_176\">Sections 120-B</a>, <a href=\"/doc/1641007/\" id=\"a_177\">124-A</a>, <a href=\"/doc/345634/\" id=\"a_178\">153-A</a>, <a href=\"/doc/305995/\" id=\"a_179\">295</a>, <a href=\"/doc/1517318/\" id=\"a_180\">436</a>,\n\n<a href=\"/doc/222396/\" id=\"a_181\">427</a> IPC and <a href=\"/doc/1056565/\" id=\"a_182\">Section 3</a> and <a href=\"/doc/895566/\" id=\"a_183\">4</a> of Explosive Substances Act,\n\n1908 and <a href=\"/doc/383323/\" id=\"a_184\">Sections 5</a> and <a href=\"/doc/535894/\" id=\"a_185\">9(B)</a> of Explosives Act, 1884. As\n\noffence was under <a href=\"/doc/345634/\" id=\"a_186\">Section 153A</a> IPC, prior sanction of the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_201\">                                190</span>\n\n\n\n\nGovernment was necessary before the case was lodged in the\n\nCourt by virtue of <a href=\"/doc/967463/\" id=\"a_187\">Section 196</a> of Cr.P.C.          Therefore, a\n\nrequest was made. After considering the aforesaid material\n\nsanction was accorded to file a charge sheet against the 29\n\naccused, whose names were listed and enclosed to the said\n\nSanction Order.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_319\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_320\">144.         The   aforesaid    V.G.A.Ashirvadam     was    also\n\nexamined in the case as PW43.        He has deposed that he\n\nserved as Under Secretary in the Government of Karnataka,\n\nDepartment of Home and Transport from 31.5.1996 to June\n\n2003. On 12.3.2001 his department received a requisition\n\nfrom DGP, COD, Bangalore, to accord sanction to prosecute\n\nthe accused in Crime No. 113/2000 of J.J. Nagar Police\n\nStation.   Along with the requisition, the DGP, COD,\n\nBangalore, sent FIR, Mahazars, FSL report and other\n\nconnected records.    After examination of the records, the\n\nGovernment accorded sanction for prosecution of the\n\naccused under <a href=\"/doc/967463/\" id=\"a_188\">Section 196</a> Cr.P.C. The stencil portion of the\n\nGovernment    Order   dated    17.3.2001,   which   bears   his\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_202\">                               191</span>\n\n\n\n\nsignature was marked as Ex.P61 and his signature was\n\nmarked as Ex.P61A. The list of the accused enclosed with\n\nEx.P61 was marked as Ex.P62 and his signature was\n\nmarked as Ex.P62A. He was cross-examined. The tenor of\n\nthe entire cross-examination is to the effect that though he is\n\nthe sanctioning authority, he has accorded sanction as per\n\nthe directions issued by the higher authorities and he has\n\nnot applied his mind independently and, therefore, the\n\nsanction is vitiated.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_321\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_322\">145.         As rightly pointed out by the trial Court, the\n\nsanctioning authority is the Government of Karnataka and\n\nnot the Under Secretary.        Therefore, the entire cross-\n\nexamination is not tenable.    We find full force in the said\n\nobservations of the learned trial Judge.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_323\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_324\">146.         It was contended that the said sanction was\n\nissued without looking into the entire facts of the case,\n\nwhich were not placed before the sanctioning authority and,\n\ntherefore, it is vitiated. Ex.P-245 was not placed before the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_203\">                             192</span>\n\n\n\n\nsanctioning authority before the sanction was accorded. In\n\nthe absence of Ex.P-245, there cannot be any sanction for\n\nprosecution under Sections 121 and 121-A. By filing an\n\napplication under Section 260, the additional charges were\n\nframed under Sections 121 and 121-A. Therefore prior\n\nsanction to prosecute the accused for these offences cannot\n\nbe inferred from the sanction order which is produced in the\n\ncase. In other words, there is no valid sanction to prosecute\n\nthe accused in respect of the offences under <a href=\"/doc/786750/\" id=\"a_189\">Section 121</a> and\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1949191/\" id=\"a_190\">121-A</a> of IPC. Ex.P61 is the sanction.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_325\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_326\">147.        The argument is that the original order of\n\nsanction is not produced and only a stencil copy is produced\n\nwithout laying foundation for adducing the        secondary\n\nevidence. The requirement of law is to produce the original\n\nsanction order   in a criminal trial, whether the accused\n\nobjects or not the document sought to be produced should\n\nbe admissible in evidence. Even if at the time of production,\n\nobjection is not raised, it could be raised at a subsequent\n\nstage including the appellate stage. The author of sanction\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_204\">                              193</span>\n\n\n\n\nis not examined. Therefore the sanction order ought to have\n\nbeen held as not proved.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_327\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_328\">148.          The tenor of the entire cross-examination is to\n\nthe effect that though they are the sanctioning authority,\n\nthey have accorded sanction as per the directions issued by\n\nthe higher authorities and have not applied their minds\n\nindependently and, therefore, the sanction is vitiated.      As\n\nrightly pointed out by the trial Court, the sanctioning\n\nauthority is the Government of Karnataka and not the Under\n\nSecretary.    Therefore, the entire cross-examination is not\n\ntenable, and there is no substance in the said contention.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_329\">149.          It was contended that the said sanction was\n\nissued without looking into the entire facts of the case,\n\nwhich were not placed before the sanctioning authority and,\n\ntherefore, it is vitiated.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_330\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_331\">150.          It was further argued that a reading of the\n\nsanction accorded does not disclose any cognizable offence.\n\nThe prosecution has to prove firstly, that the sanction\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_205\">                              194</span>\n\n\n\n\ndiscloses the facts constituting the offence; and secondly,\n\nthat if the order does not disclose the facts constituting the\n\noffence, atleast the prosecution should adduce evidence\n\nshowing the material which investigating agency had placed\n\nbefore the Sanctioning Authority.     In the instant case, on\n\nboth the counts, the prosecution has to fail.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_332\">151.        In support of the said contention reliance was\n\nplaced on the following judgments.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_333\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_334\">       In GOKULCHAND DWARKADAS MORARKA -VS- THE\n\nKING reported in AIR (35) 1948 PRIVY COUNCIL 82, at\n\nparagraphs 9 and 10, it is held as under:\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_335\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_191\">            &quot;9.   Upon this state of the evidence, the\n       respondent has argued that the view which\n       has prevailed in the High Court of Bombay is\n       wrong and that a sanction which names the\n       person to be prosecuted and specifies the\n       provision of the order which he is alleged to\n       have contravened is a sufficient compliance\n       with cl.23 of the said Order. In their Lordships&#x27;\n       view, in order to comply with the provisions of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_206\">                          195</span>\n\n\n\n\ncl.23, it must be proved that the sanction was\ngiven in respect of the facts constituting the\noffence charged. It is plainly desirable that the\nfacts should be referred to on the face of the\nsanction, but this is not essential, since cl.23\ndoes not require the sanction to be in any\nparticular form, nor even to be in writing. But if\nthe facts constituting the offence charged are\nnot shown on the face of the sanction, the\nprosecution must prove by extraneous evidence\nthat   those facts      were   placed    before    the\nsanctioning    authority.       The     sanction    to\nprosecute is an important matter; it constitutes\na condition precedent to the institution of the\nprosecution and the Government have an\nabsolute discretion to grant or withhold their\nsanction.     They are not, as the High Court\nseems to have thought, concerned merely to\nsee that the evidence discloses a prima facie\ncase   against    the    person   sought     to    be\nprosecuted. They can refuse sanction on any\nground which commends itself to them, for\nexample, that on political or economic grounds\nthey regard a prosecution as inexpedient.\nLooked at as a matter of substance it is plain\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_207\">                          196</span>\n\n\n\n\nthat    the    Government      cannot    adequately\ndischarge the obligation of deciding whether to\ngive    or    withhold   a   sanction    without    a\nknowledge of the facts of the case. Nor, in their\nLordships&#x27; view, is a sanction given without\nreference to the facts constituting the offence,\na compliance with the actual terms of cl.23.\nUnder that clause sanction has to be given to a\nprosecution for the contravention of any of the\nprovisions of the Order. A person could not be\ncharged merely with the breach of a particular\nprovision of the Order; he must be charged\nwith the commission of certain acts which\nconstitute     a   breach,   and   it   is   to   that\nprosecution - that is, for having done acts\nwhich constitute a breach of the Order - that\nthe sanction is required. In the present case,\nthere is nothing on the face of the sanction, and\nno extraneous evidence, to show that the\nsanctioning authority knew the facts alleged to\nconstitute a breach of the Order, and the\nsanction is invalid.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_192\">       10.    Mr.Megaw for the respondent has\nsuggested that this view of the law would\ninvolve in every case that the Court would be\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_208\">                                     197</span>\n\n\n\n\n        bound       to   see    that      the   case    proved\n        corresponded exactly with the case for which\n        sanction had been given.           But this is not so.\n        The giving of sanction confers jurisdiction on\n        the Court to try the case and the Judge or\n        Magistrate having jurisdiction must try the\n        case in the ordinary way under the <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_191\">Code of\n        Criminal Procedure</a>.
