The charge need not\n        follow the exact terms of the sanction, though it\n        must not relate to an offence essentially\n        different from that to which the sanction\n        relates&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_336\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1315447\" id=\"span_209\">152.         In the case of            MODH. <a href=\"/doc/1315447/\" id=\"a_192\">IQBAL AHMED Vs.\n\nSTATE OF ANDHRA PRADESH</a> reported in AIR 1979 SC\n\n677, it is held as under:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_193\">       &quot;3.   A      perusal    of   the    Resolution   of   the\n       Sanctioning Authority clearly shows that no facts\n       on the basis of which the prosecution was to be\n       sanctioned against the appellant are mentioned\n       in the sanction nor does this document contain\n       any ground on which the satisfaction of the\n       Sanctioning Authority was based and its mind\n       applied. This document merely mentions that the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_210\">                            198</span>\n\n\n\n\nsanction has been given on the basis of a note of\nthe Commissioner, Municipal Corporation which\nappears    to   have      been    placed    before    the\nCommittee. It is obvious, therefore, that this note,\nif any, must have come into existence either on\n31-3-1969 or at any date prior to this. The\nprosecution     could     have    proved     the     facts\nconstituting the offence which were placed before\nthe Sanctioning Authority by producing the note\nat the trial. But no such thing has been done.\nWhat the prosecution did was merely to examine\ntwo witnesses P.Ws. 2 and 7. P.W. 2 has\nproduced the order implementing the Resolution\nof the Sanctioning Authority which is Exhibit\nP- 10 and is dated 21st April, 1969, that is to say\nafter the sanction was given. This document no\ndoubt contains the facts constituting the offence\nbut that does not solve the legal issues that arise\nin this case. It is incumbent on the prosecution to\nprove that a valid sanction has been granted by\nthe Sanctioning Authority after it was satisfied\nthat a case for sanction has been made out\nconstituting the offence. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1315447\" id=\"span_211\">This should be done in\ntwo ways; either (1) by producing the original\nsanction   which        itself   contains   the      facts\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_212\">                           199</span>\n\n\n\n\nconstituting the offence and the grounds of\nsatisfaction and (2) by adducing evidence aliunde\nto   show   that    the   facts   placed    before     the\nSanctioning Authority and the satisfaction arrived\nat by it. It is well settled that any case instituted\nwithout a proper sanction must fail because this\nbeing a manifest difficult (sic-defect) in the\nprosecution, the entire proceedings are rendered\nvoid ab initio. In the instant case no evidence has\nbeen led either primary or secondary to prove as\nto what were the contents of the note mentioned\nin Exhibit P-16 which was placed before the\nSanctioning Authority. The evidence of P.W. 2 or\nP.W. 7 is wholly irrelevant because they were not\nin a position to say as to what were the contents\nof the note which formed the subject matter of the\nsanction by the Standing Committee of the\nCorporation. The note <a href=\"/doc/1315447/\" id=\"a_193\">referred to above</a> was the\nonly primary evidence for this purpose. Mr. Rao\nvehemently argued that although the Resolution,\nExh. P-16 does not mention the facts, the Court\nshould presume the facts on the basis of the\nevidence    given   by    P.W.    2   and   the      order\nimplementing sanction which mentions these\nfacts. This argument is wholly untenable because\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_213\">                                   200</span>\n\n\n\n\n       what the Court has to see is whether or not the\n       Sanctioning Authority at the time of giving\n       sanction was aware of the facts constituting the\n       offence and applied its mind for the same and\n       any subsequent fact which may come into\n       existence after the resolution granting sanction\n       has been passed, is wholly irrelevant. The grant\n       of sanction is not an idle formality or an\n       acrimonious     exercise        but     a   solemn       and\n       sacrosanct     act     which    affords     protection      to\n       government           servants         against        frivolous\n       prosecutions    and      must    therefore      be    strictly\n       complied with before any prosecution can be\n       launched against the public servant concerned.&quot;</span>\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_337\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1774813\" id=\"span_214\">153.          The Apex Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/1774813/\" id=\"a_194\">NARBADA DEVI\n\nGUPTA Vs. BIRENDRA KUMAR JAISWAL AND ANOTHER</a>\n\nreported in AIR 2004 SC 175 has held as under:</span>\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_194\">       &quot;16.   Reliance is heavily placed on behalf of the\n       appellant on the case of Ramji Dayawala and\n       Sons (P) Ltd., (supra). The legal position is not in\n       dispute that mere production and marking of a\n       document as exhibit by the Court cannot be held\n       to be a due proof of its contents. Its execution has\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_215\">                               201</span>\n\n\n\n\n       to be proved by admissible evidence that is by the\n       &#x27;evidence of those persons who can vouchsafe for\n       the truth of the facts in issue&#x27;. The situation is,\n       however, different whether the documents are\n       produced, they are admitted by the opposite\n       party, signatures on them are also admitted and\n       they are marked thereafter as exhibits by the\n       Court. We find no force in the argument advanced\n       on behalf of the appellant that as the mark of\n       exhibits has been put on the back portions of the\n       rent receipts near the place where the admitted\n       signatures of     the plaintiff appear, the rent\n       receipts as a whole can not be treated as having\n       been exhibited as admitted documents.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_338\">154.         From the aforesaid judgments it is clear that the\n\ngrant of sanction is an executive act. The validity of sanction\n\ncannot be tested in the light of the principles applied to\n\nquasi judicial orders.     The test to be applied is, whether\n\nrelevant material that formed the basis of allegations\n\nconstituting the offence was placed before the sanctioning\n\nauthority and the same was perused before granting the\n\nsanction. A sanction which simply names the person to be\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_216\">                              202</span>\n\n\n\n\nprosecuted and specifies the provision of the order which he\n\nis alleged to have contravened is not a sufficient compliance.\n\nIn order to make it a valid sanction it must be proved that\n\nsanction was given in respect of the facts constituting the\n\noffence charged. It is desirable that the facts should be\n\nreferred to on the face of the sanction but this is not an\n\nessential requirement.   Similarly, law does not require a\n\nsanction to be in any particular form. But, if the facts\n\nconstituting the offence charged are not shown on the face of\n\nthe sanction, the prosecution must prove by extraneous\n\nevidence that those facts were placed before the sanctioning\n\nauthority. Sanction to prosecute is an important matter. It\n\nconstitutes a condition precedent to the institution of the\n\nprosecution. The Government has an absolute discretion to\n\ngrant or withhold their sanction.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_339\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_340\">155.        When we look into the facts of the case, in the\n\nbackground of the above legal principles, Ex.P61 refers to\n\nthe facts constituting the offence charged, the number of\n\npersons who are charged and the provisions of law under\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_217\">                              203</span>\n\n\n\n\nwhich they are charged. The evidence of PW43 shows that,\n\nalong with the request to accord sanction, FIR, mahazars,\n\nFSL report and other connected records were sent to the\n\nsanctioning authority.   His evidence further discloses that,\n\nafter examination of the said records and application of\n\nmind,   the    Government     has   accorded      sanction   for\n\nprosecution     and,   therefore,   the   legal     requirement\n\ncontemplated under law is fully satisfied.        Therefore, the\n\nsanction accorded is valid and legal and does not suffer from\n\nany infirmity as contended by the counsel for the appellants.\n\n\n\nRe: POINT No.3\n\nADDITIONAL CHARGE (SECTION 216 OF THE CODE)\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_341\">156.          It was contended that, the trial Court, after\n\nhearing the arguments of the parties, framed additional\n\ncharges for the offences under <a href=\"/doc/786750/\" id=\"a_195\">Section 121</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1949191/\" id=\"a_196\">121A</a> IPC.\n\nBefore the accused could be prosecuted for the said offences,\n\nprior sanction of the Government under <a href=\"/doc/967463/\" id=\"a_197\">Section 196</a> of the\n\nCr.P.C is a must. No fresh sanction having been obtained,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_218\">                                204</span>\n\n\n\n\nthe conviction of the accused under <a href=\"/doc/786750/\" id=\"a_198\">Section 121</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1949191/\" id=\"a_199\">121A</a>\n\nIPC is vitiated and is liable to be set aside.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_342\">157.         In this regard, it is useful to see what <a href=\"/doc/1172704/\" id=\"a_200\">Section\n\n216</a> of the Cr.P.C. provides for:\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_343\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_195\">       216. Court may alter charge.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_196\">       (1) Any Court may alter or add to any charge at\n       any time before judgment is pronounced.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_197\">       (2) Every such alteration or addition shall be read\n       and explained to the accused.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_198\">       (3) If the alteration or addition to a charge is such\n       that proceeding immediately with the trial is not\n       likely, in the opinion of the Court, to prejudice the\n       accused in his defence or the prosecutor in the\n       conduct of the case the Court may, in its\n       discretion, after such alteration or addition has\n       been made, proceed with the trial as if the altered\n       or added charge had been the original charge.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_199\">       (4) If the alteration or addition is such that\n       proceeding immediately with the trial is likely, in\n       the opinion of the Court to prejudice the accused\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_219\">                                205</span>\n\n\n\n\n       or the prosecutor as aforesaid, the Court may\n       either direct a new trial or adjourn the trial for\n       such period as may be necessary.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_200\">       (5) If the offence stated in the altered or added\n       charge is one for the prosecution of which\n       previous sanction is necessary, the case shall not\n       be   proceeded   with   until   such   sanction   is\n       obtained, unless sanction has been already\n       obtained for a prosecution on the same facts as\n       those on which the altered or added charge is\n       founded.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_344\">158.         Sub-section (5) of Section 216 provides that, if\n\nthe offence stated in the altered or added charge is one for the\n\nprosecution of which previous sanction is necessary, the case\n\nshall not be proceeded with until such sanction is obtained.\n\nTherefore, it is clear that the Court has the power to alter or\n\nadd any charge at any time before judgment is pronounced. If\n\nfor the offences which are the subject matter of such\n\nadditional charge sanction is required, until such sanction is\n\nobtained the Court shall not take cognizance of such offence\n\nand proceed with the matter.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_220\">                                  206</span><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_345\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_346\">159.        However,       the     above    provision     carries   an\n\nexception to this general rule. The exception is, if sanction\n\nhad already been obtained for a prosecution, on the same\n\nfacts as those on which the altered or added charge is\n\nfounded, then no fresh sanction is required for prosecuting\n\nthe accused for the offences which is the subject matter of\n\nthe additional charge.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_347\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_348\">160.        In   the     instant    case,   after   the   framing   of\n\nadditional charges for the offences under <a href=\"/doc/786750/\" id=\"a_201\">Section 121</a> and\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1949191/\" id=\"a_202\">121-A</a> IPC on 12.12.2007, they were read over and explained\n\nto the accused. The accused pleaded not guilty and claimed\n\nto be tried. The learned State Prosecutor submitted a memo\n\nstating that he had no additional, oral or documentary\n\nevidence to prove the additional charges framed and that the\n\nevidence already led is sufficient to prove the additional\n\ncharges.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_349\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_350\">161.        The learned defence counsel also did not choose\n\nto lead any evidence on the additional charges, but sought for\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_221\">                              207</span>\n\n\n\n\nrecalling of PWs 36, 42, 43, 54 and 64 and the said request\n\nwas granted. After those witnesses were recalled, they were\n\ncross-examined in the context of the additional charges. From\n\nthis, it is clear the additional charges were framed on the\n\nsame facts on which charges has been framed earlier. Since\n\nsanction had already been obtained for prosecution on the\n\nsame facts as those on which the additional charges were\n\nalso founded, no separate sanction was necessary in law and\n\ntherefore, we do not find any substance in the said\n\ncontention of the counsel for the appellants.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_351\">162.        In so far as non-production of the original\n\nsanction order is concerned, the document produced before\n\nthe Court shows that it is a stencil copy of the original\n\nsanction order.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_352\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_353\">163.        <a href=\"/doc/1248052/\" id=\"a_203\">Section 62</a> of the Evidence Act deals with\n\nprimary evidence. `Primary evidence&#x27; means the document\n\nitself produced for the inspection of the Court. Explanation\n\n(2) to <a href=\"/doc/1248052/\" id=\"a_204\">Section 62</a> of the Evidence Act provides that where a\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_222\">                              208</span>\n\n\n\n\nnumber of documents are all made by one uniform process,\n\nas in the case of printing, lithography, or photography, each\n\nis primary evidence of the contents of the rest; but, where\n\nthey are all copies of a common original, they are not\n\nprimary evidence of the contents of the original. Primary\n\ndocumentary evidence of a transaction is the document itself\n\nand it exists and obtainable, it should be produced in\n\noriginal to prove the terms of the contract. This is the best\n\nattainable evidence.    The existence of primary evidence\n\ngenerally excludes secondary evidence.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_354\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_355\">164.        Secondary    evidence   of   contents   of   written\n\ninstruments cannot be given unless there is some legal\n\nexcuse for non-production of the original. Where a number\n\nof documents are made by a uniform process, example,\n\nprinting, photography, cyclostyle or lithography, they are not\n\ncopies in the legal sense of the term. They are all counter\n\npart originals and each is primary evidence of the contents of\n\nthe rest, but only secondary evidence of the common\n\noriginal. In order to bring a case within explanation (2) of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_223\">                               209</span>\n\n\n\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1248052/\" id=\"a_205\">Section 62</a> of the Evidence Act, the whole document with the\n\nsignature must have been made by one uniform process.\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_356\">165.          <a href=\"/doc/1456410/\" id=\"a_206\">Section 63</a> of the Evidence Act deals with\n\nSecondary evidence.     Sub-Section (2) of Section 63 deals\n\nwith copies made from the original by mechanical process\n\nwhich in themselves ensure the accuaracy of the copy and\n\ncopies compared with such copies. The first portion refers to\n\nall copies made from the original by some mechanical\n\nprocesses which ensures their accuracy, example: copies by\n\nphotograpy,    lithography,   cyclostyle,   carban   and   etc.,\n\nmentioned in Explanation (2) to <a href=\"/doc/1248052/\" id=\"a_207\">Section 62</a> of the Evidence\n\nAct.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_357\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_358\">166.          In the instant case, after the Government\n\naccorded sanction to prosecute the accused and the said fact\n\nis to be communicated to various authorities. Ex.P.61 shows\n\nthat copies of the sanction order are sent to six authorities.\n\nFor this purpose they have prepared six copies of the\n\nsanction order by the process of stencil cutting, which also\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_224\">                               210</span>\n\n\n\n\nclearly discloses the signature of the sanctioning authority.\n\nIt is not the case of the accused that Ex.P.61 is not a copy\n\nmade from the original by a Mechanical process or that it\n\ndoes not ensure accuracy. Their case is, that the original is\n\nnot produced.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_359\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_360\">167.        During    the   examination-in-chief,    P.W.43   -\n\nV.J.A.Arshirwadam, whose signature is found on Ex.P.61\n\nhas deposed to the effect that the Government accorded\n\nsanction for prosecution of the accused under <a href=\"/doc/967463/\" id=\"a_208\">Section 196</a>\n\nCr.P.C. He identified the said Government order as well as\n\nhis signature thereon. It is through him Ex.P.61 is marked.\n\nIn the cross-exmination, the entire attention of the defence\n\nwas focused to demonstrate that sanction has been accorded\n\nwithout application of mind. Even there is no suggestion that\n\nEx.P.61 is not the original sanction order or that and it is not\n\na copy made from the original. Therefore, we do not find any\n\nmerit in the contention that Ex.P.61 is inadmissible in\n\nevidence for want of original sanction order or that there is\n\nno sanction order for prosecuting the accused.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_225\">                             211</span>\n\n\n\n\nRe: POINT No.4\nSANCTION UNDER THE EXPLOSIVE SUBSTANCES ACT,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_226\">1908</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_361\">168.        The accused are prosecuted for offences under\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1056565/\" id=\"a_209\">Sections 3</a>, <a href=\"/doc/895566/\" id=\"a_210\">4</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1335368/\" id=\"a_211\">5</a> of the Explosive Substances Act, 1908 as\n\nwell as under <a href=\"/doc/1335368/\" id=\"a_212\">Section 5</a> read with <a href=\"/doc/176528/\" id=\"a_213\">Section 9B</a> of the\n\nExplosives Act, 1884. <a href=\"/doc/1090977/\" id=\"a_214\">Section 7</a> of the Explosive Substances\n\nAct, 1908 mandates that no Court shall proceed to the trial\n\nof any person for an offence against this Act except with the\n\nconsent of the District Magistrate. It was contended on\n\nbehalf of the accused that though consent had been\n\nobtained, before granting such sanction the authority had\n\nnot applied its mind and therefore such a consent was void.\n\nThe prosecution has produced the original order passed by\n\nthe Police Commissioner       and the District Magistrate,\n\nBangalore, granting consent which is marked as Ex.P90. To\n\nprove the said document the prosecution has examined the\n\nPolice Commissioner and District Magistrate of Bangalore\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_227\">                              212</span>\n\n\n\n\nCity, Shri. T. Madiyal as PW55, who has deposed that he\n\nserved as a Police Commissioner in the Bangalore City from\n\nNovember 1999 till October 2001. At that time the power of\n\nDistrict Magistrate was conferred upon him. On 12.3.2001\n\nhe received a letter from IGP, COD along with the entire\n\nrecords in J.J.Nagar Police Station Crime No.113/2000 with\n\na request to accord sanction under <a href=\"/doc/1090977/\" id=\"a_215\">Section 7</a> of the Explosive\n\nSubstances Act, 1908 to prosecute accused Nos. 1 to 29 for\n\nthe offences under <a href=\"/doc/1056565/\" id=\"a_216\">Section 3</a> and <a href=\"/doc/895566/\" id=\"a_217\">4</a> of the Explosive\n\nSubstances Act, 1908 and also under <a href=\"/doc/1335368/\" id=\"a_218\">Section 5</a> read with\n\n<a href=\"/doc/176528/\" id=\"a_219\">Section 9B</a> of the Indian Explosives Act, 1884. He perused\n\nthe entire records in that case and he was satisfied from the\n\nmaterial on the record that they were sufficient to show that\n\nthe accused persons had committed the aforesaid offence.\n\nAccordingly by exercising the power conferred upon him\n\nunder <a href=\"/doc/1090977/\" id=\"a_220\">Section 7</a> of the Explosive Substances Act, 1908 he\n\naccorded sanction as per Ex.P90. He identified the signature\n\nin the said order as Ex.P90(a).\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_228\">                                 213</span><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_362\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_363\">169.        A bare perusal of Ex. P90 discloses that PW55\n\nafter perusal of the entire records sent by the Investigating\n\nOfficer, applied   his   mind   and   only on   being   satisfied\n\nthat the accused Nos.1 to 29 prima facie had committed the\n\nabove offence issued the sanction order. Once the order itself\n\nshows that the sanctioning authority has applied mind to\n\nthe material on record; entered the witness box and deposed\n\nto that effect, the requirements of law are completely\n\ncomplied with. Therefore, the contention that there was no\n\napplication of mind before according sanction, is without any\n\nsubstance and rightly the trial Court held that the aforesaid\n\norder is valid and legal and there is no infirmity in the said\n\nfinding.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_364\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_365\">170.        It should be borne in mind that the Court\n\nshould not attach much importance to the perception of\n\nfacts and law by the sanctioning authority. Perception varies\n\nfrom person to person. If the Court on perusal of the\n\nsanction order is satisfied that the legal requirements are\n\nbroadly complied with and no prejudice is caused to the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_229\">                               214</span>\n\n\n\n\naccused, it shall not   brushed aside, such sanction order.\n\nMinor discrepancies are bound to occur when variety of men\n\ndeal with matter.   Therefore, the finding of the Trial Court\n\nthat the Sanction accorded under the <a href=\"/doc/1568384/\" id=\"a_221\">Explosive Substance\n\nAct, 1908</a>, by the competent authority under the act is valid\n\nand legal and do not call for any interference.\n\n\n\nRe: POINT NO.5\n\nADMISSIBILITY         OF      EX.P-245       (CONFESSIONAL\nSTATEMENT OF A.7)\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_366\">171.        The next legal argument canvassed by the\n\nlearned counsel for the appellant-accused is that the\n\nconfessional statement of A.7, which is marked in the case\n\nas Ex.P.245 is inadmissible in evidence and the conviction\n\nbased by taking into consideration the alleged admissions in\n\nEx.P.245 vitiates the entire order of conviction.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_367\">172.        Admissibility of Ex.P245 is challenged on several\n\ngrounds. Firstly it is not a confessional statement. Secondly,\n\nit was obtained when A7 was in police custody. Thirdly,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_230\">                              215</span>\n\n\n\n\noriginal confession statement was not produced and Ex.P245\n\nis only a certified copy, produced and marked by the\n\nprosecution without laying any foundation for leading\n\nsecondary evidence. Fourthly, the requirements of <a href=\"/doc/497457/\" id=\"a_222\">Sec.164</a>\n\nCr.P.C. were not complied before recording the confession\n\nstatement and it was obtained under threat and by promise.\n\nEven if Ex.P245 were to be held an extra-judicial confession,\n\nit would be very weak evidence on the basis of which no\n\nconviction can be recorded. At any rate, Ex.P245 cannot be\n\nused to prove the guilt of co-accused.      Fifthly, confession\n\nstatement was not furnished to the accused along with the\n\ncharge sheet but produced before the Court during the\n\ncourse of trial and therefore could not be considered at all.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_368\">173.        Repelling the above arguments, the learned\n\nPublic Prosecutor places reliance on Sec.63 r/w <a href=\"/doc/487818/\" id=\"a_223\">Sec.65</a> of\n\nthe Evidence Act and submits that the original confession\n\nstatement was produced in S.C. No. 423/2001 and therefore\n\nits certified copy was produced. PW63 who spoke about this\n\ndocument was confronted with the original and the certified\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_231\">                               216</span>\n\n\n\n\ncopy thereof was marked as Ex.P245. No objection was\n\nraised by the accused at that time with regard to marking of\n\nEx.P245. The document in question is dated 31.08.2000 and\n\nit is in the handwriting of the A-7 who after being arrested at\n\nDelhi on 08.08.2000 was in police custody only till\n\n29.08.2000. While in judicial custody, A-7 wrote this letter\n\nand handed over the same to the Jailor- PW63. Since the\n\noriginal is produced in S.C.423/2001, its photo copy was\n\nforwarded to the learned Magistrate in Cr.No.290/2000 of\n\nMagadi Road Police Station. A-7, while answering Q.No.268\n\nof statement U/s 313 <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_224\">Cr.P.C</a>. relating to the confession has\n\nsimply denied the same. Therefore, it is too late in the day to\n\ncontend   either   that   being   a   photo   copy   Ex.P245   is\n\ninadmissible in evidence or that it is not proved in\n\naccordance with law.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_369\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_370\">174.        Before     determining     the    question   whether\n\nEx.P245 is admissible in evidence, it is necessary to know\n\nand understand the meaning of &#x27;Confession&#x27;, kinds of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_232\">                               217</span>\n\n\n\n\nConfession,    the   procedure    prescribed     for   recording   a\n\nconfession and the provisions relating to its admissibility.\n\n\nWHAT IS CONFESSION?\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_371\">175.          The    expression   &quot;confession&quot;    has    not   been\n\ndefined in the <a href=\"/doc/1953529/\" id=\"a_225\">Evidence Act</a>.        `Confessions&#x27; a terminology\n\nused in the criminal law is a species of &#x27;admissions&#x27; as\n\ndefined in <a href=\"/doc/430855/\" id=\"a_226\">Section 17</a> of the Indian Evidence Act. An\n\nadmission is a statement-oral or documentary which enables\n\nthe court to draw an inference as to any fact in issue or\n\nrelevant fact. It is trite to say that every confession must\n\nnecessarily be an admission, but, every admission does not\n\nnecessarily amount to a confession.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_372\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_373\">176.          <a href=\"/doc/430855/\" id=\"a_227\">Sections 17</a> to <a href=\"/doc/813048/\" id=\"a_228\">23</a> of the Indian Evidence Act deal\n\nwith admissions whereas the law as to confessions is\n\nembodied in Ss.24 to 30 thereof and Ss.162 and 164 of\n\n<a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_229\">Cr.P.C</a>. Broadly speaking, confession is an admission made\n\nat any time by a person charged with crime, stating or\n\nsuggesting an inference that he committed the crime. A\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_233\">                              218</span>\n\n\n\n\nconfession or an admission is evidence against its maker if\n\nits admissibility is not excluded by some provision of law.\n\nLaw is clear that a confession cannot be used against an\n\naccused person unless the Court is satisfied that it was\n\nvoluntary. At that stage, the question whether it is true or\n\nfalse does not arise.      If the facts and circumstances\n\nsurrounding the making of a confession appear to cast a\n\ndoubt on the voluntariness of the person making it, the\n\ncourt may refuse to act upon the confession, even if it\n\nadmissible in evidence.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_374\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_375\">177.        Whether a confession is voluntary or not is\n\nalways a question of fact. A free and voluntary confession\n\ndeserves highest credit, because it is presumed to flow from\n\na heightened sense of guilt. The rule may therefore, be stated\n\nthus: While the evidence in proof of making of confession is\n\nalways to be suspected, but once proved to have been made\n\nvoluntarily, confession is one of the most reliable evidence in\n\nlaw. <a href=\"/doc/494844/\" id=\"a_230\">Section 25</a> of the Evidence Act bars proof of a confession\n\nmade to a police officer. Section 26 goes a step further and\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_234\">                              219</span>\n\n\n\n\nprohibits proof of confession made by any person while he is\n\nin the custody of a police officer, unless it is made in the\n\nimmediate presence of a Magistrate.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_376\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_377\">178.        <a href=\"/doc/967059/\" id=\"a_231\">Section 24</a> of the Evidence Act lays down the\n\nobvious rule that if it appears to the court that the\n\nconfession was as a result of any inducement, threat or\n\npromise proceeding from a person in authority, it is liable to\n\nbe excluded from evidence. Here the word &#x27;appears&#x27; cannotes\n\nthat such inducement, threat or promise need not be proved\n\nto the hilt and if the facts and circumstances emerging from\n\nthe evidence make it reasonably probable that it was a result\n\nof threat, inducement or pressure, the court will refrain from\n\nacting on such confession, even if it was to a Magistrate or a\n\nperson other than police officer. Confession leading to\n\ndiscovery of a fact U/s 27 of the <a href=\"/doc/1953529/\" id=\"a_232\">Indian Evidence Act</a> is an\n\nexception to the rule of exclusion of confession made by an\n\naccused while in the custody of police. Section 30 provides\n\nfor consideration of a proved confession affecting the person\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_235\">                               220</span>\n\n\n\n\nmaking it as well as the co-accused. This is briefly the\n\nscheme of the law of evidence vis-a-vis confessions.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_378\">179.         <a href=\"/doc/523607/\" id=\"a_233\">Section 162</a> of Cr.P.C. prohibits the use of any\n\nstatement made by any person to a police officer in the\n\ncourse of investigation for any purpose at any enquiry or\n\ntrial in respect of any offence under investigation. However,\n\nit can be used to a limited extent to contradict a witness as\n\nprovided for by <a href=\"/doc/1110615/\" id=\"a_234\">Section 145</a> of the Evidence Act. Sub-section\n\n(2) of <a href=\"/doc/523607/\" id=\"a_235\">Section 162</a> Cr.P.C. makes it explicit that the embargo\n\nunder this provision shall not be deemed to apply to any\n\nstatement falling within clause (1) of Section 32 or to affect\n\nthe provisions of <a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_236\">Section 27</a> of the Evidence Act.\n\n\n\nVOLUNTARY CONFESSION\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_379\">180.         <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"92569\" id=\"span_236\">In <a href=\"/doc/92569/\" id=\"a_237\">BHARAT VS. STATE OF U.P.</a> reported in\n\n[1971 (3) SCC 950]:1972 SCC (CRI) 198. HIDAYATULLAH,\n\nC.J., speaking for a three-Judge Bench observed thus:</span>\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_201\">       &quot;7.   Confessions can be acted upon if the court\n       is satisfied that they are voluntary and that they\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_237\">                         221</span>\n\n\n\n\nare true. The voluntary nature of the confession\ndepends upon whether there was any threat,\ninducement or promise and its truth is judged in\nthe context of the entire prosecution case. The\nconfession must fit into the proved facts and not\nrun   counter   to   them.    When   the   voluntary\ncharacter of the confession and its truth are\naccepted, it is safe to rely on it. Indeed a\nconfession, if it is voluntary and true and not\nmade under any inducement or threat or promise,\nis the most patent piece of evidence against the\nmaker. Retracted confession, however, stands on\na slightly different footing. As the Privy Council\nonce stated, in India it is the rule to find a\nconfession and to find it retracted later. A court\nmay take into account the retracted confession,\nbut it must look for the reasons for the making of\nthe confession as well as for its retraction, and\nmust weigh the two to determine whether the\nretraction affects the voluntary nature of the\nconfession or not. If the court is satisfied that it\nwas retracted because of an after-thought or\nadvice, the retraction may not weigh with the\ncourt if the general facts proved in the case and\nthe tenor of the confession as made and the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_238\">                              222</span>\n\n\n\n\n    circumstances of its making and withdrawal\n    warrant its user. All the same, the courts do not\n    act upon the retracted confession without finding\n    assurance from some other sources as to the guilt\n    of the accused. Therefore, it can be stated that a\n    true confession made voluntarily may be acted\n    upon with slight evidence to corroborate it, but a\n    retracted   confession     requires     the    general\n    assurance that the retraction was an after-\n    thought and that the earlier statement was true.\n    This was laid down by this Court in an earlier\n    case reported in Subramania Gounden v. The\n    State of Madras (1958 SCR 428): 1958 Cri LJ\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_202\">    238.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_380\">JUDICIAL CONFESSION\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_381\">    164. Recording of confessions and statements:\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_203\">    (1)   Any   Metropolitan   Magistrate     or    Judicial\n    Magistrate may, whether or not he has jurisdiction\n    in the case, record any confession or statement\n    made to him in the course of an investigation under\n    this Chapter or under any other law for the time\n    being in force, or at any, time afterwards before the\n    commencement of the inquiry or trial:\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_239\">                          223</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_204\">      Provided    that   no    confession     shall   be\nrecorded by a police officer on whom any power\nof a Magistrate has been conferred under any\nlaw for the time being in force.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_205\">(2) The Magistrate shall, before recording any\nsuch confession, explain to the person making it\nthat he is not bound to make a confession and\nthat, if he does so, it may be used as evidence\nagainst him; and the Magistrate shall not record\nany such confession unless, upon questioning the\nperson making it, he has reason to believe that it\nis made voluntarily.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_206\">(3) If at any time before the confession is\nrecorded,   the   person      appearing     before    the\nMagistrate states that he is not willing to make\nthe confession, the Magistrate shall not authorize\nthe detention of such person in police custody.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_207\">(4) Any such confession shall be recorded in the\nmanner provided in section 281 for recording the\nexamination of an accused person and shall be\nsigned by the person making the confession; and\nthe Magistrate shall make a memorandum at the\nfoot of such record to the following effect.\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_240\">                        224</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_208\">&quot;I have explained to (name) that he is not bound\nto make a confession and that, if he does so, any\nconfession he may make may be used as\nevidence against him and I believe that this\nconfession was voluntarily made. It was taken in\nmy presence and hearing, and was read over to\nthe person making it and admitted by him to be\ncorrect, and it contains a full and true account of\nthe statement made by him.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_209\">                                     (Signed) A.B.\n                                      Magistrate&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_382\">(5) Any statement (other than a confession) made\nunder sub-section (1) shall be recorded in such\nmanner hereinafter provided for the recording of\nevidence as is in the opinion of the Magistrate,\nbest fitted to the circumstances of the case; and\nthe Magistrate shall have power to administer\noath to the person whose statement is so\nrecorded.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_383\">(5A)(a)   In cases punishable under Section 354,\nsection 354A, section 354B, section 354C, section\n354D, sub-section(1) or sub-section(2) of section\n376, section 376A, section 376B, section 376C,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_241\">                          225</span>\n\n\n\n\nsection 376D, section 376E or <a href=\"/doc/68146/\" id=\"a_238\">section 509</a> of the\nIndian Penal Code (45 of 1860), the Judicial\nMagistrate shall record the statement of the\npersond against whom such offence has been\ncommitted in the manner prescribed in sub-\nsection (5), as soon as the commission of the\noffence is brought to the notice of the police:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_210\">      Provided that if the person making the\nstatement is temporarily or permanently mentally\nor physically disabled, the Magistrate shall take\nthe assistance of an interpreter or a special\neducator in recording the statement:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_211\">      Provided that if the person making the\nstatement is temporarily or permanently mentally\nor physically disabled, the statement made by\nthe person, with the assistance of an interpreter\nor a special educator, shall be videographed;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_212\">(b) A statement recorded under clause (a) of a\nperson,    who   is   temporarily   or   permanently\nmentally    or   physically    disabled,   shall   be\nconsidered a statement in lieu of examination-in-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_384\">chief, as specified in <a href=\"/doc/1612728/\" id=\"a_239\">Section 137</a> of the Indian\nEvidence Act, 1872 (1 OF 1872) such that the\nmaker of the statement can be cross-examined on\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_242\">                                226</span>\n\n\n\n\n       such statement, without the need for recording\n       the same at the time of trial.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_385\">       (6) The Magistrate recording a confession or\n       statement under this section shall forward it to\n       the Magistrate by whom the case is to be inquired\n       into or tried\n\n\n\nEXTRA JUDICIAL CONFESSION\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_386\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"46798252\" id=\"span_243\">181.          Extra judicial confession is a confession made to\n\nany person other than the Magistrate. The questions as to\n\nwhat is an extra judicial confession and how it should be\n\nproved are dealt in the case of <a href=\"/doc/46798252/\" id=\"a_240\">ANNEPPA AND OTHERS Vs\n\nTHE STATE OF KARNATAKA</a> reported in 1978 CRL.L.J.\n\n462 in Para 16 as under:</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_387\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_213\">       &quot;Reliance is placed by the prosecution on the\n       extra judicial confession stated to have been\n       made to PW-33 Hanumanth Rao. Extra-judicial\n       confessions should be proved in the same way as\n       other admissions or statements by the evidence\n       of the persons to whom they were made. They\n       must be proved by evidence of the most reliable\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_244\">                                 227</span>\n\n\n\n\n       character. The infirmity is that in most cases\n       owing to the absence of any writing the exact\n       words used by the accused cannot be known and\n       the witness may have misunderstood or may not\n       have remembered. For this reason extra-judicial\n       confessions are not considered of much value\n       unless the witnesses can be depended upon. It is\n       unsafe to convict a person only on such evidence\n       without corroboration.     As a rule of caution,\n       Courts    usually     require     some     material\n       corroboration to an extra judicial confession&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_388\">182.         The Privy Council in the case of PAKALA\n\nNARAYANA SWAMI&#x27;s case cited supra, has explained the\n\nmeaning of confession as well as the exculpatory statements\n\nin the following words at page 52:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_214\">       &quot;.... no statement that contains self exculpatory\n       matter can amount to a confession, if the\n       exculpatory statement is of some fact which if\n       true would negative the offence alleged to be\n       confessed.   Moreover, a confession must either\n       admit in terms the offence, or at any rate\n       substantially all the facts which constitute the\n       offence. An admission of a gravely incriminating\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_245\">                            228</span>\n\n\n\n\nfact, even a conclusively incriminating fact is not\nof itself a confession, e.g. an admission that the\naccused is the owner of and was in recent\npossession of the knife or revolver which caused\na death with no explanation of any other man&#x27;s\npossession.     Some confusion appears to have\nbeen caused by the definition of &#x27;confession&#x27; in\n<a href=\"/doc/581566/\" id=\"a_241\">Art.22</a> of Stephen&#x27;s &quot;Digest of the Law of\nEvidence&quot; which defines a confession as a\nadmission made at any time by a person charged\nwith a crime stating or suggesting the inference\nthat he committed that crime. If the surrounding\narticles are examined it will be apparent that the\nlearned author after dealing with admissions\ngenerally is applying himself to admissions in\ncriminal cases, and for this purpose defines\nconfessions so as to cover all such admissions, in\norder to have a general term for use in the three\nfollowing     articles,    confession   secured   by\ninducement, made upon oath, made under a\npromise of      secrecy.     The definition   is not\ncontained in the <a href=\"/doc/1953529/\" id=\"a_242\">Evidence Act, 1872</a>: and in that\nAct it would not be consistent with the natural\nuse of language to construe confession as a\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_246\">                                 229</span>\n\n\n\n\n       statement    by   an     accused    &quot;suggesting   the\n       inference that he committed&quot; the crime.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_215\">RETRACTED CONFESSION\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_389\">183.          A four-Judge Bench of the Apex Court speaking\n\nthrough Subba Rao J, in case of PYARE LAL BHARBAVA Vs\n\nSTATE OF RAJASTHAN reported in (1963 SUPP (1) SCR\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_390\">689), has observed thus:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_216\">       &quot;A retracted confession may form the legal basis\n       of a conviction if the court is satisfied that it was\n       true and was voluntarily made. But it has been\n       held that a court shall not base a conviction on\n       such a confession without corroboration. It is not\n       a rule of law, but is only rule of prudence. It\n       cannot even be laid down as an inflexible rule of\n       practice or prudence that under no circumstances\n       such   a    conviction   can   be    made    without\n       corroboration, for a court may, in a particular\n       case, be convinced of the absolute truth of a\n       confession and prepared to act upon it without\n       corroboration; but it may be laid down as a\n       general rule of practice that it is unsafe to rely\n       upon a confession, much less on a retracted\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_247\">                                  230</span>\n\n\n\n\n       confession, unless the court is satisfied that the\n       retracted confession is true and voluntarily made\n       and     has     been    corroborated    in    material\n       particulars.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_391\">PROBATIVE VALUE OF CONFESSION\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_392\">184.           Blackston describes confession as the weakest\n\nand most suspicious of all evidence. In para 866 of\n\nWIGMORE ON EVIDENCE, 3rd EDN., it is noted thus:\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_217\">          &quot;Now, assuming the making of a confession\n          to    be     a   completely    proved     fact-its\n          authenticity        beyond     question      and\n          conceded.--then it is certainly true that we\n          have before us the highest sort of evidence.\n          The confession of crime is usually as much\n          against a man&#x27;s permanent interests as\n          anything well can be; and, in Mr.Starkie&#x27;s\n          phrase, no innocent man can be supposed\n          ordinarily to be willing to risk life, liberty, or\n          property by a false confession.       Assuming\n          the confession as an undoubted fact, it\n          carries a persuasion which nothing else\n          does, because a fundamental instinct of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_248\">                               231</span>\n\n\n\n\n          human nature teaches each one of us its\n          significance.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_393\">                                    (Emphasis supplied)\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_394\">185.         Similar view is expressed in the treatise on LAW\n\nOF EVIDENCE by TAYLOR, VOL.I, 12th EDN., in para 865:\n\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_218\">       &quot;Indeed, all reflecting men are now generally\n       agreed that, deliberate and voluntary confessions\n       of guilt, if clearly proved, are among the most\n       effectual proofs in the law, their value depending\n       on the sound presumption that a rational being\n       will not make admissions prejudicial to his\n       interest and safety, unless when urged by the\n       promptings of truth and conscience.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_395\">186.         Noticing the conflicting views, Chief Justice\n\nM.Monior in PRINCIPLES AND DIGEST OF THE LAW OF\n\nEVIDENCE, VOL.I, NEW EDN., has discussed various\n\nauthorities and stated the rule as follows:\n\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_219\">       &quot;The rule may, therefore, be stated to be that\n       whereas the evidence in proof of a confession\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_249\">                                232</span>\n\n\n\n\n       having been made is always to be suspected, the\n       confession, if once proved to have been made and\n       made voluntarily, is one of the most effectual\n       proofs in the law.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_396\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"516808\" id=\"span_250\">187.         LORD      ATKIN       in   the   case    of   <a href=\"/doc/516808/\" id=\"a_243\">PAKALA\n\nNARAYANA SWAMI Vs EMPEROR</a> [AIR 1939 PC 47]\n\nelucidated the meaning and purport of the expression\n\n&#x27;confession&#x27; at page 52 thereof in the following words:</span>\n\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_220\">       &quot;A Confession must either admit in terms the\n       offence, or at any rate substantially all the facts\n       which constitute the offence. An admission of a\n       gravely incriminating fact, even a conclusively\n       incriminating fact is not of itself a confession.....&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_397\">       29. Confessions are considered highly reliable\n       because    no    rational    person    would    make\n       admission against his interest unless prompted\n       by his conscience to tell the truth. &quot;Deliberate and\n       voluntary confessions of guilt, if clearly proved\n       are among the most effectual proofs in law&quot;. (vide\n       Taylor&#x27;s Treatise on the Law of Evidence Vol. I).\n       However, before acting upon a confession the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_251\">                        233</span>\n\n\n\n\ncourt must be satisfied that it was freely and\nvoluntarily made. A confession by hope or\npromise of advantage, reward or immunity or by\nforce or by fear induced by violence or threats of\nviolence cannot constitute evidence against the\nmaker of confession. The confession should have\nbeen made with full knowledge of the nature and\nconsequences of the confession. If any reasonable\ndoubt is entertained by the court that these\ningredients are not satisfied, the court should\neschew the confession from consideration. So also\nthe authority recording the confession, be it a\nMagistrate or some other statutory functionary at\nthe pre-trial stage, must address himself to the\nissue whether the accused has come forward to\nmake the confession in an atmosphere free from\nfear, duress or hope of some advantage or\nreward induced by the persons in authority.\nRecognizing the stark reality of the accused being\nenveloped in a state of fear and panic, anxiety\nand despair while in police custody, the <a href=\"/doc/1953529/\" id=\"a_244\">Indian\nEvidence Act</a> has excluded the admissibility of a\nconfession made to the police officer.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_398\">30. <a href=\"/doc/497457/\" id=\"a_245\">Section 164</a> of Cr.P.C. is a salutary provision\nwhich lays down certain precautionary rules to\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_252\">                                234</span>\n\n\n\n\n       be followed by the Magistrate recording            a\n       confession so as to ensure the voluntariness of\n       the confession and the accused being placed in a\n       situation free from threat or influence of the\n       police.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_399\">\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_400\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"153998550\" id=\"span_253\">188.            The Apex Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/153998550/\" id=\"a_246\">SARWAN SINGH\n\nRATTAN SINGH Vs STATE OF PUNJAB</a>, reported in AIR\n\n1957 SC 637 (at p.643) has observed that:-</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_401\">\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_221\">       &quot;In law it is always open to the court to convict an\n       accused on his confession itself though he has\n       retracted it at a later stage. Nevertheless usually\n       courts     require   some   corroboration   to    the\n       confessional    statement   before   convicting   an\n       accused person on such statement. What amount\n       of corroboration would be necessary in such a\n       case would always be a question of fact to be\n       determined in the light of the circumstances of\n       each case.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_402\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"194120\" id=\"span_254\">189.            The Apex Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/194120/\" id=\"a_247\">STATE OF TAMIL\n\nNADU Vs S.NALINI AND OTHERS</a> reported in AIR 1999 SC\n\n2640 has held that:</span>\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_255\">                                    235</span><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_403\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_222\">        671. .... Thus, plea of guilty by an accused at the\n        commencement of the trial or in his statement\n        under <a href=\"/doc/767287/\" id=\"a_248\">Section 313</a>, Cr.P.C. will not be substantive\n        evidence     but   extra   judicial   confession    and\n        confession    recorded     by    a Magistrate      under\n        <a href=\"/doc/497457/\" id=\"a_249\">Section 164</a>, Cr.P.C. of an accused will be\n        substantive evidence. So also a confession of a\n        person recorded under <a href=\"/doc/187258181/\" id=\"a_250\">Section 15</a> of the TADA\n        Act.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_404\">190.           <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"39506072\" id=\"span_256\">In the case of <a href=\"/doc/39506072/\" id=\"a_251\">DEVENDER PAL SINGH Vs\n\nSTATE OF NCT OF DELHI AND ANOTHER</a> reported in 2002\n\nSCC (Cri) 978, it is observed as under:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_223\">       31. xxxx So the crux of making a statement\n       voluntarily   is,   what    is    intentional,   intended,\n       unimpelled by other influences, acting on one&#x27;s own\n       will, through his own conscience. Such confessional\n       statements are made mostly out of a thirst to speak\n       the truth which at a given time predominates in the\n       heart of the confessor which impels him to speak out\n       the truth.    Internal compulsion of the conscience\n       perilous situation when he wants to shed his cloak\n       of guilt and nothing but disclosing the truth would\n       dawn on him.        It sometimes becomes so powerful\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_257\">                                    236</span>\n\n\n\n\n       that he is ready to face all consequences for clearing\n       his heart.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_405\">CORROBORATION\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_406\">191.           As to the extent of corroboration required, it was\n\nobserved in SUBRAMANIA GOUNDEN&#x27;S CASE reported in\n\n(1958     SCR        428)   that   each   and   every    circumstance\n\nmentioned       in    the   retracted     confession    regarding   the\n\ncomplicity of the maker need not be separately and\n\nindependently corroborated. This is what the Court observed\n\nat pp.440-41 of <a href=\"/doc/39506072/\" id=\"a_252\">the said judgment</a>:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_224\">        &quot;It would be sufficient, in our opinion, that the\n        general trend of the confession is substantiated\n        by some evidence which would tally with what is\n        contained in the confession&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_407\">        <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"677948\" id=\"span_258\"><a href=\"/doc/677948/\" id=\"a_253\">SHANKARIA Vs STATE OF RAJASTHAN</a> reported in\n\n(1978) 3 SCC 435 was decided by a three Judge Bench of\n\nthe Apex Court and Sarkaria J, noted the twin tests to be\n\napplied to evaluate a confession as under:</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_259\">                                  237</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_225\">       (1)    Whether the        confession    was    perfectly\n              voluntary?\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_226\">       (2)    If so, whether it is true and trustworthy?\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_408\">192.          While pointing out that if the first test is not\n\nsatisfied the question of applying the second test does not\n\narise, the Court indicated a broad method for evaluation of\n\nconfession in the following terms:\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_409\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_227\">       &quot;The   Court     should     carefully      examine   the\n       confession and compare it with the rest of the\n       evidence,   in   the   light    of   the    surrounding\n       circumstances and probabilities of the case. If on\n       such examination and comparison, the confession\n       appears to be a probable catalogue of events and\n       naturally fits in with the rest of the evidence and\n       the surrounding circumstances, it may be taken\n       to have satisfied the second test.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_410\">193.          <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"684504\" id=\"span_260\">In <a href=\"/doc/684504/\" id=\"a_254\">PARMANANDA PEGU Vs STATE OF ASSAM</a>\n\nreported in (2004) 7 SCC 779 the Apex Court while\n\nadverting to the expression &quot;corroboration of material\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_261\">                               238</span>\n\n\n\n\nparticulars&quot; used in Pyare Lal Bhargava&#x27;s case has observed\n\nat page 790, para 20, thus:</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_411\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_228\">       &quot;By the use of the expression &#x27;corroboration of\n       material particulars&#x27;, the Court has not laid down\n       any proposition contrary to what has been\n       clarified in Subramania Goundan case as regards\n       the extent of corroboration required. The above\n       expression does not imply that there should be\n       meticulous examination of the entire material\n       particulars. It is enough that there is broad\n       corroboration in conformity with the general trend\n       of the confession, as pointed out in Subramania\n       Goundan case.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_412\">194.         Analysis of the legal position in paragraphs 18\n\nand 19 at page 788 of the above judgment is also worth\n\nnoting:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_229\">       &quot;18. Having thus reached a finding as to the\n       voluntary nature of a confession, the truth of the\n       confession should then be tested by the court.\n       The fact that the confession has been made\n       voluntarily, free from threat and inducement, can\n       be regarded as presumptive evidence of its truth.\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_262\">                                 239</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_230\">        Still, there may be circumstances to indicate that\n        the confession cannot be true wholly or partly in\n        which case it loses much of its evidentiary value.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_231\">        19. In order to be assured of the truth of\n        confession, this Court, in a series of decisions,\n        has evolved a rule of prudence that the court\n        should look to corroboration from other evidence.\n        However, there need not be corroboration in\n        respect of each and every material particular.\n        Broadly, there should be corroboration so that the\n        confession taken as a whole fits into the facts\n        proved by other evidence. In substance, the court\n        should have assurance from all angles that the\n        retracted confession was, in fact, voluntary and it\n        must have been true.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_413\">195.          As per SIR LAWRENCE JENKINS, a confession\n\ncan only be used to &quot;lend assurance to other evidence\n\nagainst a co-accused.&quot; The legal position was then stated\n\nthus:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_232\">        &quot;Translating these observations into concrete\n        terms they come to this. The proper way to\n        approach a case of this kind is, first to marshall\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_263\">                                240</span>\n\n\n\n\n       the evidence against the accused excluding the\n       confession altogether from consideration and see\n       whether, if it is believed, a conviction could safely\n       be based on it. If it is capable of belief\n       independently of the confession, then of course it\n       is not necessary to call the confession in aid. But\n       cases may arise where the Judge is not prepared\n       to act on the other evidence as it stands even\n       though, if believed, it would be sufficient to\n       sustain a conviction. In such an event the Judge\n       may call in aid the confession and use it to lend\n       assurance to the other evidence and thus fortify\n       himself in believing what without the aid of the\n       confession he would not be prepared to accept.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_414\">196.         It was contented by the accused that the letter\n\ncontaining the confessional statement is a judicial confession\n\nsince it is addressed to the Magistrate. But, the Magistrate\n\nhas neither recorded the confession statement of the\n\naccused as prescribed U/s 164 of <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_255\">Cr.P.C</a>., nor is he\n\nexamined in this case and therefore Ex.P245 is not\n\nadmissible in evidence.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_264\">                                     241</span><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_415\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_416\">197.           As    seen    from     <a href=\"/doc/497457/\" id=\"a_256\">Sec.164</a>    Cr.P.C,   a   judicial\n\nconfession is recorded only by a Metropolitan Magistrate or\n\nJudicial Magistrate, in the course of an investigation at any\n\ntime but, before the commencement of enquiry or trial, only\n\nafter making sure that it is made voluntarily and after\n\nexplaining to the person making it that he is not bound to\n\nmake such confession and that if made it could be used as\n\nevidence   against       him.    The      magistrate   recording    the\n\nconfession has to append a certificate at the foot as per\n\n<a href=\"/doc/91322/\" id=\"a_257\">Sec.164 (4)</a> Cr.P.C. If a confession is recorded without\n\ncomplying with these requirements, it would be inadmissible\n\nin evidence.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_417\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_418\">198.           Admittedly, A-7 did not appear before the\n\nMagistrate to whom Ex.P.245 is addressed. Nor did the\n\nMagistrate have an occasion to explain to A-7 that he is not\n\nbound to make such confession and that if he does so, it\n\nwould be used as evidence against him or to ensure that it\n\nwas    voluntarily       made.      Ex.P245     does    not    contain\n\nendorsement         as   prescribed    U/s    164(4)   <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_258\">Cr.P.C</a>.     Even\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_265\">                              242</span>\n\n\n\n\naccording to the prosecution Ex.P245 is only an extra\n\njudicial confession. Since an extra Judicial Confession is\n\ndistinct from a judicial confession, we find no merit in the\n\nargument of the accused that Ex.P245 is inadmissible in\n\nevidence for non compliance of Sec.164 Cr.P.C.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_419\">199.        The next limb of argument of the accused is that\n\na statement addressed to a magistrate but, not coming\n\nunder the purview of <a href=\"/doc/497457/\" id=\"a_259\">Sec.164</a> Cr.P.C, is also not admissible\n\nin evidence. Nor can it be considered as an extra judicial\n\nconfession or used against co-accused. We are unable\n\nappreciate this contention because, there is nothing in the\n\n<a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_260\">Code of Criminal Procedure</a> that prohibits treating a\n\nconfession or a statement as an extra judicial confession\n\nmerely because it does not satisfy the requirements of\n\n<a href=\"/doc/497457/\" id=\"a_261\">Sec.164</a> Cr.P.C.     An extra-judicial confession by its very\n\nnature is rather a weak type of evidence and requires\n\nappreciation with a great deal of care and caution; the Court\n\nwould   generally    look   for    an   independent   reliable\n\ncorroboration before placing any reliance upon it. However, if\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_266\">                               243</span>\n\n\n\n\nan extra-judicial confession is voluntary and true and made\n\nin a fit state of mind by the person making it, the same can\n\nbe relied upon by the Court and conviction can also be\n\nfounded thereon. When extra-judicial confession is retracted\n\nby an accused, there is no inflexible rule that the Court must\n\ninvariably accept the retraction. However, it would be unsafe\n\nto rely on retracted confession unless, the Court, upon\n\nconsideration of the entire evidence, comes to a definite\n\nconclusion that the retracted confession is true and\n\nvoluntarily made. The Court should find out whether there\n\nare other cogent circumstances to inspire confidence with\n\nregard to extra judicial confession.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_420\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_421\">200.        The prosecution has produced Ex.P245 dated\n\n31.08.2000, certified copy of the confessional letter written\n\nby A7 in his own handwriting; Ex.P245 (a) is A-7&#x27;s signature\n\non the last page of the said letter. Its original and the letter\n\nof Superintendent of Central Prison forwarding the same to\n\nthe Magistrate have been produced in S.C.No.423/2001 and\n\nmarked as Ex.P260 and Ex.P261 respectively. Ex.P245 runs\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_267\">                               244</span>\n\n\n\n\ninto 42 pages and A-7 has signed at the bottom of all the\n\npages. It also discloses that blank sheets of paper was\n\nsupplied to A-7 by the prison authorities at his request - 30\n\nsheets on 31.08.2000 and the remaining on 04.09.2000;\n\nsignature of the Prison Officer, the date nad UTP 2387 are\n\nfound on all the pages at the left corner.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_422\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_423\">201.        According to the prosecution, A-7 out of his free\n\nwill, voluntarily wrote the confession statement in his own\n\nhandwriting and delivered it to the prison authorities with a\n\nrequest to forward the same to the jurisdictional Magistrate.\n\nPW63, the Chief Superintendent of Central Prison Sri\n\nN.Jayaramaiah, in support of this has deposed to the effect\n\nthat A-7 was allotted UTP No. 2387 and kept in the Cell Yard\n\nof Central Prison Bangalore. As Chief Superintendent of the\n\nsaid Prison from January 2000 to October 2000, he used to\n\ngo around the entire prison and on one such routine round\n\nwith subordinates, A-7 requested him for white paper and\n\nwriting material for writing the facts pertaining to this case.\n\nHis subordinates supplied white sheets of paper to A-7 by\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_268\">                              245</span>\n\n\n\n\naffixing the seal, date, UTP number of A-7 on the left corner\n\nof the blank sheets under their signature. A-7 took nearly 4\n\nto 5 days to write this 42 pages letter, signed at the bottom\n\nof each page and handed over the same to UTP Officer in his\n\npresence. Thereafter, he forwarded the letter to the III\n\nAddl.C.M.M., Bangalore, with covering letter Ex.P246 dated\n\n07.09.2000 and Ex.P246(a) is his signature.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_424\">202.        It was argued that A-7 an Air Force Officer who\n\nhad put in 31 years of service, was arrested from the official\n\nquarters at New Delhi on 08.08.2000 and kept in police\n\ncustody till 26.08.2000. Ex.P245 was obtained by threat and\n\ncompulsion during that period; it was manipulated and\n\nproduced after A-7 was remanded to judicial custody. But\n\nPW63&#x27;s   evidence   that   A-7   was   remanded   by   the   III\n\nAddl.C.M.M., Bangalore on 26.8.2000 to judicial custody,\n\nthat the accused obtained blank sheets of paper and writing\n\nmaterial expressing while in judicial custody, his intention to\n\nwrite down what transpired in this case and accordingly he\n\nwas supplied 42 sheets of paper on which Ex.P245 is written\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_269\">                               246</span>\n\n\n\n\nwith A-7&#x27;s UTP No., signature of the officer supplying the\n\npapers   etc.,   has   not   been   discredited    despite    cross\n\nexamining him on these aspects extenensively.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_425\">203.        PW63       was     questioned         regarding    the\n\nendorsements made in the covering letter Ex.P246 and if he\n\nwas satisfied about the correctness of the contents of\n\nEx.P245 before it was forwarded to the concerned Court etc.\n\nHowever nothing is elicited to discredit the testimony of this\n\nwitness regarding A-7 writing Ex.P245 voluntarily. The\n\nsuggestions to PW63 that the accused was compelled to\n\nwrite the letter under threat and compulsion Dy.SP Appanna\n\nand PI Nataraj and that Ex.P245 is not in A-7&#x27;s handwriting\n\nrun counter to each other.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_426\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_427\">204.        The records placed before this court shows that\n\nthe prosecution through PW63&#x27;s evidence has dispelled the\n\ncontention of the accused that Ex.P245 was obtained while\n\nA-7 was in police custody. The evidence on record makes it\n\nclear that Ex.P245, a 42 page letter in English has been\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_270\">                                 247</span>\n\n\n\n\nwritten by A-7 voluntarily while in judicial custody; the III\n\nACMM, Bangalore has received the originals of Ex.P245 and\n\nEx.P246. The trial court therefore rightly rejected the\n\ncontentions of the accused in this behalf.\n\n\nSECONDARY EVIDENCE\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_428\">205.         Ex.P245 is the certified copy of the original\n\nconfession produced and marked as Ex.P.260 in S.C. No.423\n\nof 2001. It was strongly urged by the accused that Ex.P245\n\nis inadmissible in evidence and cannot be looked into. But,\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1456410/\" id=\"a_262\">section 63</a> of the Evidence Act provides for leading secondary\n\nevidence and it reads as under:-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_429\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_233\">       &quot;63. Secondary evidence.--Secondary evidence\n       means and includes--\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_234\">       (1)   Certified copies given under the provisions\n       hereinafter contained;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_235\">       (2)   Copies   made      from   the   original   by\n       mechanical processes which in themselves insure\n       the accuracy of the copy, and copies compared\n       with such copies;\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_271\">                              248</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_236\">       (3)   Copies made from or compared with the\n       original;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_237\">       (4)   Counterparts of documents as against the\n       parties who did not execute them;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_238\">       (5)   Oral accounts of the contents of a document\n       given by some person who has himself seen it.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_430\">206.         Section 65 of the evidence Act is an exception to\n\nthe rule contained under Section 64 that all documents\n\nmust be proved by primary evidence. <a href=\"/doc/662228/\" id=\"a_263\">Section 65(f)</a> of the\n\nEvidence Act provides for admissibility of only certified copy\n\nof a document of which certified copy is permitted by the Act\n\nbut no other kind of secondary evidence. Since the original\n\nletter written by A-7 was produced and marked as Ex.P260\n\nin S.C.No.423/2001, its its certified copy is produced and\n\nmarked as Ex.P245 in this case. The same is admissible in\n\nevidence and therefore, we find no substance in the\n\ncontention of the accused.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_272\">                               249</span><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_431\">\n\n\n\n\nTHREAT\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_432\">207.          It was argued by the learned counsel for the\n\naccused that there is sufficient material on record to imply\n\nthat the confession is obtained by inducement, threat or\n\ncompulsion and therefore Ex.P245 is inadmissible. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"1769219\" id=\"span_273\">In\n\nsupport of this, he relied upon the judgment of the Apex\n\nCourt in <a href=\"/doc/1769219/\" id=\"a_264\">Navjoth Sandhu</a>&#x27;s case cited supra.</span>\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_433\">208.          In this regard it is necessary to notice what\n\n<a href=\"/doc/967059/\" id=\"a_265\">Section 24</a> of the Evidence Act provides for:\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_239\">       &quot;24.     Confession   caused    by    inducement,\n       threat    or   promise,    when      irrelevant   in\n       criminal proceeding.--A confession made by\n       an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal\n       proceeding, if the making of the confession\n       appears to the Court to have been caused by any\n       inducement, threat or promise, having reference\n       to the charge against the accused person,\n       proceeding from a person in authority and\n       sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the\n       accused person grounds, which would appear to\n       him reasonable, for supposing that by making it\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_274\">                                250</span>\n\n\n\n\n       he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of\n       a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings\n       against him.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_434\">209.         Further the confession should have been made\n\nwith full knowledge of the nature and consequences of the\n\nconfession. If any reasonable doubt is entertained by the\n\ncourt that these ingredients are not satisfied, the court\n\nshould    eschew       the   confession   from   consideration.\n\nRecognizing the stark reality of the accused being enveloped\n\nin a state of fear and panic, anxiety and despair while in\n\npolice custody, the <a href=\"/doc/1953529/\" id=\"a_266\">Indian Evidence Act</a> has excluded the\n\nadmissibility of a confession made to the police officer.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_435\">210.         It is not in dispute that A-7 was working as a\n\nJunior Warrant Officer in Indian Airforce on the day of arrest\n\nand he had put in 33 years of service; Ex.P245 is in English\n\nand in A-7&#x27;s own handwriting. He took 4 to 5 days to write\n\ndown the same while in judicial custody and it runs into 42\n\npages. A-7 is an educated person, knows his rights and had\n\nthe assistance of the Counsel. If there was any threat,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_275\">                                251</span>\n\n\n\n\ncoercion, force, practiced on him on 31.8.2000, when he was\n\nsupplied with 30 blank white sheets and 12 sheets on\n\n4.9.2000, he could have narrated about the same when\n\nproduced before the Magistrate on 05.09.2000. Thereafter he\n\nwas produced before the Magistrate several times for the\n\npurpose of remand, but never mentioned about the alleged\n\nthreat, coercion and force in order to obtain the confessional\n\nstatement. Interestingly, the retraction came after a lapse of\n\n2 years and therefore can be termed as an after thought to\n\nwriggle out of the situation.    In fact, first page of Ex.P245\n\nspeaks to the effect that A-7 wrote the said confessional\n\nstatement without any compulsion, coercion, threat or force\n\nas sought to be canvassed. Under these circumstances, we\n\ndo not see any justification to interfere with the finding\n\nrecorded by the trial Court in this behalf.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_436\">211.            Ex.P245 is attacked on yet another count that\n\nits    copies    were   furnished    to   the   accused   before\n\ncommencement of trial and there is no reference to the said\n\ndocument in the charge-sheet also. That the accused were\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_276\">                              252</span>\n\n\n\n\ncompletely ignorant about the contents of this document and\n\nany incriminating evidence contained in Ex.P245 therefore\n\nought to have been brought to the notice of the co-accused\n\nwhile recording the statement U/s 313 <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_267\">Cr.P.C</a>. Since no such\n\nattempt is made contents of Ex.P245 cannot be used against\n\nthe co-accused.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_437\">\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_438\">212.        <a href=\"/doc/479826/\" id=\"a_268\">Section 230</a> Cr.P.C. dealing with fixing the date\n\nfor prosecution evidence enables the prosecution to compel\n\nthe attendance of any witness or the production of any\n\ndocument or other thing. In other words, by virtue of this\n\nprovision, the prosecution can even examine a witness who\n\nis not cited in the chargesheet and produce a document\n\nwhich is not furnished along with the police papers, for the\n\nfirst time before the Court during the trial.
