The said power is\n\nto be harmonized along with the provision contained in\n\nSections 173(8) and 173(5) as well as Section 209(c) of\n\nCr.P.C.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_277\">                                      253</span><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_439\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_440\">213.             Moving onto the question whether Ex.P245 can\n\nbe used against the co-accused who also faced trial with A-7,\n\nit is well established that a confession made by an accused\n\ncannot be used to convict his co-accused unless there is\n\ncorroborative evidence against the co-accused but a person\n\ncan be convicted solely upon his own confession even if\n\nretracted if the Court believes it to be true. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"532794\" id=\"span_278\">In this behalf it\n\nwould be useful to refer to <a href=\"/doc/532794/\" id=\"a_269\">MANOHAR SINGH Vs EMPEROR</a>,\n\nreported in AIR 1946 All 15.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_441\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_442\">214.             After   referring   to    the   earlier    decisions,   a\n\nConstitution Bench of the Apex Court in HARICHARAN\n\nKURMI Vs STATE OF BIHAR reported in 1964 (6) SCR 623\n\nobserved thus:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_240\">       &quot;In dealing with a case against an accused\n       person,     the   Court   cannot      start   with     the\n       confession of co-accused person; it must begin\n       with other evidence adduced by the prosecution\n       and after it has formed its opinion with regard to\n       the quality and effect of the said evidence, then\n       it is permissible to turn to the confession in order\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_279\">                                254</span>\n\n\n\n\n       to receive assurance to the confession of guilt\n       which the judicial mind is about to reach on the\n       said other evidence.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_443\">215.          Therefore we are of the view that though\n\nEx.P245 was not produced along with the charge sheet the\n\nprosecution could produce the same in evidence at a later\n\nstatge to substantiate its case by virtue of <a href=\"/doc/479826/\" id=\"a_270\">Sec.230</a> Cr.P.C.\n\nTherefore, in view of the aforesaid discussion, Ex.P.245 is\n\nadmissible in evidence. However it could not be used against\n\nthe co-accused unless there is corroborative evidence.\n\n\nRe: POINT NO.6\n\nACCOMPLICE EVIDENCE\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_444\">216.          The   evidence   of    PW-54   Abdul   Gafoor   is\n\nchallenged on the ground that he is an accomplice and his\n\nevidence is that of an approver. It was argued by the learned\n\ncounsel for the accused that PW54 also participated in the\n\ncrime and should have been arrayed as accused. Only\n\nthereafter, following the procedure prescribed under the law,\n\nhe could be treated as an approver. The police cannot\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_280\">                              255</span>\n\n\n\n\npardon him and use him as a witness in the case. Since no\n\nsuch pardon is granted by the Magistrate, PW54&#x27;s evidence\n\ncannot be relied upon.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_445\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_446\">217.        Per contra, the learned Spl.P.P. contended that\n\nPW54 is not an Accomplice. Nor is he examined as an\n\napprover.He has been examined as an eye witness to the\n\nCriminal Conspiracy and therefore, neither <a href=\"/doc/274935/\" id=\"a_271\">Section 133</a> of\n\nthe Evidence Act nor illustration of (b) of Section 114 thereof\n\nis attracted. PW54&#x27;s evidence is very much admissible in law.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_447\">218.        The word `accomplice&#x27; has not been defined by\n\nthe Indian Evindec Act and should therefore be presumed to\n\nhave been used in the ordinary sense. It means and signifies\n\na guilty associate or partner in crime. In other words an\n\naccomplice is a person, who concurred in the commission of\n\nan offence. Thus the term accomplice in its fullness includes\n\nin its meaning all persons concerned in the commission of a\n\ncrime, all participes criminis, whether they are considered in\n\nstrict legal propriety as principals in the first or second\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_281\">                              256</span>\n\n\n\n\ndegree or merely as accessories before or after the act. But if\n\nthe participation of accessories is limited to the knowledge\n\nthat crime is to be committed, they are not accomplices.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_448\">219.        The mere fact that a person did not reveal his\n\nknowledge of the intended crime to the authorities does not\n\nmake him an accomplice. The mere fact that a person\n\nwitnesses a crime and does not give information of it to\n\nanyone else out of fear does not warrant the extreme\n\nproposition that he is an accomplice. However, the evidence\n\nof such a man should be scanned with much caution and\n\ncare for corroboration of material particulars.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_449\">220.        Therefore, whether a person is or is not an\n\naccomplice depends upon the facts of each case. The burden\n\nof proving that a witness is an accomplice is of course upon\n\nthe party alleging it i.e. the accused.   It is for the judge to\n\ndecide if a witness is truly an accomplice. The only safeguard\n\nagainst the risk of condemning the innocent with the guilty\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_282\">                                257</span>\n\n\n\n\nlies in insisting upon independent evidence which in some\n\nmeasure implicates each accused.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_450\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_451\">221.         TAYLOR       says:     &quot;Accomplices         are   usually\n\ninterested, and always infamous witnesses, and whose\n\ntestimony    is   admitted   from      necessity,   it   being    often\n\nimpossible, without having recourse to such evidence, to bring\n\nthe principal offenders to justice&quot;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_452\">\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_453\">222.         ABBOTT, L. CJ, in his charge to the Grand Jury\n\nin MARCH 1880, 33 HOW ST TR 689 said -\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_241\">       &quot;If it should ever be laid down as a practical rule\n       in the administration of justice, that the testimony\n       of accomplices should be rejected as incredible,\n       the   most    mischievous       consequences        must\n       necessarily ensue; because it must not only\n       happen that many heinous crimes and offences\n       will pass unpunished, but great encouragement\n       will be given to bad men, by withdrawing from\n       their minds the fear of detection and punishment\n       through the instrumentality of their partners in\n       guilt, and thereby universal confidence will be\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_283\">                               258</span>\n\n\n\n\n       substituted for that distrust of each other, which\n       naturally possesses men engages in wicked\n       purposes, and which operate as one of the most\n       effectual restraints against the commission of\n       those crimes to which the concurrence of several\n       persons is required. No such rule is laid down by\n       the law of England or of any other country.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_454\">223.         While <a href=\"/doc/274935/\" id=\"a_272\">Section 133</a> of the Indian Evidence Act\n\nprovides that an accomplice shall be a competent witness\n\nagainst an accused person; and a conviction is not illegal\n\nmerely   because    it   proceeds   upon   the   uncorroborated\n\ntestimony of an accomplice., illustration (b) to Section 114\n\nthereof provides for drawing the presumption that an\n\naccomplice is unworthy of credit, unless he is corroborated in\n\nmaterial particulars. Thus <a href=\"/doc/274935/\" id=\"a_273\">Section 133</a> of the Indian Evidence\n\nAct is a rule of law and illustration (b) to Section 114 is a\n\nrule of practice which vests discretion in the Court to\n\npresume that an accomplice is unworthy of credit unless he\n\nis corroborated in material particulars.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_284\">                                259</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_455\">224.         The Apex Court in the case of BHIVA DOULU\n\nPATIL VS STATE OF MAHARASHTRA reported in AIR 1963\n\nSC 599 reconciling these two provisions has held as under:\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_242\">       &quot;The combined effect of Ss. 133 and 114,\n       illustration (b) may be stated as follows:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_243\">             According to the former, which is a rule of\n       law, an accomplice is competent to give evidence\n       and according to the latter which is a rule of\n       practice it is almost always unsafe to convict\n       upon his testimony alone. Therefore though the\n       conviction of an accused on the testimony of an\n       accomplice cannot be said to be illegal yet the\n       Courts will, as a matter of practice, not accept the\n       evidence of such a witness without corroboration\n       in material particulars. The law may be stated in\n       the words of Lord Reading C. J. in R. v.\n       Baskerville (1) as follows\n\n             &quot;There is no doubt that the uncorroborated\n       evidence of an accomplice is admissible in law (R.\n       v Attwood, 1787, 1 Leach 464). But it has been\n       Ion(,, a rule of practice at common law for the\n       judge to warn the jury of the danger of convicting\n       a prisoner on the uncorroborated testimony of an\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_285\">                                 260</span>\n\n\n\n\n       accomplice, and in the discretion of the Judge, to\n       advise them not to convict upon such evidence,\n       but the judge should point out to the jury that it is\n       within their legal province to convict upon such\n       unconfirmed evidence (R. v Stubbs, Dears 555 In\n       re Heunier, 1894 2 Q.B. 415)&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_456\">225.         The principle underlying these provisions is,\n\nAccomplice evidence is admitted from necessity as it is\n\ngenerally impossible to get sufficient evidence of many\n\nheinous    and    diabolical    crimes,   unless    one   of   the\n\nparticipators is disposed to disclose the circumstances\n\nwithin his knowledge on account of the tender of pardon.\n\nThe greatest offenders would go unpunished, if accomplice\n\nevidence were to be rejected.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_457\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_458\">226.         Though     accomplice    evidence     is   admissible\n\nagainst a co-accused, being a participator in crime and\n\ntherefore an infamous witness, his testimony is regarded\n\nwith the greatest distrust and the fullest corroboration in\n\nmaterial particulars is required for a conviction. The reasons\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_286\">                              261</span>\n\n\n\n\nwhich have led to the distrust of an accomplice&#x27;s testimony\n\nare not far to seek.   He may expect to save himself from\n\npunishment by procuring the conviction of others. It is true\n\nthat he is also charging himself, and in that respect he has\n\nburned his ships. But he can escape the consequences of\n\nthis acknowledgment, if the prosecuting authorities choose\n\nto release him provided he secures the conviction of his\n\npartner in crime. A conviction based on the uncorroborated\n\ntestimony of an accomplice is not illegal. But the experience\n\nhas taught us that it is not safe to rely upon the evidence of\n\nan accomplice unless it is corroborated.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_459\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_460\">227.        In India, Judges and Magistrates are competent\n\nto tender pardon under <a href=\"/doc/122405/\" id=\"a_274\">Section 306</a> of Cr.P.C., with a view to\n\nobtaining the evidence of any person supposed to have been\n\ndirectly or indirectly concerned in or privy to the offence\n\nunder enquiry on condition of his making a full and true\n\ndisclosure of the circumstances within his knowledge. Every\n\nperson accepting a tender becomes a witness under <a href=\"/doc/1799424/\" id=\"a_275\">Section\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_287\">                                    262</span></a>\n\n\n\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1918898/\" id=\"a_276\">306(2)</a> Cr.P.C. He becomes a witness only after the grant of\n\npardon.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_461\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_462\">228.         Section 306 of the Criminal Procedure code\n\nreads as under:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_244\">       306. Tender of pardon to accomplice. -\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_245\">       (1)With a view to obtaining the evidence of any\n       person      supposed   to    have   been   directly    or\n       indirectly concerned in or privy to an offence to\n       which this section applies, the Chief Judicial\n       Magistrate or a Metropolitan Magistrate at any\n       stage of the investigation or inquiry into, or the\n       trial of, the offence, and the Magistrate of the first\n       class inquiring into or trying the offence, at any\n       stage of the inquiry or trial, may tender a pardon\n       to such person on condition of his making a full\n       and   true    dis-closure    of   the   whole   of    the\n       circumstances within his knowledge relative to\n       the offence and to every other person concerned,\n       whether as principal or abettor, in the commission\n       thereof.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_246\">       (2)   This section applies to-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_247\">             (a)      any offence triable exclusively by the\n                      Court of Session or by the Court of a\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_288\">                          263</span>\n\n\n\n\n             Special Judge appointed under the\n             <a href=\"/doc/544006/\" id=\"a_277\">Criminal Law Amendment Act, 1952</a>\n             (46 of 1952 );\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_248\">      (b)   any     offence     punishable   with\n             imprisonment which may extend to\n             seven years or with a more severe\n             sentence.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_249\">(3)   Every Magistrate who tenders a pardon\nunder sub- section (1) shall record-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_250\">      (a)    his reasons for so doing;\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_251\">      (b)    whether the tender was or was not\n             accepted by the person to whom it\n             was made,\n             and shall, on application made by\n             the accused, furnish him with a copy\n             of such record free of cost.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_252\">(4)   Every person accepting a tender of pardon\nmade under sub- section (1)-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_253\">      (a)    shall be examined as a witness in\n             the Court of the Magistrate taking\n             cognizance of the offence and in the\n             subsequent trial, if any;\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_289\">                                 264</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_254\">             (b)    shall, unless he is already on bail, be\n                    detained        in   custody   until   the\n                    termination of the trial.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_255\">       (5)   Where a person has, accepted a tender of\n       pardon made under sub- section (1) and has been\n       examined under sub- section (4), the Magistrate\n       taking cognizance of the offence shall, without\n       making any further inquiry in the case,-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_256\">       (a)   commit it for trial-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_257\">             (i)    to the Court of Session if the, offence\n                    is triable exclusively by that Court or\n                    if the Magistrate taking cognizance is\n                    the Chief Judicial Magistrate;\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_258\">             (ii)   to a Court of Special Judge appointed\n                    under the <a href=\"/doc/544006/\" id=\"a_278\">Criminal Law Amendment\n                    Act, 1952</a> (46 of 1952 ), if the offence\n                    is triable exclusively by that Court;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_259\">       (b)   in any other case, make over the case to\n             the Chief Judicial Magistrate who shall try\n             the case himself.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_463\">229.         The very object of this provision is to allow\n\npardon to be tendered in cases where a grave offence is\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_290\">                               265</span>\n\n\n\n\nalleged to have been committed by several persons so that\n\nwith the aid of the evidence of the person pardoned the\n\noffence could be brought home to the rest. The policy is to\n\nprevent the escape of offenders from punishment in grave\n\ncases for lack of evidence by grant of pardon to accomplices\n\nfor obtaining true evidence. <a href=\"/doc/122405/\" id=\"a_279\">Section 306</a> Cr.P.C. that applies\n\nonly to offences of a more serious character therein specified,\n\nprovides safeguard in the interests both of the State and the\n\naccused. Pardon is granted on condition that full disclosure\n\nof truth will be made and the person accepting the pardon\n\ngives evidence with the assurance that it will operate as a\n\nbar to his prosecution for the offence pardoned or for any\n\nother offence committed in connection there with. A pardon\n\nmay be forfeited if the whole truth is not disclosed and the\n\nperson to whom pardon was tendered may be tried for the\n\noffence. <a href=\"/doc/122405/\" id=\"a_280\">Section 306</a> Cr.P.C. is the only method of obtaining\n\nthe evidence of co-accused.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_464\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_465\">230.        Withdrawal from prosecution under <a href=\"/doc/1037589/\" id=\"a_281\">Section 321</a>\n\nCr.P.C. is in the discretion of the executive subject however,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_291\">                             266</span>\n\n\n\n\nto the consent of the Court whereas tender of pardon under\n\n<a href=\"/doc/122405/\" id=\"a_282\">Section 306</a> Cr.P.C. is a judicial act. Pardon can be tendered\n\nat any stage of investigation. The moment an accused is\n\npardoned he is presumed to have been discharged and\n\nbecomes a witness.     The person pardoned need not be\n\narrayed as an accused in the case. The real culprit should\n\nnot be left out in the hope of obtaining evidence against\n\nother.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_466\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_467\">231.        Keeping the above principles of law in mind,\n\nwhen we peruse PW54&#x27;s evidence, it discloses that he is a\n\nresident of Solapur for the last 36 years carrying on the\n\nbusiness in beatle leaves in a shop situated at Siddeshwara\n\nMarket yard, on Solapur-Hyderabad road. He is aware of\n\nexistence   Hazrath   Moulana     Moulvi   Hussain   Siddique\n\nDeendar Channabaveshwara&#x27;s tomb in Deendar Anjuman\n\nAshram at Asifnagar, Hyderabad and that Urs is held there\n\nevery year in the month of Razab on 7th, 8th and 9th days.\n\nSince childhood he has been attending the Urs in Anjuman\n\nAshram in Asif Nagar, Hyderabad.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_292\">                              267</span><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_468\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_469\">232.        P.W.54 also has the knowledge of A-26 and A-27\n\nhailing from Hubli, Ismail Sab from Gadag, A-11 from\n\nBangalore and A-13 from Chikaballapur, A.6, A.7, A.8\n\nattending the Urs at Hyderabad besides one Iqbal and A.21.\n\nHe remembers only Khaleed Choudary from Hyderabad\n\namongst those attending the Urs. It is in his evidence that A-\n\n1 along with his sons A-2 to A-5 used to come from Mardan\n\nin Pakistan to attend the Urs at the Ashram. A-1 visited\n\nBangalore twice or thrice while going Sholapur. While at\n\nSolapur,   A-1   stayed   with   Ramdas,   Sufi,   Iqbal   and\n\nDeshmukh. During that period twice or trice, A-1 also visited\n\nPW54&#x27;s house and took meals.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_470\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_471\">233.        As per PW54&#x27;s evidence, The last occasion PW54\n\nvisited urs was on 7th day of Razab in the year 1999 and all\n\nthe persons mentioned above had also attended the urs; A-1\n\nto A-5 were accommodated in a room in the Ashram. After\n\nthe religious ceremony he returned to Sholapur on the same\n\nday for a brief staty of 3 to 4 days. Thereafter, Basheer Sab\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_293\">                             268</span>\n\n\n\n\nKokatnur met him at Solapur and informed that after he left\n\nfor Sholapur A-1 addressed them At the Ahram premises in\n\na meeting and informed that it was the time for Zihad; and\n\nfurther instructions in that regard would be issued to A-7,\n\nwho in return would pass on the same to Basheer Sab and\n\nA-25. Others would be instructed by the said Basheer Sab\n\nand A-25.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_472\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_473\">234.        PW54 refers to other meetings that took place in\n\nJanuary 2000 and April 2000 and states that A-25\n\naddressed those meeting, instructed them to collect where\n\nthe railway bridges are and that he would tell them how to\n\ndestroy the same; to raise funds for this purpose they shoot\n\nloot people and send the funds to A-1 a resident of Pakistan.\n\nA-25 also requested each one of them to pay whatever\n\namount they could. PW54 gives the number of such\n\nmeetings attended by rought at four and states that he had\n\nheard the discussions in all the four meetings; A-26 was\n\ngiven the responsibility of finding out how many railway\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_294\">                              269</span>\n\n\n\n\nbridges are there and Basha sab was required to give\n\ninformation regarding petrol pumps and also arrange funds.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_474\">235.        In December 1999 Basheer sab called PW54 over\n\ntelephone from Batakurki saying that he need Rs.30,000/-\n\nto give it to who was running short of money to buy a Tata\n\nSumo and would return the same in two months. Four days\n\nlater Sangli Basha, Taylor Basha, A-25 and Basheer Sab\n\nvisited him. He paid Sangli Basha Rs.30,000/- which was\n\ninvested in a chit; Basheer Sab who was present there\n\npromised to repay the same with interest.     He regularly\n\nattended the meetings held in the house of some of the\n\naccused and thereby knew what they were upto but was\n\nclearly warned not to disclose the same to anyone.\n\nOtherwise, their lives would be in danger.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_475\">236.        PW54&#x27;s evidence would further disclose that in\n\nJuly 2000 one Akhil, who is A.5 in Crime No.579/2003\n\nclubbed with 580/2003 on the file of the Spl. Court came to\n\nhis house at 10.00 p.m. and had brought a bomb in a\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_295\">                             270</span>\n\n\n\n\nhandbag. A-6 had given that bomb kept in a square shaped\n\ntin box to Akhil with an instruction to give it PW54 for\n\nplanting the same in a Church in Sholapur. Then P.W.54\n\npleaded his inability to carry out that instruction.   Akhil\n\nspent that night in a small room in his house with the bag\n\ncontaining the bomb. Next day when PW54 was in his shop,\n\nAkhil went there at about 9:00 am, called his uncle Siphen\n\nSheik over the phone. When that person arrived, he was also\n\nasked by Akhil to explode the bomb in a church but he too\n\nrefused.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_476\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_477\">237.        Thereafter, Akhil kept the bomb in his room for\n\nabout three days. On the fourth day, when the bomb blast in\n\nGoa and Hubli were telecasted on TV, Akhil diffused the\n\nbomb saying that he will not blast the same, kept some\n\nstones and pieces of bricks inside that box, kept the same in\n\na plastic bag and asked him to throw that into a well. After\n\nAkhil left, PW.54&#x27;s son-in-law Sikander threw the said box in\n\na well 500 feet deep at Garukula which is about 1 to 2 km\n\naway from the house as no one was using the water from\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_296\">                              271</span>\n\n\n\n\nthat well. On seeing church bomb blast videos pertaining to\n\nthis case and arrest of the accused on the TV he went to the\n\npolice, narrated all the facts to the police. He also appeared\n\nbefore the JMFC and gave statement U/s 164 <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_283\">Cr.P.C</a>. about\n\nall that he knew of the accused.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_478\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_479\">238.          Relying on this evidence, it was contended that\n\nPW54 was present at the time of the meetings wherein a\n\ncriminal conspiracy was hatched and he did not inform the\n\npolice on coming to know the intention of Akhil. Therefore he\n\nis an accomplice to the conspiracy to the crime and his\n\nevidence cannot be relied upon.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_480\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_481\">239.          The question therefore, is whether motive could\n\nbe attributed to PW54 merely because he was present in\n\nthose meetings and whether he can be treated as an\n\naccomplice?\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_482\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_483\">240.          Though PW54 participated in several meetings\n\nwhere a criminal conspiracy was hatched, visited the\n\nAshram at Asifnagar in Hyderabad, invited some of the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_297\">                              272</span>\n\n\n\n\naccused for having food in his house and gave them shelter,\n\nhe had no intention of participating in the conspiracy or\n\ncommission of the crime. He had no intention to act\n\naccording to the decisions taken in those meetings. Despite\n\nknowing that the meetings were held to commit an illegal\n\nact, PW54 was constrained to attend them until Rs.30,000/-\n\nlent by him was repaid. As PW54 has not participated in any\n\nact in pursuance of the criminal conspiracy, he cannot be\n\ncharacterised   as   an   accomplice.    Before   he   could   be\n\ncharacterized as an Accomplice, he must be a party to the\n\ncrime. He is not a party to the crime.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_484\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_485\">241.        Though PW54 went to the Magistrate and gave a\n\nstatement about all what he did know about these meetings,\n\nas he was not a party to the crime his statement cannot be\n\nconstituted as a confessional statement even under <a href=\"/doc/497457/\" id=\"a_284\">Section\n\n164</a> of Cr.P.C. Pardon pre-supposes commission of a crime.\n\nSince PW54 neither committed any crime nor was a party to\n\nthe agreement to commit a crime, he did not seek any\n\npardon from the Magistrate or the police. The contention\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_298\">                              273</span>\n\n\n\n\nthat the police gave him pardon is without any substance.\n\nPolice have recorded his statement in the course of\n\ninvestigation and they have examined him as a witness to\n\nprove the charges levelled against the accused.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_486\">242.           PW54 has been cross-examined extensively by\n\nthe accused. Nowhere in evidence, he has admitted his guilt\n\nor participation in the crime; his statement is exculpatory in\n\nnature. Therefore, he is neither an accomplice nor an\n\napprover. If only PW54 were to be treated as an Accomplice,\n\nSection 114 illustration (b) of the <a href=\"/doc/1953529/\" id=\"a_285\">Evidence Act</a> would be\n\nattracted and not otherwise. PW54 is only a prosecution\n\nwitness and not an accomplice. As such <a href=\"/doc/274935/\" id=\"a_286\">Section 133</a> r/w\n\n<a href=\"/doc/731516/\" id=\"a_287\">Section 114</a> illustration (b) of <a href=\"/doc/1953529/\" id=\"a_288\">Indian Evidence Act</a> has no\n\napplication.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_487\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_488\">243.           Whether the Court can act on his evidence is\n\ntotally a different matter. But to say that his evidence is to\n\nbe excluded as not admissible is without any basis. However,\n\nsince PW54 was present at the conspiracy meetings,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_299\">                              274</span>\n\n\n\n\naccommodated a person who came to his house with a bomb\n\nto explode a church etc., his evidence needs to be scrutinized\n\nwith greater care and caution as to material particulars.\n\n\n\nRe: POINT NO.7\nTEST IDENTIFICATION PARADE\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_489\">244.        P.W.3 - Mukunda is an eye witness in this case.\n\nHe has identified A.10, A.14 and A.15.      Insofar as A.10 is\n\nconcerned, an identification parade was conducted in which\n\nP.W.3 has identified him.    It was contended that the said\n\nidentification parade is vitiated because, even before the\n\nidentification parade, P.W.3 was shown the photographs of\n\nA.10 in the albums in the police station and therefore, the\n\nidentification parade conducted thereafter is vitiated.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_490\">245.        We are dealing with the bomb blasts that\n\noccurred in Bangalore on the day of 09-07-2000. One of\n\nthese blasts occurred in the premises of St. Peter Paul\n\nChurch in JJ Nagar and many disciples had come to the\n\nChurch for a celebration at that time. PW-3, Mukunda has\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_300\">                              275</span>\n\n\n\n\nstated that talking to the owner of the shop he was noticing\n\nthe people coming from and going into the church. At that\n\ntime, a blue colour Maruti Van with Goan registration\n\nnumber stopped near the shop, three people got out of it;\n\ntwo of them walked towards the church with a plastic bag in\n\ntheir hand while the third one waited near the van. He and\n\nthe shop keeper commented on the Goan registration\n\nnumber saying that the disciples of this church came all the\n\nway from Goa. In the meanwhile the two persons who had\n\nwalked towards the church returned without plastic cover\n\nsaying in Urdu &quot;Chalo Kaam Hogaya&quot;, meaning that the\n\nwork was over. Once those two persons got in, the van\n\nmoved in the direction of Minerva Mills.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_491\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_492\">246.        The people who PW-3 saw were later identified\n\nas A-10, A-14 and A-15. A-14 and A-15 had died during the\n\naccidental bomb blast near the Magadi Road. This rendered\n\nPW-3 as a witness who could identify the surviving accused;\n\nhe went twice to the police station to identify the accusedand\n\nwas shown the photographs. Later a test identification\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_301\">                                  276</span>\n\n\n\n\nparade was conducted. While the learned public prosecutor\n\nrelies on the evidence as to identification of the accused, the\n\nlearned counsel for the accused challenges the same\n\nseriously.   Thus,    the   whole       procedure   of   the   Test\n\nIdentification Parade has come under scrutiny.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_493\">247.         A test identification parade basically consists of\n\na line-up of people who have similar physical appearance for\n\nthe purpose of identification of the accused by the eye\n\nwitness within the scope of <a href=\"/doc/529244/\" id=\"a_289\">Section 9</a> of the Indian Evidence\n\nAct.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_494\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_495\">248.         The     procedure     to    be   followed   in    such\n\nidentification parade is given in the Karnataka Police Manual\n\nVolume 2 in ss. 1359 - 1363. It is to be conducted by a\n\nMagistrate, as far as possible. If for any substantial reasons\n\nto be recorded in the case diary, the services of a competent\n\nMagistrate are not available the identification parade may be\n\nconducted by independent persons of the locality, not in any\n\nway connected with or concerned in the case. After making\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_302\">                               277</span>\n\n\n\n\nall arrangements for the parade, the Police Officers should\n\ncompletely efface themselves, leaving it to the Magistrate or\n\nthe Panchayatdars to conduct the actual identification\n\nproceedings.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_496\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Facts\" id=\"p_497\">249.        The accused persons should as far as possible\n\nbe mingled with persons of similar description, status, build\n\nand age in the proportion of a minimum of 1:5 and a\n\nmaximum of 1:10, and they must be made to take their\n\npositions   along   with   the   persons    with     whom   they\n\nare mingled up in a line. They should not be made to stand\n\ntogether. The Magistrate or other persons conducting the\n\nparade should satisfy himself or themselves that no Police\n\nOfficer takes part in the actual identification proceedings\n\nand the witnesses are kept out of view from the premises\n\nwhere the parade is taking place and that it is not possible to\n\ncommunicate     with   them      by   signals   or   any    other\n\ncommunication. Witnesses should then be called in, one by\n\none, and they should be asked to go round the persons\n\nassembled for the parade and point out the accused, if any.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_303\">                               278</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_498\">250.        If the identification is held by a Magistrate the\n\nproceedings should be drawn up and signed by him. If the\n\nidentification is held by panchayatdars in the absence of a\n\nmagistrate, the proceedings should be drawn up by one of\n\nthe Panchayatdars and attested by all of them. Statements\n\nmade by the identifying witnesses to the panchayatdars at\n\nthe time of the identification should be recorded in the\n\nproceedings. Even   if   a   witness   makes   a   mistake,   it\n\nshould be recorded. In short, the proceedings must contain\n\na complete record of what takes place in the identification\n\nparade.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_499\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_500\">251.        After the identification by one witness is over,\n\ncare should be taken to see that the witness does not mingle\n\nor communicate with the other witnesses who are yet to\n\nidentify the accused and others. In the next round there\n\nshall be reshuffling and the accused are made to take\n\ndifferent positions. If the accused so desire, they should be\n\nallowed to change their dress also. The same procedure will\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_304\">                                  279</span>\n\n\n\n\nbe repeated in the case of other witnesses also. Any well-\n\nfounded objection raised by the accused during identification\n\nparade should be recorded.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_501\">\n\nPURPOSE OF IDENTIFICATION PARADE\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_502\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1908859\" id=\"span_305\">252.         The Apex Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/1908859/\" id=\"a_290\">HEERA AND\n\nANOTHER Vs STATE OF RAJASTHAN</a> reported in (2007)\n\n10 SCC 175 has observed thus:</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_503\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_260\">       6. As was observed by this Court in Matru v.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_504\">       <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"56524\" id=\"span_306\">State of U.P. (1971 (2) SCC 75) identification tests\n       do not constitute substantive evidence. They are\n       primarily meant for the purpose of helping the\n       investigating agency with an assurance that their\n       progress with the investigation into the offence is\n       proceeding on the right lines. The identification\n       can   only be      used   as    corroborative   of   the\n       statement in court. (See <a href=\"/doc/56524/\" id=\"a_291\">Santokh Singh v. Izhar\n       Hussain</a> (1973 (2) SCC 406). The necessity for\n       holding an identification parade can arise only\n       when the accused are not previously known to\n       the   witnesses.    The   whole     idea   of   a    test\n       identification parade is that witnesses who claim\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_307\">                         280</span>\n\n\n\n\nto have seen the culprits at the time of occurrence\nare to identify them from the midst of other\npersons without any aid or any other source. The\ntest is done to check upon their veracity. In other\nwords, the main object of holding an identification\nparade, during the investigation stage, is to test\nthe memory of the witnesses based upon first\nimpression and also to enable the prosecution to\ndecide whether all or any of them could be cited\nas eyewitnesses of the crime. The identification\nproceedings are in the nature of tests and\nsignificantly, therefore, there is no provision for it\nin the Code and the Evidence Act. It is desirable\nthat a test identification parade should be\nconducted as soon as after the arrest of the\naccused. This becomes necessary to eliminate the\npossibility of the accused being shown to the\nwitnesses prior to the test identification parade.\nThis is a very common plea of the accused and,\ntherefore, the prosecution has to be cautious to\nensure that there is no scope for making such\nallegation. If, however, circumstances are beyond\ncontrol and there is some delay, it cannot be said\nto be fatal to the prosecution.</span>\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_308\">                          281</span><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_505\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_506\">       It is trite to say that the substantive\nevidence is the evidence of identification in Court.\nApart from the clear provisions of <a href=\"/doc/529244/\" id=\"a_292\">Section 9</a> of the\nEvidence Act, the position in law is well settled by\na catena of decisions of this Court. The facts,\nwhich establish the identity of the accused\npersons, are relevant under <a href=\"/doc/529244/\" id=\"a_293\">Section 9</a> of the\nEvidence Act. As a general rule, the substantive\nevidence of a witness is the statement made in\nCourt. The evidence of mere identification of the\naccused person at the trial for the first time is\nfrom its very nature inherently of a weak\ncharacter.    The   purpose      of    a    prior        test\nidentification, therefore, is to test and strengthen\nthe    trustworthiness   of    that   evidence.     It     is\naccordingly considered a safe rule of prudence to\ngenerally look for corroboration of the sworn\ntestimony of witnesses in Court as to the identity\nof the accused who are strangers to them, in the\nform of earlier identification proceedings. This\nrule   of    prudence,   however,      is   subject       to\nexceptions, when, for example, the Court is\nimpressed by a particular witness on whose\ntestimony it can safely rely, without such or other\ncorroboration. The identification parades belong\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_309\">                                 282</span>\n\n\n\n\n       to the stage of investigation, and there is no\n       provision    in   the   Code   which     obliges   the\n       investigating agency to hold or confers a right\n       upon the accused to claim, a test identification\n       parade.     They do     not constitute    substantive\n       evidence and these parades are essentially\n       governed by <a href=\"/doc/1762984/\" id=\"a_294\">Section 162</a> of the Code. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"117134\" id=\"span_310\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"794096\" id=\"span_311\">Failure to\n       hold a test identification parade would not make\n       inadmissible the evidence of identification in\n       Court. The weight to be attached to such\n       identification should be a matter for the Courts of\n       fact. In appropriate cases it may accept the\n       evidence of identification even without insisting\n       on corroboration. [See <a href=\"/doc/794096/\" id=\"a_295\">Kanta Prashad v. Delhi\n       Administration</a> (<a href=\"/doc/117134/\" id=\"a_296\">AIR 1958 SC 350), Vaikuntam\n       Chandrappa and others v. State of Andhra\n       Pradesh</a> (AIR 1960 SC 1340), Budhsen and\n       another v. State of U.P. (AIR 1970 SC 1321) and\n       Rameshwar Singh v. State of              Jammu and\n       Kashmir (AIR 1972 SC 102)].</span></span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_507\">253.         <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"69994712\" id=\"span_312\">In <a href=\"/doc/69994712/\" id=\"a_297\">RAVI KAPUR Vs STATE OF RAJASTHAN</a>\n\nreported in (2012) 9 SCC 284 it is held that:</span>\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_261\">             The Court identification itself is a good\n       identification in the eyes of law. It is not always\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_313\">                         283</span>\n\n\n\n\nnecessary that it must be preceded by the test\nidentification parade. It will always depend upon\nthe facts and circumstances of a given case. In\none case, it may not even be necessary to hold\nthe test identification parade while in the other, it\nmay be essential to do so. Thus, no straightjacket\nformula can be stated in this regard.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_262\">      The whole idea of a Test Identification\nParade is that witnesses who claim to have seen\nthe culprits at the time of occurrence are to\nidentify them from the midst of other persons\nwithout any aid or any other source. The test is\ndone to check upon their veracity. In other words,\nthe main object of holding an identification\nparade, during the investigation stage, is to test\nthe memory of the witnesses based upon first\nimpression and also to enable the prosecution to\ndecide whether all or any of them could be cited\nas eyewitnesses of the crime.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_263\">      It is equally correct that the <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_298\">CrPC</a> does not\noblige the investigating agency to necessarily\nhold the Test Identification Parade. Failure to hold\nthe test identification parade while in police\ncustody, does not by itself render the evidence of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_314\">                            284</span>\n\n\n\n\nidentification      in     court      inadmissible         or\nunacceptable. There have been numerous cases\nwhere the accused is identified by the witnesses\nin the court for the first time. One of the views\ntaken is that identification in court for the first\ntime alone may not form the basis of conviction,\nbut this is not an absolute rule. The purpose of\nthe Test Identification Parade is to test and\nstrengthen the trustworthiness of that evidence. It\nis accordingly considered a safe rule of prudence\nto generally look for corroboration of the sworn\ntestimony of the witnesses in court as to the\nidentity of the accused who are strangers to\nthem,    in   the   form    of     earlier     identification\nproceedings. This rule of prudence is, however\nsubjected to exceptions.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_264\">58. Identification Parade is a tool of investigation\nand is used primarily to strengthen the case of\nthe prosecution on the one hand and to make\ndoubly sure that persons named accused in the\ncase are actually the culprits. The Identification\nParade    primarily      belongs     to      the   stage   of\ninvestigation by the police. The fact that a\nparticular witness has been able to identify the\naccused at an identification parade is only a\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_315\">                               285</span>\n\n\n\n\n       circumstance corroborative of the identification in\n       court. Thus, it is only a relevant consideration\n       which may be examined by the court in view of\n       other attendant circumstances and corroborative\n       evidence with reference to the facts of a given\n       case.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_508\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"18111391\" id=\"span_316\">254.            Further the Supreme Court, in the case of\n\n<a href=\"/doc/18111391/\" id=\"a_299\">MUNNA KUMAR UPADHYAY Vs THE STATE OF ANDHRA\n\nPRADESH</a> reported in 2012 6 SCC 174 laid the ground\n\nrules for the basic necessity of a test identification parade\n\nand it is as below:</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_509\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_265\">       &quot;The necessity for holding a test identification\n       parade can arise only when the accused are not\n       previously known to the witnesses. The whole\n       idea of a test identification parade is that\n       witnesses who claim to have seen the culprits at\n       the time of occurrence are to identify them from\n       the midst of the other persons without any aid or\n       any other source. The test is done to check upon\n       their veracity. In other words, the main object of\n       holding an identification parade is to test the\n       memory of the witnesses based upon first\n       impression and also to enable the prosecution to\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_317\">                                 286</span>\n\n\n\n\n       decide whether all or any of them could be cited\n       as eyewitnesses to the crime. The identification\n       proceedings are in the nature of the tests and\n       significantly therefore, there is no provision for it\n       in the Code and the Evidence Act.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_510\">255.         <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1102670\" id=\"span_318\">In the matter of <a href=\"/doc/1102670/\" id=\"a_300\">MUNSHI SINGH GAUTAM V.\n\nSTATE OF MADHYA PRADESH</a>, reported in (2005) 9 SCC\n\n631, it was stated as follows:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_266\">       &quot;It is trite to say that the substantive evidence is\n       the evidence of identification in Court. Apart from\n       the clear provisions of <a href=\"/doc/529244/\" id=\"a_301\">Section 9</a> of the Evidence\n       Act, the position in law is well settled by a catena\n       of decisions of this Court. The facts, which\n       establish the identity of the accused persons, are\n       relevant under <a href=\"/doc/529244/\" id=\"a_302\">Section 9</a> of the Evidence Act. As a\n       general rule, the substantive evidence of a\n       witness is the statement made in Court. The\n       evidence of mere identification of the accused\n       person at the trial for the first time is from its very\n       nature inherently of a weak character. The\n       purpose of a prior test identification, therefore, is\n       to test and strengthen the trustworthiness of that\n       evidence. It is accordingly considered a safe rule\n       of prudence to generally look for corroboration of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_319\">                                     287</span>\n\n\n\n\n       the sworn testimony of witnesses in Court as to\n       the identity of the accused who are strangers to\n       them,     in    the   form   of    earlier   identification\n       proceedings. This rule of prudence, however, is\n       subject to exceptions, when, for example, the\n       Court is impressed by a particular witness on\n       whose testimony it can safely rely, without such\n       or other corroboration. The identification parades\n       belong to the stage of investigation, and there is\n       no provision in the Code which obliges the\n       investigating agency to hold or confers a right\n       upon the accused to claim, a test identification\n       parade.        They do   not constitute       substantive\n       evidence and these parades are essentially\n       governed by <a href=\"/doc/1762984/\" id=\"a_303\">Section 162</a> of the Code. Failure to\n       hold a test identification parade would not make\n       inadmissible the evidence of identification in\n       Court. The weight to be attached to such\n       identification should be a matter for the Courts of\n       fact. In appropriate cases it may accept the\n       evidence of identification even without insisting\n       on corroboration.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_511\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1102670\" id=\"span_320\">256.           While referring to <a href=\"/doc/1102670/\" id=\"a_304\">MUNSHI SINGH GAUTAM</a>\n\ncase supra it is held as below in SHEO SHANKAR SINGH V\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_321\">                                 288</span>\n\n\n\n\nSTATE OF JHARKHAND AND ANR. reported in (2011) 3\n\nSCC 654:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_267\">        58.    Identification   Parade       is     a    tool   of\n        investigation     and   is    used        primarily     to\n        strengthen the case of the prosecution on\n        the one hand and to make doubly sure that\n        persons named accused in the case are\n        actually    the   culprits.   The         Identification\n        Parade primarily belongs to the stage of\n        investigation by the police. The fact that a\n        particular witness has been able to identify\n        the accused at an identification parade is\n        only a circumstance corroborative of the\n        identification in court. Thus, it is only a\n        relevant    consideration      which            may     be\n        examined by the court in view of other\n        attendant circumstances and corroborative\n        evidence with reference to the facts of a\n        given case.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_512\">257.          In HARBHAJAN SINGH V. STATE OF JAMMU\n\nAND KASHMIR reported in          (1975) 4 SCC 480, though a\n\ntest identification parade was not held, the Apex Court\n\nupheld the conviction on the basis of the identification in the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_322\">                              289</span>\n\n\n\n\nCourt corroborated by other circumstantial evidence. In that\n\ncase it was found that the appellant and one Gurmukh\n\nSingh were absent at the time of roll call and when they were\n\narrested on the night of 16th December, 1971 their rifles\n\nsmelt of fresh gunpowder and that the empty cartridge case\n\nwhich was found at the scene of offence bore distinctive\n\nmarkings showing that the bullet which killed the deceased\n\nwas fired from the rifle of the appellant. Noticing these\n\ncircumstances the Court held:-\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_268\">            <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1956474\" id=\"span_323\">&quot;In view of this corroborative evidence we\n      find no substance in the argument urged on\n      behalf of the appellant that the Investigating\n      Officer ought to have held an identification\n      parade and that the failure of Munshi Ram to\n      mention the names of the two accused to the\n      neighbours who came to the scene immediately\n      after the occurrence shows that his story cannot\n      be true. As observed by this Court in <a href=\"/doc/1956474/\" id=\"a_305\">Jadunath\n      Singh v. State of U.P.</a> (AIR 1971 SC 363) absence\n      of test identification is not necessarily fatal. The\n      fact that Munshi Ram did not disclose the names\n      of the two accused to the villages only shows that\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_324\">                                  290</span>\n\n\n\n\n       the accused were not previously known to him\n       and the story that the accused referred to each\n       other by their respective names during the course\n       of   the   incident     contains    an   element     of\n       exaggeration. The case does not rest on the\n       evidence   of    Munshi       Ram   alone     and   the\n       corroborative circumstances to which we have\n       <a href=\"/doc/1956474/\" id=\"a_306\">referred to above</a> lend enough assurance to the\n       implication of the appellant.&quot;</span>\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_513\">258.         In   the   case    of     STATE    OF    ORISSA     VS.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_514\">CHHAGANLAL SUNDARKA AND OTHERS reported in 1977\n\nCRI L.J. 319 it is held that:\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_515\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_269\">       17. ............. the law is well settled that the\n       substantive evidence is the statement of a\n       witness in Court and the purpose of T.I. parade\n       is to test that evidence, the safe rule being that\n       the sworn testimony of the witness in Court as to\n       the identity of the accused who is a stranger to\n       him, as a general rule, requires corroboration in\n       the form of an earlier identification proceeding. If\n       there is no substantive evidence about the\n       respondent having been one of the dacoits whom\n       P.W. 10 saw on the night of occurrence, then the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_325\">                                291</span>\n\n\n\n\nT.I. parade as against him cannot be of any\nassistance to the prosecution.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_516\">Identification parades are ordinarily held at the\ninstance of the Investigating Officer for the\npurpose of enabling the witnesses to identify\neither the properties which are the subject-matter\nof alleged offence or the persons who are alleged\nto have been concerned in the offence. Such tests\nor parades belong to an investigation stage and\nthey serve to provide the Investigating authority\nwith material to assure themselves that the\ninvestigation is proceeding on the right lines. It is\naccordingly desirable that such test parades are\nheld at the earliest possible opportunity. Early\nopportunity to identify also tends to minimise the\nchances      of   the    memory of       the     identifying\nwitnesses fading away by reason of long lapse\nof   time.    But       much    more     vital   factor   in\ndetermining the value of such identification\nparades is the effectiveness of the precautions\ntaken by those responsible for holding them\nagainst the identifying witnesses having an\nopportunity of seeing the persons to be identified\nby them before they are paraded with other\npersons      and    also       against   the     identifying\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_326\">                                     292</span>\n\n\n\n\n       witnesses being provided by the investigating\n       authority with other unfair aid or assistance so\n       as to facilitate the identification of the accused\n       concerned. The evidence of identification of an\n       accused for the first time in the dock is\n       inherently weak and more specially so when it is\n       done after a lapse of considerable time. But\n       where        a   direct    witness      has   consistently\n       identified the accused, both at the test parade\n       and at the trial his identification may safely be\n       accepted, unless there are other circumstances\n       indicating that the witness has probably made a\n       mistake. But when the identification in Court is\n       not supported by an earlier test, it is unsafe to\n       act   on     that      evidence    in   the   absence   of\n       exceptional circumstances such as adequate\n       descriptive particulars of the accused given\n       earlier by the witness which corroborate the\n       identification.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_517\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_518\">259.           <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"767522\" id=\"span_327\">In       the      case     of    <a href=\"/doc/767522/\" id=\"a_307\">SURYAMOORTHI        V.\n\nGOVINDASWAMY</a> reported in                    AIR 1989 SC 1410 the\n\nSupreme Court observed thus (para 10):</span>\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_328\">                                293</span><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_519\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_270\">       &quot;It is, however, in evidence that before, the\n       identification parades were held the photographs\n       of the accused persons had appeared in the local\n       daily newspapers. Besides, the accused persons\n       were in the lock-up for a few days before the\n       identification parades were held and therefore\n       the possibility of their having been shown to the\n       witnesses cannot be ruled out altogether. We do\n       not, therefore, attach much importance to the\n       identification   made    at   the   identification\n       parades.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_520\">260.         Nevertheless three of the accused were convicted\n\nby the Supreme Court since corroborative evidence was\n\navailable. The court adopted a cautious approach and since\n\nregarding other accused corroboration was not forthcoming,\n\nSupreme Court confirmed their acquittal.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_521\">261.         From the catena decisions <a href=\"/doc/767522/\" id=\"a_308\">referred above</a>, it can\n\nbe deduced that when a witness identifies an accused in\n\ncourt, the court has to appreciate the evidence in the light of\n\nits intrinsic worth, other evidence, circumstances and\n\nprobabilities. If the witness knew the accused previously it is\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_329\">                               294</span>\n\n\n\n\na matter of recognition; if not, it is a matter of identification.\n\nRecognition of a familiar person is certainly more reliable\n\nthan identification of a stranger. When a stranger witness\n\nidentifies an accused in court, the court, by way of caution\n\nor prudence may seek some assurance before accepting the\n\nidentification as correct. That assurance would be available\n\nfrom the positive result of test identification parade; however\n\nabsence of test identification parade is not invariably fatal,\n\nfor assurance may be available from other sources and\n\ncircumstances.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_522\">\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_523\">262.         <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"858257\" id=\"span_330\">In <a href=\"/doc/858257/\" id=\"a_309\">CHANDRAN V. STATE OF KERALA</a> (1987) 1\n\nKER LT 391 reported in (1986 Cri LJ 1865) and\n\nARAVINDAN V. STATE OF KERALA reported in (1989) 2\n\nKer LT (Sh. N.) page 2(2)       practically all decisions of the\n\nSupreme Court on the point have been considered. As\n\nobserved in this decision, a witness might have had a fair\n\nopportunity to see the accused, of noting his appearance and\n\nfeatures; there might have been something in the context,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_331\">                                 295</span>\n\n\n\n\nmanner and circumstance of the meeting which would\n\nrender it easy for the appearance of the accused to fix itself\n\nin the memory of the witness or something might have taken\n\nplace shortly thereafter which would have enabled the\n\nwitness to recollect the prior meeting and the person\n\nconcerned. These and a variety of other circumstances may\n\nlend assurance to the mind of the court in acting on the\n\nidentification made in court.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_524\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_525\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"189653\" id=\"span_332\">263.        Value   of   test    identification   parade,   as   a\n\ncorroborative piece of evidence, depends on the safeguards\n\nadopted in conducting it. Witness should not get an\n\nopportunity to see the accused; there must not be any\n\nsituation in which witness could be prompted. Suspects\n\nmust be mixed up among adequate number of non-suspects\n\nwho are more or less similar in appearance to that of\n\nsuspects and dressed more or less similarly. It must be\n\narranged at the earliest possible opportunity. As observed in\n\n<a href=\"/doc/189653/\" id=\"a_310\">ARAVINDAN V. STATE OF KERALA</a> (1989) 2 Ker LT (Sh.N.)\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_333\">                              296</span>\n\n\n\n\npage 2(2) value of test identify cation parade is determined\n\nby the effectiveness of precautions taken, expedition in\n\nconducting it and the attendant circumstances, Just as\n\nshowing an accused to the witness just before the parade\n\nmay    undermine    the   value    of   the   parade,   showing\n\nphotographs of the accused to the witness just before parade\n\nmay have the same consequence.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_526\">\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_527\">264.        The whole idea of holding Test Identification\n\nParade is that witnesses who claim to have seen the culprits\n\nat the time of occurrence are to identify them from the midst\n\nof other persons without any aid or any other source. The\n\ntest is done to check upon their veracity. In other words, the\n\nmain object of holding an identification parade, during the\n\ninvestigation stage, is to test the memory of the witnesses\n\nbased upon first impression and also to enable the\n\nprosecution to decide whether all or any of them could be\n\ncited as eyewitnesses of the crime.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_334\">                              297</span><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_528\">\n\n\n\n\nPHOTOGRAPHS\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_529\">265.        In the present case, the argument of the learned\n\ncounsel for the accused rests on the fact that photographs\n\nwere shown to the witness prior to identification and that\n\nthis would hinder the actual progress of the T.I Parade.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_530\">266.        In the case of LAXMIPAT CHORARIA &amp; ORS.\n\nVS STATE OF MAHARASHTRA reported in AIR 1968 SC\n\n938, a reference was made to the English cases thus:\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_271\">        &quot;that the showing of a large number of\n        photographs to a witness and asking him to\n        pick out that of the suspect is a proper\n        procedure but showing a photograph and\n        asking the witness whether it is of the\n        offender is improper. We need not refer to\n        these cases because we entirely agree with\n        the proposition. There can be no doubt that if\n        the intention is to rely on the identification of\n        the suspect by a witness, his ability to identify\n        should be tested without showing him the\n        suspect or his photograph, or furnishing him\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_335\">                                  298</span>\n\n\n\n\n           the    data   for   identification.   Showing   a\n           photograph prior to the identification makes\n           the identification worthless.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_531\">267.             In the present case, it is not clear whether a\n\ngroup of photographs or individual photos were shown to the\n\nwitness.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_532\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_533\">268.             <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"212754\" id=\"span_336\">In the matter of <a href=\"/doc/212754/\" id=\"a_311\">N.J SURAJ V. STATE</a> reported\n\nin (2004) 11 SCC 346, it was held that if a photograph is\n\nshown to a witness prior to the Test Identification Parade\n\nthen the identification so made becomes meaningless.</span>\n\n<span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"166977\" id=\"span_337\">Furthermore, in the matter of <a href=\"/doc/166977/\" id=\"a_312\">Vijayan v. State of Kerala</a>\n\n(1999) 3 SCC 54, not only was the photograph of the\n\naccused shown to the witness but it was published in all\n\nlocal newspapers. It was thus held that the test identification\n\nparade was thus rightly disbelieved by the trial Judge.</span>\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_534\">269.             In   SURYANELLI&#x27;s      case,    Justice   Chauhan\n\nobserved that:\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_338\">                                 299</span><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_535\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_272\">       &quot;Conducting   a   test   identification   parade     is\n       meaningless if the witnesses knows the accused,\n       or if they have been shown his photographs, or if\n       he has been exposed by the media to the public.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_536\">270.         <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1324327\" id=\"span_339\">In the case of <a href=\"/doc/1324327/\" id=\"a_313\">APPU AND OTHERS Vs THE\n\nSTATE</a>, reported in 1990 CRI.L.J. 2281, placing reliance on\n\nthe decision of the Apex Court in the case of LAXMIPAT\n\nCHORARIA cited supra it was held that:</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_537\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_273\">       &quot;There can be no doubt that if the intention is to\n       rely on the identification of the suspect by a\n       witness, his ability to identify should be tested\n       without   showing    him    the   suspect   or     his\n       photograph, or furnishing him the data for\n       identification. Showing a photograph prior to the\n       identification makes the identification worthless.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_538\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"204255\" id=\"span_340\">271.         The Apex Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/204255/\" id=\"a_314\">SURENDRA\n\nNARAIN @ MUNNA PANDEY VS. THE STATE OF U.P</a>\n\nreported in (1997) INSC 818 has observed thus:</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_341\">                          300</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_274\">7. .........       In   Inre   Sangiah   49     Cr.     L.J.89\nRajamannar, J discussed the matter at length\nand said thus:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_275\">I am unable to find any provision in the Code\nwhich entitles an accused to demand that an\nidentification parade should be held at or before\nthe enquiry or the trial. An identification parade\nbelongs to the stage of investigation by the\npolice. The question whether a witness has or\nhas not identified the accused during the\ninvestigation is not one which is in itself relevant\nat the trial. The actual evidence regarding\nidentification is that which is given by the\nwitnesses in the Court. The fact that a particular\nwitness    identification   parade       is     only   a\ncircumstance corroborative of the identification in\na Court. If a witness has not identified the\naccused at the parade or otherwise during the\ninvestigation the fact may be relied on by the\naccused, but I find nothing in the provisions of\nthe Code which confers a right on the accused to\ndemand     that   the    investigation    should       be\nconducted in a particular way. As M.W.N. 427\n&quot;Identification Parades are held not for the\npurpose of giving defence advocates material to\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_342\">                                301</span>\n\n\n\n\n       work on, but in order to satisfy investigating\n       officers of the bone fine of the prosecution\n       witnesses&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_539\">272.          Therefore, before the identification parade, if the\n\nwitness is shown the photographs of the accused to be\n\nidentified,   then   the   identification   so   made   becomes\n\nmeaningless, in which event the Court has to look to other\n\ncorroborative evidence available on record.       When a direct\n\nwitness has consistently identified the accused, both at the\n\ntest parade and at the trial and before the test parade, his\n\nidentification may safely be accepted.      Merely because the\n\nidentification parade conducted is defective, the judgment\n\nwould not get vitiated, when other circumstantial and\n\ncorroborative evidence is available on record to prove the\n\nidentity of the accused.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_540\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_541\">273.          We have on record Spot Mahazar, Seizure\n\nMahazar and Inquest Mahazars, which are marked in the\n\ncase through witnesses. The particulars of the same are as\n\nunder:\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_343\">                              302</span><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_542\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_276\">(a) SPOT MAHAZARS\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_543\">274.         Ex.P2   dated   10.07.2000    and    Ex.P3   dated\n\n14.07.2000 are the two spot mahazars in this case. While\n\nEx.P3 is only a spot mahazar, Ex.P2 falls under the category\n\nof spot cum seizure mahazar as it not only gives a clear\n\npicture of scene of occurrence immediately after the blast,\n\nbut also the information with regard to seizure of M.O.1 to\n\nM.O.12 from the spot.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_544\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_545\">275.         Ex.P2 dated 10.07.2000 is drawn between 8:00\n\nand 10:00 a.m. by PW52, N.Hanumanthappa P.I. of JJ Nagar\n\nPS, Bangalore in the presence of Father Tomayar PW1 and\n\nthe two panch witnesses secured for the purpose of\n\nconducting mahazar at the blast site      i.e., PW2 B.Jesudas\n\nand    one   sagainathan.    PW52,   PW1    and    PW2    have\n\nconsistently spoken about inspection of the blast site by\n\nPW52, noting the damage caused to the church building due\n\nto the impact of bomb blast, situation of main gate of the\n\nchurch on southern side etc. and also seizure of M.O.1 to 12\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_344\">                               303</span>\n\n\n\n\nunder Ex.P2.        Ex.P2(a), Ex.P2(b) and Ex.P2(c) are the\n\nsignatures of PW1, PW2 and PW52 respectively.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_546\">276.        Ex.P3 dated 14.07.2000 is drawn by PW57,\n\nV.S.D&#x27;Souza after assuming further investigation of the case\n\nby visiting the place of occurrence i.e., St. Peter and Paul\n\nChurch, in the presence of PW1 and other panch witnesses.\n\nThe scene of crime as well as existence of 11 shops on JJ\n\nmain road and 10 shops on Rayapur main road in the\n\nchurch premises are noted down in this mahazar. Ex.P3(a)\n\nand P3(b) are the signatures of PW1 and PW57 respectively\n\nand these two witnesses have deposed fully supporting the\n\nprosecution case.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_547\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_548\">277.        The accused have cross examined PW1, PW2\n\nand PW52 with regard to drawing of mahazar Ex.P2 and\n\nseizure of MO 1 to MO 12 there under elaborately. Likewise\n\nthey have also cross examined PW1 and PW57 with regard to\n\nEx.P3. However nothing worthwhile is elicited to disbelieve\n\nthe prosecution case with regard to drawing up of Ex.P2 and\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_345\">                              304</span>\n\n\n\n\nEx.P3 at the spot and seizure of MO1 to MO12 under Ex.P2\n\nin the presence of the witnesses thereto. Therefore no fault\n\ncan be found with the findings recorded by the trial Court in\n\nthis behalf.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_549\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_550\">(b) SEIZURE MAHAZAR\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_551\">278.           There are in all 7 seizures mahazars produced\n\nand marked in this case.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_552\">       The original mahazars relating to seizure of MO13 to\n\nMO29 from the house of A12; MO30 to MO37 from the house\n\nof A-13; MO38 to MO75 from A-12&#x27;s work place and MO76 to\n\nMO80, the documents in the handwriting of A-11, seized\n\nfrom his workplace by PW64 M.B.Appanna in the presence of\n\npanch witnesses, have been produced in SC 423/2001\n\npending on the file of the same Court as such they were\n\ncalled for and marked in this case as Ex.P80, Ex.P81,\n\nEx.P82 and Ex.P83 respectively. After obtaining the certified\n\ncopies of Ex.P8o to Ex.P83, they are marked as Ex.P247,\n\nEx.P248, Ex.P250 and Ex.P251 respectively.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_346\">                                 305</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_553\">279.        PW    64   states    that   on   receipt   of   credible\n\ninformation, he went to the house of A-12 at 5th Cross,\n\nGundappa Street, R.T.Nagar, Bangalore, along with the staff\n\nand panchas on 15.07.2000 and searched the entire house;\n\nthere he found the passport of Amanat Hussain Mullaha,\n\ninvitations for the conference, invitation sent to Pope Paul,\n\nletter by Deendar Ashram, invitation letter of the Ashram,\n\nlist of institutions of Bangalore City, photocopy of passport of\n\nSheikh Dawood Abdul Lathief etc. and seized them under the\n\nmahazar Ex.P.80 (Ex.P247) in the presence of panch\n\nwitnesses. PW49 a witness to this mahazar has also spoken\n\naccordingly and identified MO13 to 29 as the incriminating\n\narticles seized by PW64 from the house of A-12.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_554\">280.        PW64 states that pursuant to the voluntary\n\nstatement of A-13, he alongiwth panch witness and staff\n\nwent to the house of A-13 at No.1683/9, Prasahant Nagar,\n\nChickaballapur on 23.07.2000 and A-13 himself produced\n\nsix English pamphlets of &quot;warning&quot;, five urdu pamphlets,\n\nbook in urdu and photo copy of portions of the judgement of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_347\">                                 306</span>\n\n\n\n\nDharwad          Court,      Kannada    book      of   Deendar\n\nChannabasaveshwara and some photos in it, letter written\n\nby     Islami    Bithulmal    Chikkaballapur,   kannada   book\n\n&quot;Lingayat&quot; and Sandhyavandam Mahasankalpam etc.             He\n\nseized them under the mahazar Ex.P81 (Ex.P248) and\n\nobtained the signatures of the panch witnesses.           PW50\n\nD.N.Munikrishnappa a panch witness to this mahazar has\n\nspoken in support of the prosecution case, identified the\n\narticles seized from the house of A-13 at MO30 to MO37.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_555\">281.            PW64 states that on 29.07.2000 he took A-12 to\n\nBEML factory at New Thippasandra, where A-12 was\n\nworking as lathe machine operator.              He secured the\n\nAssistant General Manager of the factory, G.Vishwanath and\n\none N.Venkatesha, searched the workplace of A-12 in their\n\npresence. By this time having learnt from the media that A-\n\n12 was arrested in connection with bomb blast case, the\n\nfactory authorities had locked and sealed the table drawer of\n\nA-12. At the time of search this was opened and MO38 to\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_348\">                             307</span>\n\n\n\n\nMO75 were seized from there. Ex.P82 (Ex.250) is drawn\n\nevidence in this seizure.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_556\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_557\">282.        Ex.P83 (Ex.P251) dated 03.08.2000 is drawn\n\nwhile seizing MO76 to MO80 from the drawer of A-11&#x27;s table\n\nat BEML factory on 03.08.2000. A-11 was working as clerk\n\nin the Administrative Branch of BEML factory. PW64 states\n\nthat by the time he took A-11 to BEML factory, A-11&#x27;s table\n\nwas locked and sealed by the factory security on coming to\n\nknow about the arrest of A-11 and A-12 in connection with\n\nthese cases. He got opened the same and searched it in the\n\npresence of AGM of the factory G.Vishwanath and another\n\npanch witness, A.Maria Nesan, an Engineer working in the\n\nsame factory.   MO76 to MO80 were found in A-11&#x27;s table\n\ndrawer and the same was seized by drawing mahazar.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_558\">283.        Except questioning PW64 as to what prevented\n\nhim from searching the table drawer of A-11 on 29.07.2000\n\nitself when he had gone to search the workplace of A-12,\n\nnothing material is suggested to discredit   the witness to\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_349\">                              308</span>\n\n\n\n\nEx.P80 to Ex.P83 or any evidence is elicited from them to\n\ndisbelieve the prosecution case.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_559\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_560\">284.        The Trial Court while appreciating the evidence\n\nof the prosecution with regard to the seizure of material\n\nobjects has come to the right conclusion that whenever\n\ninvestigation takes place in a case, the IO investigates each\n\naccused separately and therefore PW64 could not search or\n\nseize MO76 to MO80 from A-11&#x27;s table drawer on the day of\n\nseizing MO38 to MO75 from A-12&#x27;s table drawer on\n\n29.07.2000. This reasoning appears sound.      Moreover the\n\nevidence that MO76 to MO80 are all in A-11&#x27;s handwriting is\n\nnot disputed and therefore the contention of the accused\n\nthat police fabricated them and then placed the same in A-\n\n11&#x27;s table drawer cannot be accepted. Therefore we find no\n\nreason to disturb the findings of the Trial court with regard\n\nto seizure of MO13 to MO80 under Ex.P80 to Ex.P83.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_561\">285.
