PW57 V.S.D&#x27;Souza has deposed that A-11 led\n\nhim his staff and panchas Byrappa and V.S.Venugopal to a\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_350\">                                  309</span>\n\n\n\n\nroom in house bearing No.12/45, 1st floor, &#x27;C&#x27; Cross, Behind\n\nMinerva Mills, Gopalapura, Magadi Road, Bangalore on\n\n20.01.2000, took out a suitcase kept below the cot, opened\n\nthe    same     with   a   key   in   his   pocket   and   produced\n\nincriminating materials at Ex.P129 to Ex.P134 and he seized\n\nthe same under Ex.P135.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_562\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_563\">286.          On 21.12.2000 according to PW57, A-28 led him\n\nhis staff and panchas to the house situated at BTM Layout\n\nproduced the photograph of absconding A-1 which is marked\n\nas Ex.P136 and other incriminating material at Ex.P137 to\n\nEx.P140. He seized them all under the mahazar Ex.P141 in\n\nthe presence of panchas and affixed signature as per\n\nEx.P141(a). The accused have not seriously cross examined\n\nthe IO with regard to drawing up of Ex.P135 and Ex.P141 as\n\nwell as seizure of Ex.P129 to Ex.P134 from the house of A-11\n\nand Ex.P137 to Ex.P140 from the house of A-28 thereunder\n\nrespectively.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_351\">                              310</span><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_564\">\n\n\n\n\n(c ) INQUEST MAHAZARS\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_565\">287.        A-10, A-14 and A-15 were transporting bombs in\n\na maruthi van on 09.07.2000.       The bomb so transported\n\nexploded accidently at 9.30 pm on the same day, in front of\n\nMinerva Mills, as a result of which A-14 and A-15, sustained\n\nburn injuries and died at the spot.         The inquest was\n\nconducted on 10.07.2000 at Victoria Hospital, Bangalore.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_566\">288.        Ex.P161 and Ex.P167 are the inquest mahazars\n\npertaining to A-15 and A-14 respectively. Since the original\n\ninquest mahazars are produced in SC 423/2001, the\n\ncertified copy of the same is produced in this case.\n\n\n\nPOINT NO.8\n\nMAHAZAR WITNESSES TURNED HOSTILE\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_567\">289.        It   was   contended    that   the   case   of   the\n\nprosecution that the incriminating materials were seized\n\nfrom the houses and possession of the accused cannot be\n\nbelieved as the panch witnesses have turned hostile.\n\nTherefore the said piece of evidence cannot be made the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_352\">                              311</span>\n\n\n\n\nbasis for conviction against the accused. Factually, it is not\n\na case where all the panch witnesses have turned hostile.\n\nWhen the incriminating articles were seized from the houses\n\nof these accused, the Police have taken signature of the\n\nfamily members of these accused as panch witness, apart\n\nfrom outsiders. Though these witnesses have spoken about\n\nthese accused and some of them have admitted that they are\n\nthe members of Deendar Anjuman Ashram, computers were\n\nfound in their houses at the time of search and their inter-\n\nrelationships. In the cross examination they have denied the\n\nseizure. As against this evidence, we have the evidence of\n\nother panch witnesses who have stood by their signatures to\n\nthe mahazars.     More importantly, the prosecution has\n\nexamined the Investigating Officers who recovered the\n\nmaterial objects from the houses of these accused.      All of\n\nthem have spoken about the recovery of material objects and\n\nthe search and seizures conducted by them.          All those\n\nmahazars are marked in this case. The signatures are also\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_353\">                              312</span>\n\n\n\n\nmarked.    The question is what is the effect of Mahazar\n\nwitnesses turning hostile.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_568\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_569\">290.        If panch witnesses turn hostile, which happens\n\nvery often in criminal cases, the evidence of the person, who\n\neffected the recovery would not stand vitiated. There is,\n\nhowever, no rule of law that the evidence of police officials\n\nhas to be discarded or that it suffers from some inherent\n\ninfirmity. Prudence, however, requires that the evidence of\n\nthe police officials, who are interested in the outcome of the\n\nresult of the case, needs to be carefully scrutinized and\n\nindependently appreciated. The police officials do not suffer\n\nfrom any disability to give evidence and the mere fact that\n\nthey are police officials does not by itself give rise to any\n\ndoubt about their credit worthiness.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_570\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_571\">291.        The statement of the investigating officer has to\n\nbe reliable and so trustworthy that even if the attesting\n\nwitnesses to the seizure turns hostile, the same can still be\n\nrelied upon, more so, when it is otherwise corroborated by\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_354\">                                   313</span>\n\n\n\n\nthe     prosecution   evidence.    Merely   because   the   police\n\nwitnesses have spoken about the search and the seizure of\n\ndocuments from the custody of the appellant, their version\n\ncannot be disbelieved as the independent witnesses have not\n\nsupported the search and the seizure of the documents.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_572\">292.          In the case of      MODAN SINGH V. STATE OF\n\nRAJASTHAN reported in 1978 (4) SCC 435 it was observed\n\nthus:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_277\">        &quot;where the evidence of the investigating officer\n        who recovered the material objects is convincing,\n        the evidence as to recovery need not be rejected\n        on the ground that seizure witnesses did not\n        support the prosecution version.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_573\">293.          <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"81332\" id=\"span_355\">In the case reported in 2001 [1] <a href=\"/doc/81332/\" id=\"a_315\">CRIMES 176\n\n[SC]- STATE GOVT. OF NCT OF DELHI V. SUNIL &amp; ANR.</a>, it\n\nis held as under:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_278\">              &quot;There is no requirement either under\n        <a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_316\">Section 27</a> of the Evidence Act or under <a href=\"/doc/447673/\" id=\"a_317\">Section\n        161</a> of the Code of Criminal Procedure, to obtain\n        signature of independent witnesses on the record\n        in which statement of an accused is written. The\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_356\">                             314</span>\n\n\n\n\nlegal    obligation    to    call      independent         and\nrespectable inhabitants of the locality to attend\nand witness the exercise made by the police is\ncast on the police office when searches are made\nunder Chapter VII of the Code. <a href=\"/doc/1942836/\" id=\"a_318\">Section 100(5)</a> of\nthe Code requires that such search shall be made\nin their presence and a list of all things seized in\nthe course of such search and of the places in\nwhich they are respectively found, shall be\nprepared by such officer or other person &quot;and\nsigned    by    such    witnesses&quot;.          It     must    be\nremembered that search is made to find out a\nthing or document which the searching officer has\nno prior idea where the thing or document is kept.\nHe prowls for it either on reasonable suspicion or\non some guess work that it could possibly be\nferreted out in such prowling. It is a stark reality\nthat during searches the team which conducts\nsearch would have to meddle with lots of other\narticles and documents also and in such process\nmany such articles or documents are likely to be\ndisplaced or ever strewn helter-skelter. The\nlegislative idea in insisting on such searches to be\nmade     in   the   presence      of   two        independent\ninhabitants of the locality is to ensure the safety\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_357\">                        315</span>\n\n\n\n\nof all such articles meddled with and to protect\nthe rights of the persons entitled thereto. But\nrecovery of an object pursuant to the information\nsupplied by an accused in custody is different\nfrom the searching endeavour envisaged in\nChapter VII of the Code.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_279\">      It is a fallacious impression that when\nrecovery is effected pursuant to any statement\nmade by the accused the document prepared by\nthe Investigating Officer contemporaneous with\nsuch recovery must necessarily be attested by\nindependent witnesses. Of course, if any such\nstatement leads to recovery of any article it is\nopen to the Investigating Officer to take the\nsignature of any person present at that time, on\nthe document prepared for such recovery. But if\nno witness was present or if no person had\nagreed to affix his signature on the document, it\nis difficult to lay down, as a proposition of law,\nthat the document so prepared by the police\nofficer must be treated as tainted and the\nrecovery evidence unreliable. The court has to\nconsider the evidence of the Investigating Officer\nwho deposed to the fact of recovery based on the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_358\">                          316</span>\n\n\n\n\nstatement elucidated from the accused on its own\nworth.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_280\">      It is an archaic notion that action of the\npolice officer should be approached with initial\ndistrust. We are aware that such a notion was\nlavishly entertained during British period and\npolicemen also knew about it. Its hang over\npersisted during post-independent years but it is\ntime now to start placing at least initial trust on\nthe actions and the documents made by the\npolice. At any rte, the court cannot start with the\npresumption     that    the    police   records    are\nuntrustworthy. As a Proposition of law the\npresumption should be the other way around.\nThat official acts of the police have been regularly\nperformed is a wise principle of presumption and\nrecognized even by the legislature. Hence when a\npolice officer gives evidence in court that a certain\narticle was recovered by him on the strength of\nthe statement made by the accused it is open to\nthe court to believe the version to be correct if it is\nnot otherwise shown to be unreliable. It is for the\naccused, through cross-examination of witnesses\nor through any other materials, to show that the\nevidence of the police officer is either unreliable or\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_359\">                               317</span>\n\n\n\n\n       at least unsafe to be acted upon in a particular\n       case. If the Court has any good reason to suspect\n       the truthfulness of such records of the police the\n       court could certainly take into account the fact\n       that no other independent person was present at\n       the time of recovery. But it is not a legally\n       approvable procedure to presume the police action\n       as unreliable to start with, not to jettison such\n       action merely for the reason that police did not\n       collect signatures of independent persons in the\n       documents made contemporaneous with such\n       actions.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_574\">294.         In this particular decision, there Lordships ruled\n\nthat when the Investigation Officer has made recoveries in\n\nfurtherance of the disclosure statement made by the\n\naccused, then it is not necessary that the panchas should be\n\npresent there and they should sign the recovery statement\n\nprepared by the Investigation Officer. Because<a href=\"/doc/447673/\" id=\"a_319\"> S.161</a> Cr.P.C.\n\ndoes not contemplate the presence of panchas and their\n\nsignatures. So, though the panchas were not present there\n\nand though they were present they turned hostile later, the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_360\">                                   318</span>\n\n\n\n\nrecovery can be taken as proved at the instance of the\n\nrespective accused.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_575\">\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_576\">295.         <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"914739\" id=\"span_361\">In the case of <a href=\"/doc/914739/\" id=\"a_320\">VAHAJI RAVAJI THAKORE AND\n\nANOTHER V. STATE OF GUJARAT</a> reported in 2004\n\nCRL.L.J.119 [Gujarath High Court] , it is held as under:</span>\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_281\">       35.   In view of aforesaid legal position, we are\n       of considered view that merely because the panch\n       witnesses do not support the case of the\n       prosecution, the case of the prosecution need not\n       be thrown over-board as unreliable. It must be\n       realized that the phenomenon of panch witnesses\n       turning hostile to the prosecution is not unknown\n       and is ever on the increase. It needs hardly to be\n       emphasized that the decision of a case does not\n       depend solely on the question whether the panch\n       witnesses support the prosecution or turn their\n       back on it. If the decision of the case were to\n       depend     solely    on   the     testimony        of   panch\n       witnesses        regardless      of        the   evidence   of\n       independent witnesses, in theory, it would be\n       giving a right of veto to the panchas so far as the\n       question    of     culpability        of    an   accused    is\n       concerned. If the evidence of police officer is\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_362\">                               319</span>\n\n\n\n\n       otherwise found to be true and dependable,\n       judicial pragmatism requires that merely because\n       the panchas do not support, it should not be\n       made a ground to discard his evidence.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_577\">296.         In this particular decision, their Lordships held\n\nthat though the panch witnesses turned hostile, since the\n\nInvestigation Officer recovered the material objects in\n\nfurtherance of the disclosure statement made by the\n\naccused, it has to be held that the discovery of material\n\nobject is proved at the instance of that accused on basis of\n\nthe evidence of Investigation Officer.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_578\">\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_579\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"107929320\" id=\"span_363\">297.         The Apex Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/107929320/\" id=\"a_321\">GOVINDARAJU @\n\nGOVINDA V. STATE BY SRIRAMPURA P.S</a>, reported in\n\n(2012) 4 SCC 722, held as under:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_282\">              &quot;However, it is to be noted that in such\n        cases, the statement of the investigating officer\n        has to be reliable and so trustworthy that even\n        if the attesting witnesses to the seizure turns\n        hostile, the same can still be relied upon, more\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_364\">                               320</span>\n\n\n\n\n        so, when it is otherwise corroborated by the\n        prosecution evidence, which is certainly not\n        there in the present case.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_580\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"103258668\" id=\"span_365\">298.         The Apex Court, also in the case of <a href=\"/doc/103258668/\" id=\"a_322\">SAFI MOHD\n\nV. STATE OF RAJASTHAN</a>, reported in (2013) 8 SCC 601\n\nhas held thus:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_283\">             <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"103258668\" id=\"span_366\">20. After referring to the evidence of the\n       PW-22 and PW-24 the search of the house of the\n       appellant and seizure of certain documents along\n       with diary particularly Ex D-3, handwritten map\n       prepared with certain markings, it has proved the\n       prosecution case. No doubt the independent\n       witnesses have turned hostile, but the learned\n       sessions judge has rightly accepted the testimony\n       of the police witnesses after proper appreciation\n       of their evidence and he has rightly <a href=\"/doc/103258668/\" id=\"a_323\">placed\n       reliance upon</a> the police witnesses to prove the\n       seizure of the documents from the house of the\n       appellant and therefore the same cannot be held\n       to be bad in law as contended by the learned\n       counsel for the appellant.</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_284\">             <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"503552\" id=\"span_367\">22. The learned public prosecutor has\n       rightly placed reliance on the decision of this\n       Court in <a href=\"/doc/503552/\" id=\"a_324\">Sama Alana Abdulla Vs. State of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_368\">                          321</span>\n\n\n\n\nGujarat</a>[8]. In <a href=\"/doc/503552/\" id=\"a_325\">the said decision</a> this court lays\ndown the legal principle that merely because the\npolice witnesses have spoken about the search\nand the seizure of documents from the custody of\nthe appellant, their version cannot be disbelieved\nas the independent witnesses have not supported\nthe search and the seizure of the documents. The\nobservations made by this Court in the above\nreferred case are applied to the facts of the case\nin hand to accept the proof of search and seizure\nof the documents from the house of the appellant\nwhich are very important and sensitive for the\nintegrity and security of the Nation. The said\nconclusions arrived at by the learned sessions\njudge and concurrence of the same by the High\nCourt cannot be termed as erroneous in law as\ncontended by learned counsel on behalf of the\nappellant. Therefore, the finding recorded by both\nthe courts below regarding search and seizure of\nthe documents which affect the integrity and\nsecurity of the country is the concurrent finding of\nfact rightly recorded by the High Court after\nproper appreciation and appraisal of the evidence\non record. The same cannot be interfered with by\nthis Court in exercise of its jurisdiction. Even if the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_369\">                                   322</span>\n\n\n\n\n       search is made by the Investigating Officer in\n       illegal manner, the same does not affect the\n       legality of the search and investigation made by\n       the Investigating Officer with regard to the seizure\n       of the documents from the house of the appellant\n       in view of the law <a href=\"/doc/503552/\" id=\"a_326\">laid down by</a> this Court <a href=\"/doc/503552/\" id=\"a_327\">in <a href=\"/doc/503552/\" id=\"a_328\">the\n       above case</a></a>. From the evidence produced by the\n       prosecution in the case in hand, it is clear that the\n       documents of strategic importance to the Nation\n       have been recovered from the possession of the\n       appellant and other accused and they have failed\n       to   give   satisfactory    explanation   about   the\n       documents being in their possession.&quot;</span>\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_581\">299.          <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"707363\" id=\"span_370\">In the case of <a href=\"/doc/707363/\" id=\"a_329\">ANIL @ ANDYA SADASHIV\n\nNANDOSKAR V. STATE OF MAHARASHTRA</a> reported in\n\n1996 SCC (2) 589, it is held that:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_285\">              &quot;Indeed all the 5 prosecution witnesses\n       who have been examined in support of search\n       and seizure were members of the raiding party.\n       They are all police officials. There is, however, no\n       rule of law that the evidence of police officials has\n       to be discarded or that it suffers from some\n       inherent infirmity. Prudence, however, requires\n       that the evidence of the police officials, who are\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_371\">                         323</span>\n\n\n\n\ninterested in the outcome of the result of the case,\nneeds     to   be    carefully    scrutinized        and\nindependently appreciated. The police officials do\nnot suffer from any disability to give evidence and\nthe mere fact that they are police officials does\nnot by itself give rise to any doubt about their\ncredit worthiness. We have carefully and critically\nanalyzed the evidence of all the 5 police officials.\nThere is nothing on the record to show that any\none of them was hostile to the appellant and\ndespite lengthy cross-examination their evidence\nhas     remained    unshaken     throughout.    These\nwitnesses have deposed in clear terms the details\nof the trap that was laid to apprehend the\nappellant and the manner in which he was\napprehended. Their evidence regarding search\nand seizure of the weapons from the appellant is\nstraight-forward    consistent    and    specific.    It\ninspires confidence and learned counsel for the\nappellant has not been able to point out any\nserious, let alone fatal, infirmity in their evidence.\nIn our opinion, the factum of search and seizure\nof the country-made revolver from the conscious\npossession of the appellant has been established\nby the prosecution beyond any reasonable doubt.\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_372\">                         324</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_286\">The explanation given by the prosecution, for the\nson-examination of the two panch witnesses,\nwhich is supported by the report Ex.24 filed by\nPW-4 PI Gaikwad is satisfactory. The evidence on\nthe record shows that the raiding party made\nsincere efforts to join with them two independent\npanches at the time of search and seizure and\nthey were so joined. They were also cited as\nprosecution witnesses and summoned to give\nevidence. However, despite diligent efforts made\nby the prosecuting agency to serve them, they\ncould not be Jocated or traced and therefore they\ncould not be examined at the trial. In the face of\nthe facts stated in report Ext.24, the correctness\nof which has remained virtually unchallenged\nduring the cross-examination of PW4, the non-\nexamination of the two panchas cannot be said to\nbe on account of any oblique reason. Their non\nproduction at the trial thus has not created any\ndent in the prosecution case. The prosecution\ncannot   be   accused    of   withholding   these\nwitnesses since it made every effort to trace and\nproduce them at the trial but failed on account of\nthe fact that they had left the addresses\nfurnished by them at the time of search and their\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_373\">                               325</span>\n\n\n\n\n       whereabouts could not be traced despite diligent\n       efforts made in that behalf. We, therefore, do not\n       find any reason to doubt the correctness of the\n       prosecution version relating to the apprehension\n       of the appellant, the search and seizure by the\n       raiding party and the recovery from the appellant\n       of the country-made revolver and cartridges for\n       which he could produce no licence or authority\n       because of the non-examination of the panch\n       witnesses. We find that the evidence of PW1 to\n       PW5 is reliable, cogent and trustworthy. Learned\n       counsel for the appellant then submitted that the\n       delay in sending the fire arm and the cartridges\n       to the ballistic expert rendered the report of the\n       Ballistic Expert Ext. P-17 vulnerable and the\n       conviction of the appellant unsustainable. We\n       cannot agree.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_582\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"686491\" id=\"span_374\">300.         The Apex Court in case of <a href=\"/doc/686491/\" id=\"a_330\">STATE OF KERALA\n\nV. M. M. MATHEW &amp; ANR</a>, reported in 1978 SCC (CRI) 503,\n\nthough in a somewhat different context, held as under:</span>\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_287\">       &quot;...... It is true that courts of law have to judge\n       the evidence before them by applying the well\n       recognised test of basic human probabilities......\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_375\">                                 326</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_288\">       prima facie public servants must be presumed to\n       act honestly and conscientiously and their\n       evidence has to be assessed on its intrinsic\n       worth and cannot be discarded merely on the\n       ground that being public servants they are\n       interested in the success of their case.....&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_583\">301.          The law on the point is well settled. Even if the\n\npanch witnesses turned hostile, which happens very often in\n\ncriminal cases, the evidence of the person who effected\n\nrecovery would not stand vitiated. All that is expected of the\n\nCourts is that the evidence of the police officials who are\n\ninterested in the outcome or the result of the case needs to\n\nbe carefully scrutinized and independently appreciated. The\n\npolice officials do not suffer from any disability to give\n\nevidence and the mere fact that they are police officials does\n\nnot by itself give raise to any doubt about the credit\n\nworthiness.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_584\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_585\">302.          In this case, the evidence regarding search and\n\nseizure and the material objects which are seized, which are\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_376\">                             327</span>\n\n\n\n\nin the nature of books, literature, which dates back to 1920s\n\nis straight forward, consistent and specific.     It inspires\n\nconfidence.   The accused in the cross examination have\n\nadmitted that in the mahazar conducted at the place of the\n\nblast near Minerva Mills, the police have seized pamphlets\n\nand literature. Some literatures were seized from the house\n\nof these accused. It is suggested that the pamphlets seized\n\nat the spot were fed into the computer. Therefore the seizure\n\nof the computer was also admitted.      There is nothing on\n\nrecord to presume that the police fed into the computers\n\nthese incriminating materials and that is not what the\n\naccused have stated in the statement under <a href=\"/doc/767287/\" id=\"a_331\">Section 313</a>\n\nCr.P.C. Unfortunately, the case of the accused is a complete\n\ndenial in their statement under <a href=\"/doc/767287/\" id=\"a_332\">Section 313</a> Cr.P.C.      The\n\nJudges have to judge the evidence before them by applying\n\nthe well recognised test of basic human probabilities. Prima\n\nfacie, public servants must be presumed to act honestly and\n\nconscientiously and their evidence has to be assessed on its\n\nintrinsic worth and cannot be discarded merely on the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_377\">                               328</span>\n\n\n\n\nground that being public servants they are interested in the\n\nsuccess of their case. As such under <a href=\"/doc/520370/\" id=\"a_333\">Section 114(e)</a> of the\n\nEvidence Act, the Court may draw a favourable presumption\n\nin respect of judicial and official acts, that they are regularly\n\nperformed. Viewed from that angle, their evidence regarding\n\nsearch and seizure of the weapons from the accused is\n\nstraight-forward,   consistent    and    specific.   It   inspires\n\nconfidence and learned counsel for the accused has not been\n\nable to point out any serious, let alone fatal, infirmity in their\n\nevidence. In our opinion, the factum of search and seizure of\n\nthe material objects and documents from the conscious\n\npossession of the accused has been established by the\n\nprosecution beyond any reasonable doubt.\n\n\nRe: POINT NO.9\nSECTION 27 OF THE INDIAN EVIDENCE ACT, 1872\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_586\">303.         The recoveries made under the above mahazars\n\nhave been strongly assailed on the ground that the police\n\nknew these places before and therefore the recovery of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_378\">                                329</span>\n\n\n\n\nincriminating material cannot be said to be at the instance of\n\nthe accused and there is always a possibility of the\n\ninvestigating agency planting the same.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_587\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_588\">304.        <a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_334\">Section 27</a> of the Indian Evidence Act reads as\n\nunder:\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_589\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_289\">         &quot;27. How much of information received\n         from accused may be proved.--Provided\n         that,   when    any fact    is    deposed   to   as\n         discovered     in   consequence    of   information\n         received from a person accused of any offence,\n         in the custody of a police officer, so much of\n         such information, whether it amounts to a\n         confession or not, as relates distinctly to the\n         fact thereby discovered, may be proved.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_590\">305.        If the evidence adduced by the prosecution in\n\nthis case is analyzed, it is seen that some incriminating\n\nmaterials were seized from the scene of occurance and some\n\nfrom the house and some from the establishment of the\n\naccused that too at their instance. The recoveries so made\n\nunder the above seizure mahazars has definitely led to\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_379\">                              330</span>\n\n\n\n\ndiscovery of the fact that only these accused were aware of\n\nthe place where the pamphlets, books, literatures, etc., were\n\nkept with the intention of using the same to cause\n\ndisharmony and hatred between two major communities of\n\nthis country, i.e., Hindus and Christians. It is difficult to\n\nbelieve that police could have access to these places and\n\ntherefore even if the IO were to be having prior knowledge as\n\nto where these accused lived or carried on their business or\n\nprofession, that itself cannot be a ground to disbelieve the\n\nprosecution case.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_591\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_592\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"254739\" id=\"span_380\">306.         The conditions necessary of operation of <a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_335\">Section\n\n27</a> of Evidence Act, are enunciated in <a href=\"/doc/254739/\" id=\"a_336\">PULUKURI KOTTAYA\n\nAND OTHERS Vs. EMPEROR</a> reported in AIR (34) 1947\n\nPRIVY COUNCIL, wherein it is held that :</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_290\">       &quot;10. <a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_337\">Section 27</a>, which is not artistically worded,\n       provides an exception to the prohibition imposed\n       by the preceding section, and enables certain\n       statements made by a person in police custody to\n       be proved. The condition necessary to bring the\n       section into operation is that the discovery of a\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_381\">                        331</span>\n\n\n\n\nfact in consequence of information received from a\nperson accused of any offence in the custody of a\nPolice officer must be deposed to, and thereupon\nso much of the information as relates distinctly to\nthe fact thereby discovered may be proved. The\nsection seems to be based on the view that if a\nfact is actually discovered in consequence of\ninformation given, some guarantee is afforded\nthereby that the information was true, and\naccordingly can be safely allowed to be given in\nevidence; but clearly the extent of the information\nadmissible must depend on the exact nature of\nthe fact discovered to which such information is\nrequired to relate. Normally the section is brought\ninto operation when a person in police custody\nproduces from some place of concealment some\nobject, such as a dead body, a weapon, or\nornaments, said to be connected with the crime of\nwhich the informant is accused. Mr. Megaw, for\nthe Crown, has argued that in such a case the\n&quot;fact discovered&quot; is the physical object produced,\nand that any information which relates distinctly\nto that object can be proved. Upon this view\ninformation given by a person that the body\nproduced is that of a person murdered by him,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_382\">                          332</span>\n\n\n\n\nthat the weapon produced is the one used by him\nin the commission of a murder, or that the\nornaments produced were stolen in a dacoity\nwould all be admissible. If this be the effect of\n<a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_338\">Section 27</a>, little substance would remain in the\nban imposed by the two preceding sections on\nconfessions made to the police, or by persons in\npolice   custody.    That     ban     was    presumably\ninspired by the fear of the legislature that a\nperson under police influence might be induced to\nconfess by the exercise of undue pressure. But if\nall that is required to lift the ban be the inclusion\nin the confession of information relating to an\nobject    subsequently        produced,      it   seems\nreasonable    to    suppose    that    the   persuasive\npowers of the police will prove equal to the\noccasion, and that in practice the ban will lose its\neffect. On normal principles of construction their\nLordships think that the proviso to <a href=\"/doc/387768/\" id=\"a_339\">Section 26</a>,\nadded by <a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_340\">Section 27</a>, should not be held to nullify\nthe substance of the section. In their Lordships&#x27;\nview it is fallacious to treat the &quot;fact discovered&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_593\">within the section as equivalent to the object\nproduced; the fact discovered embraces the place\nfrom which the object is produced and the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_383\">                                333</span>\n\n\n\n\n       knowledge of the accused as to this, and the\n       information given must relate distinctly to this\n       fact. Information as to past user, or the past\n       history, of the object produced is not related to its\n       discovery in the setting in which it is discovered.\n       Information supplied by a person in custody that\n       &quot;I will produce a knife concealed in the roof of my\n       house&quot; does not lead to the discovery of a knife;\n       knives were discovered many years ago. It leads\n       to the discovery of the fact that a knife is\n       concealed in the house of the informant to his\n       knowledge; and if the knife is proved to have\n       been used in the commission of the offence, the\n       fact discovered is very relevant. But if to the\n       statement the words be added &quot;with which I\n       stabbed A&quot;, these words are inadmissible since\n       they do not relate to the discovery of the knife in\n       the house of the informant.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_594\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_595\">307.         <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"812655\" id=\"span_384\">In this regard it may be profitable to refer to the\n\ndecision of the Apex Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/812655/\" id=\"a_341\">KARAN SINGH V.\n\nSTATE OF U.P.</a> reported in AIR 1973 SC 1385, wherein it\n\nis held at para 3 that there was also the fact that the blood\n\nstained knife (Ext.5) with which the murder was committed\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_385\">                               334</span>\n\n\n\n\nwas recovered at the instance of the appellant. We are not\n\nimpressed by the argument on behalf of the appellant that\n\nthis evidence is not admissible under the provisions of\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_342\">Section 27</a> of the evidence Act as the police already knew\n\nabout the place where the knife could be found. This\n\nargument is wholly without substance. This was based on\n\nthe fact that the appellant first told the police that he would\n\nshow them the knife and then took them to the place where\n\nthe knife was hidden.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_596\">\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_597\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1769219\" id=\"span_386\">308.         Further in the case of <a href=\"/doc/1769219/\" id=\"a_343\">STATE [NCT OF DELHI]\n\nV. NAVJOT SANDHU ALIAS AFSAN GURU</a> 2005 SCC [Cri]\n\n1715, it is held that:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_291\">       &quot;<a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_344\">Section 27</a> lifts the ban against the admissibility\n       of the confession/statement made to the police to\n       a limited extent by allowing proof of information\n       of a specified nature furnished by the accused in\n       police custody. In that sense <a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_345\">Section 27</a> is\n       considered to be an exception to the rules\n       embodied in <a href=\"/doc/494844/\" id=\"a_346\">Sections 25</a> and <a href=\"/doc/387768/\" id=\"a_347\">26</a>.\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_387\">                           335</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_292\">      The first requisite condition for utilizing\n<a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_348\">Section 27</a> in support of the prosecution case is\nthat the investigating police officer should depose\nthat he discovered a fact in consequence of the\ninformation received from a accused person in\npolice custody. Thus, there must be a discovery\nof fact not within the knowledge of police officer\nas a consequence of information received. Of\ncourse, it is axiomatic that the information or\ndisclosure should be free from any element of\ncompulsion.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_293\">      The next component of <a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_349\">Section 27</a> relates\nto the nature and extent of information that can\nbe proved. &quot;Discovery of fact&quot; cannot be equated\nto the object produced or found. It is more than\nthat. The discovery of fact arises by reason of\nthe fact that the information given by the\naccused exhibited the knowledge or the mental\nconsciousness of        the informant accused in\nrelation thereto. However, it is only so much of\nthe information as relates distinctly to the fact\nthereby discovered that can be proved and\nnothing more. The rest of the information has to\nbe   excluded.    The    word   &#x27;distinctly&#x27;   means\n&#x27;directly&#x27;, &#x27;indubitably&#x27;, &#x27;strictly&#x27;, &#x27;unmistakably&#x27;.\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_388\">                            336</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_294\">The word has been advisedly used to limit and\ndefine the scope of the provable information. The\nphrase &#x27;distinctly related to the fact thereby\ndiscovered&#x27; is the linchpin of the provision. This\nphrase refers to that part of the information\nsupplied by the accused, which is the direct and\nimmediate cause of the discovery. It is explicitly\nclarified in the section that there is no taboo\nagainst receiving such information in evidence\nmerely because it amounts to a confession. At\nthe same time, the last clause makes it clear that\nit is not the confessional part that is admissible\nbut it is only such information or part of it, which\nrelates distinctly to the fact discovered by means\nof   the     information    furnished.      Thus,    the\ninformation conveyed in the statement to the\npolice ought to be dissected if necessary so as to\nadmit      only   the   information    of   the   nature\nmentioned in the section. The reason behind this\npartial lifting of the ban against confessions and\nstatements made to the police, is that if a fact is\nactually      discovered     in       consequence     of\ninformation given by the accused, it affords some\nguarantee of truth of that part, and that part\nonly, of the information which was the clear,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_389\">                               337</span>\n\n\n\n\n       immediate and proximate cause of the discovery.\n       No such guarantee or assurance attaches to the\n       rest of the statement which may be indirectly or\n       remotely related to the fact discovered.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_295\">             Lastly,   &quot;discovery   of   fact&quot;    does   not\n       comprehend a pure and simple mental fact or\n       state of mind relating to a physical object\n       dissociated from the recovery of the physical\n       object [that is, it does not comprehend the second\n       limb of the definition of &quot;fact&quot; contained in\n       <a href=\"/doc/1031309/\" id=\"a_350\">Section 3</a> of the Evidence Act, 1872.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_598\">309.         <a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_351\">Section 27</a> is considered to be an exception to\n\nthe rules embodied in <a href=\"/doc/494844/\" id=\"a_352\">Sections 25</a> and <a href=\"/doc/387768/\" id=\"a_353\">26</a>. It enables certain\n\nstatements made by a person in police custody to be proved.\n\nNormally the section is brought into operation when a\n\nperson in police custody produces from some place of\n\nconcealment some object, such as a dead body, a weapon, or\n\nornaments, said to be connected with the crime of which the\n\ninformant is accused.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_390\">                              338</span><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_599\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_600\">310.        &quot;Discovery of fact&quot; cannot be equated to the\n\nobject produced or found. It is more than that. It is only so\n\nmuch of the information as relates distinctly to the fact\n\nthereby discovered that can be proved and nothing more.\n\nThe phrase &#x27;distinctly related to the fact thereby discovered&#x27;\n\nis the linchpin of the provision. This phrase refers to that\n\npart of the information supplied by the accused, which is the\n\ndirect and immediate cause of the discovery. It is not the\n\nconfessional part that is admissible but it is only such\n\ninformation or part of it, which relates distinctly to the fact\n\ndiscovered by means of the information furnished. In fact it\n\nis actually discovered in consequence of information given by\n\nthe accused, it affords some guarantee of truth of that part,\n\nand that part only, of the information which was the clear,\n\nimmediate and proximate cause of the discovery.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_601\">311.        The discovery of any fact referred to in <a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_354\">Section\n\n27</a> does not include mental or psychological fact, example,\n\nknowledge, i.e., the mental act of becoming aware of\n\nsomething or intention. The word &#x27;fact&#x27; is used in the sense\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_391\">                                339</span>\n\n\n\n\nof physical or material fact, which can be perceived by the\n\nsenses and the discovery of such facts alone can eliminate\n\nthe fear of confession being induced by improper inducing\n\nand confirming the truth of the information received from the\n\naccused.    The word &#x27;fact&#x27; does not refer to mental fact.\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_355\">Section 27</a> comes into operation when some material object\n\nis produced from somewhere in consequence of some\n\ninformation given by the accused. But the fact discovery\n\nincludes not merely the object found but also the place\n\nwhere it lay and the accused knowledge as to the place. The\n\nknowledge of the accused as to the place indicates that the\n\ndiscovery was due to the information received from him, i.e.,\n\nhe is the informant as contemplated in the Section. The fact\n\ndiscovery must be such that the proof of its existence does\n\nnot depend on the credibility of the accused or any other\n\nperson, but is confirmed by the production of the object\n\nitself, which can be perceived by the senses.    <a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_356\">Section 27</a>\n\nallows proof of such part of the information as it relates\n\ndistinctly to the fact discovery.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_392\">                              340</span><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_602\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_603\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"812655\" id=\"span_393\">312.        The Apex Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/812655/\" id=\"a_357\">KARAN SINGH V.\n\nSTATE OF U.P.</a> reported in AIR 1973 SC 1385, held that\n\nthough the Investigating Officer already knew the place\n\nwhere the incriminating article was hidden but still if that\n\nincriminating article is recovered in furtherance of the\n\ndiscovery statement given by the accused under <a href=\"/doc/1312051/\" id=\"a_358\">Section 27</a>\n\nof the Indian Evidence Act, the recovery of that incriminating\n\nobject is held to be proved at the instance of the accused on\n\nthe basis of the evidence of the Investigating Officer.\n\nTherefore, we do not find any substance in the said\n\ncontention of the accused.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_604\">\nRe: POINT NO.10\nADMISSIBILITY OF SECONDARY EVIDENCE\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_605\">313.        The material on record discloses that the\n\nKarnataka Government constituted a Special Court to try all\n\nthe four cases where bombs were blasted at four different\n\nplaces. Most of the accused in all these cases are common.\n\nThe Investigating Officer after recording the statement\n\nduring the course of investigation have gone to the houses of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_394\">                             341</span>\n\n\n\n\nmany of these accused along with them and they have seized\n\nthe articles and mahazars were drawn.        Some of these\n\nmahazars are common to all the four cases. Similarly, the\n\nconfessional statement of Syed Hasnuzama is common to all\n\nfour cases.   The originals of all these mahazars and the\n\nconfessional statement were marked in the first case, i.e.,\n\nS.C.No.423/2001. Thereafter certified copies of the same are\n\nobtained and they were produced in the other three cases.\n\nAs trial took place in the same Court and the accused were\n\nalso represented by the same Counsel in all the four cases\n\nand the Counsel for the accused has cross examined the\n\nwitnesses on the basis of the said original documents, it is\n\nnot open to the accused now to contend that the originals\n\nwere not shown to them or produced before the Court. All\n\nthe originals are produced in one case, duly marked and the\n\nCounsel for the accused have cross examined in respect of\n\nall those documents. Thereafter certified copies are obtained\n\nfrom those originals and they are produced and marked in\n\nother three cases, when the same documents are required in\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_395\">                                342</span>\n\n\n\n\nall the four cases, it is not possible to produce the original in\n\nall the four cases. In such circumstances, law provides for\n\nproduction of secondary evidence.      The certified copies are\n\nobtained from the very same Court and they are marked in\n\nthe other three cases, which is permissible in law and the\n\nsecondary evidence is admissible in evidence. Therefore, we\n\ndo not find any substance in the contention of the learned\n\nCounsel for the accused that the originals were not produced\n\nand hence secondary evidence is inadmissible in evidence.\n\n\nRe: POINT NO.11\n\nAPPRECIATION OF EVIDENCE\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_606\">314.         In the background of the aforesaid material on\n\nrecord, we shall examine the evidence produced against each\n\naccused, on the basis of which the Trial Court found them\n\nguilty.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_607\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_608\">315.         The   case   of   the   prosecution   is   that   on\n\n09.07.2000 prayer and lectures were arranged in St. Peter\n\nand Paul Church situated at J.J.Nagar Main Road to\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_396\">                             343</span>\n\n\n\n\ncelebrate the annual festival, procession was taken out in\n\nthis behalf from CAR quarters at Sirsi Circle to the Church\n\nlasted from 6:00 p.m. to 8:30 p.m. Thereafter, there was a\n\nmass prayer in the Church and the entire celebration was\n\nover by 9.45 p.m.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_609\">316.        A-10 along with deceased A-14 and A-15, in\n\nfurtherance of their conspiracy to Islamise India by creating\n\nhatredness, communal disturbance and derailment of Indian\n\neconomy planted powerful bombs in the premises of the\n\nabove church. In furtherance of their attempt, these accused\n\ncarried bombs in a blue colour Maruti Van bearing\n\nregistration No.GA-01/U-2786 belonging to A-10 for planting\n\nthe same at some other churches in Bangalore. A-10 was\n\ndriving the said Maruti van at high speed and in a rash and\n\nnegligent manner when it hit a road hump near Minerva\n\nMills,   Bangalore   resulting    in   explosion   of   bombs.\n\nConsequently the van also exploded. As a result of this, not\n\nonly the parts of the Van but also some letters of threat,\n\npamphlets, documents pertaining to the vehicle and personal\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_397\">                                344</span>\n\n\n\n\ndocuments of A-10 were found scattered all over the place;\n\nand some documents, letters of threat etc. were found in the\n\ndash board of the Maruti Van. Due to the impact of\n\nexplosion, some parts of the van also hit an oncoming\n\nMaxicab and thereby damaged the vehicle and injured its\n\ndriver.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_610\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_611\">317.         In the above incident, A-14 and A-15 who were\n\ntravelling in A-10&#x27;s Maruti Van at that time died at the spot\n\nand A-10 who was driving the van also sustained injuries.\n\nP.W.58 drew the Mahazar Ex.P162 on 10.07.2000 at the\n\nscene of occurrence between 3.00 p.m. and 5.00 p.m. in the\n\npresence of R.Gopal and S.Ramanna and seized the\n\nincriminating materials found there, in connection with\n\nS.C.423/2001, which was also tried along with this case.\n\nEx.P161 and Ex.P167 are the inquest Mahazars pertaining\n\nto the dead body of A-15 and A-14. Therefore, we have to\n\nnow consider whether the prosecution has made out the\n\ncase not only against A-10 but also against A-14 and A-15,\n\nas it was a collective overt act.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_398\">                             345</span><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_612\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_613\">318.        A-10 was aged about 45 years at the time of\n\noffence and residing with wife PW16 Sayeeda Barkath\n\nUnnissa in his father-in law PW14 Sattar Khan&#x27;s house.\n\nThough worked as an Accounts Manager in Compudyne\n\nInfosis Ltd., a Multi-National Company, A-10 projected\n\nhimself as a Chartered Accountant as he also happened to\n\nwork in a Chartered Accountant Company called Barkath &amp;\n\nCo. A-10 has studied upto B.Com but not completed C.A.\n\nand did not hold licence to practice on Income Tax and Sales\n\nTax side, that is what emerges from his statement U/s 313.\n\n<a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_359\">Cr.P.C</a>. This is the conduct of A.10, which is relevant in\n\nterms of <a href=\"/doc/482978/\" id=\"a_360\">Section 8</a> of the Indian Evidence Act.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_614\">319.        PW1 lodged first information Ex.P1, after the\n\noccurrence and Cr.No.113/2000 came to be registered on\n\nthe said basis. The place of occurrence is situated within\n\nJagajeevanram Nagar Police Station limits and P.W.52, the\n\nPolice Inspector attached to that station having visited the\n\nspot, has given evidence in detail as to how the place of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_399\">                              346</span>\n\n\n\n\noccurrence appeared soon after the blast; how this incident\n\naffected the feelings of persons belonging to Christian\n\ncommunity, provoked them to indulge in illegal acts of\n\nharming life and property of members of Muslim community\n\netc. Ex.P2 is the Panchnama drawn at the spot and M.O.1 to\n\nM.O.12 were seized thereunder. A rough sketch of the entire\n\nlocality was prepared as per Ex.P.84.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_615\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_616\">320.        PW52 has also deposed to the effect that on\n\ncoming to know that a van was blasted due to the bomb\n\nexplosion within Magadi Road Police Station limits and one\n\nof the accused i.e., A-10 injured therein was being treated in\n\nSt. John&#x27;s Hospital, he visited the said hospital but A-10 was\n\nunconscious. Interestingly, A-10 has not cross examined\n\nPW52 with regard to these aspects except putting a few\n\nquestions regarding the preparations made in connection\n\nwith the annual celebration at the Church and other\n\naccused have not even cross-examined him.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_400\">                             347</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_617\">321.        PW42 and PW48 have also spoken about the\n\ngrim situation that existed in that area consequent to the\n\nblasting of the church. Cr.No.114/2000 also came registered\n\nfor the offence punishable U/Ss.143, 144, 147, 435, 427 r/w\n\n<a href=\"/doc/999134/\" id=\"a_361\">Sec. 149</a> IPC; additional police force including KSRP and\n\nCAR was deployed to bring the situation under control. On\n\nthe basis of the report submitted by JJ Nagar Police, P.W-55\n\nthe then Commissioner of Police, Bangalore had issued\n\nprohibitory orders U/s 144(2) <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_362\">Cr.P.C</a>. as seen from Ex.P89.\n\nThis amply makes it clear that A-10 and his associates were\n\nsuccessful in their efforts to create disharmony among\n\nmembers of different communities and thereby derailed the\n\nlaw and order situation in JJ Nagar Police station limits to\n\nsome extent.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_618\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_619\">322.        In order to prove the overt act of A-10, A-14 and\n\nA-15 planting bomb in St. Peter and Paul Church, the\n\nprosecution has examined PW3, P.N.Mukunda who worked\n\nas a Salesman in stationery shop near the Church. It is in\n\nthe evidence of this witness that the shop in which he works\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_401\">                                 348</span>\n\n\n\n\nis open from 6.30 a.m. to 11.00 pm on all the seven days in\n\na week and he used to go out only for breakfast and lunch.\n\nOn 09.07.2000 there was a festival in the Church and at\n\nabout 8.30 pm on when he was talking to the owner outside\n\nthe shop, a blue colour Maruthi van with Goa registration\n\nnumber stopped in front of Medical Store situated adjacent\n\nto their shop. Two persons got down from the van and\n\nwalked towards the church with a plastic cover in their hand\n\nwhile the third person stood near the van at a distance of 5\n\nfeet   from   their   shop.   Noticing   this,   the   shop   owner\n\nNanjundaiah told that the devotees had come from Goa.\n\nSometime later those two persons returned without the\n\nplastic bag, saying in urdu &quot;Chalo Kaam Hogaya&quot;. The third\n\nperson standing near the van drove the van with the other\n\ntwo persons towards Minerva Mills.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_620\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_621\">323.          PW3 further states that at about 9.50 pm or\n\n10.00 pm on the night, he heard a huge blast sound from\n\ninside the Church and saw people running in all directions.\n\nTherefore he locked the shop within five minutes; owner of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_402\">                              349</span>\n\n\n\n\nthe shop went towards the Church and he went back home\n\nas he was frightened. He was called to the police station\n\ntwice along with Nanjundaiah, Ravi Prasad and Chinna\n\nDorai. Photos Ex.P4 and P5 were shown to them. Two or\n\nthree months later he was required by the police to go to\n\nBangalore Central Jail for identifying the accused. When he\n\nwent there, ten persons were standing in the presence of the\n\nMagistrate and he could identify A-10 S.M.Ibrahim as the\n\nperson driving the blue maruti van with Goa registration\n\nnumber on that fateful night. Ex.P6 is the test identification\n\nparade report and Ex.P6(a) is PW3&#x27;s signature. PW3 is\n\nextensively cross-examined with regard to Identification\n\nParade and pleads ignorance about where exactly A-10 was\n\nstanding among the ten persons or their dress colour, but\n\nsays that some were wearing lungis and the other persons\n\npresent there did not resemble A-10.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_622\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_623\">324.        After additional charge was framed, PW3 was\n\ncalled for further cross-examination. He admitted that earlier\n\nto the date of incident, he had not seen A-10, A-14 or A-15\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_403\">                               350</span>\n\n\n\n\nand that his statement was recorded at about 11.30 a.m.\n\nafter the news of bomb blast in the church was published in\n\nthe papers.     He has deposed that A-14 was the person\n\nholding the bag and during test identification parade, he\n\nnoticed that A-10 had sustained burn injuries on the face\n\nand hands and was holding a stick. However this witness\n\nhas denied the suggestion that he identified A-10 on the\n\nbasis of the information published in the newspapers and\n\ndue to compulsion from the police.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_624\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_625\">325.          P.W.57 is the Investigating Officer, who arranged\n\nfor the Test identification parade of A-10 through Tahsildar,\n\nBangalore North Taluka only after obtaining necessary\n\npermission from the III ACMM, Bangalore. Accordingly,\n\nP.W.56 conducted the test identification parade in the\n\nCentral Prison premises. As seen from the evidence PW56\n\nsecured A-10 and six others; they were made to stand in a\n\nrow. A mahazar was drawn and each witness was called to\n\nidentify the accused separately and they identified A-10 as\n\nper Ex.P99, Ex.P100, Ex.P6, Ex.P101, Ex.P102 and Ex.P103.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_404\">                                  351</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_626\">326.          It was contended that the identification parade\n\nis contrary to law and therefore, the identification of A-10 by\n\nPW3 cannot be acted upon. It is true that whenever an\n\nidentification parade is done after showing the photograph of\n\nthe accused in the police station, it loses its significance.\n\nThe Court cannot consider it to be a good piece of\n\nsupplementary evidence. But it is settled law that the\n\nidentification parade belongs to the stage of investigation.\n\nThere is no provision in the Code, which obliges the\n\nInvestigating agency from holding or conferring a right upon\n\nthe accused to an Identification parade.              The Court\n\nidentification itself is a good identification in the eyes of law.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_627\">327.          It     is   not   always   necessary   that    court\n\nidentification must be preceded by the Test Identification\n\nParade. This depends upon the facts and circumstances of a\n\ngiven case.        When a witness identifies an accused in the\n\nCourt, the Court has to appreciate the evidence with\n\nreference to the law, intrinsic worth of such identification,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_405\">                                352</span>\n\n\n\n\nother      evidence   on   record,   circumstances   and   the\n\nprobabilities. When a stranger is a witness and identifies an\n\naccused in the Court, the Court by way of caution or\n\nprudence may seek some assurance before accepting such\n\nidentification. That assurance would be available from the\n\npositive result of the test identification parade. However\n\nabsence of test identification parade is not invariably fatal,\n\nfor assurance may be available from other source and\n\ncircumstance. Showing of a large number of photographs to\n\nthe witness and then asking him to pick out the photograph\n\nof the suspect is also a recognised procedure. The material\n\non record clealy establishes that A.10 was the owner of the\n\nMaruthi Omni Van bearing registration No.GA-01/U-2786.\n\nHe was driving the vehicle on 09.07.2000 at 9.30 p.m. when\n\nit was blasted because of the bombs which were carried in\n\nthe van.     It is also not in dispute that he was seriously\n\ninjured.    Other occupants A.14 and A.15 died at the spot.\n\nP.W.5, the father of A.15 has categorically stated that the\n\nsaid van belongs to him. He was driving the vehicle. P.W.3\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_406\">                              353</span>\n\n\n\n\nhas identified the said vehicle, which was kept in the\n\nbasement floor of the Prison. The very argument that A.10\n\nbeing injured in the bomb blast was walking with the help of\n\na stick and was made to stand with the stick; others made to\n\nstand in the line for identification did not look like him\n\nfortifies the prosecution theory that A.10 had sustained\n\ninjuries in the bomb blast and was struggling to walk; and\n\ntherefore had a stick in the hand when made to stand for\n\nidentification parade. Therefore, in the instant case, even if\n\nthe identification of A-10 by PW3 at the time of identification\n\nparade is held to be not acceptable, other material on record\n\nclearly establishes the complicity of A-10 in the commission\n\nof the offence.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_628\">\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_629\">328.         P.W.5, the father of A-15 has deposed that that\n\nA-10 and A-15 were friends. Intially A-10 used to frequent\n\ntheir house on a scooter to meet A-15 and later he used to\n\ncome in a blue or black colour van. A-15 told that he wanted\n\nto learn driving the said van and continued the friendship\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_407\">                               354</span>\n\n\n\n\nwith A-15. He in fact opposed the said idea and advised his\n\nson to learn driving by joining a driving school so that he\n\ncould get a driving licence. It is also forthcoming from PW5&#x27;s\n\nevidence that his son A-15 had studied up to B.Com. On\n\n09.07.2000 at 1.30 a.m. there was a phone call informing\n\nabout his son&#x27;s hospitalization in Victoria due to car\n\naccident.   He went to Victoria Hospital and was there till\n\n5.00 a.m; A.11 had also come to the Hospital. Then, Police\n\nfrom Magadi Police Station came there and took him to the\n\nStation for investigation.   In the Police Station he came to\n\nknow that his son died at the spot.     Later, from the news\n\npapers, he learnt that his son died in a bomb blast and there\n\nwere three persons in the van at that time. He also came to\n\nknow that the persons died in the accident are his son, A.15\n\nand A.14; the driver of the van, A.10, was seriously injured.\n\nHe and A.10 brought the dead body of his son to his house\n\nand 8.30 pm, in the night and buried the dead body. He has\n\nidentified A.10 before the Court. The cross-examination of\n\nthis witness shows that A-10 sometimes attended Darshe\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_408\">                             355</span>\n\n\n\n\nQuran programs held in his house, but would leave\n\nimmediately thereafter as he was not interested in religious\n\nprogram owing to young age.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_630\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_631\">329.        PW6, the father-in-law of A-14 too has identified\n\nA-10 before the Court stating that the said accused attended\n\nthe programs at PW5&#x27;s house and also the Urs at Hyderabad\n\nbut claims to be not aware of A-10, A-14 and A-15 moving\n\nabout together.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_632\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_633\">330.        PW16 Sayeeda Barkath Unnissa and PW17\n\nGulzaar Begum are sisters. They are the daughters of PW14\n\nSattar Khan. PW16 who is the wife of A-10 has deposed that\n\nher husband was working as an Accounts Manager in\n\nCompudine Infosystem and has computer knowledge. From\n\nthese three witnesses the prosecution was only able to elicit\n\nas to PW16 and A-10 are residing in the house belonging to\n\nPW14 at Varthur and it has telephone connection bearing\n\nNo.28538537. The prosecution treated them hostile and\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_409\">                               356</span>\n\n\n\n\ncross examined at length but the efforts bore no fruits as\n\ncould be seen from the evidence on record.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_634\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_635\">331.         PW60, G.A.Bawa, the then ACP of Chickpet Sub-\n\nDivision conducted further investigation in Cr.No.290/2000\n\nof Magadi Road Police Station by visiting the house of A-10\n\non 10.07.2000 and searched the same in the presence of\n\nPanchas, recovered 46 incriminating articles including a\n\ncomputer system from there under a Mahazar. Ex.P182 is\n\nthe certified copy of the said mahazar. According to the said\n\nwitness on 12.07.2000 when the seized computer system\n\nwas booted in the presence of panchas, through PC 3028\n\nLaxminarayana      who   is   well   versed   in   operating   the\n\ncomputers,   the   startup    screen   displayed    the   warning\n\n&quot;Christians, stop conversion or quit India&quot;.          Thereafter,\n\nhe got it packed and sealed with the seal GAB for subjecting\n\nthe same to further examination by the experts and drew the\n\npanchanama, certified copy of which is at Ex.P183.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_410\">                               357</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_636\">332.        Ex.P184 and P185 are the certified copies of\n\nPost-mortem report pertaining to A-15 dated 11.07.2000 and\n\nA-14 dated 19.07.2000 respectively received by PW60 from\n\nVictoria Hospital, Bangalore. He has also identified A-10\n\nbefore the court. Cross-examination of this witness mostly\n\nrelates to his computer knowledge and the attempts made to\n\nsecure   information   from   the   computer   by   employing\n\nunqualified persons. His evidence that file pertaining to A-10\n\nin that computer could not be opened for want of pass word\n\nhas remained unchallenged. Such being the case the\n\ncontention of A-10 that on the basis of the hand bills found\n\nnear Minerva Mills and other places, PW60 got the above\n\nmaterials fed in the computer cannot be believed that too\n\nwhen he clearly admits that he does not even have basic\n\nknowledge of the computers.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_637\">\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_638\">333.        From the above it is clear that the accused do\n\nnot dispute seizure of computer system from A-10&#x27;s house by\n\nPW60 and that its startup screen displayed the warning\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_411\">                               358</span>\n\n\n\n\n&quot;Christians stop conversions or quit India&quot; which is found\n\neven in the hand bills seized from the blast site near Minerva\n\nMills. Seizure of hand bills nears the place of the blast at\n\nMinerva Mills is also not seriously disputed. It is pertinent to\n\nnote that even PW14 to PW16 have admitted seizure of\n\ncomputer    from   A-10&#x27;s   residence.     Following   are   the\n\npamphlets seized from the accused.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_412\"> 359</span><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_639\">\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_413\"> 360</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_414\">                             361</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_640\">334.        These   pamplets      clearly   demonstrates   the\n\nmischief, which these accused were indulging in. They were\n\nsending threatening letters and making publications in the\n\nname of Hindu organization to the Christians and to the\n\nHindus in the name of Christians.      They wanted to create\n\ndisharmony between these two communities.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_641\">335.        The other vital documentary evidence which\n\nshows the role of A10 in the conspiracy and the commission\n\nof the offences is his bank transaction and credit cards. On\n\n10.09.2000 P.W.57 - V.S. D&#x27;Souza, the I.O. made a request\n\nto Standard Chartered Bank, Bangalore, Merchantile Co-\n\noperative Bank, Amanath Co-operative Bank, Vijaya Bank\n\nand Syndicate Bank to furnish the details of accounts of\n\nA.10. On 11.09.2000 the Standard Chartered Bank, as per\n\nEx.P112, furnished the account extract of A.10. His request\n\nletter to the Bank is marked as Ex.P113.        Amanath Co-\n\noperative Bank gave the required information as per Ex.P49\n\nand his request to them was marked as Ex.P48. Similarly,\n\non 13.09.2000, Vijaya Bank gave the accounts extracts of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_415\">                                   362</span>\n\n\n\n\nA.10, which are marked as Ex.P73 to Ex.P76 and his request\n\nto the Bank is marked as Ex.P77. Ex.P66 and Ex.P69 are\n\nthe extracts from Syndicate Bank, which he received on the\n\nsame day.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_642\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_643\">336.         P.W.38 is the manager of Amanath Co-Operative\n\nBank, Gangenahalli Branch, Bangalore and his evidence\n\ndiscloses that A-10 and A-12 S.B. A/c in that bank and\n\nEx.P49 and P50 are the particulars relating thereto and\n\nEx.P51 is the statement accounts of A-10. This witness has\n\nnot been cross examined at all.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_644\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_645\">337.         P.W.45 Vishwanath Rai was the Senior Branch\n\nManager in Syndicate Bank, Koramangala from 1998 to\n\n2002 and he has deposed that A-10 had S.B. A/c No.6929 in\n\ntheir    branch.    Ex.P67   is   the   certified    copy    of   A-10&#x27;s\n\nrequisition for opening an account during 1997; Ex.P68 is\n\nthe certified copy of A-10&#x27;s specimen signature.
