In the confession statement of A.7 at Ex.P.245, there\n\nis a reference to the role of A.14 in the conspiracy. A.7 has\n\nstated when A.6 told all the members to co-operate with A.24\n\nas he was selected by A.1 and he had to damage Railways,\n\nIOC, PCI, Electric (grid), Government Installations by Sarria.\n\nIn between A.7 informed and told them to choose themselves\n\nwith whom they are familiar. Then, A.20 told that he would\n\nmanage    Railways.   A.19   told   that   he   would   manage\n\nGovernment Installations, Ijahar Baig told that him that he\n\nwould manage roads. A.14 told that he would manage\n\nelectricity and Sarria. Tayyab told that he would look for 100\n\nplaces.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_764\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_765\">411.        According to A.7&#x27;s confession, one day accused\n\nNo.14 brought a letter from A.1 stating that they wanted\n\nsome information about locations of SON.          A.7 gave the\n\ninformation to A.6.   A.6 and A.7 told Maqbul (son-in-law)\n\nthat if he can get Grid (electric) survey, then they could make\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_498\">                            445</span>\n\n\n\n\nout the address out of that. When A.7 took leave to attend\n\nBakrid festival and A.6&#x27;s daughter&#x27;s marriage, he came to\n\nknow that some persons had gone to survey Sarria in\n\nKarnataka i.e., A.9, Maqbul, Habib and one person from\n\nVijayawada, A.14 from Hyderabad, Tayyab and Khaleed\n\nChoudhary. They went to Bhatakurki, Ramdurga and Sangli\n\nwith one Gramin Bank employee. They tried to do Sarria at\n\nSangli, but could not. From Sangli, except A.14 everybody\n\ncame back to Bangalore.     A.14 had come by motorcycle\n\nwhich was arranged by Gaffur of Solapur.    This evidence,\n\nclearly establishes the involvement of A.14 in all the\n\naforesaid activities of Deendar Anjuman Ashram.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_766\">412.        P.W-19-M.   Murthy   has   spoken     about   the\n\nassociation of A.15 with A.10.   Similarly, P.W-20-Prathap\n\nSingh also has spoken about the persons who were visiting\n\nthe house of P.W-5.     Similarly P.W-21-Srinivas also has\n\nspoken about the association of A.14 with A.11, A.12 and\n\nA.13.   Siddique has been identified in the album by these\n\nwitnesses. This evidence clearly demonstrates that A.15&#x27;s\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_499\">                              446</span>\n\n\n\n\nfather himself has spoken about the association of A.15 with\n\nA.10 and the evidence on record shows that A.10, A15 and\n\nA.14 were in the Maruthi Van carrying explosives and while\n\nproceeding towards Minerva Circle.      The bombs exploded\n\nkilling A.14 and A.15. This evidence clearly demonstrates the\n\ncomplicity of A.15 in the activity of carrying on explosives on\n\nthe date of the incident.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_767\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_768\">413.        A.18 was aged about 37 years on the date of his\n\nevidence.   He was the owner of a cloth shop and a civil\n\ncontractor, residing at Movva, Krishna District, State of\n\nAndhra Pradesh. P.W.64 - M.B.Appanna, the Investigating\n\nOfficer in S.C. No.423/2001 under Ex.P261 recovered 15\n\narticles from the house of A.11.     One such incriminating\n\narticle seized was, a slip containing the hand writing of A.18.\n\nIt was produced in SC 423/2001 as Ex.P265; A.18 wrote the\n\nslip containing the names of various accused persons and\n\nthe work entrusted to each of the accused to the dictation of\n\nabsconding A.2. Thus a chart of action plan was prepared\n\nby A.18 on the direction of A.2. This shows the overt act of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_500\">                             447</span>\n\n\n\n\nA.18 and the link between him, A.2 and A.11. In A.7&#x27;s\n\nconfessional statement Ex.P245 the role of A.18 in the\n\nconspiracy is clearly spelt out.     A.18 was a qualified\n\nengineer; he had dedicated himself to the cause of Deendar\n\nAnjuman Ashram, prepared sketches before the crimes were\n\nexecuted and communicated with the other members about\n\nthe plans hatched and execution of such plans. This clearly\n\nproves A.18&#x27;s involvement in the conspiracy and the crime,\n\nwhich was committed in pursuance of the said conspiracy.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_769\">414.         A.19 was aged about 30 years on the date of the\n\nincident. He is the son-in-law of A.13 and was a resident of\n\nNo.10-4-469,    Deendar   Anjuman    Ashram,   Asif   Nagar,\n\nHyderabad.     He was sent to Mardan, Pakistan to receive\n\ntraining in arms, ammunitions and explosives. Accordingly,\n\nhe was trained in Mardan. He was an expert in preparing,\n\nplanting and blasting the bombs. He was a Videographer by\n\nprofession. In the confessional statement of A.7 at Ex.P245,\n\nhe has spoken about the role of A.19 in the conspiracy. At\n\npara.22 he has stated that on 3rd of Urs at about 11.00 hrs,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_501\">                              448</span>\n\n\n\n\nsome of the members of Deendar Anjuman Organization had\n\nmet in A.19&#x27;s Apartment in Mehdipatnam along with A.3. In\n\nthe said meeting A.3 told that each selected area members\n\nshould choose another five followers, who can be taken in\n\nconfidence and they should be given the responsibility of\n\nrailways, transportation, Government depots like TCI, IOC,\n\nelectrical station, Nefaque and lastly targeting VIPs in South\n\nlike Chief Minister, State Minister and VIPs. Later he told\n\nabout Sarria and after meeting all the members, he told\n\nthem to take collective decisions and that secrecy should be\n\nmaintained and lastly he told them to be ready for Qurbani\n\nand punctuality while doing some job. Then he proposed to\n\nhave separate meeting after choosing five members. At\n\npara.23 A.7 has stated that on 2nd January, 2000, the\n\nBhatakurki batch came with Tata Sumo. But A.6 and A.7\n\nwent to Hyderabad and met A.24, A.19, A.20, Tayyab, Ijahar\n\nBaig and lastly Khaleed Choudhary also came over there. All\n\nof them sat in A.19&#x27;s flat where they attended the second\n\nmeeting of third day of Urs. There A.6 told the persons to\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_502\">                             449</span>\n\n\n\n\nco-operate with A.24, as he was selected by A.1 and they had\n\nto damage Railways, IOC, FCI, Electric (Grid), Government\n\ninstallations by Sarria, Road TPT. In between A.7 interfered\n\nand asked them to choose in which each were familiar. At\n\nthat time, A.19 chose Government Installations. At para.24\n\nhe has stated that A.7 and his brother A.6 went to\n\nHyderabad. From there they went to A.24 and he took them\n\nto A.19&#x27;s apartment. There A.24, A.14, A.19, A.20 and Ijhar\n\nBaig met and they wanted money for site for keeping the\n\nexplosives. A.6 told them to go to a chit fund company and\n\ntake one chit by contributing the amount by themselves.\n\nAfter that A.20 took them to next room and gave a letter\n\ncontaining that they have to meet one Madani of Jamat-\n\nIslam President through one Chote Mulla of Bangalore, who\n\nis Secretary, Islam-E-Jamat and that he would take them to\n\nMadani.   At para.32, he has stated   that in the second or\n\nthird week of May, 2000 Anwar@Hanif brought one floppy\n\nfrom A.24 stating that it was from A.19 regarding DRDL\n\ninformation. At para.35 he has stated that on 21.06.2000\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_503\">                             450</span>\n\n\n\n\nwhen A.7 arrived at A.19&#x27;s apartment, his brother A.6 along\n\nwith A.18 and driver A.21 also came there.      A.2 went for\n\nsome marriage dinner and he came to A.19&#x27;s room and then\n\ntold that they would go to Bangalore.    Around 24.00 hrs.,\n\nthey started to Bangalore in Tata Sumo.        They reached\n\nBangalore at 16.00 hrs and had lunch. Then, A.18 and A.19\n\nwent for taking Xerox copies of the documents which A.7\n\nhad bought as per the instructions of A.2. Thereafter A.19\n\nand A.18 came back after getting the Xerox copies.     All of\n\nthem sat in A.10&#x27;s house and they had a meeting.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_770\">415.        The aforesaid material discloses that A.19 was a\n\nVideographer by occupation and was a resident of Deendar\n\nAnjuman Ashram, Asif Nagar, Hyderabad.         He had been\n\ntrained in arms, ammunitions and explosives at Mardan in\n\nPakistan. After returning to India, he held secret conspiracy\n\nmeetings with the Zonal Chiefs of Deendar Anjuman\n\nAshram, deceased A.6 his brother A.7 and absconding A.2.\n\nHe also participated in the secret conspiracy meetings in new\n\nAshoka Lodge, Hyderabad in the house of A.26 at Hubli as\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_504\">                               451</span>\n\n\n\n\nwell as meeting in the house of A.25 at Bhatkurki and other\n\nplaces. He did all these overt acts to commit jihad, nifaaq\n\nand siria activities ultimately to achieve the aims and\n\nobjections of the banned Deendar Anjuman Organization.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_771\">416.        A.20 was aged about 34 years.          He was the\n\nProprietor of a Grocery shop situated in Deendar Anjuman\n\nAshram, Asifnagar, Hyderabad.       He is residing at No.10-2-\n\n409 situated within the premises of the said Ashram. He was\n\nalso sent to Mardan, Pakistan to receive training in arms and\n\nammunitions, preparation, planting and blasting of bombs.\n\nAfter being trained in Pakistan, A.20 came to Hyderabad,\n\nparticipated in conspiracy meetings, visited Bangalore, Hubli\n\nand other places, attended conspiracy meetings along with\n\nA.25 and planted bomb in the Church at Goa and blasted it.\n\nHe participated in jihad, nifaaq and siria activities to achieve\n\nthe aims and objectives of Deendar Anjuman Organisation.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_772\">417.        P.W.54 - Abdul Gafoor has spoken about the\n\nparticipation of A.20 in these activities, in his evidence.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_505\">                              452</span>\n\n\n\n\nP.W.54 in his evidence has categorically stated that after Akil\n\nleft his house, the next day, A.25 called him to inform that\n\nhe and A.20 blasted a Church at Goa and the rest of the\n\ninformation he could get, by watching TV.          A.7 in his\n\nconfessional statement at Ex.P245 has referred to the role of\n\nA.20 in the conspiracy and activities in pursuance of the\n\nconspiracy. He has stated at para.19 that A.20 had gone to\n\nPakistan for training in arms, ammunitions and explosives.\n\nAt para.23, he has referred to the participation of A.20 in the\n\nmeeting at Mastan Basha&#x27;s house, which he was not sure.\n\nAgain in January, 2000, when Bhatakurki batch came in\n\nTata sumo, A.7 and A.6 went to Hyderabad and met A.24,\n\nA.19, A.20 and others. All of them sat in A.19&#x27;s apartment\n\nwhere they attended the meeting on 3rd day of Urs. There A.6\n\ntold the persons to co-operate with A.24, as he was selected\n\nby A.1 and they had to damage Railways, IOC, FCI, Electric\n\n(Grid), Government installations by Sarria, Road TPT. In\n\nbetween A.7 interfered and asked them to choose in which\n\neach were familiar. At that time, A.20 told that he would\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_506\">                             453</span>\n\n\n\n\nmanage Railways. Again, he has referred to another meeting\n\nin August or September, 2000 in A.19&#x27;s apartment, where\n\nA.20 was present along with others. They wanted money to\n\npurchase a site for keeping the explosives. A.6 told them to\n\ntake one chit from chit fund company by contributing the\n\namount themselves. After that A.20 took them to next room\n\nand gave a letter containing that they had to meet one\n\nMadani of Jamat-Islam President through one P.W.7, and\n\nfurther told that he would take two code letter chart to\n\nMadani, which shows as to how to contact and what are the\n\nthings to be done. The code in another correspondence\n\nshows &quot;Mother Father Safe&quot; means Sarria is OK, &quot;Mother\n\nFather Unwell&quot; means Sarria is to be carried out and etc.,.\n\nA.20 gave one copy of the third message in which there was\n\nan instruction to look for transportation rom Bengal if\n\nexplosives   come   and   A.20    gave   an   E-mail   address\n\n`adnan@USA.net&#x27;, if any messages are to be sent. It is\n\nfurther stated that later, some of the members met in Hubli\n\nand discussed about A.20&#x27;s letter and procurement of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_507\">                             454</span>\n\n\n\n\nrevolvers. Then they all left to Nuzvid where they discussed\n\nabout A.20&#x27;s program, which he had brought from A.1 in\n\nMarch 2000. At para.38 it is stated that SP and DSP took\n\nA.7 to Hyderabad to cross-verify as to what A.7 had told was\n\ncorrect or not. He did not know who was with A.1 in\n\nPakistan. A.20 told that one Ahmedi and others were there\n\nwith A.1.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_773\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_774\">418. A.25 was aged about 56 years on the date of the\n\nincident. He was a resident of Bhatakurki village, Ramadurg\n\nTaluk, Belgaum District. A.25 told P.W.54 - Abdul Gafoor that\n\n`ghoda&#x27; means pistol and that he would arrange for it in Goa.\n\nThereafter, in the presence of A.25 and other members\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_514\">                              461</span>\n\n\n\n\ndeceased A.6 held a conspiracy meeting in the house of\n\nP.W.54. A.24 is one of the persons,\n\nwho had gone to Pakistan, to take training in arms,\n\nammunitions and explosives. A.7 told them to give respect to A.25 and select\n\nthe job whatever they could do. From this evidence, it is clear that A.25 was the\n\nPresident of Deendar Anjuman Organisation, Bhatakurki\n\nBranch and that he was also an active member of the\n\norganization. He was delivering\n\nlecturer in Mosque.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_789\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_790\">427.        A.26 was aged about 44 years on the date of the\n\nincident. He was a railway employee at Hubli residing at\n\nKolekar flat, Old Hubli, Dharwad District. He was the\n\nSecretary of Deendar Anjuman Organisation, Hubli Branch.\n\nHe is the sister&#x27;s son of of A.12. P.W.54 - Abdul Gafoor in\n\nhis evidence has spoken about the overt acts and complicity\n\nof A.26 in the activities of Deendar Anjuman organization.\n\nHe has deposed that he was an active member of the said\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_517\">                               464</span>\n\n\n\n\norganization and so he was visiting that Ashram every year\n\nduring the celebration of Urs. A.26 was known to him since\n\nlong time. He identified him before the Court. A.7 in his confessional statement at Ex.P.245\n\nhas stated the role of A.26 in the conspiracy and in achieving\n\nthe object of the Deendar Anjuman Ashram. A.26 is one of\n\nthe persons, who had gone to Pakistan, to receive training in\n\narms, ammunitions and explosives. A.7 has spoken about\n\nthe presence of A.26 in the 3rd day of Urs in September 1999\n\nat 7.40 hours at the premises of Deendar Anjuman Ashram,\n\nAsif Nagar, Hyderabad. It is stated that after Ramjan festival\n\nA.13 came to Nuzvid to know about the programme. As\n\nnothing   was    important,   A.6,   A.7   and   A.13   went   to\n\nVijayawada. There A.6 and A.13 told the members there that\n\ntheir first priority was money and for that they had to plan\n\nfor Sarria.   In the meantime, A.26 and A.27 came over to\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_518\">                             465</span>\n\n\n\n\nMastan Basha&#x27;s house, where he explained about the\n\nRailway culverts to be surveyed in Hubli area and inform\n\nhim. There, Iqbal Ahmed and A.26 exchanged their e-mail\n\naddresses.   Then next, A.7 and A.6 went to Hubli.      A.11\n\ninformed A.26 about, the coming to Hubli home. They met\n\nA.26 and discussed about A.20&#x27;s letter and procurement of\n\nrevolvers. From the aforesaid evidence, it is clear that A.26\n\nhad taken an active part in achieving the object of the\n\nDeendar Anjuman Ashram.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_793\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_794\">429.         A.27 was aged about 37 years on the date of the\n\nincident.    He was running a commission agency of\n\ncomputers at Hubli. He was residing at Asar Oni, Old Hubli,\n\nDist. Dharwad. He was closely associated with A.26. P.W.54\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_795\">- Abdul Gafoor has narrated in his evidence, the overt act\n\ncommitted by A.27. He has deposed that A.27 was regularly\n\nattending the celebration of urs in Deendar Anjuman\n\nAshram, Hyderabad every year. He identified him before the\n\nCourt. He attended monthly last Saturday meetings in the\n\nhouse of Sangli Basha at Batakurki. A.7 in his confessional\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_519\">                              466</span>\n\n\n\n\nstatement at Ex.P245 has stated about the role of A.27 in\n\nthe conspiracy. It is stated that after Ramjan festival A.13\n\ncame to Nuzvid to know about the programme. As nothing\n\nwas important, A.6, A.7 and A.13 went to Vijayawada. There\n\nA.6 and A.13 told the members there that their first priority\n\nwas money and for `that they had to plan for Sarria. In the\n\nmeantime, A.26 and A.27 came over to Mastan Basha&#x27;s\n\nhouse, where he explained about the Railway culverts to be\n\nsurveyed in Hubli area and inform him.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_796\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_797\">430.        <a href=\"/doc/1946503/\" id=\"a_374\">Section 10</a> of the Evidence Act introduces the\n\ndoctrine of agency and if the conditions laid down therein are\n\nsatisfied, the act done by one is admissible against the co-\n\nconspirators. But this section will come into play only when\n\nthe Court is satisfied that there is reasonable ground to\n\nbelieve that two or more persons have conspired together to\n\ncommit an offence or an actionable wrong, that is to say,\n\nthere should be a prima facie evidence that a person was a\n\nparty to the conspiracy before his acts can be used against\n\nhis co-conspirators. Once such a reasonable ground exists,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_520\">                              467</span>\n\n\n\n\nanything said, done or written by one of the conspirators in\n\nreference to the common intention, after the said intention\n\nwas entertained is relevant against the others not only for\n\nthe purpose of proving the existence of the conspiracy but\n\nalso for proving that the other person was a party to it.\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1946503/\" id=\"a_375\">Section 10</a> reads thus:\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_798\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_303\">SECTION 10 OF THE EVIDENCE ACT\n\n      &quot;10. Things said or done by conspirator in\n      reference to common design.- Where there is\n      reasonable ground to believe that two or more\n      persons have conspired together to commit an\n      offence or an actionable wrong, anything said,\n      done or written by any one of such persons in\n      reference to their common intention, after the time\n      when such intention was first entertained by any\n      one of them, is a relevant fact as against each of\n      the persons believed to be so conspiring, as well\n      for the purpose of proving the existence of the\n      conspiracy as for the purpose of showing that\n      any such person was a party to it.&quot;\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_521\">                                468</span>\n\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_799\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1912859\" id=\"span_522\">431.         This particular aspect of the law has been\n\nconsidered by the Privy Council in <a href=\"/doc/1912859/\" id=\"a_376\">MIRZA AKBAR Vs. KING\n\nEMPEROR</a>, (AIR 1940 PC 176) at p.180, where Lord Wright\n\nsaid that there is no difference in principle in Indian Law in\n\nview of <a href=\"/doc/1946503/\" id=\"a_377\">sec. 10</a> of the Evidence Act. It is held thus:</span>\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_304\">       &quot;16. In their Lordships&#x27; judgment, the Judicial\n       Commissioner misconstrued the effect of Section\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_305\">       10. The English rule on this matter is in general\n       well settled. It is a common law rule not based\n       on, or limited by, express statutory words. The\n       leading; case of The Queen v. Blake (1844) 6 Q.B.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_306\">       <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1912859\" id=\"span_523\">126. illustrates the two aspects of it, because that\n       authority shows both what is admissible and\n       what is inadmissible. What <a href=\"/doc/1912859/\" id=\"a_378\">in that case</a> was held\n       to be admissible against the conspirator was the\n       evidence of entries made by his fellow conspirator\n       contained in various documents actually used for\n       carrying out the fraud. But a document not\n       created   in   the   course   of   carrying   out   the\n       transaction, but made by one of the conspirators\n       after the fraud was completed, was held to be\n       inadmissible against the other. No doubt what\n       was contained in it amounted to a statement\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_524\">                        469</span>\n\n\n\n\nevidencing what had been done and also the\ncommon intent with which at the time it had been\ndone, but it had nothing to do with carrying the\nconspiracy into effect. Lord Denman said at p.\n138 that the evidence must be rejected the\nprinciple that a mere statement made by one\nconspirator to a third party or any act not done in\npursuance of the conspiracy is not evidence for or\nagainst another conspirator.</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_307\">17. Patteson J. described it as &quot;a statement made\nafter the conspiracy was effected&quot;. Williams J.\nsaid that it merely related &quot;to a conspiracy at that\ntime completed&quot;. Coleridge J. said that it &quot;did not\nrelate to the furtherance of the common object&quot;.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_800\">The words relied upon in <a href=\"/doc/1946503/\" id=\"a_379\">Section 10</a> of the Indian\nEvidence Act are &quot;in reference to their common\nintention&quot;. These words may have been chosen as\nhaving the same significance as the word &#x27;\nrelated&#x27; used by Williams and Coleridge JJ.\nWhere the evidence is admissible it is in their\nLordships&#x27; judgment on the principle that the\nthing done, written or spoken, was something\ndone in carrying out the conspiracy and was\nreceivable as a step in the proof of the conspiracy\n(per Patteson J. at p. 139). The words written or\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_525\">                        470</span>\n\n\n\n\nspoken may be a declaration accompanying an\nact and indicating the quality of the act as being\nan act in the course of the conspiracy: or the\nwords written or spoken may in themselves be\nacts done in the course of the conspiracy. This\nbeing the principle, their Lordships think the\nwords of <a href=\"/doc/1946503/\" id=\"a_380\">Section 10</a> must be construed in\naccordance with it and are not capable of being\nwidely construed so as to include a statement\nmade by one conspirator in the absence of the\nother with reference to past acts done in the\nactual course of carrying out the conspiracy, after\nit has been completed. The common intention is in\nthe past. In their Lordships&#x27; judgment, the words\n&quot;common intention&quot; signify a common intention\nexisting at the time when the thing was said,\ndone or written by the one of them. Things said,\ndone or written while the conspiracy was on foot\nare relevant as evidence of the common intention,\nonce reasonable ground has been shown to\nbelieve in its existence. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1560714\" id=\"span_526\">But it would be a very\ndifferent matter to hold that any narrative or\nstatement or confession made to a third party\nafter the common intention or conspiracy was no\nlonger operating and had ceased to exist is\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_527\">                               471</span>\n\n\n\n\n       admissible against the other party. There is then\n       no common intention of the conspirators to which\n       the statement can have reference. In their\n       Lordships&#x27; judgment <a href=\"/doc/1946503/\" id=\"a_381\">Section 10</a> embodies this\n       principle. That is the construction which has been\n       rightly applied to <a href=\"/doc/1946503/\" id=\"a_382\">Section 10</a> in decisions in India,\n       for instance, in <a href=\"/doc/1560714/\" id=\"a_383\">Emperor v. Vaishampayan</a> (1931)\n       I.L.R. 55 Born. 839 : s.c. 33 Bom.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1265598\" id=\"span_528\"><a href=\"/doc/1265598/\" id=\"a_384\">L.R. 1159,\n       Emperor v. Abani Bhushan Chuckerbutty</a> (1910)\n       I.L.R. 38 Cal. 169. In these cases the distinction\n       was   rightly drawn between communications\n       between conspirators while the conspiracy was\n       going on with reference to the carrying out of the\n       conspiracy and statements made, after arrest or\n       after the conspiracy has ended, by way of\n       description of events then past.&quot;</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_801\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_802\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"493883\" id=\"span_529\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1912859\" id=\"span_530\">432.         The decision of the Privy Council in <a href=\"/doc/1912859/\" id=\"a_385\">Mirza\n\nAkbar</a>&#x27;s case has been referred to with approval in <a href=\"/doc/493883/\" id=\"a_386\">SARDUL\n\nSINGH CAVEESHAR V. THE STATE OF BOMBAY</a>, [1958]\n\nSCR 161 at p.193, wherein Jagannadhadas, J., said:</span></span>\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_308\">       <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1912859\" id=\"span_531\">&quot;The limits of the admissibility of evidence in\n       conspiracy case under<a href=\"/doc/1946503/\" id=\"a_387\"> s.10</a> of the Evidence Act\n       have been authoritatively <a href=\"/doc/493883/\" id=\"a_388\">laid down by</a> the Privy\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_532\">                                 472</span>\n\n\n\n\n       Council in <a href=\"/doc/1912859/\" id=\"a_389\">Mirza Akbar v. The King Emperor</a>,\n       (supra). In that case, their Lordships of the Privy\n       Councilheld that <a href=\"/doc/1946503/\" id=\"a_390\">sec.10</a> of the Evidence Act must\n       be construed in accordance with the principle that\n       the thing done, written or spoken, was something\n       done in carrying out the conspiracy and was\n       receivable as a step in the proof of the conspiracy.</span>\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_803\">       They notice that evidence receivable under<a href=\"/doc/1946503/\" id=\"a_391\"> s. 10</a>\n       of the Evidence Act of &quot;anything said, done or\n       written,      by   one   of   such   persons&quot;   (i.e.,\n       conspirators) must be &quot;in reference to their\n       common intention.&quot; But their Lordships held that\n       in the context (notwithstanding the amplitude of\n       the above phrase) the words therein are not\n       capable of being widely construed having regard\n       to the well-known principle above enunciated.&quot;\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_804\">433.         In this connection, one may with advantage refer\n\nto American Jurisprudence, Vol.11<a href=\"/doc/813048/\" id=\"a_392\"> S. 23</a>, at page 559, where\n\nit is stated thus:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_309\">             &quot;The conspiracy is held to be continued and\n       renewed as to all its members wherever and\n       whenever any member of the conspiracy acts in\n       furtherance of the common design.&quot;\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_533\">                                 473</span>\n\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_805\">434.         In UNITED STATES V. KISSEL, (1909) 218 US\n\n601: 54 LAW ED 1168 Mr. Justice Holmes in answer to a\n\ncontention that the prosecution was barred by the law of\n\nlimitation said:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_310\">             &quot;But when the plot contemplates bringing to\n       pass a continuous result that will not continue\n       without     the   continuous   co-operation    of   the\n       conspirators to keep it up, and there is such\n       continuous co-operation, it is a perversion of\n       natural thought and of natural language to call\n       such continuous co-operation a cinematographic\n       series of distant conspiracies, rather than to call it\n       a single one ..... A conspiracy is constituted by an\n       agreement, it is true, but it is the..... result of the\n       agreement, rather than the agreement itself, just\n       as a partnership, although constituted by a\n       contract, is not the contract, but is a result of it.\n       .....A conspiracy is a partnership in criminal\n       purposes. That as such it may have continuation\n       in time is shown by the rule that an overt act of\n       one partner may be the act of all without any new\n       agreement specifically directed to that act.&quot;\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_534\">                                  474</span>\n\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_806\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1769219\" id=\"span_535\">435.        The Apex Court in the said case of <a href=\"/doc/1769219/\" id=\"a_393\">&#x27;STATE (NCT\n\nOF DELHI) v. NAVJOT SANDHU ALIAS AFSAN GURU&#x27;</a>\n\nreported in 2005 SCC (Crl) 1715 has held at paragraphs\n\n101 and 103 as under:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_311\">       101. One more principle which deserves notice\n       is   that    cumulative    effect    of   the     proved\n       circumstances should be taken into account in\n       determining the guilt of the accused rather than\n       adopting an isolated approach to each of the\n       circumstances. Of course, each one of the\n       circumstances     should        be   proved     beyond\n       reasonable doubt. Lastly, in regard to the\n       appreciation of evidence relating to conspiracy,\n       the Court must take care to see that the acts or\n       conduct of the parties must be conscious and\n       clear enough to infer their concurrence as to the\n       common design and its execution.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_312\">436.        The Apex Court in Ferojuddin&#x27;s case observed\n\nthus at para 25: (SCC p.607)\n\n            &quot;Thus,     one   who        enters    into     a\n            conspiratorial relationship is liable for\n            every    reasonably        foreseeable     crime\n            committed by every other member of the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_536\">                                     475</span>\n\n\n\n\n              conspiracy       in    furtherance         of   its\n              objectives, whether or not he knew of the\n              crimes or aided in their commission.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_313\">437.          <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"928322\" id=\"span_537\">In <a href=\"/doc/928322/\" id=\"a_394\">AJAY AGGARWAL VS. UNION OF INDIA</a>\n\nreported in 1993 (3) SCC 609, while discussing the question\n\nwhether the conspiracy is a continuing offence, the following\n\npertinent observations were made by K. Ramaswamy, J,\n\nspeaking for the Bench at para 11: (SCC p.618)</span>\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_314\">       <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"117134\" id=\"span_538\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"493883\" id=\"span_539\">108.     Thus,    a    distinction    was     maintained\n       between     the       conspiracy     and    the    offences\n       committed pursuant to the conspiracy. It is only\n       in order to prove the existence of conspiracy and\n       the parties to the conspiracy, that a rule of\n       evidence is enacted in <a href=\"/doc/1946503/\" id=\"a_395\">Section 10</a> based on the\n       principle of agency. We may recall that <a href=\"/doc/1946503/\" id=\"a_396\">Section\n       10</a> of the Evidence Act provides that anything\n       said, done or written by one of the conspirators\n       in reference to the common intention of all of\n       them can be proved as a relevant fact as\n       against each of the conspirators, subject to the\n       condition prescribed in the opening part of the\n       section. Thus, the evidence which is in the\n       nature of hearsay is made admissible on the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_540\">                            476</span>\n\n\n\n\nprinciple that there is mutual agency amongst\nthe conspirators. It is in the context of <a href=\"/doc/1946503/\" id=\"a_397\">Section 10</a>\nthat the relevant observations were made in the\nfirst <a href=\"/doc/493883/\" id=\"a_398\">Caveeshar</a> case (<a href=\"/doc/117134/\" id=\"a_399\">AIR 1957 SC 747) and\nNalini</a>&#x27;s case at page 517. In the former case,\nJagannadhadas,        J,     after   referring   to    the\npassage in Roscoe&#x27;s Criminal Evidence (16th\nEdn.)</span></span> that &quot;an overt act committed by any one of\nthe conspirators is sufficient, on the general\nprinciples of agency, to make it the act of all&quot;,\nobserved that\n     &quot;the principle underlying the reception of\n     evidence under Section 10 of the Evidence Act\n     of the statements, acts and writings of one co-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_807\">     conspirator as against the other is on the\n     theory of agency&quot; (SCR p.205).\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_808\">113. It may be noted that by the 1977 Act, the\noffence of conspiracy at common law was\nabolished      and    a     statutory     definition    of\n&quot;conspiracy to commit the offence&quot; was enacted.\nThe provision that was interpreted by the House\nof Lords is not in pari materia with the provision\nin    the   <a href=\"/doc/1569253/\" id=\"a_400\">Indian   Penal       Code</a>.   However,      one\nclarification is needed. If there is proof to the\neffect that the accused played a role, attended\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_541\">                           477</span>\n\n\n\n\nto certain things or took steps consistent with\nthe common design underlying the conspiracy,\nthat will go a long way in establishing the\ncomplicity of the accused, though it is not a legal\nrequirement that the conspirator should do any\nparticular act beyond the agreement to commit\nthe offence.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_809\">      44.    Privacy      and   secrecy         are   more\ncharacteristics of a conspiracy, than of a loud\ndiscussion in an elevated place open to public\nview. Direct evidence in proof of a conspiracy is\nseldom available, offence of conspiracy can be\nproved      by   either    direct    or    circumstantial\nevidence. It is not always possible to give\naffirmative evidence about the date of the\nformation of the criminal conspiracy, about the\npersons who took part in the formation of the\nconspiracy, about the object, which the objectors\nset   before     themselves         as    the   object   of\nconspiracy, and about the manner in which the\nobject of conspiracy is to be carried out, all this\nis necessarily a matter of inference.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_810\">      48. As noted above, the essential ingredient\nof the offence of criminal conspiracy is the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_542\">                      478</span>\n\n\n\n\nagreement to commit an offence. In a case where\nthe agreement is for accomplishment of an act\nwhich by itself constitutes an offence, then in\nthat event no overt act is necessary to be proved\nby the prosecution because in such a situation,\ncriminal conspiracy is established by proving\nsuch   an   agreement.   Where   the   conspiracy\nalleged is with regard to commission of a serious\ncrime of the nature as contemplated in Section\n120B read with the proviso to sub-section (2) of\nSection 120A, then in that event mere proof of an\nagreement between the accused for commission\nof such a crime alone is enough to bring about a\nconviction under Section 120B and the proof of\nany overt act by the accused or by any one of\nthem would not be necessary. The provisions, in\nsuch a situation, do not require that each and\nevery person who is a party to the conspiracy\nmust do some overt act towards the fulfillment of\nthe object of conspiracy, the essential ingredient\nbeing an agreement between the conspirators to\ncommit the crime and if these requirements and\ningredients are established, the act would fall\nwithin the trappings of the provisions contained\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_543\">                               479</span>\n\n\n\n\n       in section 120B [See: S.C. Bahri v. State of Bihar\n       (AIR 1994 SC 2420)].\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_811\">           <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"854140\" id=\"span_544\">49. The conspiracies are not hatched in the\n       open, by their nature, they are secretly planned,\n       they can be proved even by circumstantial\n       evidence, the lack of direct evidence relating to\n       conspiracy has no consequence. [See: <a href=\"/doc/854140/\" id=\"a_401\">E.K.\n       Chandrasenan v. State of Kerala</a> (AIR 1995 SC\n       1066)].</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_812\">       <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"474644\" id=\"span_545\">51. Where trustworthy evidence establishing all\n       links of circumstantial evidence is available, the\n       confession of a co-accused as to conspiracy even\n       without corroborative evidence can be taken into\n       consideration.    (See <a href=\"/doc/474644/\" id=\"a_402\">Baburao Bajirao Patil v.\n       State of Maharashtra</a>). It can in some cases be\n       inferred from the acts and conduct of the parties.</span>\n       (See Shivnarayan Laxminarayan Joshi v. State\n       of Maharashtra)\n\n\nSection 30 of the Evidence Act\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_813\">438.       Similarly, <a href=\"/doc/1974480/\" id=\"a_403\">Section 30</a> of the Evidence Act, which\n\ndeals with consideration of proved confession affecting\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_546\">                               480</span>\n\n\n\n\nperson making it and others jointly under trial for same\n\noffence, is quoted below:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_315\">       &quot;30.   Consideration     of   proved   confession\n       affecting person making it and others jointly\n       under trial for same offence - When more\n       persons than one are being tried jointly for the\n       same offence, and a confession made by one of\n       such persons affecting himself and some other of\n       such persons is proved, the Court may take into\n       consideration such confession as against such\n       other person as well as against the person who\n       makes such confession.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_316\">       Explanation--&#x27;Offence&#x27; as used in this section,\n       includes the abetment of, or attempt to commit, the\n       offence.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_814\">439.          This Section says that when more persons than\n\none are being tried jointly for the same offence and a\n\nconfession, made by one of such persons affecting himself\n\nand some other of such persons, the Court may take into\n\nconsideration such confession against the maker of the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_547\">                               481</span>\n\n\n\n\nconfession as well as against such other person when such a\n\nconfession is proved in Court.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_815\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_816\">440.         Sir John Beaumont, speaking for the Privy\n\nCouncil, in BHUBONI SAHU V. THE KING (AIR 1949 PC\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_817\">257), an oft-quoted authority, observed in regard to <a href=\"/doc/1974480/\" id=\"a_404\">Section\n\n30</a> of the Evidence Act, thus:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_317\">       &quot;<a href=\"/doc/1974480/\" id=\"a_405\">Section 30</a> seems to be based on the view that\n       an admission by an accused person of his own\n       guilt affords some sort of sanction in support of\n       the truth of his confession against others as well\n       as himself. But a confession of a co-accused is\n       obviously evidence of a very weak type. It does\n       not indeed come within the definition of &#x27;evidence&#x27;\n       contained in <a href=\"/doc/1031309/\" id=\"a_406\">Section 3</a>. It is not required to be\n       given on oath, nor in the presence of the accused,\n       and it cannot be tested by cross-examination. It\n       is a much weaker type of evidence than the\n       evidence of an approver which is not subject to\n       any of those infirmities.    <a href=\"/doc/1974480/\" id=\"a_407\">Section 30</a>, however,\n       provides that the Court may take the confession\n       into consideration and thereby, no doubt, makes\n       it evidence on which the Court may act; but the\n       section does not say that the confession is to\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_548\">                               482</span>\n\n\n\n\n       amount to proof.    Clearly there must be other\n       evidence. The confession is only one element in\n       the consideration of all the facts proved in the\n       case; it can be put into the scale and weighed\n       with the other evidence. The confession of a co-\n       accused can be used only in support of other\n       evidence and cannot be made the foundation of a\n       conviction.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_818\">441.         <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1924452\" id=\"span_549\">In <a href=\"/doc/1924452/\" id=\"a_408\">KASHMIRA SINGH V. STATE OF MADHYA\n\nPRADESH</a>, (1952 Cri LJ 839) the Apex Court approved the\n\nprinciples <a href=\"/doc/1924452/\" id=\"a_409\">laid down by</a> the Privy Council in Bhuboni Sahu&#x27;s\n\ncase (supra) and observed:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_318\">       &quot;But cases may arise where the Judge is not\n       prepared to act on the other evidence as it stands\n       even though, if believed, it would be sufficient to\n       sustain a conviction. In such an event the Judge\n       may call in aid the confession and use it to lend\n       assurance to the other evidence and thus fortify\n       himself in believing what without the aid of the\n       confession he would not be prepared to accept.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_819\">442.         <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1924452\" id=\"span_550\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"331743\" id=\"span_551\">In <a href=\"/doc/331743/\" id=\"a_410\">HARI CHARAN KURMI AND JOGIA HAJAM\n\nV. STATE OF BIHAR</a> (1964 (2) Cri LJ 344), a Constitution\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_552\">                                  483</span>\n\n\n\n\nBench of this Court after referring to Bhuboni Sahu&#x27;s case\n\n(<a href=\"/doc/1924452/\" id=\"a_411\">supra) and Kashmira Singh</a>&#x27;s case (supra), observed thus:</span></span>\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_319\">     &quot;Normally, if a statement made by an accused\n     person is found to be voluntary and it amounts to\n     a confession in the sense that it implicates the\n     maker, it is not likely that the maker would\n     implicate himself untruly, and so, <a href=\"/doc/1974480/\" id=\"a_412\">S.30</a> provides\n     that such a confession              may be      taken into\n     consideration even against a co-accused who is\n     being      tried    along   with    the   maker     of   the\n     confession.....        When    <a href=\"/doc/1974480/\" id=\"a_413\">S.30</a>    provides     that   the\n     confession of a co-accused may be taken into\n     consideration, what exactly is the scope and effect\n     of such taking into consideration, is precisely the\n     problem which has been raised in the present\n     appeals.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_820\">     It   was     held    that   technically   construed,     the\n     definition of the term &quot;evidence&quot; in<a href=\"/doc/1031309/\" id=\"a_414\"> S.3</a> would not\n     apply to confession. It was observed:\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_821\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_320\">     &quot;Even so, <a href=\"/doc/1974480/\" id=\"a_415\">S.30</a> provides that a confession may be\n     taken into consideration not only against its\n     maker, but also against a co-accused person; that\n     is to say, though such a confession may not be\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_553\">                                484</span>\n\n\n\n\n       evidence as strictly defined by<a href=\"/doc/1031309/\" id=\"a_416\"> S.3</a> of the Act, it is\n       an element which may be taken into consideration\n       by the criminal Court and in that sense, it may be\n       described as evidence in a non-technical way. But\n       it is significant that like other evidence which is\n       produced before the Court, it is not obligatory on\n       the Court to take the confession into account.\n       When evidence as defined by the Act is produced\n       before the Court, it is the duty of the Court to\n       consider that evidence.    What weight should be\n       attached to such evidence, is a matter in the\n       discretion of the Court. But a Court cannot say in\n       respect of such evidence that it will just not take\n       that evidence into account.     Such an approach,\n       can, however, be adopted by the Court in dealing\n       with a confession, because<a href=\"/doc/1974480/\" id=\"a_417\"> S.30</a> merely enables\n       the Court to take the confession into account.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_822\">443.         In the cases<a href=\"/doc/1924452/\" id=\"a_418\">, <a href=\"/doc/1924452/\" id=\"a_419\">referred to above</a></a>, it was held that\n\nconfession of a co-accused is not evidence as defined in\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1031309/\" id=\"a_420\">Section 3</a> of the Evidence Act and that <a href=\"/doc/1974480/\" id=\"a_421\">Section 30</a> enables\n\nthe Court to take into consideration the confession of a co-\n\naccused to lend assurance to other evidence against the co-\n\naccused.     The expression &#x27;may take into consideration&#x27;\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_554\">                              485</span>\n\n\n\n\nmeans that the use of the evidence of confession of an\n\naccused may be used for purposes of corroborating the\n\nevidence on record against the co-accused and that no\n\nconviction can be based on such confession.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_823\">444.        It is also to be borne in mind that the evidence of\n\nconfession of co-accused is not required to be given on oath,\n\nnor is given in the presence of the accused, and its veracity\n\ncannot be tested by cross-examination. Though the evidence\n\nof an accomplice is free from these shortcomings yet an\n\naccomplice is a person who having taken part in the\n\ncommission of offence, to save himself, betrayed his former\n\nassociates and placed himself on a safer plank-&#x27;a position in\n\nwhich he can hardly fail to have a strong bias in favour of\n\nthe prosecution&#x27; the position of the accused who has given\n\nconfessional statement implicating a co-accused is that he\n\nhas placed himself on the same plank and thus he sinks or\n\nsails along with the co-accused on the basis of his\n\nconfession. For these reasons, in so far as use of confession\n\nof an accused against a co-accused is concerned, rule of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_555\">                                486</span>\n\n\n\n\nprudence cautions the judicial discretion that it cannot be\n\nrelied upon unless corroborated generally by other evidence\n\non record.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_824\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_825\">445.           A plain reading of <a href=\"/doc/1974480/\" id=\"a_422\">Section 30</a> of the Evidence Act\n\ndiscloses that when the following conditions exist, namely, (i)\n\nmore persons than one are being tried jointly; (ii) the joint\n\ntrial of the persons is for the same offence; (iii) a confession\n\nis made by one of such persons (who are being tried jointly\n\nfor the same offence); (iv) such a confession affects the\n\nmaker as well as such persons who are being tried jointly for\n\nthe same offence; and (v) such a confession is proved in\n\nCourt.   The    Court   may    take   into   consideration   such\n\nconfession against the maker thereof as well as against such\n\npersons who are being jointly tried for the same offence.\n\n\nSECTION 313 <a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_423\">Cr.P.C</a>. STATEMENT\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_826\">446.           The learned Special Public Prosecutor submitted\n\nthat though the incriminating materials were put to the\n\naccused by way of statement under <a href=\"/doc/767287/\" id=\"a_424\">Section 313</a> Cr.P.C. and\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_556\">                                   487</span>\n\n\n\n\nan     opportunity   was   given        to   them       to     explain     the\n\nincriminating evidence against them, the accused have\n\nchosen to remain silent.          They have not explained the\n\nincriminating circumstances.        Therefore, their silence and\n\nfailure to explain the circumstances is a strong circumstance\n\nthat could be put against them, which would be counted as\n\nproviding    a   missing   link    for       building        the   chain    of\n\ncircumstances. In this context, it is necessary to look into\n\nSection 313 of the Cr.P.C.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_827\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_828\">447.          <a href=\"/doc/767287/\" id=\"a_425\">Section 313</a> of the Cr.P.C. reads as under:\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_321\">        313.Power to examine the accused.-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_322\">        (1) In every inquiry or trial, for the purpose of\n        enabling the accused personally to explain any\n        circumstances appearing in the evidence against\n        him, the Court -\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_323\">        (a) may at any stage, without previously warning\n        the accused put such questions to him as the\n        Court considers necessary;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_324\">        (b) shall, after the witnesses for the prosecution\n        have been examined and before he is called on\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_557\">                               488</span>\n\n\n\n\n       for his defense, question him generally on the\n       case:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_325\">       Provided that in a summons-case, where the\n       Court    has    dispensed      with   the   personal\n       attendance of the accused, it may also dispense\n       with his examination under clause (b).\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_326\">       (2) No oath shall be administered to the accused\n       when he is examined under sub-section (1).\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_327\">       (3) The accused shall not render himself liable to\n       punishment     by   refusing     to   answer   such\n       questions, or by giving false answers to them.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_328\">       (4) The answers given by the accused may be\n       taken into consideration in such inquiry or trial,\n       and put in evidence for or against him in any\n       other inquiry into, or trial for, any other offence\n       which such answers may, tend to show he has\n       committed.\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_829\">448.           The object of <a href=\"/doc/767287/\" id=\"a_426\">Section 313</a> Cr.P.C. is to give an\n\nopportunity to the accused to explain any circumstance\n\nagainst him in the evidence. It is intended to secure to the\n\naccused an opportunity to explain the evidence against him\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_558\">                                489</span>\n\n\n\n\nand therefore, to benefit the accused and not to operate as to\n\nhis detriment. This section indicates that in every enquiry or\n\ntrial with a view to enable the accused to explain anything\n\nagainst him in the evidence, (a) the Court may at any stage\n\nput questions to the accused, and (b) shall after the\n\nexamination of all prosecution witnesses question him\n\ngenerally.     No oath shall be given when the accused is\n\nexamined by Court under Sub-section (1). Examination of\n\naccused cannot be in the form of cross-examination. A\n\nstatement of accused recorded under <a href=\"/doc/767287/\" id=\"a_427\">Section 313</a> Cr.P.C. is\n\nnot to be treated as evidence much less as a substantive\n\npiece of evidence. The act of examination of the accused\n\nunder Section 313 is a solemn act of the Trial Court and\n\nshould   not    be   treated   as    an   empty   formality.   The\n\nexamination of the accused is a very important duty which\n\nshould be performed with particular care and in the proper\n\nway.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_830\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_831\">449.         The Section is intended mainly for the benefit of\n\nthe accused and also to help the Court in finding the truth.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_559\">                              490</span>\n\n\n\n\nThe principle on which it is based is that before the\n\ndamaging points in the prosecution evidence are used\n\nagainst the accused for determining his guilt, it is essential\n\nthat his pointed attention should be drawn to them one by\n\none avoiding the form of cross-examination in order to afford\n\nhim an opportunity of giving an explanation consistent with\n\nhis innocence. The correct method of performing the duty in\n\n<a href=\"/doc/767287/\" id=\"a_428\">Section 313</a> Cr.P.C. is not to ask generally if the accused has\n\nanything to say about the charges or the evidence against\n\nhim, but to place before him separately one by one in short\n\nsentences all the vital and salient parts of the evidence\n\nappearing against him in the simplest possible language so\n\nthat he can realize what things he has got to explain and to\n\nask him after putting to the accused each material fact\n\nagainst him whether he wanted to say anything about the\n\nmatter. It is irregular to roll up several distinct matters of\n\nevidence in single question. The questions should be split-\n\nup so as to deal with each distinct feature or material piece\n\nof evidence separately. The accused is not bound to answer\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_560\">                                 491</span>\n\n\n\n\nor say anything nor is there any compulsion.        If he says\n\nanything he does it voluntarily, knowing that under the law\n\nhe     could   have   stood   mute.   The   guarantee   against\n\ntestimonial compulsion in <a href=\"/doc/366712/\" id=\"a_429\">Article 20(3)</a> of the Constitution of\n\nIndia afforded by the words &quot;to be a witness&quot; is not confined\n\nto the statement of the accused called to the witness-stand,\n\nbut may well extend to compelled testimony previously\n\nobtained from him.       The accused cannot be punished for\n\nrefusing to answer.      The answers of the accused may be\n\ntaken into consideration for or against him.      It should be\n\nremembered that the question to the accused must relate to\n\nthe circumstances in the evidence which go against him and\n\nnot the inference which flows from the circumstances. The\n\nCourt must put aside all Counsel, and the accused shall be\n\nbrought face to face in order that the Court may hear the\n\ndefence from his own lips.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_832\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_833\">450.           The examination under Sub-Section (1)(a) can be\n\nmade at any stage, but as the questioning is only for the\n\npurpose of explaining any evidence appearing against the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_561\">                                 492</span>\n\n\n\n\naccused, it follows that the Court cannot question before any\n\nevidence is recorded against him which requires explanation.\n\nExamination is not an idle formality but has to be carried\n\nout in the interest of justice and fair play to the accused.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_834\">451.          Explaining the importance of this statement, the\n\nApex Court in the case of STATE OF MAHARASHTRA V/S.\n\nSURESH reported in 2000(1) SCC 471 held thus:\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_329\">       &quot;When the attention of the accused is drawn to\n       such circumstances, that inculpate him in relation\n       to the commission of the crime, and he fails to\n       offer an appropriate explanation or gives a false\n       answer with respect to the same, the said act\n       may be accounted as providing a missing link for\n       completing    the      chain   of    circumstances.\n       Undoubtedly, the prosecution has to prove its\n       case beyond reasonable doubt.         However, in\n       certain circumstances, the accused has to furnish\n       some     explanation      to   the    incriminating\n       circumstances, which has come in evidence, put\n       to him. A false explanation may be accounted as\n       providing a missing link for completing the chain\n       of circumstances.&quot;\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_562\">                                493</span>\n\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_835\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"194754\" id=\"span_563\">452.         The Apex Court again in the case of <a href=\"/doc/194754/\" id=\"a_430\">Mani\n\nKumar Thapa Vs. State of Sikkim</a> reported in (2002)7\n\nSCC 157 placing        reliance on the aforesaid judgment in\n\nSuresh&#x27;s case has reiterated the aforesaid legal position.</span>\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_836\">453.         Again in the case of Avatar Singh and others\n\nV/s. State of Punjab reported in (2002)7 SCC 419, the\n\nApex Court held thus:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_330\">       &quot;The object of examination under Section 313 is\n       well known i.e., it is to afford an opportunity to\n       the   accused    to   explain   the   circumstances\n       appearing in the evidence against him.         His\n       silence and failure to explain the circumstances is\n       a one strong circumstance that could be put\n       against him.    A case of drawing presumption\n       under <a href=\"/doc/731516/\" id=\"a_431\">Section 114</a> of the Evidence Act can\n       perhaps be made out.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_837\">454.         The Apex Court in the case of Rohtash kumar\n\nV/s. State of Haryana reported in 2013 Crl.L.J., 3183 at\n\npara 22 has held as under:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_331\">       &quot;It is obligatory on the part of the accused while\n       being examined under <a href=\"/doc/767287/\" id=\"a_432\">Section 313</a> Cr.P.C. to\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_564\">                               494</span>\n\n\n\n\n       furnish some explanation with respect to the\n       incriminating circumstances associated with him,\n       and the Court must take note of such explanation\n       even in a case of circumstancial evidence, to\n       decide whether or not, the chain of circumstances\n       is complete.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_838\">455.         Again in the case of Harivadan Babubhai Patel\n\nV/s. State of Gujarat reported in (2013) 7 SCC 45 at para\n\n28 had held as under:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_332\">       Though all the incriminating circumstances which\n       point to the guilt of the accused had been put to\n       him, yet he chose not to give any explanation\n       under <a href=\"/doc/767287/\" id=\"a_433\">Section 313</a> of Cr.P.C. except chasing the\n       mode of denial. It is well settled in law that when\n       the attention of the accused is drawn to the said\n       circumstances, that inculpated him in the crime\n       and he fails to offer appropriate explanation or\n       gives a false answer, the same can be counted as\n       providing a missing link for building the chain of\n       circumstances.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_333\">456.         In support of the said contention, the learned\n\ncounsel for the accused relied on the judgment of the Apex\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_565\">                                 495</span>\n\n\n\n\nCourt in the case of Inspector of Customs, Akhnoor, J. &amp;\n\nK.V.Yash Pal and another reported in 2009 Criminal Law\n\nJournal page 2251 wherein at Paragraph Nos.20, 21 and\n\n22, it is held as under:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_334\">              &quot;20. What is thee object of examination\n       of an accused under Section 313 of the Code?\n       The section itself declares the object in explicit\n       language that it is &quot;for the purpose of enabling\n       the   accused       personally    to        explain   any\n       circumstances       appearing     in        the   evidence\n       against him&quot;. In Jai Dev -vs- State of Punjab\n       (AIR 1963 SC 612) Gajendragadkar, J. (as he\n       then was) speaking for a three-Judge Bench\n       has focused on the ultimate test in determining\n       whether the provision has been fairly complied\n       with. He observed thus:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_335\">              &quot;The   ultimate     test        in     determining\n       whether or not the accused has been fairly\n       examined under Section 342 would be to\n       inquire whether, having regard to all the\n       questions put to him, he did get an opportunity\n       to say what he wanted to say in respect of\n       prosecution case against him.               If it appears\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_566\">                        496</span>\n\n\n\n\nthat the examination of the accused person\nwas defective and thereby a prejudice has\nbeen caused to him, that would no doubt be a\nserious infirmity.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_336\">      21. Thus it is well settled that the\nprovision is mainly intended to benefit the\naccused and as its corollary to benefit the\nCourt in reaching the final conclusion.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_337\">      22. At the same time it should be borne\nin mind that the provision is not intended to\nnail him to any position, but to comply with the\nmost salutary principle of       natural   justice\nenshrined in the maxim audi alteram partem.\nThe word &quot;may&quot; in clause (a) of sub-section (1)\nin Section 313 of the Code indicates, without\nany doubt, that even if the Court does not put\nany question under that clause the accused\ncannot raise any grievance for it.      But if the\nCourt fails to put the needed question under\nclause (b) of the sub-section it would result in a\nhandicap    to   the   accused    and     he   can\nlegitimately claim that no evidence, without\naffording him the opportunity to explain, can be\nused against him. It is now well settled that a\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_567\">                             497</span>\n\n\n\n\n       circumstance about which the accused was not\n       asked to explain cannot be used against him&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_839\">457.        Though the explanation offered by the accused\n\nunder <a href=\"/doc/767287/\" id=\"a_434\">Section 313</a> Cr.P.C. is not a substantive piece of\n\nevidence nor evidence under <a href=\"/doc/1031309/\" id=\"a_435\">Section 3</a> of the Indian\n\nEvidence Act, the said statement could be taken into\n\nconsideration by the Court while deciding the guilt of the\n\naccused.   It can be counted as providing a missing link for\n\nbuilding the chain of circumstances. His silence and failure\n\nto explain the circumstances is one strong circumstance that\n\ncould be inferred against him. A false explanation may be\n\naccounted as providing a missing link for completing the\n\nchain of circumstances. Similarly, though the statement of\n\naccused recorded under <a href=\"/doc/767287/\" id=\"a_436\">Section 313</a> Cr.P.C is not to be\n\ntreated as evidence, much less as substantive piece of\n\nevidence, the said statement could be taken aid of to lend\n\ncredence to the evidence led by the prosecution. However,\n\nthe said statement could be taken into consideration by the\n\nCourts while deciding the guilt of the accused.    It can be\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_568\">                              498</span>\n\n\n\n\ncounted as providing a missing link for building the chain of\n\ncircumstances with silence and failure to explain the\n\ncircumstances is one strong circumstances that could be put\n\nagainst him.     A false explanation may be counted as\n\nproviding a missing link for completing the chain of\n\ncircumstances. Therefore, when an opportunity is given to\n\nthe accused to have their say explaining the incriminating\n\ncircumstances which are proved against them, if they have\n\nchosen to be silent and if they have chosen to flatly deny all\n\nthose allegations that would be a strong circumstance,\n\nwhich can be counted as providing a missing link for\n\ncompleting the chain of circumstances. Certainly, the Court\n\ncan take into consideration the said conduct of the accused\n\nwhile deciding the guilt of the accused.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_840\">\nBURDEN OF PROOF\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_841\">458.        Further it was contended that the evidence on\n\nrecord do not establish the charge against all the accused.\n\nNot only the prosecution has to prove the charges levelled\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_569\">                              499</span>\n\n\n\n\nagainst the accused, but the said charges have to be proved\n\nbeyond reasonable doubt.       Viewed from that angle, the\n\nevidence on record do not establish the guilt of the accused\n\nbeyond reasonable doubt.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_842\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_843\">459.        The onus of proving everything essential to the\n\nestablishment of the charge against the accused lies on the\n\nprosecution as every man is presumed to be innocent. The\n\nprosecution must stand or fall on its own legs and it cannot\n\nderive any strength from the weakness of the defence. The\n\nweakness of the defence can only be called as additional link\n\nto aid the prosecution. It is not the law that where there is\n\nnay infirmity or lacuna in the prosecution case, the same\n\ncould be cured or supplied by a false defence or a plea which\n\nis not accepted, by a Court. The accused merely has to cast\n\na reasonable doubt in the prosecution case to be acquitted.\n\nThe prosecution cannot derive any advantage from the falsity\n\nor other infirmities of the defence version, so long as it does\n\nnot discharge its initial burden of proving its case beyond all\n\nreasonable doubt or any evidence in defence is produced, the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_570\">                              500</span>\n\n\n\n\nsilence of the accused being of no consequence. In criminal\n\ncases, the main burden, i.e, of proving the guilt of the\n\naccused beyond reasonable doubt always rests on the\n\nprosecution and never shifts even though any statute\n\nprovides for the drawing of some presumption against the\n\naccused in certain cases.     The burden of proving guilty\n\nintention lies upon the prosecution where the intent is\n\nexpressly stated as part of the definition of crime.       The\n\nburden of proving intention or knowledge is on the\n\nprosecution and the burden is not on the accused of showing\n\nabsence of knowledge. It is the duty of the Public Prosecutor\n\nto call every witness who can throw any light on the enquiry\n\nwhether they support the prosecution theory or the defence\n\ntheory.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_844\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_845\">460.        Burden of proof lies upon the person who has to\n\nprove a fact and it never shifts, but the Onus of proof shifts.\n\nSuch a shifting of Onus is a continuous process in\n\nevaluation of evidence. So basically the burden lies on the\n\nProsecution but there are times when the accused is called\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_571\">                              501</span>\n\n\n\n\nupon to prove that his case falls under an exception. Then\n\nthe onus is on the accused and it is considered as\n\ndischarged if the accused person succeeds in proving the\n\npreponderance of probability in his favor and he need not\n\nprove his stand beyond reasonable doubt.            It is the\n\nprosecution which has to prove the guilt of the accused\n\nbeyond reasonable doubt. What is beyond reasonable doubt\n\nhas been the subject matter of various decisions of the Apex\n\nCourt.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_846\">\nSTANDARD OF PROOF IN CRIMINAL CASES\n\nBEYOND REASONABLE DOUBT\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_847\">461.        The criminal jurisprudence as has developed on\n\nthe basis of British model, is that the offence alleged is\n\nrequired to be proved &quot;beyond all reasonable doubt. Proof\n\nbeyond reasonable doubt does not mean proof beyond a\n\nshadow of a doubt. &quot;Reasonable doubt&quot;, does not mean a\n\nvague, speculative or whimsical doubt or uncertainty, nor a\n\nmerely possible doubt of the truth of the fact to be proved. It\n\nalso does not mean proof to a mathematical certainty nor\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_572\">                               502</span>\n\n\n\n\nproof beyond the possibility of a mistake. A reasonable doubt\n\nis not an imaginary, trivial of merely possible doubt, but a\n\nfair doubt based upon reason and common sense. It must\n\ngrow out of the evidence in the case.      The requirement in\n\ncriminal case of proof &quot;beyond reasonable doubt&quot; to support\n\nconviction, therefore, does not mean proof beyond all\n\npossible doubts. What is to be noted is that the doubt which\n\nis required to be removed is of a reasonable man and not\n\nevery kind of doubt based on surmise or guess.That degree is\n\nwell settled.   It need not reach certainty, but it must carry a\n\nhigh degree of probability. The courts must strictly be\n\nsatisfied that no innocent person, innocent in the sense of\n\nnot being guilty of the offence of which he is charged, is\n\nconvicted, even at the risk of letting of some guilty persons.\n\nWhile the protection given by the criminal process to the\n\naccused-persons is not to be eroded, at the same time,\n\nuninformed legitimisation of trivialities would make a\n\nmockery of administration of criminal justice.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_573\">                                  503</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_848\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1035123\" id=\"span_574\">462.          The Apex Court in case of <a href=\"/doc/1035123/\" id=\"a_437\">SHIVAJI SAHEBRAO\n\nBOBADE V. STATE OF MAHARASHTRA</a>, reported in 1973\n\nSCC (CRI) 1033, held as under:-</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_849\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_338\">       &quot;The dangers of exaggerated devotion to the rule\n       of benefit of doubt at the expense of social\n       defence and to the soothing sentiment that all\n       acquittals are always good regardless of justice\n       to the victim and the community, demand\n       especial emphasis in the contemporary context of\n       escalating    crime   and    escape.    The    judicial\n       instrument has a public accountability. The\n       cherished principles or golden thread of proof\n       beyond reasonable doubt which runs through\n       the web our our law should not be stretched\n       morbidly to embrace every hunch, hestiancy and\n       degree of doubt ..... &quot; . . . . . The evil of acquitting\n       a guilty person light-heartedly as a learned\n       author (Glanville Williams in &#x27;Proof of Guilt&#x27;) has\n       sapiently observed, goes much beyond the\n       simple fact that just one guilty person has gone\n       unpunished. If unmerited acquittals become\n       general. they tend to lead to a cynical disregard\n       of the law, and this in turn leads to a public\n       demand for harsher legal presumptions against\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_575\">                                   504</span>\n\n\n\n\n       indicted &#x27;persons&#x27; and more severe punishment\n       of those who are found guilty. Thus too frequent\n       acquittals of the guilty may lead to a ferocious\n       penal    law,      eventually   eroding   the   judicial\n       protection of the guiltless . . . . .&quot; &quot;a miscarriage\n       of justice may arise from the acquittal of the\n       guilty no less than from the conviction of the\n       innocent.......&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_850\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"10271\" id=\"span_576\">463.           The Apex Court in case of <a href=\"/doc/10271/\" id=\"a_438\">STATE OF U.P. V.\n\nKRISHNA GOPAL AND ANOTHER</a> reported in 1988 SCC\n\n(Crl.) page 928, after referring to the several judgments has\n\nheld as under:-</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_851\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_339\">       25.     A person has, no doubt, a profound right\n       not to be convicted of an offence which is not\n       established by the evidential standard of proof\n       beyond reasonable doubt. Though this standard\n       is a higher standard, there is, however, no\n       absolute standard. What degree of probability\n       amounts to `proof&#x27; is an exercise particular to\n       each case. Referring to the inter-dependence of\n       evidence and the confirmation of one piece of\n       evidence by another a learned author says: (The\n       Mathematics of         Proof-II&quot;: Glanville Williams:\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_577\">                          505</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_340\">Criminal Law Review, 1979, by Sweet and\nMaxwell, p. 340 (342).\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_341\">      &quot;The simple multiplication rule does not\n      apply if the separate pieces of evidence\n      are dependent. Two events are dependent\n      when they tend to occur together, and the\n      evidence of such events may also be said\n      to be dependent. In a criminal case,\n      different pieces of evidence directed to\n      establishing that the defendant did the\n      prohibited act with the specified state of\n      mind are generally dependent. A juror\n      may feel doubt whether to credit an\n      alleged confession, and doubt whether to\n      infer guilt from the fact that the dependant\n      fled from justice. But since it is generally\n      guilty rather than innocent people who\n      make confessions, and guilty rather than\n      innocent people who run away, the two\n      doubt are not to be multiplied together. The\n      one piece of evidence may confirm the\n      other.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_342\">Doubts would be called reasonable if they are\nfree from a zest for abstract speculation. Law\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_578\">                             506</span>\n\n\n\n\ncannot afford any favourite other than truth. To\nconsitute reasonable doubt, it is must be free\nfrom an over emotional response. Doubts must\nbe actual and substantial doubts as to the guilt\nof the accused-person arising from the evidence,\nor from the lack of it, as opposed to mere vague\napprehensions. A reasonable doubt is not an\nimaginary, trivial or a merely possible doubt; but\na fair doubt based upon reason and common-\nsense. lt must grow out of the evidence in the\ncase.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_343\">26.     The    concepts    of   probability,   and       the\ndegrees of it, cannot obviously be expressed in\nterms of units to be mathematically enumerated\nas to how many of such units constitute proof\nbeyond        reasonable    doubt.     There        is   an\nunmistakable         subjective-element        in        the\nevaluation of the degrees of probability and the\nquantum of proof. Forensic probability must, in\nthe last analysis, rest on a robust common-sense\nand, ultimately, on the trained intuitions of the\njudge. While the protection given by the criminal\nprocess to the accused-persons is not to be\neroded,       at   the     same    time,   uninformed\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_579\">                                  507</span>\n\n\n\n\n       legitimisation   of   trivialities   would   make   a\n       mockery of administration of criminal justice.\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_852\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"21652\" id=\"span_580\">464.         The Apex Court in case of <a href=\"/doc/21652/\" id=\"a_439\">GURBACHAN SINGH\n\nVS. SATPAL SINGH AND OTHERS</a>, reported in (1990) 1\n\nSCC PAGE 445, held as under:-</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_853\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_344\">       &quot;4. ....... Criminal charges must be brought home\n       and proved beyond all reasonable doubt.
