While\n       civil case may be proved by mere preponderance\n       of evidence, in criminal cases the prosecution\n       must prove the charge beyond reasonable doubt.\n       (See Mancini v. Director of Public Prosecutions,\n       [1942] AC 1, Woolmington v. The Director of\n       Public Prosecutions, [1935] AC 462). It is true\n       even today, as much as it was before. There\n       must not be any &#x27;reasonable doubt&#x27; about the\n       guilt of the accused in respect of the particular\n       offence charged. The courts must strictly be\n       satisfied that no innocent person, innocent in the\n       sense of not being guilty of the offence of which\n       he is charged, is convicted, even at the risk of\n       letting of some guilty persons. Even after the\n       introduction of<a href=\"/doc/538436/\" id=\"a_440\"> s. 498A</a> of the I.P.C. and<a href=\"/doc/1953529/\" id=\"a_441\"> s. 113A</a>\n       of the Indian Evidence Act, the proof must be\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_581\">                                508</span>\n\n\n\n\nbeyond any shadow of reasonable doubt. There\nis a higher standard of proof in criminal cases\nthan in civil cases, but there is no absolute\nstandard        in    either   of    the    cases   (See     the\nobservations of Lord Denning in Bater v. Bater,\n[1950] 2 AER 458 at 459) but the doubt must be\nof a reasonable man. The standard adopted\nmust be the standard adopted by a prudent man\nwhich, of course, may vary from case to case,\ncircumstances to circumstances. Exaggerated\ndevotion to the rule of benefit of doubt must not\nnurture fancilful doubts or lingering suspicions\nand thereby destroy social defence. Justice\ncannot be made sterile on the plea that it is\nbetter to let hundred guilty escape than punish\nan innocent. Letting guilty escape is not doing\njustice, according to law.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_345\">5.    The conscience of the court can never be\nbound by any rule but that is coming itself\ndictates the consciousness and prudent exercise\nof the judgment. Reasonable doubt is simply that\ndegree     of        doubt     which       would    permit    a\nreasonable and just man to come to a conclusion.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_346\">Reasonableness            of    the        doubt    must     be\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_582\">                                509</span>\n\n\n\n\n       commensurate with the nature of the offence to\n       be investigated.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_854\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1283572\" id=\"span_583\">465.          The Apex Court in case of <a href=\"/doc/1283572/\" id=\"a_442\">SUCHA SINGH AND\n\nANOTHER VS. STATE OF PUNJAB</a>, (2003) 7 SCC PAGE\n\n643, has held as under:-</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_855\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_347\">       20.    Exaggerated devotion to the rule of benefit\n       of doubt must not nurture fanciful doubts or\n       lingering suspicion and thereby destroy social\n       defence. Justice cannot be made sterile on the\n       plea that it is better to let hundred guilty escape\n       than punish an innocent. Letting guilty escape is\n       not doing justice according to law. Prosecution is\n       not required to meet any and every hypothesis\n       put forward by the accused. A reasonable doubt\n       is not an imaginary, trivial or merely possible\n       doubt, but a fair doubt based upon reason and\n       common sense. It must grow out of the evidence\n       in the case. If a case is proved perfectly, it is\n       argued that it is artificial; if a case has some\n       inevitable flaws because human beings are prone\n       to err, it is argued that it is too imperfect. One\n       wonders      whether      in    the     meticulous\n       hypersensitivity to eliminate a rare innocent from\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_584\">                                   510</span>\n\n\n\n\n       being punished, many guilty persons must be\n       allowed to escape. Proof beyond reasonable\n       doubt is a guideline, not a fetish. Vague hunches\n       cannot take the place of judicial evaluation.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_348\">           <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"235169\" id=\"span_585\">&quot;A judge does not preside over a criminal\n           trial, merely to see that no innocent man is\n           punished. A judge also presides to see that\n           a guilty man does not escape. Both are\n           public    duties.&quot;   (Per   Viscount   Simon   in\n           Stirland v. Director of Public Prosecution\n           (1944 AC 315) quoted in <a href=\"/doc/235169/\" id=\"a_443\">State of U.P. v. Anil\n           Singh</a> (AIR 1988 SC 1998).</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_349\">           Doubts would be called reasonable if they\n           are free from a zest for abstract speculation.\n           Law cannot afford any favourite other than\n           truth.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_856\">466.          The Apex Court in case of ASHOK DEBBARMA\n\n@ ACHAK DEBBARMA VS STATE OF TRIPURA, reported in\n\n(2014) 4 SCC PAGE 747, has referred to the case of\n\nCOMMONWEALTH V. JOHN W. WEBSTER 5 CUSH. 295,\n\n320 (1850), MASSACHUSETTS COURT, AS EARLY AS IN\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_586\">                                    511</span>\n\n\n\n\n1850, wherein the expression the reasonable doubt is\n\nexplained as follows:\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_857\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_350\">              &quot;Reasonable doubt ... is not a mere\n       possible doubt; because everything relating to\n       human     affairs,    and    depending    on   moral\n       evidence, is open to some possible or imaginary\n       doubt. It is that state of the case which, after\n       the entire comparison and consideration of all\n       the evidence, leaves the minds of the jurors in\n       that condition that they cannot say they feel an\n       abiding conviction.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_351\">467.          The Apex Court with reference to the above\n\njudgment held that:\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_352\">       &quot;In our criminal justice system, for recording guilt\n       of the accused, it is not necessary that the\n       prosecution should prove the case with absolute\n       or mathematical certainty, but only beyond\n       reasonable    doubt.    Criminal     Courts,    while\n       examining    whether        any   doubt   is   beyond\n       reasonable doubt, may carry in their mind, some\n       &quot;residual doubt&quot;, even though the Courts are\n       convinced of the accused persons&#x27; guilt beyond\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_587\">                                 512</span>\n\n\n\n\n       reasonable doubt. For instance, in the instant\n       case, it was pointed out that, according to the\n       prosecution, 30-35 persons armed with weapons\n       such as fire arms, dao, lathi etc., set fire to the\n       houses of the villagers and opened fire which\n       resulted in the death of 15 persons, but only 11\n       persons were charge- sheeted and, out of which,\n       charges were framed only against 5 accused\n       persons. Even out of those 5 persons, 3 were\n       acquitted, leaving the appellant and another, who\n       is absconding. The court, in such circumstances,\n       could have entertained a &quot;residual doubt&quot; as to\n       whether the appellant alone had committed the\n       entire crime, which is a mitigating circumstance to\n       be taken note of by the court, at least when the\n       court is considering the question whether the case\n       falls under the rarest of the rare category.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_353\">468.          Further the Apex Court observing that Residual\n\ndoubt is a mitigating circumstance, sometimes, used and\n\nurged before the Jury in the United States and, generally,\n\nnot found favour by the various Courts in the United States,\n\nreferred to the judgment in the case of DONALD GENE\n\nFRANKLIN V. JAMES A. LYNAUGH, DIRECTOR, TEXAS\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_588\">                               513</span>\n\n\n\n\nDEPARTMENT OF CORRECTIONS (487 US 164 (1988);101\n\nL ED 2D 155), wherein while dealing with the death\n\nsentence, the Court held as follows:\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_354\">             &quot;The petitioner also contends that the\n       sentencing procedures followed in his case\n       prevented     the   jury    from   considering,   in\n       mitigation of sentence, any &quot;residual doubts&quot; it\n       might have had about his guilt. Petitioner uses\n       the phrase &quot;residual doubts&quot; to refer to doubts\n       that may have lingered in the minds of jurors\n       who were convinced of his guilt beyond a\n       reasonable doubt, but who were not absolutely\n       certain of his guilt. Brief for Petitioner 14. The\n       plurality and dissent reject the petitioner&#x27;s\n       &quot;residual doubt&quot; claim because they conclude\n       that the special verdict questions did not\n       prevent the jury from giving mitigating effect to\n       its &quot;residual doubt[s]&quot; about petitioner&#x27;s guilt.\n       This conclusion is open to question, however.\n       Although the jury was permitted to consider\n       evidence presented at the guilt phase in the\n       course   of   answering      the   special   verdict\n       questions, the jury was specifically instructed to\n       decide    whether     the    evidence    supported\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_589\">                                 514</span>\n\n\n\n\n       affirmative answers to the special questions\n       &quot;beyond a reasonable doubt.&quot; Because of this\n       instruction, the jury might not have thought that,\n       in sentencing the petitioner, it was free to\n       demand proof of his guilt beyond all doubt.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_355\">469.         The    Supreme     Court    also   referred   to   the\n\njudgment in the case of CALIFORNIA V. BROWN 479 U.S.\n\n541 and other cases, wherein the the US Courts took the\n\nview that,\n\n\n       &quot;Residual doubt&quot; is not a fact about the\n       defendant or the circumstances of the crime,\n       but a lingering uncertainty about facts, a state\n       of    mind    that   exists    somewhere   between\n       &quot;beyond a reasonable doubt&quot; and &quot;absolute\n       certainty.&quot; Petitioner&#x27;s &quot;residual doubt&quot; claim is\n       that the States must permit capital sentencing\n       bodies to demand proof of guilt to &quot;an absolute\n       certainty&quot; before imposing the death sentence.\n       Nothing in our cases mandates the imposition\n       of this heightened burden of proof at capital\n       sentencing.\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_590\">                                515</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_356\">Then the Supreme Court observed that:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_357\">       &quot;We also, in this country, as already indicated,\n       expect the prosecution to prove its case beyond\n       reasonable   doubt,     but   not   with   &quot;absolute\n       certainty&quot;. But, in between &quot;reasonable doubt&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_858\">       and &quot;absolute certainty&#x27;, a decision maker&#x27;s\n       mind may wander possibly, in a given case, he\n       may go for &quot;absolute certainty&quot; so as to award\n       death sentence, short of that he may go for\n       &quot;beyond reasonable doubt&quot;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_859\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_860\">470.        The     judicial    instrument        has   a     public\n\naccountability. The law would fail to protect the community\n\nif it permitted fanciful possibilities to deflect the course of\n\njustice. If the evidence is so strong against a man as to leave\n\nonly a remote possibility in his favour which can be\n\ndismissed with sentence &#x27;of course, it is possible but not in\n\nthe least probable,&#x27; the case is proved beyond reasonable\n\ndoubt. Under our existing jurisprudence in a criminal\n\nmatter, we have to proceed with presumption of innocence,\n\nbut at the same time, that presumption is to be judged on\n\nthe basis of conceptions of a reasonable prudent man.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_591\">                               516</span>\n\n\n\n\nSmelling doubts for the sake of giving benefit of doubt is not\n\nthe law of the land. If a case is proved perfectly, it is argued\n\nthat it is artificial; if a case has some flaws inevitable\n\nbecause human beings are prone to err, it is argued that it is\n\ntoo imperfect. One wonders whether in the meticulous\n\nhypersensitivity to eliminate a rare innocent from being\n\npunished, many guilty persons must be allowed to escape.\n\nProof beyond reasonable doubt is a guideline, not a fetish.\n\nThe evil of acquitting a guilty person light-heartedly goes\n\nmuch beyond the simple fact that just one guilty person has\n\ngone unpunished. If unmerited acquittals become general.\n\nthey tend to lead to a cynical disregard of the law, and this\n\nin turn leads to a public demand for harsher legal\n\npresumptions against indicted &#x27;persons&#x27; and more severe\n\npunishment of those who are found guilty. A miscarriage of\n\njustice may arise from the acquittal of the guilty no less than\n\nfrom the conviction of the innocent.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_861\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_862\">471.        Exaggerated devotion to the rule of benefit of\n\ndoubt must not nurture fanciful doubts or lingering\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_592\">                              517</span>\n\n\n\n\nsuspicion and thereby destroy social defence. Justice cannot\n\nbe made sterile on the plea that it is better to let hundred\n\nguilty escape than punish an innocent. Letting guilty escape\n\nis not doing justice according to law.      A judge does not\n\npreside over a criminal trial, merely to see that no innocent\n\nman is punished. A judge also presides to see that a guilty\n\nman does not escape. Both are public duties.        If accused\n\npersons are let off light heartedly picking holes in the\n\nprosecution case without any sense of responsibility then the\n\nvery existence of the Criminal justice delivery system could\n\nbe questioned, having regard to the expenses involved in\n\nmaintaining    the   administration.   It   also   amount   to\n\nabdicating a solemn duty.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_863\">\nCONCLUSION\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_864\">472.          We have set out above the evidence regarding\n\nthe role played by each accused in the commission of crime.\n\nThe evidence on record clearly establishes that each of the\n\naccused is a party to the conspiracy. Conspiracy is hatched\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_593\">                              518</span>\n\n\n\n\nin secrecy and thereore, direct evidence in proof of the same\n\nis seldom available. It is a matter of inference. The law does\n\nnot require that each and every person, who is a party to the\n\nconspiracy must do some overtact towards the fulfillment of\n\nthe object of conspiracy. A conspiracy is nothing but a\n\npartnership for criminal purposes. An overtact of one\n\naccused may be the act of all without any new agreement\n\nspecifically directed to that act. One who enters into a\n\nconspiratorial relationship is liable for every reasonably\n\nforeseeable crime committed by every other member of the\n\nconspiracy in furtherance of its objectives whether or not, he\n\nknew of the crimes committed or aided in their commission.\n\nWhoever is a party to the conspiracy is liable to be punished\n\nunder <a href=\"/doc/1897847/\" id=\"a_444\">Section 120-B</a> of IPC. An act done by one accused is\n\nadmissible against the co-conspirators in view of <a href=\"/doc/1946503/\" id=\"a_445\">Section 10</a>\n\nof the Evidence Act. It can, in some cases be inferred from\n\nthe acts and conduct of the parties. The conspiracy hatched\n\nby these accused is continuous and renewed as to all the\n\nmembers whenever any member of the conspiracy acts in\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_594\">                              519</span>\n\n\n\n\nfurtherance of the common design. The cumulative effects of\n\nthe proved circumstances should be taken into account in\n\ndetermining the guilt of the accused. Where trustworthy\n\nevidence establishing all links of circumstantial evidence is\n\navailable, the confession of a co-accused as to conspiracy\n\neven without corroborative evidence can be taken into\n\nconsideration by virtue of <a href=\"/doc/1974480/\" id=\"a_446\">Section 30</a> of the Evidence Act.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_865\">473.        The material on record clearly establishes that\n\nall these accused are members of Deendar Anjuman Ashram.\n\nThe common intention and object which brought them\n\ntogether could be easily gathered from the documentary\n\nevidence such as books, literature, pamphlets which are\n\nseized from them for which, they have offered no explanation\n\nat all.   The literature published by this Ashram and its\n\nrecovery from the possession of the accused in this case\n\nclearly establishes the &#x27;mensrea&#x27;, with which all these\n\nconspirators came together. The pamphlets and letters\n\nseized from the accused also proves the Mensrea. They are\n\nall bound by a common philosophy, i.e., Islam is the only\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_595\">                             520</span>\n\n\n\n\nreligion in the world; Mohammed Paigambar is the only\n\nJagadguru and Allah is the only God in this world. The aim\n\nof all these accused is to Islamise the whole humanity. They\n\nbelieve in the prophecy that India would become Islam State;\n\nthere would be war between India and Pakistan and\n\nPakistan will burn for 8 days and Northern India will suffer\n\n70-75% loss.   Then A.1 would come with 9 lakh followers\n\n(Pathans) to India and in order to enable him to conquer\n\nSouthern India, the accused should make hangama and\n\ncause dhamaka in the Southern States. They believe that in\n\norder to achieve this so called noble object, called Jihad,\n\nthey could indulge in nifaaq and sirria activities.    They\n\ncontinued to conspire within the State of Karnataka at\n\nseveral places and committed acts against the State by\n\nblasting bombs in Church with the intention of causing\n\ndisharmony between the various sections of public.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_866\">474.        All of the accused have grievance against\n\nChristians because the Christian Missionaries are trying to\n\nconvert poor Muslims into Christianity.    They wanted to\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_596\">                             521</span>\n\n\n\n\nblast the Churches in Karnataka, the place of worship of\n\nChristians. They wanted to make it appear that it is the\n\nHindu Organizations which are behind the said blasts, so\n\nthat Christians may attack Hindus. This would also give an\n\nimpression that Christians are not safe in India.       They\n\nwanted the Western world to take note of this situation, that\n\nIndia does not believe in Secularism and Christianity is in\n\ndanger in India.   Thus, they wanted to create disharmony\n\nand unrest in the Society and the country.     They wanted\n\ncommunal clashes to take place in India. Taking advantage\n\nof such a situation, A.1 would come to Southern India with a\n\nstrong contingent of nine lakh followers (Pathans) and\n\nestablish Muslim rule in Southern India. In order to achieve\n\nthis object, they stealthily declared war against India. They\n\nconspired to overawe the Government of India. They wanted\n\nto show Criminal force against the Government of India and\n\nthe State of Karnataka. They wanted to create apprehension\n\nor alarm of fears in the minority communities in India, thus\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_597\">                               522</span>\n\n\n\n\ndisturbing the normal tranquility of a civilized Society by use\n\nof force or atleast by show and the threat of it.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_867\">475.        The evidence on record also establishes the\n\nseries of meetings held at various places in Andhra Pradesh\n\nand Karnataka, where plans were hatched to give effect to\n\nthe objects of the said organization. The evidence of kith and\n\nkin of the accused discussed earlier, speaks volumes about\n\nthe participation of these accused in such meetings at\n\nBangalore. The admitted relationship between the accused\n\nclearly demonstrate that they were intimately related to each\n\nother and each one of them was frequenting the other. It is\n\nproved that except A.13 all the other accused were tried in\n\nS.C. No. 95/2001 and after trial, these 18 accused were\n\nconvicted and underwent sentence in the Hyderabad Court.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_868\">476.        The material seized from the possession of these\n\naccused show that they were in possession of the literature\n\nof the said Ashram, photographs of the original founder as\n\nwell as A.1. Some of the accused even possessed computers\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_598\">                             523</span>\n\n\n\n\nin which they had stored all these materials.       They were\n\ngenerating   pamphlets    which   were      meant   to   bring\n\ndisharmony between Hindus and Christians. The evidence\n\non record, particularly the M.Os. seized from the house of\n\nA.10, A.11, A.12 and A.27 prove that these accused created\n\nthe warning and letters of threat in the name of Hindus\n\naddressed to Christians and in the name of Christians\n\naddressed to Hindus and circulated the same.             These\n\nwarning letters and letters of threat were found in the\n\npossession of all the accused persons when their houses and\n\nwork places were searched and it was seized by the\n\nInvestigating Officers P.W.57 and P.W.64.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_869\">477.         The sole object behind this exercise of the\n\naccused was to create Nifaaq i.e., communal disturbance in\n\nthe entire country. Similarly, the accused also made use of\n\nthe books containing the literature defaming religious\n\nentities like Lingayaths, Brahmins, Jains and Buddhists and\n\nall other major communities of India.           They further\n\nattempted to propagate that all these non-muslim religious\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_599\">                               524</span>\n\n\n\n\nentities are the by-products of Islam and originated in Arab\n\ncountries. The books referred to earlier were written, printed\n\nand published by the Deendar Anjuman Organization,\n\ncreated hatred and ill-will between the followers of different\n\nreligions of India. In all these writings, an attempt is made\n\nto showcase that, these non-muslim religions prevailing in\n\nIndia are weak and without any principles; and adopt\n\npractices to fool the gullible public. In contrast to this, they\n\nattempted to project that Islam religion is a very powerful\n\ndominating religion in the whole world having very powerful\n\nbasis and principles. There is ample material on record to\n\nshow that the prosecution has proved the offence under\n\n<a href=\"/doc/345634/\" id=\"a_447\">Section 153-A</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1641007/\" id=\"a_448\">124-A</a> of IPC against A.7 to A.13, A.18 to\n\nA.22 and A.24 to A.27.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_870\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_871\">478.        The evidence on record also discloses that A.10,\n\nA.11 and A.12 knew how to operate computers, to send\n\nemails. They were in constant touch with A.1 and his sons,\n\nwho were residents of Mardan, Pakistan and exchanged\n\ninformation about civil and military installations in India.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_600\">                              525</span>\n\n\n\n\nThey collected all vital information concerning the security of\n\nthe nation and passed on the said information through e-\n\nmail to A.1 who was at Pakistan, besides storing the same in\n\nfloppies.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_872\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_873\">479.          The accused persons are not innocent persons.\n\nIt is not a case of these accused persons being falsely\n\nimplicated in this case. Many of them had gone to Mardan\n\nin Pakistan for receiving training in arms and ammunitions\n\nincluding explosive substances as is clear from the passport\n\nseized. They had prepared a written sketch/plan to execute\n\nthe bomb blasts in various Churches.          The same was\n\ncirculated and discussed in the conspiracy meetings held at\n\nvarious places.     They were also sharing the information\n\nreceived from A.1 in Pakistan. The presence of A.1 and his\n\nchildren at Deendar Anjuman Ashram during Urs is also\n\nproved.     The meetings with them is also established.    The\n\nentrustment of responsibility to each of them is also proved.\n\nThese accused were already convicted in the Hyderabad\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_601\">                                  526</span>\n\n\n\n\ncase. A.1 and his children who are accused in the case are\n\nabsconding.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_874\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_875\">480.          For the purpose of raising funds for their illegal\n\nactivities the accused resorted to sirria-dacoity, robbed a\n\nbank official, who was carrying cash and utilized the said\n\nfunds in procuring explosives, timers etc., and for the\n\nmanufacture of the bombs. Whenever Siria was conducted,\n\nexpenses towards the same was defrayed and the balance\n\nwas sent to A.1.      The accused had even bought a second\n\nhand Tata Sumo for their conveyance out of money so\n\nraised. They procured explosives, geletine sticks, timer and\n\nother parts required for preparing bombs and they prepared\n\nthem. Thereafter the bombs were transported to the place of\n\nthe blast. A.10 had opened nearly 11 Bank accounts. He\n\nhad a credit card in his name. He had furnished additional\n\ncredit cards to his wife, A.2, A.4 and the son of A.12. The\n\ntelephones,    both   landline    and   Mobile   were   used   to\n\ncommunicate with each other. When credit cards were used\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_602\">                                527</span>\n\n\n\n\nby A.1 in Pakistan, the amounts were debited to the account\n\nof A.10, which he has promptly paid.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_876\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_877\">481.        The mahazars showing the spot of the incident,\n\nthe place where the incriminating materials were kept and\n\nthe seizure of the material, on the information furnished by\n\nthe    accused   is   proved   beyond   reasonable   doubt   by\n\nexamining the Investigating Officer and Panch witnesses. In\n\nfact, no explanation is offered by the accused in their\n\nstatement under <a href=\"/doc/767287/\" id=\"a_449\">Section 313</a> Cr.P.C, except a blanket denial.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_878\">482.        The above clearly establishes the offences of\n\ncriminal conspiracy entered into with the intention of waging\n\nwar against the Government as well as conspiring to overawe\n\nthe Government by means of criminal force or the show of\n\ncriminal force under <a href=\"/doc/591631/\" id=\"a_450\">Sections 120A</a>, <a href=\"/doc/786750/\" id=\"a_451\">121</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1949191/\" id=\"a_452\">121A</a> of the\n\nIndian Penal Code.       Procurement of explosives, carrying\n\nexplosives in a van for keeping those explosives in Church\n\nwith the timer and exploding the same are all proved.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_603\">                               528</span>\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_879\">483.        The oral and documentary evidence on record\n\ncoupled with undisputed fact that these accused are\n\nconvicted by the Hyderabad Court for the offence of\n\nconspiracy is sufficient to hold that the prosecution has\n\nproved beyond reasonable doubt its case against the\n\naccused.     However,    in   a     criminal   case,   based   on\n\ncircumstantial evidence, in order to convict an accused,\n\nthere should be a chain connecting each event or incident.\n\nIn the nature of offence involved in this case, there can\n\nhardly be any direct evidence. Since conspiracies are\n\nhatched in secrecy, seldom direct evidence is available of\n\nsuch conspiracies.      In this case, in addition to the\n\ncircumstantial evidence, placed on record by the prosecution\n\nin proof of the conspiracy and the acts done in pursuance of\n\nthe conspiracy beyond reasonable doubt, we also have the\n\ndirect evidence by way of confessional statement of A.7, who\n\nis a party to all these conspiracies. Similarly, we have the\n\nevidence of P.W-54 who was present in some of the\n\nconspiracy meetings.    Though the evidence on record is\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_604\">                             529</span>\n\n\n\n\nsufficient to hold the accused guilty, we have called in aid\n\nthe confessional statement of A.7 to lend assurance to other\n\nevidence and to fortify the conclusion that the accused are\n\nguilty of the said offence. We have also drawn support from\n\nthe evidence of P.W-54 for the purpose of corroboration alone\n\nas the evidence to prove a conspiracy can only be\n\ncircumstantial and not direct.   Realizing the probative value\n\nthe Courts would attach to the confessional statement of A.7\n\nand the evidence of P.W.54, a futile attempt was made to\n\ndemonstrate that these are inadmissible in evidence and\n\ntherefore should not be looked into. But we have already set\n\nout in detail how the confessional statement and the\n\nevidence of P.W-54 is valid, legal and admissible, by\n\nexhaustively referring to the law on the point, as such the\n\nsaid contention cannot be sustained.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_880\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_881\">484.        The learned Sessions Judge, has set out in detail\n\nthe evidence adduced by the prosecution, to prove the\n\noffence under Rule 5 of the Explosives Rules, 1983 read with\n\n<a href=\"/doc/176528/\" id=\"a_453\">Section 9-B</a> of the Indian Explosives Act, 1884 and also\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_605\">                              530</span>\n\n\n\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1056565/\" id=\"a_454\">Sections 3</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1335368/\" id=\"a_455\">5</a> of Explosives Substance Act, 1908. In fact\n\nthe said findings are not challenged in the appeal as no\n\narguments were addressed by the learned counsel for the\n\naccused. Probably the reason being that accused has already\n\nundergone the sentence imposed for those offences.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_882\">485.        Therefore, we are satisfied that the prosecution\n\nhas proved its case beyond reasonable doubt, the guilt of the\n\naccused in respect of the offences alleged against them. The\n\nfinding of the Sessions Court holding the accused guilty of\n\nall the offences is based on legal evidence.     There is no\n\njustification whatsoever to interfere with the well considered\n\njudgment of the Sessions Court and therefore we find no\n\nmerit in this appeal.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_883\">\nRe: POINT NO.12\n\nSENTENCE\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_884\">486.        On the question of sentence, the learned Judge\n\nof the Sessions Court after taking into consideration the\n\njudgments of the Apex Court on the point and after\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_606\">                                 531</span>\n\n\n\n\nconsidering the aggravating circumstances as pointed out by\n\nthe      learned   Public    Prosecutor    and    the    mitigating\n\ncircumstances as pointed out by the learned Counsel for the\n\naccused, found no grounds for imposing lenient punishment\n\non these accused. Therefore except A.10, life imprisonment\n\nwas imposed for other accused.            In so far as, A.10 is\n\nconcerned, in the facts of the case, the learned Sessions\n\nJudge was of the view that a case of imposition for\n\nimposition for death sentence is made out, as it falls under\n\nthe category of rarest of rare cases. After imposing the death\n\nsentence under Section 366(1), the trial Judge submitted the\n\nproceedings to this Court for confirmation.         Therefore, the\n\nquestion that arises for our consideration is:\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_358\">   (a)      &quot;Whether the learned Judge of the Sessions\n            Court is justified in imposing death sentence\n            on A.10, treating it as a rarest of rare cases?\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_359\">   (b)      Whether imposition of life imprisonment on\n            other accused is proper?\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_607\">                                532</span>\n\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_885\">POINT (a)\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_886\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1201493\" id=\"span_608\">487.          The law on the point is fairly well settled. The\n\nleading judgment on this point is the Constitution Bench\n\nJudgment of the Apex Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/1201493/\" id=\"a_456\">BACHAN SINGH\n\nVs. STATE OF PUNJAB</a> reported in 1982(3) SCC 24.\n\nThough <a href=\"/doc/1201493/\" id=\"a_457\">the said judgment</a> was rendered in the context of\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1560742/\" id=\"a_458\">Section 302</a> of IPC, the guidelines <a href=\"/doc/1201493/\" id=\"a_459\">laid down in</a> that\n\njudgment aptly applies to the offece under <a href=\"/doc/786750/\" id=\"a_460\">Section 121</a> IPC\n\nalso.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_887\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_888\">488.          In MACHI SINGH AND OTHERS Vs. STATE OF\n\nPUNJAB reported in 1983(3) SCC 470, the Apex Court\n\nsummarized the principles emerging from BACHAN SINGH&#x27;s\n\ncase as under:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_360\">              &quot;38. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1201493\" id=\"span_609\">In this background the guidelines\n            indicated in <a href=\"/doc/1201493/\" id=\"a_461\">Bachan Singh</a> case, 1980 (2) SCC\n            684 will have to be culled out and applied to\n            the facts of each individual case where the\n            question of imposing of death sentence arises.</span>\n            <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1201493\" id=\"span_610\">The   following   propositions   emerge   from\n            <a href=\"/doc/1201493/\" id=\"a_462\">Bachan Singh</a> case(supra):</span>\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_611\">                   533</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_361\">      &quot;(i)    The extreme penalty of death\nneed not be inflicted except in gravest cases\nof extreme culpability.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_362\">      (ii)    Before opting for the death\npenalty the circumstances of the &quot;offender&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_889\">also require to be taken into consideration\nalong with the circumstances of the &quot;crime&quot;.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_890\">      .\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_891\">      (iii)    Life imprisonment is the rule\nand death sentence is an exception. In other\nwords death sentence must be imposed only\nwhen life imprisonment appears to be an\naltogether inadequate punishment having\nregard to the relevant circumstances of the\ncrime, and provided, and only provided, the\noption to impose sentence of imprisonment for\nlife cannot be conscientiously exercised\nhaving     regard   to   the   nature   and\ncircumstances of the crime and all the\nrelevant circumstances.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_892\">    (iv)     A balance sheet of aggravating\nand mitigating circumstances has to be\ndrawn up and in doing so the mitigating\ncircumstances have to be accorded full\nweightage and a just balance has to be\nstruck between the aggravating and the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_612\">                              534</span>\n\n\n\n\n          mitigating circumstances before the option is\n          exercised.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_893\">                39.      In order to apply these\n          guidelines inter alia the following questions\n          may be asked and answered:\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_894\">                (a)     Is there something uncommon\n          about the crime which renders sentence of\n          imprisonment for life inadequate and calls for\n          a death sentence?\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_895\">                (b)     Are the circumstances of the\n          crime such that there is no alternative but to\n          impose death sentence even after according\n          maximum weightage to the mitigating\n          circumstances which speak in favour of the\n          offender?\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_896\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_897\">489.         <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"717201\" id=\"span_613\">In <a href=\"/doc/717201/\" id=\"a_463\">RONNY ALIAS RONALD JAMES ALWARIS\n\nAND OTHERS VS. STATE OF MAHARASHTRA</a>, 1998 (3)\n\nSCC 625, the Apex Court held as under:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_363\">             &quot;45.   These principles have been applied\n       in various judgments of this Court thereafter and\n       it is unnecessary to multiply the cases here.\n       Whether the case is one of the rarest of the rare\n       cases is a question which has to be determined\n       on the facts of each case. Suffice it to mention\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_614\">                                       535</span>\n\n\n\n\n       that the choice of the death sentence has to be\n       made only in the rarest of the rare cases and that\n       where culpability of the accused has assumed\n       depravity or where the accused is found to be an\n       ardent criminal and menace to the society and;\n       where the crime is committed in an organised\n       manner and is gruesome, cold-blooded, heinous\n       and atrocious; where innocent and unarmed\n       persons are attacked and murdered without any\n       provocation, the case would present special\n       reason for purposes of sub-section (3) of <a href=\"/doc/1266667/\" id=\"a_464\">Section\n       354</a> of the Criminal Procedure Code.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_898\">490.         <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1460234\" id=\"span_615\">In <a href=\"/doc/1460234/\" id=\"a_465\">STATE OF MAHARASHTRA VS. GORAKSHA\n\nAMBAJI ADSU</a> reported in 2011 (7) SCC 437, the Apex\n\nCourt made the following observation:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_364\">             <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"84225270\" id=\"span_616\">&quot;30.        The        principles       governing      the\n       sentencing policy in our criminal jurisprudence\n       have more or less been consistent, right from the\n       pronouncement           of     the     Constitution        Bench\n       judgment of this Court in <a href=\"/doc/84225270/\" id=\"a_466\">Bachan Singh v. State of\n       Punjab</a>,(2010) 8 SCC 775. Awarding punishment\n       is   certainly     an        onerous        function   in    the\n       dispensation of criminal justice. The court is\n       expected     to    keep       in     mind    the   facts    and\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_617\">                            536</span>\n\n\n\n\ncircumstances of a case, the principles of law\ngoverning award of sentence, the legislative\nintent of special or general statute raised in\n[pic]the   case    and    the     impact    of   awarding\npunishment. These are the nuances which need\nto be examined by the court with discernment\nand in depth.</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_365\">       31. The legislative intent behind enacting\n<a href=\"/doc/324405/\" id=\"a_467\">Section 354(3)</a> CrPC clearly demonstrates the\nconcern of the legislature for taking away a\nhuman life and imposing death penalty upon the\naccused. Concern for the dignity of the human life\npostulates resistance to taking a life through\nlaw\u00e2\u20ac\u2122s instrumentalities and that ought not to\nbe done, save in the rarest of rare cases, unless\nthe    alternative       option    is      unquestionably\nforeclosed. In exercise of its discretion, the court\nwould also take into consideration the mitigating\ncircumstances and their resultant effects.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_366\">       <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1201493\" id=\"span_618\">32.   The     language       of   Section   354(3)\ndemonstrates the legislative concern and the\nconditions which need to be satisfied prior to\nimposition of death penalty. The words, &quot;in the\ncase of sentence of death, the special reasons for\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_619\">                        537</span>\n\n\n\n\nsuch sentence&quot; unambiguously demonstrate the\ncommand of the legislature that such reasons\nhave to be recorded for imposing the punishment\nof death sentence. This is how the concept of the\nrarest of rare cases has emerged in law. Viewed\nfrom that angle, both the legislative provisions\nand judicial pronouncements are at ad idem in\nlaw. The death penalty should be imposed in the\nrarest of rare cases and that too for special\nreasons to be recorded. To put it simply, a death\nsentence is not a rule but an exception. Even the\nexception   must    satisfy   the    prerequisites\ncontemplated under <a href=\"/doc/324405/\" id=\"a_468\">Section 354(3)</a> CrPC in light\nof the dictum of the Court in <a href=\"/doc/1201493/\" id=\"a_469\">Bachan Singh</a>(supra).</span>\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_367\">      <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1201493\" id=\"span_620\">34.   The judgment in <a href=\"/doc/1201493/\" id=\"a_470\">Bachan Sing</a>(supra),\ndid not only state the above guidelines in some\nelaboration, but also specified the mitigating\ncircumstances which could be considered by the\nCourt while determining such serious issues and\nthey are as follows: (SCC p. 750, para 206)\n\n      &quot;206. ...      &quot;Mitigating circumstances. - In\nthe exercise of its discretion in the above cases,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_621\">                         538</span>\n\n\n\n\nthe court shall take into account the following\ncircumstances:</span>\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_368\">(1)   That the offence was committed under the\ninfluence   of   extreme   mental      or    emotional\ndisturbance.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_369\">(2)   The age of the accused. If the accused is\nyoung or old, he shall not be sentenced to death.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_370\">(3)   The probability that the accused would not\ncommit criminal     acts of     violence    as   would\nconstitute a continuing threat to society.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_371\">(4)   The probability that the accused can be\nreformed and rehabilitated. The State shall by\nevidence prove that the accused does not satisfy\nConditions (3) and (4) above.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_372\">(5)   That in the facts and circumstances of the\ncase the accused believed that he was morally\njustified in committing the offence.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_373\">(6)   That the accused acted under the duress or\ndomination of another person.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_374\">(7)   That the condition of the accused showed\nthat he was mentally defective and that the said\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_622\">                                539</span>\n\n\n\n\ndefect impaired his capacity to appreciate the\ncriminality of his conduct.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_899\">      35.       Now,     we     may     examine   certain\nillustrations        arising     from     the     judicial\npronouncements of this Court.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_900\">      36.       <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"978866\" id=\"span_623\">In <a href=\"/doc/978866/\" id=\"a_471\">D.K. Basu v. State of W.B.</a>,(2002)\n1 SCC 351, this Court took the view that custodial\ntorture and consequential death in custody was\nan offence which fell in the category of the rarest\nof rare cases. While specifying the reasons in\nsupport of such decision, the Court awarded\ndeath penalty <a href=\"/doc/978866/\" id=\"a_472\">in that case</a>.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_901\">      37.       In Santosh Kumar Satishbhushan\nBariyar v. State of Maharashtra, (1972) 2 SCC\n640, this Court also spelt out in paras 56 to 58\nthat nature, motive, impact of a crime, culpability,\nquality         of       evidence,        socio-economic\ncircumstances, impossibility of rehabilitation are\nthe factors which the court may take into\nconsideration while dealing with such cases. In\nthat case the friends of the victim had called him\nto see a movie and after seeing the movie, a\nransom call was made, but with the fear of being\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_624\">                                 540</span>\n\n\n\n\ncaught, they murdered the victim. The Court felt\nthat there was no evidence to show that the\ncriminals        were     ...     incapable       of    reforming\nthemselves, that it was not a rarest of the rare\ncase, and therefore, declined to award death\nsentence to the accused.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_902\">         38.     Interpersonal                  circumstances\nprevailing between the deceased and the accused\nwas also held to be a relevant consideration\nin Vashram        Narshibhai        Rajpara      v.    State    of\nGujarat, (1996) 8 SCC 167, where constant\nnagging by family was treated as the mitigating\nfactor, if the accused is mentally unbalanced and\nas   a    result      murders       the     family    members.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_903\">Similarly,      the     intensity     of    bitterness    which\nprevailed       and     the     escalation      of    simmering\nthoughts into a thirst for revenge and retaliation\nwere also considered to be a relevant factor by\nthis Court in different cases.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_904\">         39.     This Court in Satishbhushan Bariya\n(supra)        also   considered           various    doctrines,\nprinciples and factors which would be considered\nby the Courts while dealing with such cases. The\nCourt     discussed        in     some       elaboration       the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_625\">                        541</span>\n\n\n\n\napplicability of the doctrine of rehabilitation and\nthe doctrine of prudence. While considering the\napplication of the doctrine of rehabilitation and\nthe extent of weightage to be given to the\nmitigating circumstances, it noticed the nature of\nthe evidence and the background of the accused.\nThe conviction in that case was entirely based\nupon the statement of the approver and was a\ncase purely of circumstantial evidence. Thus,\napplying the doctrine of prudence, it noticed the\nfact that the accused were unemployed, young\nmen in search of job and they were not criminals.\nIn execution of a plan proposed by the appellant\nand accepted by others, they kidnapped a friend\nof theirs. The kidnapping was done with the\nmotive of procuring ransom from his family but\nlater they murdered him because of the fear of\ngetting caught, and later cut the body into pieces\nand disposed it off at different places. One of the\naccused had turned approver and as already\nnoticed, the conviction was primarily based upon\nthe statement of the approver.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_905\">      41. The above principle, as supported by\ncase illustrations, clearly depicts the various\nprecepts which would govern the exercise of\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_626\">                               542</span>\n\n\n\n\n       judicial discretion by the courts within the\n       parameters spelt out under <a href=\"/doc/324405/\" id=\"a_473\">Section 354(3)</a> CrPC.\n       Awarding of death sentence amounts to taking\n       away the life of an individual, which is the most\n       valuable right available, whether viewed from the\n       constitutional point of view or from the human\n       rights point of view. The condition of providing\n       special reasons for awarding death penalty is not\n       to be construed linguistically but it is to satisfy\n       the basic features of a reasoning supporting and\n       making award of death penalty unquestionable.\n       The circumstances and the manner of committing\n       the crime should be such that it pricks the judicial\n       conscience of the court to the extent that the only\n       and inevitable conclusion should be awarding of\n       death penalty.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_906\">\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_907\">491.         The Supreme Court in the case of RAMNARESH\n\nAND OTHERS VS. STATE OF CHATTISGARH reported in\n\n2012 (4) SCC 257, noticed the aggravating and mitigating\n\ncircumstances with respect to a crime and held as follows:\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_375\">             &quot;76. The law enunciated by this Court in its\n       recent judgments, as already noticed, adds and\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_627\">                               543</span>\n\n\n\n\nelaborates the principles that were stated in\nBachan Singh,(1980) 2 SCC 684, and thereafter,\nin Machhi Singh,(1983) 3 SCC 470. The aforesaid\njudgments, primarily dissect these principles into\ntwo   different     compartments-one                being        the\n&quot;aggravating      circumstances&quot;          while      the      other\nbeing the &quot;mitigating circumstances&quot;. The court\nwould consider the cumulative effect of both these\naspects and normally, it may not be very\nappropriate for the court to decide the most\nsignificant   aspect     of    sentencing         policy        with\nreference to one of the classes under any of the\nfollowing heads while completely ignoring other\nclasses under other heads. To balance the two is\nthe primary       duty   of     the court.          It   will    be\nappropriate for the court to come to a final\nconclusion upon balancing the exercise that\nwould help to administer the criminal justice\nsystem better and provide an effective and\nmeaningful     reasoning            by     the       court       as\ncontemplated under <a href=\"/doc/324405/\" id=\"a_474\">Section 354(3)</a> CrPC.</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_376\">Aggravating circumstances\n\n      (1)     The      offences          relating        to      the\ncommission of heinous crimes like murder, rape,\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_628\">                         544</span>\n\n\n\n\narmed dacoity, kidnapping, etc. by the accused\nwith a prior record of conviction for capital felony\nor offences committed by the person having a\nsubstantial history of       serious   assaults    and\ncriminal convictions.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_377\">      (2) The offence was committed while the\noffender was engaged in the commission of\nanother serious offence.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_378\">      (3) The offence was committed with the\nintention to create a fear psychosis in the public\nat large and was committed in a public place by a\nweapon    or   device   which    clearly   could    be\nhazardous to the life of more than one person.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_379\">      (4) The offence of murder was committed\nfor ransom or like offences to receive money or\nmonetary benefits.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_380\">      (5) Hired killings.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_381\">      (6) The offence was committed outrageously\nfor want only while involving inhumane treatment\nand torture to the victim.\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_629\">                             545</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_382\">        (7) The offence was committed by a person\nwhile in lawful custody.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_383\">        (8)   The    murder     or    the   offence    was\ncommitted to prevent a person lawfully carrying\nout his duty like arrest or custody in a place of\nlawful confinement of himself or another. For\ninstance, murder is of a person who had acted in\nlawful discharge of his duty under <a href=\"/doc/73521/\" id=\"a_475\">Section 43</a>\nCrPC.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_384\">        (9)   When    the     crime    is   enormous    in\nproportion like making an attempt of murder of\nthe entire family or members of a particular\ncommunity.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_385\">        (10) When the victim is innocent, helpless or\na person relies upon the trust of relationship and\nsocial norms, like a child, helpless woman, a\ndaughter or a niece staying with a father/uncle\nand is inflicted with the crime by such a trusted\nperson.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_386\">        (11) When murder is committed for a motive\nwhich evidences total depravity and meanness.\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_630\">                            546</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_387\">          (12) When there is a cold-blooded murder\nwithout provocation.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_388\">          (13) The crime is committed so brutally that\nit pricks or shocks not only the judicial conscience\nbut even the conscience of the society.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_908\">Mitigating circumstances\n\n          (1) The manner and circumstances in and\nunder which the offence was committed, for\nexample,        extreme     mental     or    emotional\ndisturbance        or     extreme     provocation     in\ncontradistinction to all these situations in normal\ncourse.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_909\">          (2) The age of the accused is a relevant\nconsideration but not a determinative factor by\nitself.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_910\">          (3) The chances of the accused of not\nindulging in commission of the crime again and\nthe probability of the accused being reformed and\nrehabilitated.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_911\">          (4) The condition of the accused shows that\nhe   was      mentally    defective   and   the   defect\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_631\">                           547</span>\n\n\n\n\nimpaired       his   capacity    to     appreciate   the\ncircumstances of his criminal conduct.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_912\">         (5) The circumstances which, in normal\ncourse of life, would render such a behaviour\npossible and could have the effect of giving rise to\nmental imbalance in that given situation like\npersistent harassment or, in fact, leading to such\na peak of human behaviour that, in the facts and\ncircumstances of the case, the accused believed\nthat he was morally justified in committing the\noffence.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_913\">         (6)   Where    the     court    upon    proper\nappreciation of evidence is of the view that the\ncrime was not committed in a preordained\nmanner and that the death resulted in the course\nof commission of another crime and that there\nwas a possibility of it being construed as\nconsequences to the commission of the primary\ncrime.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_914\">         (7) Where it is absolutely unsafe to rely\nupon the testimony of a sole eyewitness though\nthe prosecution has brought home the guilt of the\naccused.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_632\">                                  548</span><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_915\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_916\">492.         From the aforesaid judgments, it is clear that\n\nthe extreme penalty of death sentence need not be imposed\n\nexcept in rarest case of extreme culpability.                 The life\n\nimprisonment is a rule and death sentence is an exception.\n\nA death sentence must be imposed only when the life\n\nimprisonment      appears   to     be   of   altogether    inadequate\n\npunishment. A balance sheet of aggravating and mitigating\n\ncircumstances has to be drawn up and in doing so the\n\nmitigating circumstances have to be accorded full weightage.\n\nThe Court has to find out whether there is anything\n\nuncommon about the crime which renders the sentence of\n\nimprisonment for life inadequate and calls for a death\n\nsentence.    Where the crime is committed in an organised\n\nmanner      and   is   gruesome,    cold-blooded,      heinous      and\n\natrocious,   where     innocent    and       unarmed      persons   are\n\nattacked and murdered without any provocation, the case\n\nwould present special reason for purposes of sub-section (3)\n\nof <a href=\"/doc/1266667/\" id=\"a_476\">Section 354</a> of the Criminal Procedure Code.                A death\n\npenalty should be imposed in rarest of rare cases and that\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_633\">                                 549</span>\n\n\n\n\ntoo for special reasons to be recorded. A death sentence is\n\nnot a rule but an exception.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_917\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_918\">493.           In the background, the mitigating circumstances\n\nthat emerge in this case are as under:\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_919\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_389\">       (1)     In the explosion which took place at St.\n\n       Peter and Paul Church, situated in JJ Nagar\n\n       main road, Bangalore, though the bomb blasted,\n\n       it resulted in only damage to the building to a\n\n       small extent.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_390\">       (2)     In the light of the said blast, when\n\n       trouble broke out near the Church, it resulted in\n\n       clashes between Muslims and Christians, who\n\n       also indulged in pelting stones at each others\n\n       houses and damaging autorickshaws, again, no\n\n       life was lost.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_391\">       (3)     Though,   they   indulged   in   printing,\n\n       publishing and circulating the pamphlets and\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_634\">                          550</span>\n\n\n\n\nliterature in the name of Christians attacking\n\nHindus and in the name of Hindus attacking\n\nChristians, no clashes took place between these\n\ntwo communities on account of such hand bills\n\nand no loss of life was reported.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_392\">(4)    The van driven by A.10 was carrying the\n\nbombs, A.14 and A.15 and its blast near\n\nMinerva Mills resulted in death of A.14 and A.15\n\nand causing injuries to A.10 himself.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_393\">(5)    He    is   the   resident    of   Bangalore,\n\nemployed in a private firm and earning about 2\n\n\u00bd lakh per annum. Though he made available\n\nfunds to other accused including A.4, nothing\n\nsubstantial turns out from the said payments.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_394\">(6)    From the date of arrest, A.10 is in\n\njuidical custody in connection with one or the\n\nother case, initiated against him in Andhra\n\nPradesh, Karnataka and Goa.\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_635\">                           551</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_395\">(7)       The literature which was seized from the\n\ncustody of A.10 shows that in so far as religious\n\nmatters are concerned, they were not very\n\nintelligent and easily misguided by A.1, which\n\nresulted     in   their   involvement   of   these\n\nconspiracies and the acts pursuant to such\n\nconspiracies.\n</blockquote>\n\n<pre id=\"pre_6\">(8)       No life is lost on account of the said\n\nbomb blast;\n\n\n(9)       The intention was not to kill any person\n\n</pre><blockquote id=\"blockquote_396\">at all. The intention was to bring hatred among\n\ndifferent communities, which may give rise to\n\nclashes between them. There was no intention\n\nto kill innocent and unarmed persons and\n\ncertainly there was no intention to murder any\n\nperson.\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_636\">                                      552</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_397\">       (10)      A.10 was unable to comprehend that\n\n       these acts of blasting the bombs and destroying\n\n       the Church buildings would neither enable him\n\n       to destroy India nor other religions in the world.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_398\">       It     only     shows   his    lack   of    knowledge,\n\n       understanding of the society, the country and\n\n       the affairs world over.\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_920\">494.             The    Sessions     Court   has    not   taken   into\n\nconsideration the aforesaid mitigating circumstances, while\n\nimposing death penalty. However the offence proved against\n\nhim is waging war against the Government of India or\n\nattempt to wage such war which is punishable with death\n\nand it is a very grave offence against the society and the\n\ncountry.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_921\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_922\">495.             We have seen from the evidence on record that\n\nA.10 was day dreaming about Islamizing this country. With\n\nthe resources at his command and the other conspirators,\n\nthey could not have achieved the said object.               In those\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_637\">                                553</span>\n\n\n\n\ncircumstances and in view of the mitigating circumstances,\n\nimposition of death sentence would not be proper. Ends of\n\nJustice would be met if the punishment of imprisonment for\n\nlife, instead of the capital punishment of death, is imposed.\n\nTherefore, in so far as A.10 is concerned, the sentence is\n\nreduced to imprisonment for life, by confirming the judgment\n\nof conviction passed by the Sessions Judge.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_923\">496.          But a few words of explanation are necessary to\n\nbe     mentioned   here   as   regards    the   meaning   of   life\n\nimprisonment.       The Supreme Court in the case of MD.\n\nMUNNA Vs. UNION OF INDIA AND OTEHRS reported in AIR\n\n2005 SC 3440, has clearly observed as below:\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_399\">       13.    <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1596139\" id=\"span_638\">In <a href=\"/doc/1596139/\" id=\"a_477\">K.M. Nanavati vs. State of Maharashtra</a>\n       AIR 1962 SC 605 the High Court of Bombay had\n       sentenced   the    offender   to   undergo   rigorous\n       imprisonment for life. The appeal was dismissed by\n       this Court with the observation that the High Court\n       rightly passed the sentence of imprisonment for life.</span>\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_924\">       Therefore, we are of the view that &quot;imprisonment for\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_639\">                          554</span>\n\n\n\n\nlife&quot; is to be treated as &quot;rigorous imprisonment for\nlife&quot;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_925\"> 14.     <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"108787168\" id=\"span_640\">In <a href=\"/doc/108787168/\" id=\"a_478\">Naib Singh</a>&#x27;s case (supra), it was further\n held as under :</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_400\"> &quot;17. In the first place, a distinction between\n &#x27;imprisonment for life&#x27; and &quot;imprisonment for a\n term&quot; has been maintained in the Penal Code in\n several of its provisions Secondly, by its very\n terms <a href=\"/doc/1681167/\" id=\"a_479\">Section 60</a> is applicable to a case where &quot;an\n offender is punishable with imprisonment which\n may be of either description&quot; and it is only in such\n case that it is competent for the court to direct that\n &quot;such imprisonment shall be either wholly rigorous\n or wholly simple or that any part of such\n imprisonment shall be rigorous and the rest\n simple.&quot; And it is clear that whenever an offender\n is punishable with &quot;imprisonment for life&quot; he is not\n punishable with &quot;imprisonment which may be of\n either description&quot;, in other words <a href=\"/doc/1681167/\" id=\"a_480\">Section 60</a>\n would be in applicable.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_401\"> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"10271\" id=\"span_641\">18. However, for the reasons discussed above and\n in view of the authoritative pronouncements made\n by the Privy Council and this Court in Kishori Lal\n case and <a href=\"/doc/10271/\" id=\"a_481\">Gopal Godse</a> case respectively, it will\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_642\">                         555</span>\n\n\n\n\nhave to be held that the position in law as regards\nnature of punishment involved in a sentence of\nimprisonment for life is well settled and the\nsentence of imprisonment for life has to be\nequated to rigorous imprisonment for life.........&quot;</span>\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_926\">18.    In Godse&#x27;s case, the Constitution Bench of\nthis Court held that the sentence of imprisonment\nfor life is not for any definite period and the\nimprisonment for life must, prima facie, be treated\nas imprisonment for the whole of the remaining\nperiod of the convict person&#x27;s natural life. It was\nalso held in paragraph 5 as follows :\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_402\">&quot;......It does not say that transportation for life\nshall be deemed to be transportation for twenty\nyears for all purposes; nor does the amended\nsection     which     substitutes       the   words\n&quot;imprisonment for life&quot; for &quot;transportation for life&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_927\">enable the drawing of any such all-embracing\nfiction. A sentence of transportation for life or\nimprisonment for life must prime facie be treated\nas transportation or imprisonment for the whole of\nthe remaining period of the convicted person&#x27;s\nnatural life.&quot;\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_643\">                         556</span><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_928\">\n\n\n\n\nSummarising the decision, it was held in para 8\nas under:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_403\">&quot;Briefly stated the legal position is this : <a href=\"/doc/1953529/\" id=\"a_482\">Before Act</a>\nXXVI of 1955 a sentence of transportation for life\ncould be undergone by a prisoner by way of\nrigorous imprisonment for life in a designated\nprison in India. After the said Act, such a convict\nshall be dealt with in the same manner as one\nsentenced to rigorous imprisonment for the same\nterm. Unless the said sentence is commuted or\nremitted    by   appropriate   authority   under   the\nrelevant provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/1569253/\" id=\"a_483\">Indian Penal Code</a> or the\n<a href=\"/doc/445276/\" id=\"a_484\">Code of Criminal Procedure</a>, a prisoner sentenced\nto life imprisonment is bound in law to serve the\nlife term in prison. The rules framed under the\n<a href=\"/doc/626516/\" id=\"a_485\">Prisons Act</a> enable such a prisoner to earn\nremissions ordinary, special and State        and the\nsaid remissions will be given credit towards his\nterm of imprisonment. For the purpose of working\nout the remissions the sentence of transportation\nfor life is ordinarily equated with a definite period,\nbut it is only for that particular purpose and not\nfor any other purpose. As the sentence of\ntransportation for life or its prison equivalent, the\nlife imprisonment, is one of indefinite duration, the\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_644\">                                557</span>\n\n\n\n\n       remissions so earned do not in practice help such\n       a convict as it is not possible to predicate the time\n       of his death. That is why the rules provide for a\n       procedure to enable the appropriate Government to\n       remit the sentence under<a href=\"/doc/1571667/\" id=\"a_486\"> S. 401</a> of the Code of\n       Criminal Procedure on a consideration of the\n       relevant factors, including the period of remissions\n       earned. The question of remission is exclusively\n       within the province of the appropriate Government;\n       and in this case it is admitted that, though the\n       appropriate Government made certain remissions\n       under<a href=\"/doc/1571667/\" id=\"a_487\"> S. 401</a> of the Code of Criminal Procedure, it\n       did not remit the entire sentence. We, therefore,\n       hold that the petitioner has not yet acquired any\n       right to release.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_929\">       19.   We are bound by the above dicta laid down\n       by the Constitution Bench and we hold that life\n       imprisonment is not equivalent to imprisonment for\n       fourteen years or for twenty years as contended\n       by the petitioner.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_930\">\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_931\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"604676\" id=\"span_645\">497.         The Supreme Court in the case of <a href=\"/doc/604676/\" id=\"a_488\">SWAMY\n\nSHRADDANANDA ALIAS MURALI MANOHAR MISHRA Vs.\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_646\">                                  558</span>\n\n\n\n\nSTATE OF KARNATAKA</a> reported in AIR 2007 SC 2531\n\nhas held as under:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_404\">       &quot;100.          However, while saying so, we direct\n       that in a case of this nature life sentence must be\n       meant to be life sentence. Such a direction can\n       be    given,    as   would      appear    from   some\n       precedents.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_405\">       Therefore in this case too life imprisonment imposed\n\non A.10 is for the rest of his life and it shall be rigorous.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_406\">POINT (b)\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_932\">498.         In so far as convicting the other accused to\n\nundergo life imprisonment is concerned, the offences which\n\nthey have committed are also grave in nature and it would\n\nthreaten the unity and intergrity of this country apart from\n\ndestroying     the    communal    harmony       prevailing   in   the\n\ncountry/society. It is a crime against society, nation and the\n\nprinciple of co-existence for which this country is known for,\n\nsince centuries;       This noble principle is sought to be\n\ndestroyed by these evil minded acts.            In this regard it is\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_647\">                             559</span>\n\n\n\n\nnecessary to notice that all the accused are the members of\n\nDeendar Anjuman Organization. The facts set out above as\n\nreflected in the various books demonstrate that all of them\n\nare interested in Islamizing India, propogating Islam and\n\nwarned the world that they have to accept that Mohammed\n\nPaigambar is the only Guru. They believe in the prophecy\n\nthat India would become a Islam State, there would be war\n\nbetween India and Pakistan and Pakistan will burn for 8\n\ndays and Northern India will suffer 70-75% loss. A.1 would\n\ncome with 9 lakh followers (Pathans) to India which enables\n\nhim to conquer Southern India. So that, by that time, if they\n\ncreate hangama and dhamaka in Southern India, it would\n\nenable him to capture the Southern India and rule this\n\nCountry. It only shows how foolish they are. However, all of\n\nthem have whole-heartedly contributed their might and\n\nmind, indulged in sirria to raise funds for the aforesaid\n\nobject.   Some of them went to Mardan in Pakistan for\n\nreceiving training in preparation and operation of arms and\n\nammunitions including explosive materials, preparation and\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_648\">                                       560</span>\n\n\n\n\nblasting of bombs. Actually in the mango grove situated at\n\nNuzvid, they participated in the preparation of bombs, its\n\ndetonation, they prepared plans, carried explosives, kept\n\nthem in the Church with the timer, which resulted in a blast.\n\nThey wanted to see how Hindus and Christians fight among\n\nthemselves, so that peace of the society is destroyed. With\n\nthis object, they printed pamphlets, fed them in the\n\ncomputers, generated them and circulated them, with the\n\nfond hope that Christians and Hindus would lock horns. In\n\na country which believes in peaceful co-existence of all\n\nreligions and which believes in unity in diversity; and a\n\ncountry where all religious faiths have prospered, the action\n\nof these accused is unpardonable.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_933\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_934\">499.           In fact, Rashtrakavi Kuvempu in the State\n\nAnthem of Karnataka has vividly put how these religions\n\nexist in the State. It reads thus:\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_935\">\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_407\">       \u00b8\u00c0\u00aa\u00c0\u00f0 d\u00a3\u00c1AU\u00c0z\u00c0 \u00b1\u00c1AwAi\u00c0\u00c4 v\u00c9\u00c6\u00c3l, g\u00c0\u00b9P\u00c0g\u00c0 P\u00c0AU\u00c0\u00bc\u00c0 \u00b8\u00c9\u00bc\u00c9Ai\u00c0\u00c4\u00c4\u00aa\u00c0 \u00a3\u00c9\u00c6\u00c3l\n       &quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_408\">   \u00bbAz\u00c0\u00c6 P\u00c9\u00e6\u00ca\u00b8\u00c0\u00db \u00aa\u00c0\u00c4\u00c4\u00b8\u00c0\u00af\u00c1\u00e4\u00a3\u00c0, \u00a5\u00c1g\u00c0\u00b9P\u00c0 e\u00c9\u00ca\u00a3\u00c0g\u00c0\u00c4z\u00c1\u00e5\u00a3\u00c0&quot;\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_649\">                              561</span>\n\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_936\">(This garden of calamity belongs to all sects, its scenery\n\nattracts the admirers, this is garden of Hindus, Christians,\n\nMulsims, Parsis and Jains)\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_937\">500.        The State is known for communal harmony,\n\npeaceful co-existence and all faiths have taken deep roots in\n\nthe State. Any attempt to destroy the serene atomosphere\n\ncannot be countenanced.       The action of the accused in\n\nattempting to destroy the social fabric is a heinous crime,\n\nwhich cannot be pardoned. In fact, the wife of A.14, when\n\nshe came to know that her husband died in a blast, which\n\ntook place in a car, she took his death in a stride and did not\n\nthink of committing suicide. But after a month or so when\n\nevents unfolded and it became clear the blast was on\n\naccount of anti-national activity by the members of Deendar\n\nAnjuman Ashram, she could not digest that her husband\n\nwas an anti-national.   That young lady was shell shocked\n\nand she poured kerosene on herself and committed suicide.\n\nHer marriage had been performed in Deendar Anjuman\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_650\">                              562</span>\n\n\n\n\nAshram at Asifnagar, Hyderabad, during Urs in 1999, at the\n\ninstance of A.1 without proper verification.     She did not\n\ndream that this A.14 who was the resident of Hyderabad was\n\na criminal and he had indulged in these heinous crimes.\n\nThis innocent lady of the State lost her life, because she was\n\nbrought up in altogher a different culture.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_938\">501.        The said Deendar Anjuman Organization was\n\nbanned in the year 2001.       The proceedings to ban this\n\nOrganization was commenced in the year 2000 and it was\n\nconcluded in the year 2001 and the said ban is being\n\ncontinued year to year.      As on today, it is a banned\n\nOrganization.   Therefore, it is of utmost importance that a\n\nstrong message is to be sent to all these mis-guided, evil\n\nminded elements in the society and in particular, the youth\n\nof the Muslim community to desist from indulging in any\n\nsuch anti-national activities lest the entire community gets a\n\nbad name. This is a country and the State, which equally\n\nbelongs to all religious denominations.\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_651\">                                563</span><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_939\">\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_940\">502.         The Constitution confers to each citizen of this\n\nCountry, an absolute freedom to profess, practice and\n\npropogate religion. The right to propogate religion does not\n\nmean   one     religious   denomination     can    convert   people\n\nbelonging to other relgious denomination by force, threat or\n\ninducement and such acts are unconstitutional.               Taking\n\nadvantage of     Constitutional right of freedom to practice,\n\nprofess and propagate any religion, if unconstitutional things\n\nare done, the same requires to be dealt with a firm hand.\n\nThere is no question of showing any mercy on such persons.\n\nAny mercy shown to them would only result in their\n\nindulging in such activities, which would be against the\n\ninterest of the very community, as the evidence on record\n\nshows that most of the witnesses are the the family members\n\nof the accused, but were not aware of the real motive and\n\nintention behind the acts of these accused.\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_941\">503.         After   consideraing    all   the    aggravating   and\n\nmitigating circumstances, the learned Sessions Judge has\n\nrightly imposed life imprisonmesnt on all these accused.        No\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_652\">                                564</span>\n\n\n\n\ngrounds are made out for interfering with the said order. It\n\nwould send a strong messasge and act as a deterrent to all\n\nfence sitters who have imaginary and fanciful ideas of\n\nIslamising the whole of India. Such stringent punishment is\n\nthe need of the hour in cases of this kind to make such\n\npersons to fall in line and be a part of the main stream of the\n\ncountry. The sentence of life imprisonment to these accused\n\nshould be an eye opener to such misguided elements in the\n\nSociety.     Therefore, we do not see any justification to\n\ninterfere with the imposition of life imprisonment to the\n\naforesaid accused.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_942\">\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_943\">504.          In the result, we pass the following:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_409\">                           ORDER\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_410\">       (a)   Criminal Appeal No.1203/2008 is partly allowed.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_411\">       (b)   The Judgment of Conviction against A.10 is\n\n             hereby confirmed on all counts. However, the\n\n             sentence of death is modified by reducing it to\n\n             sentence of imprisonment for life.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_412\">       (c)   Criminal Appeal No.24/2009 is dismissed.\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_653\">                            565</span>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_413\">     (d)   Criminal Reference No.3/2008 is disposed of\n\n           with modification as above and accordingly it is\n\n           answered.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_944\">                                            Sd/-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_945\">                                           JUDGE\n\n\n                                            Sd/-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_946\">                                           JUDGE\n\n\n\n\nksp/sps\n </p>",
